topics.title: Static and Dynamic Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Robot Stability?

**Stability** in mobile robotics is the ability of a robot to maintain its structural integrity and intended posture without tipping over, falling, or losing balance, under the influence of gravity, inertial forces, and contact constraints with the ground. It is the single most critical design parameter for any legged, wheeled, or hybrid mobile platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *"Stability of a robot refers to its capacity to remain upright and in equilibrium while subjected to gravitational and dynamic forces. It is classified into **Static Stability** (motion-less or quasi-static conditions) and **Dynamic Stability** (during active motion)."*

---

## 1.2 Static Stability — Formal Definition

**Static stability** is the property of a robot to remain balanced when it is **stationary** (velocity = 0, acceleration = 0), or moving with negligible inertial effects such that Newton's equations reduce to pure force-balance.

Mathematically, a robot is **statically stable** if and only if the **vertical projection of its Center of Mass (CoM)** onto the ground plane lies **strictly inside** the **Support Polygon** (also called the *Polygon of Support* or *Contact Polygon*).

### 🔑 Key Metric: **The Support Polygon**
The support polygon is the convex hull formed by all ground-contact points (for legged robots) or the convex hull of wheel-ground contact patches (for wheeled robots). For a humanoid on two feet, this is essentially a rectangle traced by the four corners of the shoes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Center of Mass (CoM / CG):** The unique point where the weighted relative position of distributed mass sums to zero. Denoted $\mathbf{r}_{CoM}$. Units: **meters (m)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Stability Margin ($S_{sm}$):** The shortest distance from the CoM projection to any edge of the support polygon. Units: **meters (m)**.

---

## 1.3 Dynamic Stability — Formal Definition

**Dynamic stability** is the property of a robot to remain balanced **while in motion**, where inertial forces, Coriolis effects, centripetal accelerations, and contact friction cannot be neglected.

The key analytical tool is the **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** — introduced by Miomir Vukobratović (1972). A robot is dynamically stable if the ZMP lies strictly inside the support polygon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Moment Point (ZMP):** The point on the ground about which the **sum of moments due to gravity and inertial forces** equals zero. If this point migrates outside the support polygon, the robot tips over.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy 🧍‍♂️🚶

Imagine you are standing still with your feet together (a tiny support polygon). To stay upright, your body weight line must pass through that small area between your feet — that is **static stability**.

Now imagine you are walking on a moving train. Even if your body weight line momentarily leaves your foot's support polygon, you do **not** fall — because your forward momentum acts like an invisible "supporting leg" pushing you upright. That is **dynamic stability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:**
> - **Static** → CoM projection ∈ Support Polygon (no motion).
> - **Dynamic** → ZMP ∈ Support Polygon (during motion).

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU 📊

| Aspect | Static Stability | Dynamic Stability |
|---|---|---|
| **Applicable Robots** | Slow, heavy, multi-legged (e.g., 6-legged crawlers) | Fast, bipedal, wheeled (e.g., ASIMO, humanoid) |
| **Computation Cost** | Low (geometric) | High (requires inertial tensors) |
| **Key Tool** | CoM Projection | ZMP / FRI (Foot Rotation Indicator) |
| **Exam Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Stability — Condition and Margin

### 2.1.1 The Fundamental Condition

For a robot with total mass $M$ at center of mass $\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = (x_c, y_c, z_c)$, and a support polygon $\mathcal{P}$ defined by $n$ vertices $(x_i, y_i)$ on the ground ($z=0$):

The robot is **statically stable** if:
$$\mathbf{p}_{CoM}^{ground} = (x_c, y_c) \in \text{int}(\mathcal{P})$$

where $\text{int}(\mathcal{P})$ denotes the *interior* of the support polygon. If $(x_c, y_c)$ lies *on* the boundary, the robot is in a marginally stable (critical) state — any perturbation will tip it.

### 2.1.2 Static Stability Margin

For a planar support polygon, the margin is computed by checking the signed distance from $(x_c, y_c)$ to every edge.

Given edge $i$ from vertex $(x_i, y_i)$ to $(x_{i+1}, y_{i+1})$, the **signed distance** from $(x_c, y_c)$ to the line containing edge $i$ is:

$$d_i = \frac{(y_{i+1} - y_i) \cdot x_c - (x_{i+1} - x_i) \cdot y_c + x_{i+1} y_i - y_{i+1} x_i}{\sqrt{(y_{i+1} - y_i)^2 + (x_{i+1} - x_i)^2}}$$

The **Static Stability Margin** is:
$$S_{sm} = \min_{i=1}^{n}(d_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion:** $S_{sm} > 0$ → Statically Stable. $S_{sm} = 0$ → Critical. $S_{sm} < 0$ → Unstable (Tip-over).

### 2.1.3 Energy Stability Margin (Advanced)

Proposed by Messuri & Klein (1985), this metric accounts for the **height** of the CoM:

$$S_{em} = \min_i \left( h \cdot \left| \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{u}_i \right| \cdot \Delta t \right) \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

where $h$ is the CoM height, $\mathbf{g}$ is gravity vector, $\mathbf{u}_i$ is the unit vector to edge $i$, and $\Delta t$ is the tipping time. This is more physically meaningful than geometric margin.

---

## 2.2 Dynamic Stability — The Zero Moment Point (ZMP)

### 2.2.1 Derivation Logic

Consider a bipedal robot with CoM at height $h$ above the ground. The robot has linear acceleration $\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM}$ and angular acceleration. The ground exerts reaction forces at the feet. The ZMP is defined as the point where the **net moment about that point is zero**.

For a simplified single-mass model (cart-table model) used by Kagami et al.:

$$x_{ZMP} = x_c - \frac{z_c \cdot \ddot{x}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$$

$$y_{ZMP} = y_c - \frac{z_c \cdot \ddot{y}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$$

### 2.2.2 The Cart-Table Model Assumptions

1. All mass is concentrated at the CoM (lumped mass).
2. The support polygon is a thin table on wheels (massless).
3. The contact between wheels and ground is **rolling without slipping**.
4. Ground reaction force is normal to the ground plane.

### 2.2.3 Stability Criterion via ZMP

$$\text{Robot is dynamically stable} \iff (x_{ZMP}, y_{ZMP}) \in \text{int}(\mathcal{P}_{support})$$

If $(x_{ZMP}, y_{ZMP})$ exits $\mathcal{P}_{support}$, the foot begins to **rotate** about an edge — this is the **Foot Rotation Indicator (FRI)** point which lies on the boundary of the support polygon.

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value / norm expressions inside the table below use `\vert` instead of `\|` to prevent Markdown table syntax breaking.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Stability Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CoM Projection Condition | $(x_c, y_c) \in \text{int}(\mathcal{P})$ | dimensionless | Strict interior |
| 2 | Static Stability Margin | $S_{sm} = \min_i(d_i)$ | $\text{m}$ | $S_{sm} > 0$ |
| 3 | Energy Stability Margin | $S_{em} = \min_i(h \cdot \vert \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{u}_i \vert)$ | $\text{J}$ | $S_{em} > 0$ |
| 4 | ZMP $x$-coordinate | $x_{ZMP} = x_c - \dfrac{z_c \ddot{x}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$ | $\text{m}$ | Inside $\mathcal{P}$ |
| 5 | ZMP $y$-coordinate | $y_{ZMP} = y_c - \dfrac{z_c \ddot{y}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$ | $\text{m}$ | Inside $\mathcal{P}$ |
| 6 | Effective Force Balance | $M \mathbf{g} + M \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = \sum \mathbf{F}_i$ | $\text{N}$ | Newton-Euler |
| 7 | Moment about ZMP | $\sum_i (\mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_{ZMP}) \times \mathbf{F}_i = 0$ | $\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$ | Equilibrium |
| 8 | Tipping Threshold | $\tan\theta_{tip} = \dfrac{S_{sm}}{h}$ | radians | $\theta < \theta_{tip}$ |
| 9 | Stability Index (Sugihara) | $S_I = \dfrac{S_{sm}}{h}$ | dimensionless | $S_I > 0$ |
| 10 | Gait Stability (Trot) | $\Delta t_{support} < \Delta t_{swing}$ | $\text{s}$ | Dynamic catch |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Stability Type Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **Humanoid Robotics** (Honda ASIMO, Boston Dynamics Atlas) | Walking, running, stairs | Dynamic (ZMP-based) |
| **Hexapod Industrial Crawlers** (e.g.,救援 robots) | Mine inspection, pipe inspection | Static (CoM-based) |
| **Quadrupedal Robots** (Spot, ANYmal) | Rough terrain locomotion | Dynamic + Static (gait-dependent) |
| **Mars Rovers** (Curiosity, Perseverance) | Wheeled terrain traversal | Static (slow speeds) |
| **Human Prosthetics** | Active above-knee prostheses | Dynamic (ankle push-off) |
| **Wheeled Delivery Robots** | Last-mile delivery on flat ground | Static (low CoM design) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Static Stability Margin for a Triangular Support Polygon

### Problem Setup
A three-legged robot has its feet at the ground contact points:
- Foot A: $(0, 0)$
- Foot B: $(1.0, 0)$
- Foot C: $(0.5, 0.866)$

The CoM projects onto the ground at $(0.45, 0.30)$. The CoM height is $h = 0.6 \text{ m}$. Compute the static stability margin and the tipping angle threshold.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the three edges** of the support polygon $\mathcal{P}$:

| Edge | From | To |
|---|---|---|
| $E_1$ | $(0, 0)$ | $(1.0, 0)$ |
| $E_2$ | $(1.0, 0)$ | $(0.5, 0.866)$ |
| $E_3$ | $(0.5, 0.866)$ | $(0, 0)$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the signed distance formula** to each edge. Use the standard form:

$$d_i = \frac{(y_{i+1} - y_i)x_c - (x_{i+1} - x_i)y_c + x_{i+1}y_i - y_{i+1}x_i}{\sqrt{(y_{i+1} - y_i)^2 + (x_{i+1} - x_i)^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Distance to Edge $E_1$** (from $(0,0)$ to $(1.0, 0)$):

$$d_1 = \frac{(0 - 0)(0.45) - (1.0 - 0)(0.30) + (1.0)(0) - (0)(0)}{\sqrt{(0)^2 + (1.0)^2}}$$

$$d_1 = \frac{0 - 0.30 + 0 - 0}{\sqrt{1}} = \frac{-0.30}{1} = -0.30 \text{ m}$$

**Interpretation:** The CoM projection lies **0.30 m below** the line containing $E_1$ (i.e., outside the polygon on this side).

**Step 4 — Distance to Edge $E_2$** (from $(1.0, 0)$ to $(0.5, 0.866)$):

$$d_2 = \frac{(0.866 - 0)(0.45) - (0.5 - 1.0)(0.30) + (0.5)(0) - (0.866)(1.0)}{\sqrt{(0.866)^2 + (-0.5)^2}}$$

$$d_2 = \frac{(0.866)(0.45) - (-0.5)(0.30) + 0 - 0.866}{\sqrt{0.75 + 0.25}}$$

$$d_2 = \frac{0.3897 + 0.15 - 0.866}{1.0} = \frac{-0.3263}{1.0} = -0.3263 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Distance to Edge $E_3$** (from $(0.5, 0.866)$ to $(0, 0)$):

$$d_3 = \frac{(0 - 0.866)(0.45) - (0 - 0.5)(0.30) + (0)(0.866) - (0)(0.5)}{\sqrt{(-0.866)^2 + (0.5)^2}}$$

$$d_3 = \frac{(-0.866)(0.45) - (-0.5)(0.30) + 0 - 0}{1.0}$$

$$d_3 = \frac{-0.3897 + 0.15}{1.0} = -0.2397 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Static Stability Margin:**

$$S_{sm} = \min\{d_1, d_2, d_3\} = \min\{-0.30, -0.3263, -0.2397\} = -0.3263 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result:**

> [!WARNING]
> Since $S_{sm} = -0.3263 \text{ m} < 0$, the robot is **STATICALLY UNSTABLE**. The CoM projection lies outside the support polygon. The robot will tip over, rotating about **Edge $E_2$** (the edge connecting foot B to foot C) — the edge with the smallest (most negative) signed distance.

**Step 8 — Compute the tipping angle threshold** if the robot were hypothetically repositioned. Using:

$$\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left(\frac{S_{sm}}{h}\right)$$

For a *marginally stable* configuration where $S_{sm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ and $h = 0.6 \text{ m}$:

$$\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left(\frac{0.05}{0.6}\right) = \arctan(0.0833) \approx 4.76^\circ$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ZMP Computation for a Bipedal Walking Step

### Problem Setup
A bipedal robot is walking. At the instant of analysis:
- CoM position: $\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = (0.30 \text{ m}, \ 0.0 \text{ m}, \ 0.85 \text{ m})$
- CoM acceleration: $\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} = (1.2 \text{ m/s}^2, \ 0.0 \text{ m/s}^2, \ -0.4 \text{ m/s}^2)$
- Gravitational acceleration: $\mathbf{g} = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (downward)

The support polygon during single-support phase is a rectangle with corners at $(\pm 0.10, \pm 0.06)$ m. Determine whether the robot is dynamically stable.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the ZMP formula in $x$-direction:**

$$x_{ZMP} = x_c - \frac{z_c \cdot \ddot{x}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$$

Substituting $x_c = 0.30$, $z_c = 0.85$, $\ddot{x}_c = 1.2$, $g = 9.81$, $\ddot{z}_c = -0.4$:

$$x_{ZMP} = 0.30 - \frac{(0.85)(1.2)}{9.81 + (-0.4)}$$

$$x_{ZMP} = 0.30 - \frac{1.02}{9.41}$$

$$x_{ZMP} = 0.30 - 0.1084$$

$$x_{ZMP} = 0.1916 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ZMP formula in $y$-direction:**

$$y_{ZMP} = y_c - \frac{z_c \cdot \ddot{y}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$$

Substituting $y_c = 0.0$, $\ddot{y}_c = 0.0$:

$$y_{ZMP} = 0.0 - \frac{(0.85)(0.0)}{9.41} = 0.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the ZMP location:**

$$\mathbf{r}_{ZMP} = (0.1916 \text{ m}, \ 0.0 \text{ m})$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the support polygon** $\mathcal{P} = [-0.10, +0.10] \times [-0.06, +0.06]$:

- $x$-coordinate check: $0.1916 \notin [-0.10, +0.10]$ ❌
- The ZMP lies **0.0916 m outside** the support polygon in the $+x$ direction.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

> [!WARNING]
> The robot is **DYNAMICALLY UNSTABLE** at this instant. The ZMP has migrated outside the support polygon, indicating that the foot will begin to rotate about its front edge. This typically causes the robot to pitch forward (face-plant) unless corrective ankle torques or CoM trajectory adjustments are applied immediately.

**Step 6 — Suggested corrective action:** Reduce forward acceleration $\ddot{x}_c$ until ZMP re-enters the support polygon. Solving for the maximum allowable $\ddot{x}_c$ at the boundary $x_{ZMP} = 0.10$ m:

$$0.10 = 0.30 - \frac{0.85 \cdot \ddot{x}_c^{max}}{9.41}$$

$$\frac{0.85 \cdot \ddot{x}_c^{max}}{9.41} = 0.20$$

$$\ddot{x}_c^{max} = \frac{0.20 \times 9.41}{0.85} = 2.214 \text{ m/s}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Any forward acceleration above **2.214 m/s²** will cause the robot to lose dynamic stability in this configuration. A motion planner must enforce $\ddot{x}_c \le 2.214 \text{ m/s}^2$ throughout the single-support phase.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Static and Dynamic Stability

**Claim:** In the limit of zero acceleration ($\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_{CoM} \to \mathbf{0}$), the ZMP reduces to the CoM projection.

**Proof:**

Starting from:
$$x_{ZMP} = x_c - \frac{z_c \ddot{x}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$$

Let $\ddot{x}_c \to 0$ and $\ddot{z}_c \to 0$. Then:

$$x_{ZMP} \to x_c - \frac{z_c \cdot 0}{g + 0} = x_c - 0 = x_c$$

Similarly:
$$y_{ZMP} \to y_c$$

Hence, in the static limit:
$$\lim_{\ddot{\mathbf{r}} \to \mathbf{0}} \mathbf{r}_{ZMP} = \mathbf{r}_{CoM}^{ground}$$

This proves the **mathematical consistency** between static and dynamic stability frameworks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.2 Block Diagram: ZMP-Based Stability Feedback Control

This block diagram shows how a humanoid robot uses ZMP measurement in a closed-loop feedback controller to maintain dynamic balance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Walking Trajectory] --> B[CoM Planner Module]
    B --> C[Inverse Dynamics Solver]
    C --> D[Joint Torque Commands]
    D --> E[Bipedal Robot Body]
    
    E --> F[Force Torque Sensors at Feet]
    F --> G[ZMP Calculator Block]
    
    G --> H[Measured ZMP Position]
    H --> I[Comparator with Reference ZMP]
    A --> I
    
    I --> J[Error Signal eZMP]
    J --> K[PID or LQR Controller]
    K --> L[CoM Trajectory Correction]
    L --> B
    
    E --> M[Gravity and Inertial Forces]
    M --> G
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Static Stability Margin. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Static Stability Margin ($S_{sm}$) is defined as the **shortest perpendicular distance** from the vertical projection of the robot's Center of Mass (CoM) onto the ground plane to any boundary edge of the support polygon. Mathematically:

$$S_{sm} = \min_{i=1}^{n}(d_i)$$

where $d_i$ is the signed distance from the CoM projection to the $i$-th edge of the $n$-sided support polygon.

**Stability Criteria:**
- $S_{sm} > 0$ → Robot is **statically stable** (CoM projection is strictly inside the polygon).
- $S_{sm} = 0$ → Robot is at the **critical tipping boundary** (any disturbance will cause fall).
- $S_{sm} < 0$ → Robot is **statically unstable** (CoM projection lies outside the polygon; robot will tip about the nearest edge).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** A larger $S_{sm}$ implies greater robustness against external disturbances such as wind, ground irregularities, or payload shifts. Hexapod robots (6 legs) typically achieve larger $S_{sm}$ than bipeds (2 legs) because their support polygon is larger.

---

### Question 2: What is the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) and why is it preferred for bipedal robots? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the specific point on the ground plane at which the **net moment of the ground reaction force** (generated by gravity and inertial forces acting on the robot) is zero. It was introduced by Miomir Vukobratović in 1972 for the analysis of dynamic balance in legged locomotion.

**Mathematical Definition:**

For a lumped-mass model at CoM position $\mathbf{r}_c = (x_c, y_c, z_c)$ and acceleration $\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_c$:

$$x_{ZMP} = x_c - \frac{z_c \ddot{x}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}, \quad y_{ZMP} = y_c - \frac{z_c \ddot{y}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$$

**Why Preferred for Bipedal Robots:**
1. **Direct measurability** — ZMP can be measured in real-time using **force-torque sensors** placed at the robot's ankles/feet.
2. **Closed-loop control compatibility** — The measured ZMP can be compared against a reference ZMP trajectory to generate corrective ankle and hip torques via PID/LQR control.
3. **Real-time computability** — Requires only CoM kinematics and inertial parameters, not full-body dynamics.
4. **Industrial adoption** — Used by Honda ASIMO, Toyota HRP series, and Aldebaran NAO robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always define ZMP with both *geometric* meaning (point on ground) AND *physical* meaning (zero moment condition). Both definitions are required for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📌 Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**

#### (a) Derive the equation for the Zero Moment Point (ZMP) using the cart-table model for a bipedal robot in single-support phase. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Definition:**
Consider a cart of mass $M$ mounted on a massless table of height $h$, with the cart constrained to move horizontally. The cart represents the lumped Center of Mass of the robot.

**Step 2 — Free Body Diagram and Force Analysis:**

Forces acting on the system:
- Gravity: $\mathbf{F}_g = (0, 0, -Mg)$ acting at the cart position.
- Inertial force due to cart acceleration: $\mathbf{F}_I = (-M\ddot{x}, 0, -M\ddot{z})$
- Ground reaction force at the contact point: $\mathbf{F}_R = (F_{Rx}, 0, F_{Rz})$

Newton's Second Law (vertical equilibrium):
$$F_{Rz} - Mg - M\ddot{z} = 0 \implies F_{Rz} = M(g + \ddot{z})$$

**Step 3 — Moment about an arbitrary ground point $(x_p, 0, 0)$:**

The moment due to all forces about this point must equal the rate of change of angular momentum $\dot{\mathbf{H}}$:

$$\sum \mathbf{M}_{(x_p, 0, 0)} = (x_c - x_p) F_{Rz} - (z_c - 0) F_{Rx} - M(x_c - x_p)\ddot{z} + M z_c \ddot{x} = 0$$

(The $\dot{\mathbf{H}}$ terms for a point-mass model reduce to zero about the CoM, so they cancel here.)

**Step 4 — Definition of ZMP:**

The ZMP $(x_{ZMP}, y_{ZMP})$ is the specific ground point where the net moment about the horizontal axes is **zero**. Setting $x_p = x_{ZMP}$ and solving for the horizontal moment equilibrium (taking moments about the $y$-axis at $(x_{ZMP}, 0, 0)$):

$$x_{ZMP} F_{Rz} - z_c F_{Rx} = 0$$

Substituting $F_{Rz} = M(g + \ddot{z})$ and $F_{Rx} = M\ddot{x}$ (since horizontal reaction balances inertial force):

$$x_{ZMP} \cdot M(g + \ddot{z}) = z_c \cdot M\ddot{x}$$

**Step 5 — Final ZMP Equation:**

$$\boxed{x_{ZMP} = x_c - \frac{z_c \ddot{x}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}}$$

(Here, $F_{Rx}$ appears with a sign convention that, when combined with Newton's second law, produces the standard form above. The key derivation insight is the moment-equilibrium condition.)

By symmetry in the lateral direction:
$$y_{ZMP} = y_c - \frac{z_c \ddot{y}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c}$$

---

#### (b) A quadruped robot is standing on a rectangular support polygon with feet at $(0.3, 0.25)$, $(0.3, -0.25)$, $(-0.3, -0.25)$, $(-0.3, 0.25)$. The CoM projection is at $(0.05, 0.0)$ and the CoM height is $h = 0.4$ m. Calculate: (i) the Static Stability Margin, (ii) the tipping angle threshold, and (iii) whether the robot is statically stable. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the four edges of the support polygon:**

| Edge | From | To |
|---|---|---|
| $E_1$ | $(0.3, 0.25)$ | $(0.3, -0.25)$ |
| $E_2$ | $(0.3, -0.25)$ | $(-0.3, -0.25)$ |
| $E_3$ | $(-0.3, -0.25)$ | $(-0.3, 0.25)$ |
| $E_4$ | $(-0.3, 0.25)$ | $(0.3, 0.25)$ |

**Step 2 — Compute signed distance to each edge** with $(x_c, y_c) = (0.05, 0.0)$:

**Edge $E_1$** (vertical edge at $x = 0.3$):
$$d_1 = \frac{(0.3 - 0.3)(0.05) - (-0.25 - 0.25)(0.0) + (-0.3)(0.25) - (-0.25)(0.3)}{\sqrt{(-0.5)^2 + 0^2}}$$

Simplifying: $d_1 = 0 - 0 + (-0.075 + 0.075) = 0$... Wait, recomputing carefully:

$$d_1 = \frac{(y_2 - y_1)x_c - (x_2 - x_1)y_c + x_2 y_1 - y_2 x_1}{\sqrt{(y_2 - y_1)^2 + (x_2 - x_1)^2}}$$

For $E_1$: $x_1=0.3, y_1=0.25, x_2=0.3, y_2=-0.25$:
$$d_1 = \frac{(-0.25 - 0.25)(0.05) - (0.3 - 0.3)(0.0) + (0.3)(0.25) - (-0.25)(0.3)}{\sqrt{(-0.5)^2 + 0^2}}$$

$$d_1 = \frac{(-0.5)(0.05) - 0 + 0.075 + 0.075}{0.5} = \frac{-0.025 + 0.15}{0.5} = \frac{0.125}{0.5} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Edge $E_2$** (horizontal edge at $y = -0.25$): $x_1=0.3, y_1=-0.25, x_2=-0.3, y_2=-0.25$:
$$d_2 = \frac{(-0.25 - (-0.25))(0.05) - (-0.3 - 0.3)(0.0) + (-0.3)(-0.25) - (-0.25)(0.3)}{\sqrt{0 + (-0.6)^2}}$$

$$d_2 = \frac{0 - 0 + 0.075 + 0.075}{0.6} = \frac{0.15}{0.6} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Edge $E_3$** (vertical edge at $x = -0.3$): $x_1=-0.3, y_1=-0.25, x_2=-0.3, y_2=0.25$:
$$d_3 = \frac{(0.25 - (-0.25))(0.05) - (-0.3 - (-0.3))(0.0) + (-0.3)(-0.25) - (0.25)(-0.3)}{0.5}$$

$$d_3 = \frac{(0.5)(0.05) - 0 + 0.075 + 0.075}{0.5} = \frac{0.025 + 0.15}{0.5} = \frac{0.175}{0.5} = 0.35 \text{ m}$$

**Edge $E_4$** (horizontal edge at $y = 0.25$): $x_1=-0.3, y_1=0.25, x_2=0.3, y_2=0.25$:
$$d_4 = \frac{(0.25 - 0.25)(0.05) - (0.3 - (-0.3))(0.0) + (0.3)(0.25) - (0.25)(-0.3)}{0.6}$$

$$d_4 = \frac{0 - 0 + 0.075 + 0.075}{0.6} = \frac{0.15}{0.6} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Static Stability Margin:**
$$S_{sm} = \min\{0.25, 0.25, 0.35, 0.25\} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Tipping Angle Threshold:**
$$\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left(\frac{S_{sm}}{h}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{0.25}{0.4}\right) = \arctan(0.625) \approx 32.0^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{S_{sm} = 0.25 \text{ m} > 0 \implies \text{Robot is STATICALLY STABLE}}$$

The robot can tolerate an external tilt of up to **32°** before tipping.

---

### 📌 Question B (Choice 2 - Alternative) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]**

#### (a) Explain with neat diagrams the concept of Support Polygon and Center of Mass projection for static stability analysis. How does the number of legs affect stability in legged robots? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Support Polygon Definition:**
The **Support Polygon** $\mathcal{P}$ is defined as the convex hull of all points of contact between the robot and the ground. For a legged robot, these contact points are the foot-ground patches. For a wheeled robot, they are the tire contact points.

**2. Center of Mass (CoM) Projection:**
The Center of Mass $\mathbf{r}_{CoM}$ is the mass-weighted average position of all body parts. Its vertical projection onto the ground plane is the point $(x_c, y_c)$. For a rigid body composed of $n$ point masses $m_i$ at positions $\mathbf{r}_i$:

$$\mathbf{r}_{CoM} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \mathbf{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}$$

**3. Stability Condition:**
A statically stable robot must satisfy:
$$(x_c, y_c) \in \text{int}(\mathcal{P})$$

**4. Effect of Number of Legs:**

| Robot Type | Legs | Static Stance Stability | Typical Support Polygon |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biped (Humanoid) | 2 | **Marginally stable** at rest | Small rectangle |
| Triped | 3 | Conditionally stable | Triangle |
| Quadruped | 4 | Stable | Quadrilateral |
| Hexapod | 6 | **Highly stable** | Hexagon (large area) |
| Octopod | 8 | Maximally stable | Octagon |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** More legs → Larger support polygon → Higher static stability margin → Better disturbance rejection. This is why insects (6 legs) and spiders (8 legs) are inherently more stable than bipedal humans.

**5. Static Stability Margin Variation:**

For a robot with leg span $L$ and CoM at height $h$:
- **Biped (2 legs):** $S_{sm} \approx L/4$ (small margin)
- **Quadruped (4 legs):** $S_{sm} \approx L/2$ (moderate margin)
- **Hexapod (6 legs, tripod gait):** $S_{sm} \approx L\sqrt{3}/2$ (large margin)

**6. Tipping Condition:**
A robot tips when the CoM projection exits the support polygon. The critical tipping angle (for a robot of height $h$ and support polygon half-width $w$) is:
$$\theta_{critical} = \arctan\left(\frac{w}{h}\right)$$

---

#### (b) A bipedal robot walks with CoM at $(0.25, 0.0, 0.80)$ m and acceleration $(0.6, 0.0, -0.2)$ m/s². The single-support polygon is a $0.2 \text{ m} \times 0.12 \text{ m}$ rectangle centered at the origin. Determine: (i) the ZMP coordinates, (ii) whether the robot is dynamically stable, and (iii) the maximum allowable forward acceleration for stability. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters:**
- $x_c = 0.25$ m, $y_c = 0.0$ m, $z_c = 0.80$ m
- $\ddot{x}_c = 0.6$ m/s², $\ddot{y}_c = 0.0$ m/s², $\ddot{z}_c = -0.2$ m/s²
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Support polygon: $[-0.10, +0.10] \times [-0.06, +0.06]$

**Step 2 — Compute ZMP $x$-coordinate:**
$$x_{ZMP} = x_c - \frac{z_c \ddot{x}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c} = 0.25 - \frac{(0.80)(0.6)}{9.81 + (-0.2)}$$

$$x_{ZMP} = 0.25 - \frac{0.48}{9.61} = 0.25 - 0.04995 = 0.20005 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ZMP $y$-coordinate:**
$$y_{ZMP} = y_c - \frac{z_c \ddot{y}_c}{g + \ddot{z}_c} = 0.0 - \frac{(0.80)(0.0)}{9.61} = 0.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Determine Stability:**
$$\mathbf{r}_{ZMP} = (0.200, \ 0.0) \text{ m}$$

Support polygon boundaries: $x \in [-0.10, +0.10]$.

Since $x_{ZMP} = 0.200 > 0.10$, the ZMP lies **0.10 m outside** the support polygon.

> [!WARNING]
> **Robot is DYNAMICALLY UNSTABLE.** The foot will rotate about its front edge — the robot will pitch forward.

**Step 5 — Compute Maximum Allowable Forward Acceleration:**

For ZMP to just reach the boundary $x_{ZMP} = 0.10$ m (front edge of support):

$$0.10 = 0.25 - \frac{0.80 \cdot \ddot{x}_c^{max}}{9.61}$$

$$\frac{0.80 \cdot \ddot{x}_c^{max}}{9.61} = 0.15$$

$$\ddot{x}_c^{max} = \frac{0.15 \times 9.61}{0.80} = \frac{1.4415}{0.80} = 1.802 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\ddot{x}_c^{max} \approx 1.802 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

The robot must limit its forward acceleration to **1.802 m/s²** or below. To achieve faster walking, the controller must either (a) lower the CoM height $z_c$, (b) increase the foot footprint, or (c) implement ankle-torque-based ZMP compensation.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator:** In the ZMP formula, students often write $x_{ZMP} = x_c - z_c \ddot{x}_c / g$ instead of $x_c - z_c \ddot{x}_c / (g + \ddot{z}_c)$. **Loss: 2 marks** in derivation questions.
> 
> 2. **Sign confusion in signed distance:** The signed distance $d_i$ can be positive or negative. Students frequently take absolute values, losing the directional information that indicates WHICH edge the robot will tip over. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 3. **Static vs Dynamic Confusion:** Students sometimes apply CoM projection condition to a moving robot, or apply ZMP to a stationary robot. Remember: Static → CoM projection; Dynamic → ZMP. **Loss: 2-3 marks.**
> 
> 4. **Skipping units in final answers:** KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks if units are missing. Always write "$m$", "$m/s^2$", "$J$", etc.
> 
> 5. **Drawing without labeling:** When asked for diagrams, students draw support polygons but forget to label the vertices, the CoM projection, and the stability margin distance. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 
> 6. **Not stating the conclusion explicitly:** Always end your solution with a clear boxed answer and a sentence stating "The robot is statically stable/unstable" or "The robot is dynamically stable/unstable". Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for the inference/conclusion.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Static and Dynamic Stability**

### ✅ Static Stability Essentials
- ⭐ The **CoM projection** must lie strictly **inside** the support polygon.
- ⭐ **Support Polygon** = Convex hull of all ground contact points.
- ⭐ **Static Stability Margin** $S_{sm} = \min_i(d_i)$ — shortest distance from CoM projection to polygon edges.
- ⭐ **Stability Criterion:** $S_{sm} > 0$ (stable), $S_{sm} = 0$ (critical), $S_{sm} < 0$ (unstable).
- ⭐ **More legs = larger support polygon = higher stability.**
- ⭐ **Tipping angle:** $\theta_{tip} = \arctan(S_{sm}/h)$.
- ⭐ **Energy Stability Margin** (Messuri-Klein) accounts for CoM height — more physically meaningful.

### ✅ Dynamic Stability Essentials
- ⭐ **ZMP** is the point on the ground where net moment = 0.
- ⭐ **ZMP Formula:** $x_{ZMP} = x_c - z_c \ddot{x}_c / (g + \ddot{z}_c)$ and analogous for $y$.
- ⭐ **Dynamic Stability Criterion:** ZMP must lie inside support polygon.
- ⭐ **Cart-Table Model** assumes lumped mass at CoM + massless table.
- ⭐ **FRI** (Foot Rotation Indicator) = the actual point where moment is zero; if FRI ≠ ZMP, foot is rotating (unstable).
- ⭐ **CMP** (Centroidal Moment Pivot) = horizontal component of total ground reaction force application point.
- ⭐ **Limit Case:** When $\ddot{\mathbf{r}} \to \mathbf{0}$, ZMP $\to$ CoM projection (consistency between frameworks).

### ✅ Critical Numerical Values to Memorize
- ⭐ $g = 9.81$ m/s² (standard gravitational acceleration)
- ⭐ Hexapod stability margin ≈ $L\sqrt{3}/2$ where $L$ is leg span.
- ⭐ Quadruped stability margin ≈ $L/2$.
- ⭐ Biped stability margin ≈ $L/4$.

### ✅ Common Exam Question Types
- ⭐ Compute $S_{sm}$ given polygonal feet positions and CoM projection.
- ⭐ Derive ZMP from moment equilibrium.
- ⭐ Determine if robot is stable for given CoM and acceleration.
- ⭐ Find maximum allowable acceleration for ZMP to remain inside support polygon.
- ⭐ Compare stability of biped vs quadruped vs hexapod.
- ⭐ Apply static stability analysis to specific legged/wheeled configurations.

### ✅ Real-World Robots Using These Concepts
- ⭐ **ASIMO, Atlas, NAO** — ZMP-based dynamic stability control.
- ⭐ **Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYmal** — ZMP + model-predictive control.
- ⭐ **Mars Rovers (Curiosity)** — Static stability with low CoM.
- ⭐ **BigDog (Legged Squad Support System)** — Dynamic stability with ZMP and active balancing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Degrees of Freedom
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Degrees of Freedom

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics, the **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is defined as the **number of independent parameters (variables)** that uniquely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of a robotic mechanism or its end-effector with respect to a reference coordinate frame. Each Degree of Freedom corresponds to one independently controllable axis of motion — either translational (linear) or rotational (angular).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *"A Degree of Freedom (DOF) is an independent displacement or rotation that can be specified to define the location of a rigid body in space. A rigid body in 3D space possesses a maximum of **6 Degrees of Freedom** — 3 translational and 3 rotational."*

Mathematically, the total Degrees of Freedom of a robot is the sum of the degrees of freedom of all its individual joints:

$$DOF_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} DOF_{joint,i}$$

where $n$ is the total number of actuated joints in the kinematic chain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Human-Arm Analogy:**
> Imagine your own **human arm** reaching for a coffee cup. Your shoulder lets you swing forward/back, lift up/down, and rotate (3 DOF). Your elbow lets you bend (1 DOF). Your wrist lets you tilt up/down, side-to-side, and twist (3 DOF). That is **7 DOF** total! Even though a cup has 6 DOF in 3D space, the extra 1 DOF in your arm lets you choose the most *comfortable* elbow angle — this is the famous "redundancy" concept in robotics.

A **translational DOF** moves a point along a straight line (like an elevator).  
A **rotational DOF** rotates a body about an axis (like a revolving door).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:**  
> **T**ranslation = **T**raight line (X, Y, Z axes → 3 DOF)  
> **R**otation = **R**evolution around an axis (Roll, Pitch, Yaw → 3 DOF)  
> **Total Rigid Body in 3D = 6 DOF**

### 1.3 DOF Summary for Common Robotic Platforms

| Robot Type | Typical DOF | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Differential Drive (Roomba) | **2** | $(x, y)$ translation only |
| Car (Ackermann Steering) | **3** | $(x, y, \theta)$ on plane |
| Quadcopter (Drone) | **4 or 6** | 4 rotors → 4 DOF basic; full pose = 6 |
| Industrial 6-Axis Arm | **6** | Full spatial reach and orientation |
| Humanoid Robot (Full Body) | **20–30+** | Many joints, highly redundant |
| Human Arm (Anatomical) | **7** | Shoulder(3) + Elbow(1) + Wrist(3) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Degrees of Freedom

A DOF can be **Translational** OR **Rotational** — never both at the same joint. The 6-DOF of a rigid body in 3D space are:

**Translational DOF (Position):**
- **X-axis:** Forward / Backward motion (Surge)
- **Y-axis:** Left / Right motion (Sway)
- **Z-axis:** Up / Down motion (Heave)

**Rotational DOF (Orientation):**
- **Roll:** Rotation about the X-axis
- **Pitch:** Rotation about the Y-axis
- **Yaw:** Rotation about the Z-axis

### 2.2 Types of Robot Joints and Their DOF Contributions

| Joint Type | Symbol | DOF Provided | Motion Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute (R) | 🔄 | **1** | Rotation about a single axis |
| Prismatic (P) | ⬌ | **1** | Linear sliding along an axis |
| Helical (H) | 🌀 | **1** | Combined rotation + translation (screw) |
| Cylindrical (C) | ⬜ | **2** | Rotation + sliding along same axis |
| Spherical (S) | ⚽ | **3** | Ball-and-socket, 3 rotations |
| Planar (E) | ⬛ | **3** | 2 translations + 1 rotation in a plane |
| Universal (U) | ⨯ | **2** | 2 intersecting rotational axes |

### 2.3 Grübler's Formula — The Heart of DOF Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grübler's Formula** (also called Kutzbach Criterion) is the most asked formula in KTU exams for DOF problems. **Memorize both versions!**

**For Planar Mechanisms (2D):**

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

**For Spatial Mechanisms (3D):**

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

**Where:**
- $M$ = Mobility (DOF) of the mechanism
- $n$ = Total number of **links** (including ground)
- $j_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** (1-DOF joints: R, P, H)
- $j_2$ = Number of **higher pairs** (2-DOF joints: C, S, E)
- $j_3$ = Number of **3-DOF joints** (Spherical)
- $j_4$ = Number of **4-DOF joints**
- $j_5$ = Number of **5-DOF joints**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the formula work?** The $3(n-1)$ term gives the total independent motions of all links except ground. Each 1-DOF joint removes 2 DOF (constrains the two perpendicular motions), each 2-DOF joint removes 1 DOF, and so on. This is a constraint-based DOF analysis.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield)

| Concept | Formula | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|
| DOF of a rigid body in 2D | $3$ | (2 trans + 1 rot) |
| DOF of a rigid body in 3D | $6$ | (3 trans + 3 rot) |
| Total robot DOF | $\sum DOF_{joint}$ | Scalar (integer) |
| Grübler (Planar) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Scalar (integer) |
| Grübler (Spatial) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Scalar (integer) |
| Manipulator workspace | Function of DOF, link lengths, joint limits | Volume / Area |
| Position vector (3D) | $\vec{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ | 3 components |
| Orientation (RPY) | $[\phi, \theta, \psi]$ | 3 angles |
| Homogeneous transform size | $4 \times 4$ matrix | 16 elements |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robot Arm Design:** Knowing the DOF helps decide if the robot can reach all positions/orientations in a workspace (e.g., a 6-DOF arm can achieve any 6-DOF pose, while a 4-DOF arm cannot).
- **Redundancy Analysis:** DOF > 6 means a *redundant* robot, useful for obstacle avoidance.
- **Mechanism Synthesis:** DOF calculation tells designers if a mechanism will move (M=1), be a structure (M=0), or have special motion (M>1).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why a Rigid Body in 3D has Exactly 6 DOF

**Step 1 — Define the Configuration of a Point:**  
A single point in 3D Euclidean space requires **3 coordinates** to specify its position: $(x, y, z)$. This gives **3 translational DOF**.

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Orientation of a Rigid Body:**  
A rigid body has an *orientation* in addition to a position. Orientation requires **3 independent angles** (e.g., Roll $\phi$, Pitch $\theta$, Yaw $\psi$).

$$R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Combine into the Pose Vector:**  
The complete configuration (pose) of a rigid body is given by a **6-dimensional vector**:

$$\xi = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{DOF_{rigid\_body\_3D} = 3_{(translation)} + 3_{(rotation)} = 6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** These 6 are *minimum* and *sufficient* — you cannot describe a rigid body's pose in 3D with fewer than 6 numbers (without constraints like being on a plane).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Grübler's Formula for Planar Mechanisms

**Step 1 — Start with a Free Body:**  
Each free link in a plane has **3 DOF** (2 translations + 1 rotation).

**Step 2 — Total DOF of Unconnected Links:**  
For $n$ links (including the fixed ground link), total unconstrained DOF = $3n$.

**Step 3 — Subtract the DOF of the Ground:**  
One link is fixed (ground) and loses all 3 DOF. So free DOF $= 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$.

**Step 4 — Subtract Constraints Imposed by Joints:**  
- A 1-DOF joint (revolute or prismatic) removes **2** independent motions, contributing $-2j_1$.
- A 2-DOF joint removes **1** independent motion, contributing $-j_2$.

**Step 5 — Final Mobility Equation:**

$$\boxed{M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Example 1: Four-Bar Linkage (Planar)

**Given:** A planar four-bar linkage has 4 links (1 ground + 3 moving) and 4 revolute joints.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \quad (\text{ground} + 3 \text{ moving links}) \\
j_1 &= 4 \quad (\text{all 4 joints are 1-DOF revolute}) \\
j_2 &= 0 \\
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
M &= 3(3) - 8 \\
M &= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{M = 1 \text{ DOF (One input motion drives the whole mechanism)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A four-bar linkage with M=1 is a *function generator* — one actuator (e.g., a motor on the input crank) is enough to fully control the mechanism. This is the basis of car windshield wipers and sewing machines.

---

### 3.4 Worked-Out Example 2: Slider-Crank Mechanism (Planar)

**Given:** A slider-crank has 4 links (ground, crank, connecting rod, slider) and joints: 3 revolute + 1 prismatic.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \\
j_1 &= 4 \quad (\text{3 R-joints} + \text{1 P-joint, all 1-DOF}) \\
j_2 &= 0 \\
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
M &= 9 - 8 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{M = 1 \text{ DOF (Used in IC engines and pumps)}}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked-Out Example 3: Planar 3R Robot Arm

**Given:** A planar manipulator with 3 revolute joints connecting 3 moving links to ground.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \quad (\text{ground} + 3 \text{ links}) \\
j_1 &= 3 \quad (\text{3 revolute joints}) \\
j_2 &= 0 \\
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 0 \\
M &= 9 - 6 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{M = 3 \text{ DOF (Can reach any }(x,y,\theta)\text{ in its workspace)}}$$

---

### 3.6 Worked-Out Example 4: Spatial 6-DOF Industrial Robot

**Given:** A standard 6R industrial robot (like KUKA or ABB) — 6 revolute joints in 3D space, 7 links (1 ground + 6 moving).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 7 \quad (\text{ground} + 6 \text{ links}) \\
j_1 &= 6 \quad (\text{6 revolute joints, all 1-DOF}) \\
j_2 &= j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0 \\
M &= 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
M &= 6(6) - 30 \\
M &= 36 - 30 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{M = 6 \text{ DOF (Full pose control in 3D)}}$$

---

### 3.7 Worked-Out Example 5: Spherical-Wrist Robot with Redundancy

**Given:** A 7R spatial manipulator (7 revolute joints) — like the human-arm analogy. 8 links (1 ground + 7 moving), 7 revolute joints.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 8 \\
j_1 &= 7 \\
j_2 &= j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0 \\
M &= 6(8-1) - 5(7) \\
M &= 6(7) - 35 \\
M &= 42 - 35 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{M = 7 \text{ DOF (Redundant: can do same task infinitely many ways)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Redundancy = M > 6:** Such a robot has infinitely many joint configurations to reach the same end-effector pose. This is exploited in obstacle avoidance and singularity avoidance.

---

### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Joint Type vs DOF

| Mechanism / Joint Setup | $n$ | $j_1$ | $j_2$ | DOF (M) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4-Bar Linkage | 4 | 4 | 0 | **1** | Wipers, walking toys |
| Slider-Crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | **1** | IC Engine piston |
| 5-Bar Planar | 5 | 5 | 0 | **2** | Planar 2-DOF robot |
| 3R Planar Arm | 4 | 3 | 0 | **3** | SCARA-type pick-place |
| RP Planar | 3 | 2 | 0 | **1** | Cartesian picker |
| 6R Spatial | 7 | 6 | 0 | **6** | Industrial robot arm |
| 7R Spatial (Redundant) | 8 | 7 | 0 | **7** | Humanoid arms |
| RR Spatial (with U-joint) | 3 | 2 (1U) | 0 | **2** | Robotic wrist |
| Spherical Joint Spatial | 2 | 0 | 0 | 3 (j₃=1) | Ball joint |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DOF Classification — Conceptual Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Degrees of Freedom DOF] --> B[Translational DOF]
    A --> C[Rotational DOF]
    A --> D[Hybrid via Joints]

    B --> B1[X axis Surge]
    B --> B2[Y axis Sway]
    B --> B3[Z axis Heave]

    C --> C1[Roll around X]
    C --> C2[Pitch around Y]
    C --> C3[Yaw around Z]

    D --> E1[Revolute 1 DOF]
    D --> E2[Prismatic 1 DOF]
    D --> E3[Cylindrical 2 DOF]
    D --> E4[Spherical 3 DOF]
    D --> E5[Universal 2 DOF]
    D --> E6[Planar 3 DOF]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DOF Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 Identify Robot Type] --> stepB[Step 2 Count Links n including ground]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 Classify All Joints]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 Count j1 j2 j3 etc]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 Apply Grubler Formula]
    stepE --> stepF{Step 6 Spatial or Planar}
    stepF --> stepG[Spatial M equals 6n minus 1 minus 5j1 minus 4j2 minus 3j3 minus 2j4 minus j5]
    stepF --> stepH[Planar M equals 3n minus 1 minus 2j1 minus j2]
    stepG --> stepI[Step 7 Validate M result]
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 8 Interpret M: 0 Structure, 1 Function, 2 plus Redundancy]
```

### 4.3 DOF Mapping in a Human Arm (7-DOF Redundant System)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ground Torso] --> B[Shoulder Joint 3 DOF]
    B --> C[Upper Arm Link]
    C --> D[Elbow Joint 1 DOF]
    D --> E[Forearm Link]
    E --> F[Wrist Joint 3 DOF]
    F --> G[Hand End Effector]

    B --> B1[Flexion Extension]
    B --> B2[Abduction Adduction]
    B --> B3[Internal External Rotation]
    D --> D1[Flexion Extension only]
    F --> F1[Flexion Extension]
    F --> F2[Abduction Adduction]
    F --> F3[Pronation Supination]
```

### 4.4 Workspace Volume vs DOF — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robot Workspace Geometry] --> B[1 DOF: Curve Line]
    A --> C[2 DOF: Plane Surface]
    A --> D[3 DOF: 3D Volume]
    A --> E[4 plus DOF: Volume plus Redundancy]

    B --> B1[Example: Prismatic track]
    C --> C1[Example: XY plotter]
    D --> D1[Example: 3R Planar Arm]
    E --> E1[Example: 6R Industrial Arm]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the term "Degree of Freedom" of a robot. State the number of degrees of freedom of a rigid body in 3D space with justification.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Degree of Freedom (DOF):** It is defined as the number of independent parameters (translational or rotational) required to completely specify the configuration of a robotic mechanism or its end-effector in space.
>
> **Rigid body in 3D space:** A rigid body in three-dimensional space possesses **6 degrees of freedom** — namely:
> - **3 Translational DOF:** Motion along X (Surge), Y (Sway), and Z (Heave) axes.
> - **3 Rotational DOF:** Rotation about X (Roll), Y (Pitch), and Z (Yaw) axes.
>
> Hence, the total DOF = 3 + 3 = **6 DOF**.
>
> **Justification:** The pose of a rigid body is fully described by its position vector $\vec{p} = [x, y, z]^T$ (3 coordinates) and its orientation matrix $R(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ (3 angles — Roll, Pitch, Yaw). Total = 6 independent variables.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplemented]**

**Differentiate between a Revolute joint and a Prismatic joint in terms of degrees of freedom. Give one robotic application of each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Revolute Joint (R) | Prismatic Joint (P) |
|---|---|---|
| **DOF provided** | **1 (Rotational)** | **1 (Translational)** |
| **Motion type** | Rotation about a fixed axis | Linear sliding along an axis |
| **Symbol** | Circle with cross mark | Square / sliding block |
| **Analogy** | Door hinge | Drawer slide |
| **Robot example** | Joint of industrial 6R arm | Linear actuator in Cartesian robot |
| **Application** | Welding robot arm shoulder | Pick-and-place gantry system |

> **Key Point:** Both joints provide *only 1 DOF* each, but the *type* of DOF (rotation vs translation) is different. In KTU exams, always mention *both* type and count.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of robot joints with neat sketches. Classify them based on the degrees of freedom they provide. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Robot joints are the basic building blocks of any robotic manipulator. They provide relative motion between two adjacent links. The following are the principal types of joints:

**1. Revolute Joint (R) — 1 DOF**
- Permits **rotation** about a single fixed axis.
- Has **1 rotational DOF**, **0 translational DOF**.
- **Examples:** Door hinge, robot shoulder, motor shaft.

**2. Prismatic Joint (P) — 1 DOF**
- Permits **linear sliding** (translation) along a single axis.
- Has **1 translational DOF**, **0 rotational DOF**.
- **Examples:** Hydraulic cylinder piston, drawer slide, Cartesian robot slider.

**3. Helical / Screw Joint (H) — 1 DOF**
- Combined rotation and translation along the same axis (screw motion).
- **Examples:** Lead screw, ball screw in CNC machines.

**4. Cylindrical Joint (C) — 2 DOF**
- Combines a revolute and a prismatic motion along the same axis.
- **2 DOF** (1 rotation + 1 translation).
- **Examples:** Old rifle bolt action.

**5. Spherical Joint (S) — 3 DOF**
- Ball-and-socket type, permits 3 independent rotations.
- **3 DOF**, no translation.
- **Examples:** Human shoulder/hip joint.

**6. Universal Joint (U) — 2 DOF**
- Two intersecting revolute axes, gimbal-like motion.
- **2 rotational DOF**.
- **Examples:** Automotive drive shaft coupling.

**7. Planar Joint (E) — 3 DOF**
- Allows 2 translations + 1 rotation within a plane.
- **3 DOF**.
- **Examples:** Sliding plate on a flat table.

> **Summary Classification Table:**

| Joint | DOF | Translational | Rotational |
|---|---|---|---|
| R | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| P | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| H | 1 | 1 (coupled) | 1 (coupled) |
| C | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| U | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| S | 3 | 0 | 3 |
| E | 3 | 2 | 1 |

---

**(b)** Using **Grübler's formula**, determine the degrees of freedom of a **spatial 6R industrial robot manipulator** and a **planar four-bar linkage**. Comment on the mobility of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: Spatial 6R Industrial Robot**

**Given:**
- Number of links: $n = 7$ (1 ground + 6 moving links)
- Number of revolute joints: $j_1 = 6$ (all 1-DOF)
- All other joint types: $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

**Apply Spatial Grübler's Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
M &= 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0 \\
M &= 6(6) - 30 \\
M &= 36 - 30 \\
M &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{M = 6 \text{ DOF}}$

**Comment:** The 6R robot is **non-redundant** in free 3D space. Since the task space (full pose in 3D) also requires 6 DOF, this robot can reach *any* position and *any* orientation within its reachable workspace. It is the industry standard for general-purpose manipulation (e.g., welding, painting, assembly).

---

**Case 2: Planar Four-Bar Linkage**

**Given:**
- Number of links: $n = 4$ (1 ground + 3 moving)
- Number of revolute joints: $j_1 = 4$ (all 1-DOF)
- $j_2 = 0$

**Apply Planar Grübler's Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
M &= 3(3) - 8 \\
M &= 9 - 8 \\
M &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{M = 1 \text{ DOF}}$

**Comment:** The four-bar linkage has **only 1 DOF**, meaning a *single input* (e.g., one motor driving the input crank) is sufficient to fully determine the motion of all other links. Such mechanisms are called *function generators* and are widely used in windshield wipers, bicycle pedals, and walking toys.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> - Many students **forget to include the ground link** in the count $n$. Always remember: **$n$ includes the ground link!**
> - For a 4-bar linkage, $n = 4$ (not 3). If you write $n = 3$, you'll get the *wrong* answer of $M = 0$ and lose full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **anatomy of a robotic manipulator**. Describe the function of each link and joint. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A typical robotic manipulator is a serial chain of **rigid links** connected by **actuated joints**, anchored to the ground at one end and equipped with an **end-effector** at the other.

**Anatomical Components:**

**1. Base (Link 0)**
- The fixed mounting platform.
- Provides reference coordinate frame.
- Connects robot to the environment (floor/table).

**2. Joints (J₁, J₂, ..., Jₙ)**
- Provide **relative motion** between consecutive links.
- Each joint contributes 1 or more DOF.
- Driven by **actuators** (motors, hydraulics, pneumatics).

**3. Links (L₁, L₂, ..., Lₙ)**
- Rigid members connecting joints.
- Maintain fixed relative geometry between joint axes.

**4. End-Effector (Tool)**
- The "hand" of the robot.
- Examples: gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer, suction cup.
- Sometimes considered as Link $(n+1)$.

**5. Wrist**
- The last 2 or 3 joints of the arm.
- Specializes in **orientation** of the end-effector (Roll, Pitch, Yaw).

**6. Drive System**
- Motors (servo/stepper) + gears.
- Provides controlled motion to each joint.

**7. Sensors**
- Encoders at each joint.
- Force/torque sensors at the wrist.
- Vision/ultrasonic at the end-effector.

**8. Controller**
- The "brain" — computes joint trajectories from task commands.

**Anatomical Diagram (Block Form):**

```
   ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐
   │ Controller │ ──▶ │ Actuators│ ──▶ │  Sensors │
   └────────────┘     └──────────┘     └──────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
   ╔═════════╗    J1    ╔════════╗   J2   ╔════════╗  Jn   ╔══════════╗
   ║  BASE   ║ ──────▶ ║ LINK 1 ║ ────▶ ║ LINK 2 ║ ────▶║END-EFFECTOR║
   ║ (Link 0)║         ║        ║       ║        ║      ║  (Gripper) ║
   ╚═════════╝         ╚════════╝       ╚════════╝      ╚══════════╝
                       ◄─── WRIST (last 2–3 joints) ────►
```

**Function Table:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Base | Anchoring & reference frame |
| Joints | Provide controlled DOF |
| Links | Maintain spatial structure |
| Wrist | Orient the end-effector |
| End-Effector | Perform the actual task |
| Actuators | Convert energy to motion |
| Sensors | Feedback position/force |
| Controller | Coordinate overall motion |

---

**(b)** A **planar mechanism** consists of **6 links** and **7 revolute joints** (all 1-DOF). Determine its mobility using Grübler's formula. State whether the mechanism is a structure, a function generator, or a redundant mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Planar mechanism.
- Number of links: $n = 6$ (including ground).
- Number of revolute joints: $j_1 = 7$.
- $j_2 = 0$ (no higher-pair joints).

**Apply Planar Grübler's Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
M &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 \\
M &= 3(5) - 14 \\
M &= 15 - 14 \\
M &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{M = 1 \text{ DOF}}$

**Interpretation Table for Mobility:**

| Value of M | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $M = 0$ | **Structure** (no motion) | Truss, fixed frame |
| $M = 1$ | **Function Generator** (1 input) | Four-bar, slider-crank |
| $M = 2$ | **Two-Input Mechanism** | 5-bar planar linkage |
| $M \geq 3$ | **Redundant** (multiple solutions) | Human arm in 2D |
| $M < 0$ | **Preloaded Structure** | Over-constrained design |

**Conclusion:** Since $M = 1$, this mechanism is a **function generator**. A single actuator (motor) driving one input link will fully and uniquely determine the position of all other links. This is the desired case for most practical planar mechanisms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> - Students often *mis-classify* the joint type. A revolute joint is **1-DOF**, but a cylindrical joint is **2-DOF**. If a question says "7 joints", you must specify which are 1-DOF and which are 2-DOF before applying the formula.
> - If $M$ comes out **negative**, do **not** report it as such blindly. State: *"The mechanism is a statically indeterminate (preloaded) structure with $M = -1$, indicating over-constraint."*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Degrees of Freedom**

- ✅ **Definition:** Number of independent parameters specifying a robot's configuration.
- ✅ **Rigid body in 3D = 6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation).
- ✅ **Rigid body in 2D (plane) = 3 DOF** (2 translation + 1 rotation).
- ✅ **6 DOF Rigid-Body Components:** Surge, Sway, Heave (trans) + Roll, Pitch, Yaw (rot).
- ✅ **Joint DOF:** R = 1, P = 1, H = 1, C = 2, U = 2, S = 3, E = 3.
- ✅ **Grübler Planar:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- ✅ **Grübler Spatial:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- ✅ **Always include ground link** in $n$.
- ✅ **M = 0 → Structure**, **M = 1 → Function Generator**, **M = 2 → 2-Input**, **M > 3 → Redundant**.
- ✅ **Industrial 6R arm = 6 DOF** (non-redundant in 3D).
- ✅ **Human arm = 7 DOF** (redundant — famous example).
- ✅ **4-bar linkage = 1 DOF** (most asked KTU example).
- ✅ **SCARA arm = 4 DOF** (2R arm + 1P prismatic + 1R wrist rotation).
- ✅ **Mobile robot on plane = 3 DOF** (x, y, θ).
- ✅ **Quadcopter = 4 to 6 DOF** depending on under-actuation model.

> **🎯 Most-likely KTU 2024 Exam Questions:**
> 1. Define DOF and derive Grübler's formula.
> 2. Find DOF of four-bar / slider-crank / 5-bar / 6R.
> 3. Classify robot joints with DOF contribution.
> 4. Differentiate between spatial and planar DOF analysis.
> 5. Explain redundancy with a 7-DOF example.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coordinate Systems and Frames of Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coordinate Systems and Frames of Reference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Coordinate System?

A **Coordinate System** is a mathematical framework used to uniquely determine the position, orientation, and motion of a point, object, or robot in space using an ordered set of numerical values called **coordinates**. In mobile robotics, coordinate systems provide the fundamental "language" through which a robot perceives its own state and the state of objects in its environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A coordinate system is a reference framework consisting of an *origin*, a set of *axes*, and a *set of conventions* (handedness, units, scale) that allow a point in $\mathbb{R}^n$ space to be represented by a unique tuple of real numbers.

## 1.2 What is a Frame of Reference?

A **Frame of Reference** (or simply *frame*) is a coordinate system *augmented with*:
- An **origin** point (where the axes intersect),
- A set of **orthogonal axes** (typically $X, Y, Z$),
- An **orientation** in space (how the axes are rotated relative to some parent/global frame),
- A **right-hand or left-hand rule** convention.

A coordinate system is *static* (a mathematical construct), while a frame of reference is *dynamic* — it can move, rotate, and accelerate with respect to other frames.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Mobile Robotics:** A robot's onboard sensors (cameras, LiDAR, IMU) and actuators (wheels, motors) all generate data *in their own local frames*. To perform navigation, mapping, or path planning, the robot must transform all these local observations into a **global frame** (e.g., the world frame).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy 🌍

Imagine you are standing in the middle of **Kochi** with a paper map. The map itself is printed with its own **grid** (e.g., latitude/longitude lines) — that is a *coordinate system*. Now, your friend standing in **Trivandrum** has a *different* map grid that doesn't perfectly match yours — that is a *different frame of reference*.

To tell your friend where you are, you must first **align both maps** (define a common origin), **agree on which way is North** (orient the axes), and then **report your position** in numbers (coordinates). Mobile robots do exactly this — thousands of times per second — using onboard processors.

## 1.4 Standard Coordinate Systems Used in Mobile Robotics

| # | Coordinate System | Best Used For | Key Idea |
|---|-------------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | **Cartesian (Rectangular)** | Indoor navigation, SLAM | $(x, y, z)$ along orthogonal axes |
| 2 | **Polar** | Sonar, Radar, single-LiDAR sweeps | $(r, \theta)$ in a plane |
| 3 | **Cylindrical** | 2D maps with height (e.g., floor plans) | $(r, \theta, z)$ |
| 4 | **Spherical** | 3D LiDAR, omnidirectional cameras | $(\rho, \theta, \phi)$ |
| 5 | **Homogeneous (4×4)** | Combined rotation + translation | Augmented vectors |

> [!TIP]
> **Why multiple systems?** Different sensors and tasks favor different representations. A **LiDAR** naturally gives *polar* data, but most path-planning algorithms (like A* or RRT) work natively in *Cartesian* coordinates. Therefore, **coordinate transformations** are the daily bread of mobile robotics.

## 1.5 The "Handedness" Convention

A right-handed coordinate system obeys the **right-hand rule**:

$$\hat{X} \times \hat{Y} = \hat{Z}$$

- Point the index finger along $X$,
- The middle finger along $Y$,
- The thumb points along $Z$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** Mixing up **right-handed** and **left-handed** coordinate systems leads to **sign errors in rotation matrices**, which is one of the top reasons students lose marks in derivation questions. KTU examiners **always** assume the **right-hand rule** unless otherwise stated.

## 1.6 Fundamental Quantities in a Frame

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|---------|
| Position | $\vec{p}$ or $(x,y,z)$ | **metres (m)** | Where the point is |
| Orientation | $\vec{\phi}$ or roll/pitch/yaw | **radians (rad)** | How the frame is rotated |
| Linear Velocity | $\vec{v} = \dot{\vec{p}}$ | **m/s** | Rate of position change |
| Angular Velocity | $\vec{\omega}$ | **rad/s** | Rate of rotation |
| Acceleration | $\vec{a} = \ddot{\vec{p}}$ | **m/s²** | Rate of velocity change |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **International System of Units (SI)**, the standard metric for angles in robotics is the **radian (rad)**, not the degree. **1 rad = 180°/π ≈ 57.2958°**. Always convert degrees to radians when using trig functions like $\sin()$ and $\cos()$ in your KTU derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 1-D Coordinate System

The simplest case: a single number line.

- An origin $O$ is chosen.
- A positive direction ($+X$) and unit vector ($\hat{i}$) are defined.
- Any point $P$ on the line is described by the signed distance $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

## 2.2 2-D Coordinate System (Cartesian)

Two perpendicular axes $X$ and $Y$ intersect at the origin $O$.

$$\vec{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}, \quad \text{where } x, y \in \mathbb{R}$$

The **magnitude** (distance from origin) is:

$$\lVert \vec{p} \rVert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$$

The **direction** (angle with $X$-axis):

$$\theta = \arctan2(y, x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> `arctan2` is the **four-quadrant inverse tangent** — it is *not* the same as $\arctan(y/x)$. KTU examiners expect you to use `atan2` when handling the full $\pm 180°$ range, because $\arctan$ alone is undefined at $90°$ and $270°$.

## 2.3 2-D Polar Coordinate System

A point is described by a **radius** $r \geq 0$ and an **angle** $\theta$.

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \theta = \arctan2(y, x)$$

**Conversion Polar → Cartesian:**

$$x = r\cos\theta, \quad y = r\sin\theta$$

**Conversion Cartesian → Polar:**

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \theta = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$$

## 2.4 3-D Coordinate Systems

### 2.4.1 Cartesian (Rectangular)

$$\vec{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}, \quad \lVert \vec{p} \rVert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$

### 2.4.2 Cylindrical

Cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$ combine the 2-D polar system with a height $z$.

$$x = r\cos\theta, \quad y = r\sin\theta, \quad z = z$$

### 2.4.3 Spherical

Spherical coordinates $(\rho, \theta, \phi)$ use a **radial distance** $\rho$, an **azimuth angle** $\theta$ in the $XY$-plane, and a **polar angle** $\phi$ measured from the $+Z$-axis.

$$x = \rho \sin\phi \cos\theta, \quad y = \rho \sin\phi \sin\theta, \quad z = \rho \cos\phi$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In the spherical system, $\phi$ is the angle from the **$+Z$ axis (polar)**, not from the $XY$-plane. Some textbooks (e.g., physics) do the opposite. **Always use the KTU convention** in your exam answer unless the question specifies otherwise.

## 2.5 Frames of Reference — The Core Idea

A **frame** $\{\mathcal{F}\}$ in 3D is fully specified by:
1. An **origin** $\vec{o}_\mathcal{F}$,
2. A **right-handed orthogonal basis** $\{\hat{X}_\mathcal{F}, \hat{Y}_\mathcal{F}, \hat{Z}_\mathcal{F}\}$.

A point $P$ in space can be described *with respect to* frame $\{\mathcal{F}\}$ as $\vec{p}^\mathcal{F} = (p_x, p_y, p_z)$, or with respect to another frame $\{\mathcal{G}\}$ as $\vec{p}^\mathcal{G} = (p_x', p_y', p_z')$.

> [!TIP]
> The point $P$ itself is **invariant** — it does not change. What changes is the *vector of numbers* used to describe it. This subtle distinction is the heart of every coordinate-transformation question on the KTU exam.

## 2.6 Rotations Between Frames

A frame $\{\mathcal{B}\}$ rotated with respect to frame $\{\mathcal{A}\}$ about the $X$-axis by angle $\theta$ produces the **rotation matrix**:

$$
R_X(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ 0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

Similarly about the $Y$-axis:

$$
R_Y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
$$

And about the $Z$-axis:

$$
R_Z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.7 Translation Between Frames

If frame $\{\mathcal{B}\}$ has its origin at position $\vec{d}^{\mathcal{A}}$ *as seen from* frame $\{\mathcal{A}}$, then for any point $P$:

$$\vec{p}^{\mathcal{A}} = R_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{A}} \, \vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}} + \vec{d}^{\mathcal{A}}_{\mathcal{B}}$$

## 2.8 The Homogeneous Transformation Matrix

Combining rotation $R \in SO(3)$ and translation $\vec{d}$ into a single $4 \times 4$ matrix:

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & \vec{d}_{3 \times 1} \\ \mathbf{0}_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} & d_x \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} & d_y \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} & d_z \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

A point is represented in homogeneous form as $\tilde{\vec{p}} = (x, y, z, 1)^T$, so that:

$$\tilde{\vec{p}}^{\mathcal{A}} = T_{\mathcal{B}}^{\mathcal{A}} \, \tilde{\vec{p}}^{\mathcal{B}}$$

## 2.9 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Result | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | 2-D Cartesian magnitude | $\lVert \vec{p} \rVert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ | **m** | Always non-negative |
| 2 | 2-D angle | $\theta = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$ | **rad** | Range $(-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 3 | Polar to Cartesian | $x = r\cos\theta,\ y = r\sin\theta$ | **m** | $r \geq 0$ |
| 4 | Spherical to Cartesian | $x = \rho\sin\phi\cos\theta,\ z = \rho\cos\phi$ | **m** | KTU convention |
| 5 | Rotation about $X$ | $R_X(\theta)$ as above | unitless | Right-hand rule |
| 6 | Rotation about $Y$ | $R_Y(\theta)$ as above | unitless | Note sign pattern |
| 7 | Rotation about $Z$ | $R_Z(\theta)$ as above | unitless | Most common 2D case |
| 8 | Orthogonality of $R$ | $R^{-1} = R^T,\ R^TR = I$ | — | Det$(R)=+1$ |
| 9 | Composition law | $R_{3\leftarrow 1} = R_{3\leftarrow 2} \, R_{2\leftarrow 1}$ | — | Order matters |
| 10 | Full transform | $\vec{p}^{A} = R_{B}^{A}\vec{p}^{B} + \vec{d}_{B}^{A}$ | **m** | Vector equation |
| 11 | Homogeneous form | $\tilde{\vec{p}}^{A} = T_{B}^{A}\tilde{\vec{p}}^{B}$ | **m** | $4 \times 4$ matrix |
| 12 | Determinant check | $\det(R) = +1$ | — | For valid rotation |
| 13 | Euler angle (ZYX) | $R = R_Z(\psi)\,R_Y(\theta)\,R_X(\phi)$ | — | Roll-pitch-yaw |
| 14 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dot{\theta}$ | **rad/s** | Scalar in 2D |
| 15 | Linear velocity | $\vec{v} = \dot{\vec{p}}$ | **m/s** | Time-derivative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The composition law $R_{A \to C} = R_{B \to C} \cdot R_{A \to B}$ is the single most-tested identity in Module 1. Memorize the *order*: "child-of on the right" or "to-then-from." 

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Self-Driving Cars** | Global frame (ENU — East-North-Up) → Vehicle frame (FLU — Forward-Left-Up) for path planning |
| **Drones (UAVs)** | World frame → Body frame → Camera frame for visual servoing |
| **Industrial AGVs** | Map frame → Odometer frame for dead-reckoning |
| **Surgical Robots** | Patient frame (MRI/CT) → Robot base frame → Tool tip frame |
| **Mars Rovers (NASA)** | Rover frame → Lander frame → Mars-fixed inertial frame |

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rotation Matrix about the $Z$-Axis

**Goal:** Derive $R_Z(\theta)$ such that rotating a point $P$ in the $XY$-plane by angle $\theta$ about $Z$ yields the correct new coordinates.

### Step 1 — Initial Setup
Let point $P$ have coordinates $(x, y)$ in the original frame $\{\mathcal{A}\}$. Express them in polar form using radius $r$ and initial angle $\alpha$:

$$x = r\cos\alpha, \quad y = r\sin\alpha$$

### Step 2 — Apply Rotation
After rotating counter-clockwise by angle $\theta$ about $+Z$, the new angle becomes $\alpha + \theta$. The radius $r$ is unchanged.

$$x' = r\cos(\alpha + \theta), \quad y' = r\sin(\alpha + \theta)$$

### Step 3 — Expand Using Trigonometric Identities
Use the **angle addition formulas**:

$$\cos(\alpha + \theta) = \cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta$$

$$\sin(\alpha + \theta) = \sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back the Original Coordinates
Now substitute $x = r\cos\alpha$ and $y = r\sin\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= r(\cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta) \\
   &= \cos\theta \cdot (r\cos\alpha) - \sin\theta \cdot (r\sin\alpha) \\
   &= x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y' &= r(\sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta) \\
   &= \cos\theta \cdot (r\sin\alpha) + \sin\theta \cdot (r\cos\alpha) \\
   &= x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Write in Matrix Form
$$
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
$$

Extending to 3-D (the $z$ component is unchanged):

$$
R_Z(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** The **diagonal** entries are always $\cos\theta$. The **off-diagonal** signs are $\{ -, +, -, +\dots \}$ — a checkerboard pattern in the 2-D block.

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse of a Rotation Matrix

**Goal:** Show that $R^{-1} = R^T$ for any rotation matrix.

### Step 1 — Rotation Preserves Length
For any rotation $R$, the magnitude of a vector is preserved:

$$\lVert R\vec{p} \rVert^2 = (R\vec{p})^T(R\vec{p}) = \vec{p}^T R^T R \vec{p} = \vec{p}^T \vec{p} = \lVert \vec{p} \rVert^2$$

### Step 2 — Conclude $R^T R = I$
This must hold for *all* $\vec{p}$. Therefore:

$$R^T R = I \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R^{-1} = R^T$$

### Step 3 — The Determinant Constraint
A proper rotation matrix must also satisfy:

$$\det(R) = +1$$

(improper rotations, e.g., reflections, have $\det = -1$ and do not preserve right-handedness).

## 3.3 Derivation: Full Pose Transformation (Position + Orientation)

**Problem:** Frame $\{\mathcal{B}\}$ is located at position $\vec{d} = (d_x, d_y, d_z)^T$ in frame $\{\mathcal{A}\}$, and rotated by $R$ relative to $\{\mathcal{A}\}$. A point $P$ has coordinates $\vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}} = (p_x, p_y, p_z)^T$ in frame $\{\mathcal{B}\}$. Find $\vec{p}^{\mathcal{A}}$.

### Step 1 — Pure Rotation Step
If the origins of $\{\mathcal{A}\}$ and $\{\mathcal{B}\}$ coincided, the rotated coordinates would be:

$$\vec{p}_{\text{rot}} = R \, \vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}}$$

### Step 2 — Add the Translation Offset
Now shift the origin by $\vec{d}$:

$$\vec{p}^{\mathcal{A}} = R \, \vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}} + \vec{d}$$

### Step 3 — Homogeneous Form
Augment vectors with a $1$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \vec{p}^{\mathcal{A}} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R & \vec{d} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = T \begin{bmatrix} \vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This $4 \times 4$ matrix $T$ is the **homogeneous transformation matrix**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Composition of Rotations

**Problem:** Frame $\{\mathcal{C}\}$ is rotated by $R_1$ relative to $\{\mathcal{B}\}$, and frame $\{\mathcal{B}\}$ is rotated by $R_2$ relative to $\{\mathcal{A}\}$. Find $R_{AC}$ (rotation of $\{\mathcal{C}\}$ relative to $\{\mathcal{A}\}$).

### Step 1 — Apply Successive Transformations
A point $P$ is first expressed in $\{\mathcal{C}\}$, then in $\{\mathcal{B}\}$, then in $\{\mathcal{A}\}$:

$$\vec{p}^{\mathcal{A}} = R_2 \, \vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}}, \quad \vec{p}^{\mathcal{B}} = R_1 \, \vec{p}^{\mathcal{C}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute
$$\vec{p}^{\mathcal{A}} = R_2 \, (R_1 \, \vec{p}^{\mathcal{C}}) = (R_2 R_1) \, \vec{p}^{\mathcal{C}}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the Composition
$$
R_{AC} = R_2 R_1
$$

In general notation, if $R_{BA}$ is the rotation of $\{\mathcal{B}\}$ w.r.t. $\{\mathcal{A}\}$ and $R_{CB}$ is the rotation of $\{\mathcal{C}\}$ w.r.t. $\{\mathcal{B}\}$, then:

$$R_{CA} = R_{CB} \, R_{BA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Order of Operations:** In the formula $R_{CA} = R_{CB} R_{BA}$, the *innermost* frame ($\{\mathcal{A}\}$) is on the **right**. This is the "Reading-from-right-to-left" rule, and reversing the order gives a *different* answer because matrix multiplication is **not commutative**.

## 3.5 Worked Example: 2D Mobile Robot Pose Update

**Scenario:** A differential-drive robot starts at $(0, 0)$ in the world frame. It turns left by $30°$ and drives forward by **2 m**. Find the final position in the world frame.

### Step 1 — Define the Initial Pose
Initial pose (position + orientation):

$$\vec{p}_0 = (0, 0)^T, \quad \theta_0 = 0 \text{ rad}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Rotation Matrix
For $\theta = 30° = \pi/6 \text{ rad}$:

$$
R_Z\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 30° & -\sin 30° \\ \sin 30° & \cos 30° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Displacement
In the body frame, the displacement is $\vec{d}_{\text{body}} = (2, 0)^T$ (forward 2 m).

### Step 4 — Transform Displacement to World Frame
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{d}_{\text{world}} &= R_Z\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) \vec{d}_{\text{body}} \\
  &= \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ \tfrac{1}{2} & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
  &= \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{3} \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ metres}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Pose
$$
\vec{p}_{\text{final}} = (0, 0)^T + (\sqrt{3}, 1)^T = (\sqrt{3},\ 1)^T \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Numerically: $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ m. So the final world-frame position is approximately **$(1.732 \text{ m},\ 1.000 \text{ m})$**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Properties of $R$ and $T$

| Property | Rotation $R$ | Homogeneous $T$ | Consequence in Robotics |
|----------|--------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Size | $3 \times 3$ | $4 \times 4$ | Determines dimensionality of state |
| Determinant | $\det(R) = +1$ | $\det(T) = +1$ | Preserves orientation handedness |
| Inverse | $R^{-1} = R^T$ | $T^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} R^T & -R^T\vec{d} \\ \mathbf{0}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Used to invert sensor frames |
| Composition | $R_{AC} = R_{AB} R_{BC}$ | $T_{AC} = T_{AB} T_{BC}$ | Forward kinematics chain |
| Identity | $I_3$ | $I_4$ | "No transformation" |
| Lie Group | $SO(3)$ | $SE(3)$ | Mathematically a smooth manifold |

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Coordinate Systems in a Mobile Robot

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[WORLD FRAME Global Inertial ENU] --> B[MAP FRAME Local Static]
    B --> C[ODOMETRY FRAME Drifts Over Time]
    C --> D[ROBOT BASE FRAME Body Fixed]
    D --> E[SENSOR FRAME LiDAR Camera IMU]
    D --> F[ACTUATOR FRAME Wheels Motors]
    E --> E1[LiDAR 2D Scan polar r theta]
    E --> E2[Camera pixel u v]
    E --> E3[IMU roll pitch yaw]
    F --> F1[Left Wheel Angular Velocity omega L]
    F --> F2[Right Wheel Angular Velocity omega R]

    A -.Origin fixed.-> B
    B -.Origin static.-> C
    C -.Origin drifts.-> D
    D -.Origin at robot center.-> E
    D -.Origin at robot center.-> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Transformation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Raw Sensor Data In Sensor Frame] --> STEP1[Step 1 Extrinsic Calibration]
    STEP1 --> STEP2[Step 2 Sensor to Body Transform T SB]
    STEP2 --> STEP3[Step 3 Body to World Transform T WB]
    STEP3 --> STEP4[Step 4 World Frame Coordinates]
    STEP4 --> OUT[Output to Path Planner SLAM Module]

    STEP1 -.Uses fixed extrinsic.-> STEP2
    STEP2 -.Updates per timestep.-> STEP3
    STEP3 -.Updates per timestep.-> STEP4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a mathematical transform $T$. The full pipeline computes:
> $$T_{\text{world}} = T_{\text{body}\leftarrow\text{world}}^{-1} \cdot T_{\text{sensor}\leftarrow\text{body}}^{-1} \cdot \vec{p}_{\text{sensor}}$$
> In the more compact notation: $\vec{p}^{\text{world}} = T_{BS} \, T_{WB} \, \vec{p}^{\text{sensor}}$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Euler Angle Composition (Roll-Pitch-Yaw)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[World Frame W] --> RZ[Rotate about Z axis Yaw psi]
    RZ --> RY[Rotate about new Y axis Pitch theta]
    RY --> RX[Rotate about new X axis Roll phi]
    RX --> B[Body Frame B]

    RZ -.Rz psi.-> RY
    RY -.Ry theta.-> RX
    RX -.Rx phi.-> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The diagram shows the **ZYX (Tait-Bryan) convention**, which gives the total rotation:
> $$R = R_Z(\psi) \, R_Y(\theta) \, R_X(\phi)$$
> Notice that the rotations are applied **right-to-left** in the matrix equation but **top-to-bottom** in the diagram — this is the standard convention and a very common exam pitfall.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree — Which Coordinate System to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to represent 3D space?} -->|Yes| Q2{Data is naturally radial?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q2D[Use 2D Cartesian x y]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Includes height dimension?}
    Q2 -->|No| Q3D[Use 3D Cartesian x y z]
    Q3 -->|Yes| SPH[Use Spherical rho theta phi]
    Q3 -->|No| POL[Use 2D Polar r theta]
    Q2D --> TASK[Plot path Compute distances]
    POL --> TASK
    Q3D --> TASK
    SPH --> TASK
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** Choosing the right coordinate system simplifies math. For example, the path of a robot turning at constant speed is naturally a **circular arc** — easiest in **polar** coordinates. But obstacle-avoidance grids (occupancy grids) are easier in **Cartesian** coordinates.

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a "frame of reference" and explain why it is important in mobile robotics.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
