| Joint $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ (m) | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) |
|-----------|------------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | $\theta_1$ (var) | 0 | 0 | $-\pi/2$ |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ (var) | $d_2$ (var) | 0 | $+\pi/2$ |
| 3 | 0 | $d_3$ (var) | 0 | 0 |

Note: Joint 2 is the prismatic joint, and joint 3 (in some conventions) is the prismatic. Here we use the standard RRP arrangement: $\theta_1, \theta_2$ revolute; $d_3$ prismatic.

**Forward Kinematics Matrix:**

$$\begin{aligned} ^0T_3 = {}^0T_1 \cdot {}^1T_2 \cdot {}^2T_3 = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 c_2 & -c_1 s_2 & -s_1 & -d_3 s_1 \\ s_1 c_2 & -s_1 s_2 & c_1 & d_3 c_1 \\ -s_2 & -c_2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Loss of DOF Conditions:**
- When $d_3 = 0$ and $\theta_2 = 0$, the wrist center coincides with the shoulder — **boundary singularity**.
- When $\sin\theta_2 = 0$ (arm folded), the Jacobian determinant is zero — **interior singularity**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **D-H parameters** are *four*: $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$ — exactly four because spatial rigid-body motion in 3D has 6 DOF, and 2 are eliminated by the common-normal constraint.
- **Forward Kinematics** is the *multiplication* of cascaded D-H matrices; it is **unique and well-posed**.
- **Inverse Kinematics** is the *inversion*; it is **non-unique, may not exist, and may have singularities**.
- Always identify **elbow-up and elbow-down** solutions for planar IK.
- For 6-DOF arms with **spherical wrists**, use **Pieper's decoupling**: position kinematics first, then orientation kinematics.
- The **Jacobian singularity** is where IK fails or becomes unstable — these are critical points to avoid in trajectory planning.
- A redundant manipulator ($n > 6$) has **infinite IK solutions**, exploited for **obstacle avoidance** and **singularity avoidance**.
- The **workspace** of a 2-link arm is an annulus: $\vert a_2 - a_3 \vert \leq R \leq a_2 + a_3$.
- Always **check joint limits** when selecting among multiple IK solutions.
- **Numerical IK methods** (Newton-Raphson, Jacobian Transpose, Damped Least Squares / Levenberg-Marquardt) are used when closed-form solutions are infeasible (e.g., 7-DOF KUKA LWR).
- Remember the canonical D-H matrix:

$$\begin{aligned} ^{i-1}T_i = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i c\alpha_i & s\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i c\alpha_i & -c\theta_i s\alpha_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & s\alpha_i & c\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Kinematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Differential Kinematics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Differential Kinematics** is the branch of mobile robotics that establishes the mathematical relationship between the **actuator-level velocities** (typically the angular velocities of the robot's driven wheels) and the **resulting motion of the robot's body** (its linear and angular velocities in the global/world frame), without considering the forces or torques that cause the motion. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, differential kinematics is foundational to **motion planning** and **control of wheeled mobile robots (WMRs)**. It is generally divided into two complementary problems: 1. **Forward Differential Kinematics (FDK):** Given the wheel angular velocities $(\omega_r, \omega_l)$ and geometric parameters, compute the robot's linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ (and hence its rate of change of posture $\dot{q}$). 2. **Inverse Differential Kinematics (IDK):** Given a desired robot body velocity $(v, \omega)$, compute the required wheel angular velocities $(\omega_r, \omega_l)$. The robot's instantaneous **posture** (or **configuration**) in the 2D plane is represented by the state vector: $$q = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$ where $x$ and $y$ are the Cartesian coordinates of a reference point on the robot (typically the midpoint between the wheels), and $\theta$ is the orientation angle measured from the global $X$-axis to the robot's local $x$-axis. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Shopping Cart at the Supermarket"** > Imagine pushing a **rear-wheel-drive shopping cart** with two big drive wheels at the back and a small free-spinning caster at the front. > - If you turn **both back wheels at the SAME speed in the SAME direction**, the cart rolls **straight ahead** (only linear velocity $v$, no rotation). > - If you spin the **left wheel forward and the right wheel backward at the SAME speed**, the cart **rotates in place** about its own center (only angular velocity $\omega$, no linear translation). > - If you spin **both wheels forward but at DIFFERENT speeds**, the cart follows a **circular arc** (both $v$ and $\omega$ are non-zero). > > Differential kinematics is essentially the **"mathematical recipe"** that quantifies exactly how these wheel-speed combinations produce the cart's overall motion. ## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters (Constants of the System) The geometric parameters that govern differential kinematics of a standard differential-drive robot are: - **$r$** — Radius of each driven wheel (meters, **m**). - **$L$** — Distance (track width) between the centers of the two driven wheels (meters, **m**). - **$b$** — Sometimes used for the wheelbase (distance from drive axle to caster), though for pure 2D differential drive analysis only $r$ and $L$ are strictly required. ## 1.4 The Unicycle / Posture Model (Rate of Change Equation) The most fundamental kinematic model of a unicycle-type robot is: $$\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$ This compact form tells us that: - $\dot{x} = v\cos\theta$ - $\dot{y} = v\sin\theta$ - $\dot{\theta} = \omega$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The presence of $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ reveals that the model is **nonlinear**. This is a hallmark of mobile robot kinematics and the reason the **Jacobian** is the central object of study in differential kinematics. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning Differential kinematics of a differential-drive mobile robot proceeds along the following logic chain: 1. **Identify the actuator signals.** The two DC motors driving the left and right wheels produce angular velocities $\omega_l$ and $\omega_r$ (rad/s). 2. **Convert wheel spin to ground contact points.** Each wheel touches the ground at a contact point moving at linear speed $v_{wheel} = r \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ (assuming no slip — the **no-slip / pure rolling constraint**). 3. **Apply rigid-body kinematics.** The robot is a rigid body, so the velocities of all points on it are related by the body's linear velocity $v$ at the center and the angular velocity $\omega$ about the Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR). 4. **Compute body velocities at the wheel locations.** The left and right wheels are located at distance $L/2$ on either side of the reference point. Their tangential velocities must equal the rolling constraint. 5. **Invert to map desired body motion to wheel commands.** Solve the system of two linear equations for $\omega_r$ and $\omega_l$. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Key Result - **Why two equations?** The differential-drive robot has exactly **two degrees of freedom (2-DOF)** in its planar motion (translation + rotation in the plane), and exactly **two independent actuators** (the two wheels). The square system is exactly determined. - **Why the midpoint reference?** Choosing the midpoint of the drive axle as the reference point decouples the two wheel equations symmetrically, leading to clean formulas. - **How does the ICR appear?** When the wheels spin at different rates, the robot pivots around a point on the line connecting the wheel contact points, at a distance $R = v/\omega$ from the robot's center. If $\omega \to 0$ (wheels equal), the ICR is at infinity (straight line motion). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for any KTU question on differential kinematics of a differential-drive robot. Memorize it. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions | |---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------| | 1 | Left wheel linear contact velocity | $v_l = r \, \omega_l$ | m/s | No-slip assumption | | 2 | Right wheel linear contact velocity | $v_r = r \, \omega_r$ | m/s | No-slip assumption | | 3 | Robot linear velocity (FDK) | $v = \dfrac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2}$ | m/s | Average of the two wheel speeds | | 4 | Robot angular velocity (FDK) | $\omega = \dfrac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$ | rad/s | Difference scaled by track width | | 5 | Right wheel speed (IDK) | $\omega_r = \dfrac{2v + \omega L}{2r}$ | rad/s | Inverse mapping | | 6 | Left wheel speed (IDK) | $\omega_l = \dfrac{2v - \omega L}{2r}$ | rad/s | Inverse mapping | | 7 | Instantaneous radius of curvature | $R = \dfrac{v}{\omega} = \dfrac{L(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2(\omega_r - \omega_l)}$ | m | $\omega \neq 0$ required | | 8 | Posture update (forward integration) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta \cdot \Delta t$ | m | Euler integration | | 9 | Posture update (forward integration) | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v\sin\theta \cdot \Delta t$ | m | Euler integration | | 10 | Posture update (forward integration) | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \cdot \Delta t$ | rad | Euler integration | | 11 | Forward Jacobian (mapping wheel $\to$ body) | $J = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix}$ | m, 1/m | Maps $[\omega_r, \omega_l]^T \to [v,\omega]^T$ | | 12 | Inverse Jacobian (mapping body $\to$ wheel) | $J^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/r & L/(2r) \\ 1/r & -L/(2r) \end{bmatrix}$ | 1/m, m | Maps $[v,\omega]^T \to [\omega_r,\omega_l]^T$ | | 13 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ | m/s | Sideways motion forbidden | | 14 | Body to world velocity transform | $\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$ | — | Fundamental posture equation | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Differential Kinematics Differential kinematics is the mathematical backbone for: - **Autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs)** in warehouses (Amazon Kiva bots, for instance, are differential-drive). - **Floor-cleaning robots** (iRobot Roomba, Xiaomi Mi Robot). - **Agricultural row-crop robots** navigating between plant lines. - **Mobile manipulators** where the mobile base must position the manipulator's end-effector. - **Planetary rovers** (NASA's Mars rovers use variants of this model, modified for skid-steer and rocker-bogie suspensions). In KTU examinations, the most frequently tested application is **mapping the encoder readings of the wheels into the robot's pose estimate** (dead reckoning / odometry) and **converting a planned $(v, \omega)$ trajectory into PWM signals to the motors**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Differential Kinematics of a Differential-Drive Robot ### Setup Consider a differential-drive mobile robot with: - Two identical wheels, each of radius $r$, separated by distance $L$ (track width). - A reference point $P$ at the midpoint of the drive axle. - Left wheel angular velocity $\omega_l$ (positive = forward). - Right wheel angular velocity $\omega_r$ (positive = forward). - $v$ = linear velocity of point $P$ in the robot's forward direction. - $\omega$ = angular velocity of the robot (positive = counter-clockwise). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Apply the no-slip rolling condition.** If the wheel rolls without slipping, the linear speed of the wheel's contact point with the ground equals $r \cdot \omega_{wheel}$: $$v_l = r \, \omega_l \qquad \text{and} \qquad v_r = r \, \omega_r$$ > *Textual logic:* Each wheel's rotation is translated into a tangential ground velocity proportional to its radius. **Step 2: Express the left and right wheel velocities in terms of the body velocities $v$ and $\omega$.** The point $P$ moves forward at $v$ and rotates at $\omega$. The left wheel is at a perpendicular distance $L/2$ to the left of $P$. By rigid-body kinematics, the velocity of the left wheel's contact point in the forward direction is: $$v_l = v - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$ > *Textual logic:* The forward velocity at any point on a rigid body is the linear velocity of the center plus the tangential contribution $\omega \times \text{(lever arm)}$. A point to the **left** of the center moves **slower** than the center if $\omega > 0$ (CCW rotation). Similarly, the right wheel is at a perpendicular distance $L/2$ to the right of $P$. Its forward velocity is: $$v_r = v + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$ > *Textual logic:* A point to the **right** moves **faster** than the center for CCW rotation. **Step 3: Combine the rigid-body and no-slip conditions.** $$r \, \omega_l = v - \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$r \, \omega_r = v + \omega \cdot \frac{L}{2} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 4: Solve for $v$ by adding equations (i) and (ii).** $$r(\omega_l + \omega_r) = 2v$$ $$v = \frac{r(\omega_l + \omega_r)}{2}$$ > *Textual logic:* Adding eliminates $\omega$ (it appears with opposite signs), leaving twice the linear velocity. **Step 5: Solve for $\omega$ by subtracting equation (i) from (ii).** $$r(\omega_r - \omega_l) = \omega L$$ $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L}$$ > *Textual logic:* Subtracting eliminates $v$, isolating the rotational contribution scaled by the track width $L$. **Step 6: Stack the two results in matrix form (Forward Jacobian).** $$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \omega_r \\ \omega_l \end{bmatrix}$$ This matrix $J$ is the **forward differential kinematic Jacobian**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse Differential Kinematics ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Start from the forward equations.** $$v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ **Step 2: Solve (iv) for the difference $\omega_r - \omega_l$.** $$\omega_r - \omega_l = \frac{\omega L}{r}$$ **Step 3: Solve (iii) for the sum $\omega_r + \omega_l$.** $$\omega_r + \omega_l = \frac{2v}{r}$$ **Step 4: Add and subtract the two results.** Add: $\;\;2\omega_r = \dfrac{2v}{r} + \dfrac{\omega L}{r}$ $$\boxed{\omega_r = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r}}$$ Subtract: $\;\;2\omega_l = \dfrac{2v}{r} - \dfrac{\omega L}{r}$ $$\boxed{\omega_l = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r}}$$ **Step 5: Stack in matrix form (Inverse Jacobian).** $$\begin{bmatrix} \omega_r \\ \omega_l \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/r & L/(2r) \\ 1/r & -L/(2r) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$ > *Textual logic:* The inverse mapping exists and is unique because the Jacobian is square and non-singular for $L \neq 0$ and $r \neq 0$. This is why a differential-drive robot is **completely controllable** in its 2-DOF planar motion. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Radius of Curvature of the Path **Step 1:** The robot, when $\omega_r \neq \omega_l$, follows a circular arc of radius $R$ given by the ratio of linear to angular velocity: $$R = \frac{v}{\omega}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the forward kinematics expressions: $$R = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)/2}{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)/L} = \frac{L(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2(\omega_r - \omega_l)}$$ **Step 3:** Special case check. - If $\omega_r = \omega_l = \omega_0$, then $\omega = 0$ and the formula $v/\omega$ is undefined (0/0). Physically, this corresponds to **straight-line motion with $R \to \infty$**, which is consistent. - If $\omega_r = -\omega_l$, then $v = 0$ but $\omega \neq 0$. The robot **rotates in place** about its own center, so $R = 0$. Plugging in: $R = 0/(2 \cdot 2\omega_0) = 0$. ✓ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Posture (Configuration) Update via Euler Integration To simulate or track the robot's path, the continuous-time posture equation: $$\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$ is discretized using Euler's method with timestep $\Delta t$: $$q_{k+1} = q_k + \dot{q}_k \cdot \Delta t$$ Componentwise: $$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$ $$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$ $$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$ > *Textual logic:* At each discrete step, we hold the velocity and orientation constant over the short interval $\Delta t$ and accumulate the displacement. This is the basis of **dead reckoning / odometry** using wheel encoders. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Non-Holonomic Constraint Derivation A differential-drive robot **cannot translate sideways**. Mathematically, the velocity component perpendicular to its heading must vanish. The robot's heading unit vector is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ and its perpendicular (lateral) unit vector is $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$. The lateral velocity component is: $$v_{\text{lateral}} = \dot{x}(-\sin\theta) + \dot{y}(\cos\theta) = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$$ > *Textual logic:* This is the **non-holonomic constraint** (Pfaffian form $A(q)\dot{q} = 0$). It cannot be integrated to a position-level constraint, which is why mobile robots cannot simply "move sideways" to reorient — they must perform complex maneuvers. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (Frequently tested in KTU).** A differential-drive robot has $r = 0.05$ m and $L = 0.30$ m. At a particular instant, the wheel angular velocities are $\omega_r = 10$ rad/s and $\omega_l = 6$ rad/s. Find: - (a) Linear velocity $v$ - (b) Angular velocity $\omega$ - (c) Radius of curvature $R$ - (d) Time to complete a full circle along the arc **Solution:** (a) Using $v = r(\omega_r + \omega_l)/2$: $$v = 0.05 \times (10 + 6)/2 = 0.05 \times 8 = 0.4 \text{ m/s}$$ (b) Using $\omega = r(\omega_r - \omega_l)/L$: $$\omega = 0.05 \times (10 - 6)/0.30 = 0.05 \times 4/0.30 = 0.2/0.30 = 0.6667 \text{ rad/s}$$ (c) Radius of curvature: $$R = v/\omega = 0.4 / 0.6667 = 0.6 \text{ m}$$ (d) Time for full circle: $$T = 2\pi/\omega = 2\pi / 0.6667 \approx 9.425 \text{ s}$$ > *Sanity check:* Circumference $= 2\pi R = 2\pi(0.6) = 3.77$ m. Distance covered at $v = 0.4$ m/s for $9.425$ s is $0.4 \times 9.425 = 3.77$ m. ✓ Matches. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Differential-Drive Robot — Physical Schematic > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram provides a **functional/architectural view** of the differential-drive robot's kinematic structure, mapping how wheel inputs propagate to body motion. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Controller / Planner] --> B[Desired Body Velocities v, omega] B --> C["Inverse Jacobian J inverse
1/r L/2r
1/r L/2r negative"] C --> D[Right Wheel omega_r] C --> E[Left Wheel omega_l] D --> F[Right Motor PWM] E --> G[Left Motor PWM] F --> H[Right Wheel Contact Point v_r] G --> I[Left Wheel Contact Point v_l] H --> J["Forward Kinematics
v = r omega_r + omega_l divided by 2
omega = r omega_r minus omega_l divided by L"] I --> J J --> K[Robot Body Linear Velocity v] J --> L[Robot Body Angular Velocity omega] K --> M[Pose Update x, y, theta] L --> M M --> N[Wheel Encoders Feedback] N --> A ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Differential Kinematics Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["ACTUATOR DOMAIN"] A1[omega_r in rad per s] A2[omega_l in rad per s] end subgraph FWDK["FORWARD DIFFERENTIAL KINEMATICS"] B1["v linear equals r times omega_r plus omega_l divided by 2"] B2["omega equals r times omega_r minus omega_l divided by L"] end subgraph OUTPUT["BODY DOMAIN"] C1[v in m per s] C2[omega in rad per s] end subgraph POSTURE["POSTURE INTEGRATION"] D1[xdot equals v cos theta] D2[ydot equals v sin theta] D3[thetadot equals omega] end A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B2 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C2 C1 --> D1 C1 --> D2 C2 --> D3 D1 --> E1[x k plus 1] D2 --> E2[y k plus 1] D3 --> E3[theta k plus 1] ``` ## 4.3 Kinematic States & Path Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR A[omega_r equals omega_l equal positive] --> B[Straight Line Motion R equals infinity] C[omega_r equals negative omega_l] --> D[In place Rotation R equals 0] E[omega_r not equal to omega_l same sign] --> F[Circular Arc Motion R equals L omega_r plus omega_l over 2 omega_r minus omega_l] G[omega_r equals omega_l equal 0] --> H[Stationary v equals 0 omega equals 0] ``` ## 4.4 Coordinate Frame Relationships ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WORLD["GLOBAL FRAME Xw, Yw"] W1[Robot Posture x, y, theta] end subgraph BODY["ROBOT BODY FRAME Xb, Yb"] B1[v along Xb] B2[omega about Zb] end W1 --- TRANSFORM["Rotation by theta
xdot equals v cos theta
ydot equals v sin theta"] TRANSFORM --- B1 B1 --- B2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **rotation by $\theta$** between the body frame and the world frame is the source of the **nonlinearity** in the posture equation. This is the central reason mobile-robot motion planning is mathematically richer than fixed-base manipulator kinematics. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the forward and inverse differential kinematics problem for a mobile robot. Why is the differential-drive configuration considered completely controllable in 2D planar motion?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Forward Differential Kinematics (1 mark):** Given the wheel angular velocities $(\omega_r, \omega_l)$ and robot geometric parameters $(r, L)$, compute the body linear and angular velocities $(v, \omega)$ that describe the robot's motion. - **Inverse Differential Kinematics (1 mark):** Given a desired body velocity $(v, \omega)$ in the robot's frame, compute the required wheel angular velocities $(\omega_r, \omega_l)$ that will produce this motion. - **Controllability (1 mark):** The differential-drive configuration has exactly **2 independent actuators** (two driven wheels) corresponding to the **2 DOF** of planar rigid-body motion (translation and rotation). The Jacobian is square ($2 \times 2$) and non-singular (determinant $= -r^2/L \neq 0$), so the inverse mapping always exists uniquely — hence the robot is completely controllable. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State and explain the non-holonomic constraint for a differential-drive mobile robot. Why does this constraint make motion planning non-trivial?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement of constraint (1.5 marks):** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$. This states that the component of the robot's velocity perpendicular to its heading must be zero — the robot cannot move sideways. - **Implication (1.5 marks):** This is a **non-integrable (non-holonomic)** velocity-level constraint. It cannot be reduced to a position-level constraint like $f(x,y,\theta) = 0$. Consequently, the robot's posture is **not globally controllable by configuration alone** — to move from one orientation to another, it must follow a path involving both translation and rotation (e.g., parallel-parking maneuvers). This is why classical motion-planning algorithms must be augmented with steering/behaviour components, unlike holonomic systems (e.g., mecanum wheels) where straight-line paths in any direction are always feasible. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]` **(a) Derive the forward differential kinematics equations for a differential-drive mobile robot with wheel radius $r$ and track width $L$, clearly stating all assumptions. Express the result in matrix (Jacobian) form. (7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Assumptions (1 mark):** - The robot moves on a flat, horizontal plane. - Wheels roll without slipping (pure rolling condition). - The reference point $P$ is at the midpoint of the drive axle. - Wheels are identical in radius. 2. **No-slip condition (1 mark):** $$v_l = r\omega_l, \quad v_r = r\omega_r$$ 3. **Rigid-body relations (2 marks):** The left wheel is at distance $L/2$ to the left of $P$, the right wheel at $L/2$ to the right. For a robot moving with linear velocity $v$ and angular velocity $\omega$ (CCW positive), the forward velocity of each wheel is: $$v_l = v - \omega \frac{L}{2}, \quad v_r = v + \omega \frac{L}{2}$$ 4. **Combine and solve (2 marks):** - Add: $r(\omega_l + \omega_r) = 2v \Rightarrow v = r(\omega_r + \omega_l)/2$. - Subtract: $r(\omega_r - \omega_l) = \omega L \Rightarrow \omega = r(\omega_r - \omega_l)/L$. 5. **Matrix form (1 mark):** $$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \omega_r \\ \omega_l \end{bmatrix}$$ **(b) A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m and track width $L = 0.25$ m. The right and left wheel encoders register angular velocities of 8 rad/s and 4 rad/s respectively. Compute (i) the robot's linear velocity, (ii) its angular velocity, (iii) the radius of curvature of its path, and (iv) the wheel speeds required to execute a pure rotation at 0.5 rad/s. (7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** (i) **Linear velocity (1.5 marks):** $$v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} = \frac{0.04 \times (8 + 4)}{2} = \frac{0.04 \times 12}{2} = 0.24 \text{ m/s}$$ (ii) **Angular velocity (1.5 marks):** $$\omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} = \frac{0.04 \times (8 - 4)}{0.25} = \frac{0.04 \times 4}{0.25} = \frac{0.16}{0.25} = 0.64 \text{ rad/s}$$ (iii) **Radius of curvature (2 marks):** $$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{0.24}{0.64} = 0.375 \text{ m}$$ (iv) **Wheel speeds for pure rotation at 0.5 rad/s (2 marks):** For pure rotation, $v = 0$, so $\omega_r = -\omega_l$. From $\omega = r(\omega_r - \omega_l)/L$ with $\omega_l = -\omega_r$: $$\omega = \frac{r(2\omega_r)}{L} \Rightarrow \omega_r = \frac{\omega L}{2r} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.25}{2 \times 0.04} = \frac{0.125}{0.08} = 1.5625 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega_l = -1.5625 \text{ rad/s} \quad (\text{i.e., left wheel spins backward})$$ ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a) Derive the inverse differential kinematics of a differential-drive robot starting from the forward equations. (7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **State forward equations (1 mark):** $$v = \frac{r(\omega_r + \omega_l)}{2} \quad (1), \quad \omega = \frac{r(\omega_r - \omega_l)}{L} \quad (2)$$ 2. **Isolate sum and difference (2 marks):** From (1): $\omega_r + \omega_l = 2v/r$. From (2): $\omega_r - \omega_l = \omega L/r$. 3. **Add and subtract (2 marks):** - Adding: $2\omega_r = 2v/r + \omega L/r \Rightarrow \omega_r = (2v + \omega L)/(2r)$. - Subtracting: $2\omega_l = 2v/r - \omega L/r \Rightarrow \omega_l = (2v - \omega L)/(2r)$. 4. **Matrix form (1 mark):** $$\begin{bmatrix} \omega_r \\ \omega_l \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/r & L/(2r) \\ 1/r & -L/(2r) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$ 5. **State Jacobian invertibility (1 mark):** Since the forward Jacobian has determinant $-r^2/L \neq 0$ for $r, L > 0$, the inverse Jacobian exists and is unique — confirming the system is non-singular. **(b) A mobile robot must follow a path that requires a linear velocity of 0.5 m/s and an angular velocity of 0.2 rad/s. The wheel radius is 0.05 m and the track width is 0.4 m. (i) Compute the required wheel angular velocities. (ii) If the right wheel can rotate at a maximum of 15 rad/s, what is the maximum linear velocity the robot can achieve while maintaining the same angular velocity? (iii) Determine the pose of the robot after 2 seconds if it starts at (0, 0, 0) and maintains the commanded velocities, using Euler integration with $\Delta t = 0.5$ s. (7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** (i) **Wheel speeds (2 marks):** $$\omega_r = \frac{2v + \omega L}{2r} = \frac{2(0.5) + 0.2(0.4)}{2(0.05)} = \frac{1.0 + 0.08}{0.1} = \frac{1.08}{0.1} = 10.8 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega_l = \frac{2v - \omega L}{2r} = \frac{1.0 - 0.08}{0.1} = \frac{0.92}{0.1} = 9.2 \text{ rad/s}$$ (ii) **Maximum linear velocity (2.5 marks):** With $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s fixed and $\omega_r = 15$ rad/s as the limit, using $v = (\omega_r + \omega_l)r/2$ and $\omega_l = \omega_r - \omega L/r$: $$\omega_l = 15 - \frac{0.2 \times 0.4}{0.05} = 15 - 1.6 = 13.4 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$v_{max} = \frac{(15 + 13.4) \times 0.05}{2} = \frac{28.4 \times 0.05}{2} = \frac{1.42}{2} = 0.71 \text{ m/s}$$ (iii) **Pose after 2 s (2.5 marks):** With $\Delta t = 0.5$ s, perform 4 Euler steps: - **Step 1:** $x = 0 + 0.5\cos(0)(0.5) = 0.25$; $y = 0 + 0.5\sin(0)(0.5) = 0$; $\theta = 0 + 0.2(0.5) = 0.1$ rad. - **Step 2:** $x = 0.25 + 0.5\cos(0.1)(0.5) = 0.25 + 0.5(0.9950)(0.5) = 0.25 + 0.2487 = 0.4987$; $y = 0 + 0.5\sin(0.1)(0.5) = 0.5(0.0998)(0.5) = 0.0249$; $\theta = 0.1 + 0.2(0.5) = 0.2$ rad. - **Step 3:** $x = 0.4987 + 0.5\cos(0.2)(0.5) = 0.4987 + 0.5(0.9801)(0.5) = 0.4987 + 0.2450 = 0.7437$; $y = 0.0249 + 0.5\sin(0.2)(0.5) = 0.0249 + 0.5(0.1987)(0.5) = 0.0249 + 0.0497 = 0.0746$; $\theta = 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.3$ rad. - **Step 4:** $x = 0.7437 + 0.5\cos(0.3)(0.5) = 0.7437 + 0.5(0.9553)(0.5) = 0.7437 + 0.2388 = 0.9825$ m; $y = 0.0746 + 0.5\sin(0.3)(0.5) = 0.0746 + 0.5(0.2955)(0.5) = 0.0746 + 0.0739 = 0.1485$ m; $\theta = 0.4$ rad. **Final pose after 2 s:** $x \approx 0.98$ m, $y \approx 0.15$ m, $\theta = 0.4$ rad ($\approx 22.9°$). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Sign convention errors (–2 to –3 marks):** Students frequently mix up the sign of $\omega$ in equations. By KTU convention, $\omega > 0$ is **counter-clockwise (CCW)**. A left wheel moving slower than the right wheel means the robot turns **right** (clockwise, $\omega < 0$). Always check: $\omega_r > \omega_l \Rightarrow \omega > 0$ (CCW turn), $\omega_r < \omega_l \Rightarrow \omega < 0$ (CW turn). > 2. **Confusing $L$ with the wheelbase to a caster (–1 mark):** In pure differential-drive analysis, $L$ is the **distance between the two drive wheels**, not the wheelbase to a front caster. The caster does not appear in the basic kinematic equations. > 3. **Forgetting the no-slip assumption (–1 mark):** Always **state explicitly** that the wheels roll without slipping. Without this, the formulas $v_l = r\omega_l$ etc. are invalid. > 4. **Units inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** $r$ and $L$ must be in **meters**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $v$ in **m/s**. Mixing cm with m loses marks. > 5. **Matrix form skipped (–1 mark):** The KTU 2024 scheme's CO1/CO2 mapping expects a clear Jacobian matrix presentation. A derivation without the final matrix is incomplete. > 6. **In Euler integration, computing $\theta$ before $x$ and $y$ (–1 mark):** The order matters — compute $\theta_{k+1}$ first because $x_{k+1}$ and $y_{k+1}$ depend on $\theta_k$ (or $\theta_{k+1}$ depending on convention). State your convention explicitly. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Differential Kinematics (Module 1):** - ☐ **Posture vector:** $q = [x, y, \theta]^T$ — defines the robot's state in 2D. - ☐ **Posture update equation:** $\dot{q} = [\cos\theta \cdot v, \;\sin\theta \cdot v, \;\omega]^T$ — the **fundamental nonlinear model**. - ☐ **Forward Kinematics Jacobian:** $J = \begin{bmatrix} r/2 & r/2 \\ r/L & -r/L \end{bmatrix}$ — maps wheel speeds to body velocities. - ☐ **Inverse Kinematics Jacobian:** $J^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/r & L/(2r) \\ 1/r & -L/(2r) \end{bmatrix}$ — maps body velocities to wheel speeds. - ☐ **Key FDK formulas:** $v = r(\omega_r + \omega_l)/2$ and $\omega = r(\omega_r - \omega_l)/L$. - ☐ **Key IDK formulas:** $\omega_r = (2v + \omega L)/(2r)$ and $\omega_l = (2v - \omega L)/(2r)$. - ☐ **Pure straight-line motion:** $\omega_r = \omega_l$ (and $\omega = 0$). - ☐ **Pure in-place rotation:** $\omega_r = -\omega_l$ (and $v = 0$). - ☐ **Radius of curvature:** $R = v/\omega$ (undefined for straight line, zero for in-place rotation). - ☐ **Non-holonomic constraint:** $\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0$ — sideways motion is **forbidden**. - ☐ **No-slip (pure rolling) assumption:** $v_{wheel} = r \cdot \omega_{wheel}$ — must be stated. - ☐ **Reference point convention:** Always the **midpoint of the drive axle** unless stated otherwise. - ☐ **Euler integration for pose:** $x_{k+1} = x_k + v\cos\theta_k \Delta t$, similarly for $y$ and $\theta$. - ☐ **Differential-drive is holonomic? NO!** It is **non-holonomic** because of the constraint above — this is a common trap question. - ☐ **Jacobian determinant:** $\det(J) = -r^2/L \neq 0$ — guarantees invertibility and complete controllability. - ☐ **Time for one full circle:** $T = 2\pi/\omega$ — useful for trajectory planning questions. > [!NOTE] > **Final Study Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** > Differential kinematics is a **gateway topic** for mobile robotics. Make sure you can derive the Jacobian matrix in under 5 minutes from scratch. The KTU valuation pattern rewards **clean, well-labeled derivations** with explicit assumptions. Always end with the **matrix form** and explicitly mention the **no-slip assumption**. Master this topic and Module 1's CO1/CO2 attainment becomes straightforward.

topics.title: Inverse Differential Kinematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inverse Differential Kinematics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inverse Differential Kinematics (IDK)** is the problem of determining the **joint velocities** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ of a robotic manipulator required to produce a specified **end-effector twist** $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix}$ (linear and angular velocity) in the task space. It is the *velocity-level inverse* of the geometric inverse kinematics problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Inverse Differential Kinematics is the formulation that solves $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{q})$ using the manipulator Jacobian, generally expressed as $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{v}$, with appropriate handling of redundancy and singularities."

In matrix form, the **forward differential kinematics** is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\xi} = \begin{bmatrix} \boldsymbol{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

The **inverse differential kinematics** therefore requires solving:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \boldsymbol{\xi}
$$

where $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **geometric Jacobian** matrix, $m$ is the dimension of the task-space velocity ($m = 6$ for spatial, $m = 3$ for planar), and $n$ is the number of joints.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Driving a Car in Reverse Gear:**
> Imagine you are reversing a car into a tight parking slot. You have a target *trajectory* (the parking lines) and you know the velocity at which you want the **rear bumper** (end-effector) to move. However, you control the **steering wheel, accelerator, and brake** (joint velocities). Inverse Differential Kinematics is the "reverse-engineering" math: *given the desired bumper motion, what should the wheels and pedals do?*

A more engineering-friendly analogy: think of a **robotic arm stirring coffee** ☕. You want the spoon tip to trace a perfect circle at $0.5$ rad/s (end-effector velocity). The IDK problem computes **how fast each motor at the shoulder, elbow, and wrist must spin** to make that happen — every single instant.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|:------:|:--------|:--------------:|
| $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Joint position vector | **rad** or **m** |
| $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Joint velocity vector | **rad/s** or **m/s** |
| $\boldsymbol{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ | Linear end-effector velocity | **m/s** |
| $\boldsymbol{\omega} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ | Angular end-effector velocity | **rad/s** |
| $\boldsymbol{\xi} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ | End-effector twist (spatial) | **m/s, rad/s** |
| $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ | Jacobian matrix | **dimensionless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The inverse problem is *not* a simple matrix inversion for all manipulators. The structure of $\mathbf{J}$ (square, tall, wide) dictates the solution method, and **singularities** ($\det \mathbf{J} = 0$) make the problem ill-conditioned, producing unbounded joint velocities.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Differential Kinematics Pipeline

The relationship between joint-space velocity and Cartesian-space velocity is a **first-order kinematic mapping** through the Jacobian. The pipeline is:

1. **Geometric Model** $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q})$ — direct kinematics.
2. **Time Derivative** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — forward differential kinematics.
3. **Jacobian Identification** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ — defines the linear map.
4. **Inverse Problem** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ — what we solve here.

## 2.2 The Three Structural Cases

The structure of $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ falls into **three categories**, and the solution method changes for each.

### Case A — Square Jacobian ($m = n$)
The Jacobian is square, the manipulator is **non-redundant**, and the configuration is **non-singular**.

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

- $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ exists **iff** $\det \mathbf{J} \neq 0$.
- Solution is **unique** in non-singular regions.
- Example: a 6-DOF manipulator in the general spatial case (square $6 \times 6$).

### Case B — Tall Jacobian ($m < n$, Redundant Manipulator)
The Jacobian has **more columns than rows** — the manipulator has more joints than required by the task. Infinite solutions exist for $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ that yield the same $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$.

The **Moore–Penrose Pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1}$ gives the **minimum-norm** joint velocity:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+} \, \dot{\mathbf{x}} + \left( \mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} \right) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

where $\left( \mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} \right)$ is the **null-space projector**, and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ is an *arbitrary* vector exploited to optimize secondary objectives (e.g., obstacle avoidance, joint-limit avoidance, manipulability maximization).

### Case C — Wide Jacobian ($m > n$, Under-Constrained / Under-Actuated)
The Jacobian has **fewer columns than rows** — the task demands more DOFs than the joints provide. Generally, **exact tracking is impossible** except on the *constraint surface* $\dot{\mathbf{x}} \in \mathcal{R}(\mathbf{J})$. The least-squares best fit:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \left( \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{\top} \right)^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

## 2.3 Singularities — The Failure Modes

A **kinematic singularity** occurs when the Jacobian loses rank:

$$
\det \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{J}) = 0
$$

Near singularities:
- Joint velocities become **extremely large** (mathematically infinite at the singular configuration).
- The manipulator **loses one or more DOFs** in Cartesian space.
- **Two classical singularity types**:
    - **Boundary singularities** — at the workspace boundary (e.g., fully extended arm).
    - **Interior singularities** — inside the workspace (e.g., aligned wrist joints of a 6R PUMA-type arm).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ without checking singularity conditions. Always state the assumption $\det \mathbf{J} \neq 0$ and identify the singular configurations.

## 2.4 Regularization: Damped Least Squares (DLS)

To **stabilize** the solution near singularities, **Wampler (1986)** and **Nakamura & Hanafusa (1986)** independently proposed the **Damped Least Squares (DLS)** method:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \left( \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{\top} + \lambda^2 \mathbf{I} \right)^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the **damping factor** (typically $\lambda \in [0.01, 0.1]$). The DLS solution **trades exact tracking** for **bounded joint velocities** near singularities. As $\lambda \to 0$, the DLS solution approaches the exact pseudoinverse solution.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Validity |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:---------|
| 1 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Forward differential kinematics | Always |
| 2 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Inverse (square, non-singular) | $m = n$, $\det \mathbf{J} \neq 0$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1}$ | Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse | $m < n$ (full row rank) |
| 4 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Minimum-norm solution | $m < n$ redundant |
| 5 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}} + (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ | General redundant solution | $m < n$ |
| 6 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Least-squares (under-constrained) | $m > n$ (full row rank) |
| 7 | $\det \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = 0$ | Singularity condition | Loss of DOF |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top} + \lambda^2 \mathbf{I})^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Damped Least Squares | Near singularities |
| 9 | $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})}$ | Yoshikawa manipulability | $0 \le w \le 1$ |
| 10 | $H(\mathbf{q}) = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \mathbf{J}$ | Joint-space inertia weighting | Redundancy resolution |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Inverse Differential Kinematics is Deployed:**
> - **Industrial welding robots** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC) — real-time tracking of weld seams at 1 kHz.
> - **Surgical robots** (Da Vinci) — tremor-free scaling of surgeon hand motion to instrument tip velocity.
> - **Autonomous mobile manipulation** — base + arm coordination for fetching objects in human environments.
> - **Space manipulators** (Canadarm on the ISS) — capture of free-flying satellites using Jacobian-based resolved-rate control.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Direct Kinematics to the Jacobian

**Starting Point.** The direct kinematics equation of an $n$-DOF serial manipulator is:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q}) \quad \text{with} \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}, \; \mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}
$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time using the **chain rule**:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{d \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{q})}{dt} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \frac{d \mathbf{q}}{dt}
$$

Defining the **Jacobian matrix** as the partial derivative $\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{f}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_{ij} = \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial q_j} \quad \text{(element-wise definition)}
$$

We arrive at the **forward differential kinematics** equation:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

This is a **linear map** from joint velocity space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to task velocity space $\mathbb{R}^{m}$, valid locally (first-order approximation).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverting the Linear Map (Square Case)

For $m = n$ and $\det \mathbf{J} \neq 0$, the linear map is **bijective**. Multiply both sides of $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ on the left by $\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})$:

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{I}_n \, \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Therefore, the **unique joint velocity solution** is:

$$
\boxed{\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pseudoinverse for Redundant Manipulators

For $m < n$, $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is **tall**, no exact inverse exists. We seek $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ minimizing the quadratic cost:

$$
\min_{\dot{\mathbf{q}}} \; \Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

Form the **Lagrangian**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{\top} \dot{\mathbf{q}} + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^{\top} \left( \dot{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} \right)
$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{J}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\lambda}
$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\lambda}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dot{\mathbf{x}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\lambda} = \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

Solving for $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ (assuming $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top}$ is invertible, which holds when $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) = m$):

$$
\boldsymbol{\lambda} = \left( \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{\top} \right)^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \left( \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{\top} \right)^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}}}
$$

where $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \left( \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{\top} \right)^{-1}$ is the **Moore–Penrose right pseudoinverse**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Null-Space Projection for Secondary Objectives

For a redundant manipulator ($m < n$), the general solution to $\mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{N} \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

where $\mathbf{N} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is a matrix whose columns span the **null space** of $\mathbf{J}$, and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is any vector. To verify this is a valid general solution, we apply $\mathbf{J}$:

$$
\mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{J} \mathbf{N} \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = \dot{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{0} = \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

since $\mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{I}_m$ and $\mathbf{J} \mathbf{N} = \mathbf{0}$.

A canonical choice is $\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J}$ (the **null-space projector**). Proof that $\mathbf{N}$ projects onto $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{J})$:

- $\mathbf{J} (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J}) = \mathbf{J} - (\mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{+}) \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{J} - \mathbf{I}_m \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{0}$ ✓
- $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J})^2 = \mathbf{I} - 2 \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} + \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{I} - 2 \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} + \mathbf{J}^{+} (\mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{+}) \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{I} - 2 \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} + \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J}$ ✓ (idempotent).

Hence the **complete general solution** is:

$$
\boxed{\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}} + \left( \mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} \right) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Damped Least Squares

Near singularities, the pseudoinverse $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1}$ becomes ill-conditioned because $(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1}$ amplifies small singular values. We modify the cost:

$$
\min_{\dot{\mathbf{q}}} \; \Vert \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} - \dot{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2 + \lambda^2 \Vert \dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert^2
$$

Differentiate the scalar cost $C = (\mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \dot{\mathbf{x}})^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \dot{\mathbf{x}}) + \lambda^2 \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{\top}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ and set $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
2 \mathbf{J}^{\top} \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}} - 2 \mathbf{J}^{\top} \dot{\mathbf{x}} + 2 \lambda^2 \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
(\mathbf{J}^{\top} \mathbf{J} + \lambda^2 \mathbf{I}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \left( \mathbf{J}^{\top} \mathbf{J} + \lambda^2 \mathbf{I} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{J}^{\top} \dot{\mathbf{x}}}
$$

By the **matrix inversion lemma** (Woodbury identity), an equivalent form is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{\top} \left( \mathbf{J} \mathbf{J}^{\top} + \lambda^2 \mathbf{I} \right)^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Singular Value Insight:** Using SVD $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{\top}$, the DLS solution becomes $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{V} \, \text{diag}\!\left( \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_i^2 + \lambda^2} \right) \mathbf{U}^{\top} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$. As $\sigma_i \to 0$, the gain saturates at $\sigma_i / \lambda^2$ instead of diverging to infinity — this is the regularization effect.

## 3.6 Analytical Worked Example — 2R Planar Manipulator

Consider a **2R planar manipulator** with link lengths $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m and joint angles $q_1, q_2$.

**Step 1.** Direct kinematics:

$$
x = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2), \quad y = l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Step 2.** Partial derivatives (Jacobian entries):

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} = -l_1 \sin q_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} = -l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2), \quad \frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} = l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Step 3.** Assemble the Jacobian:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 \sin q_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) & -l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\ l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) & l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4.** Determinant:

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2
$$

**Step 5.** Singularities: $\det \mathbf{J} = 0 \iff \sin q_2 = 0 \iff q_2 = 0$ (arm fully stretched) or $q_2 = \pi$ (arm folded back).

**Step 6.** Inverse Jacobian (valid for $\sin q_2 \neq 0$):

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \frac{1}{l_1 l_2 \sin q_2} \begin{bmatrix} l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) & l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \\ -l_1 \cos q_1 - l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & -l_1 \sin q_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7.** Solve for joint velocities given end-effector velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}
$$

This explicit inversion is the **canonical KTU-style exam problem** for 2R and 3R manipulators.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inverse Differential Kinematics Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task-Space Velocity Spec] --> B{Reference Frame Check}
    B --> C[Convert to Body Frame v b]
    C --> D[Compute Jacobian J at current q]
    D --> E{Jacobian Structure}
    E -->|Square m = n| F[Check det J not eq 0]
    E -->|Tall m less than n| G[Compute Pseudoinverse J plus]
    E -->|Wide m greater than n| H[Least Squares via J transpose J J transpose inverse]
    F --> I{Is det J near 0?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Activate Damped Least Squares]
    I -->|No| K[Compute J inverse v]
    G --> L{Null-Space Task?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Add Null Space Projector term]
    L -->|No| N[Compute J plus v]
    H --> O[Compute J transpose J J transpose inverse v]
    J --> P[Output Joint Velocities qdot]
    K --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Integrate qdot to get q new]
    Q --> R[Send Commands to Joint Actuators]
    R --> S[Measure New Joint Positions]
    S --> D
```

## 4.2 Information Flow: From Desired Twist to Joint Actuation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired End Effector Twist xi desired] --> B[Inverse Differential Kinematics Solver]
    C[Current Joint Angles q] --> B
    B --> D[Joint Velocity Vector qdot]
    D --> E[Joint Level Servo Controllers]
    E --> F[Joint Torques tau]
    F --> G[Robot Dynamics tau = M qddot + C qdot + g]
    G --> H[Actual Joint Acceleration qddot]
    H --> I[Actual Joint Velocities qdot via Integration]
    I --> J[Actual Joint Positions q via Integration]
    J --> K[Actual End Effector Pose x actual]
    K --> L[Feedback to Inverse Solver]
    L --> B
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right IDK Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need Joint Velocities] --> B{Is Jacobian Square?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Is det J equal 0?}
    B -->|No| D{Is Jacobian Tall m less than n?}
    C -->|No| E[Use Exact Inverse: qdot = J inverse v]
    C -->|Yes| F[Use Damped Least Squares]
    D -->|Yes| G{Is Null Space Task Required?}
    D -->|No| H[Use Least Squares: J transpose J J transpose inverse]
    G -->|Yes| I[Use Pseudoinverse plus Null Space Projector]
    G -->|No| J[Use Minimum Norm Pseudoinverse]
    E --> K[Output Joint Velocities]
    F --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    H --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node is a process or decision, each arrow is a data flow. The closed loop in Section 4.2 represents the **resolved-rate control architecture** — a classical IDK-based real-time control scheme first formulated by Whitney (1969).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between forward and inverse differential kinematics. State the role of the Jacobian in this context.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Forward Differential Kinematics | Inverse Differential Kinematics |
|:-------|:--------------------------------|:---------------------------------|
| **Given** | Joint velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | End-effector twist $\boldsymbol{\xi}$ |
| **Find** | End-effector velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Joint velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ |
| **Equation** | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ |
| **Uniqueness** | Always unique (linear map) | Non-unique if redundant |
| **Existence** | Always exists | May not exist at singularities |

The **Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the **time-varying linear operator** that maps joint velocity space to task-space velocity. It is the **bridge** between the two problems, encoding both the manipulator's geometry and its instantaneous dexterity (manipulability).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a kinematic singularity? Why is the inverse Jacobian ill-behaved near it?**

**Model Answer:**

A **kinematic singularity** is a configuration $\mathbf{q}_s$ where the Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}_s)$ loses its full rank, i.e., $\det \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}_s) = 0$ or the minimum singular value $\sigma_{\min} = 0$.

> The inverse Jacobian is **ill-behaved** because the term $(\det \mathbf{J})^{-1}$ in the adjugate formula $\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\det \mathbf{J}} \text{adj}(\mathbf{J})$ **blows up to infinity** as $\det \mathbf{J} \to 0$, producing **physically unrealizable unbounded joint velocities** even for modest desired end-effector motions. This corresponds to a loss of one or more degrees of freedom in the Cartesian space — the manipulator cannot move in certain directions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Two sub-parts `(a) 7 marks` and `(b) 7 marks` must be answered.

---

### **Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP Module 1]**

**(a)** For a **2R planar manipulator** with link lengths $l_1, l_2$ and joint angles $q_1, q_2$, derive the Jacobian matrix. Find the configurations at which the manipulator encounters kinematic singularities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the end-effector of the 2R manipulator is required to move with a linear velocity $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$ m/s at the configuration $q_1 = 30^\circ, q_2 = 45^\circ$ with $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m. Using the inverse Jacobian, compute the required joint velocities. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *(7 marks)*

The direct kinematics of the 2R planar manipulator:

$$
x = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
y = l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$
\dot{x} = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)
$$

Rearranging into $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 \sin q_1 - l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) & -l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) & l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Singularity condition:** $\det \mathbf{J} = 0$:

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{q_2 = 0 \; \text{or} \; q_2 = \pi}
$$

These correspond to the **fully extended** and **fully folded** arm configurations, respectively. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

**Part (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1.** Substitute $q_1 = 30^\circ$, $q_2 = 45^\circ$, $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m:

$$
q_1 + q_2 = 75^\circ
$$

$$
\sin 30^\circ = 0.5, \quad \sin 45^\circ = 0.7071, \quad \sin 75^\circ = 0.9659
$$

$$
\cos 30^\circ = 0.8660, \quad \cos 45^\circ = 0.7071, \quad \cos 75^\circ = 0.2588
$$

**Step 2.** Construct the Jacobian:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -(0.5) - (0.9659) & -0.9659 \\ (0.8660) + (0.2588) & 0.2588 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4659 & -0.9659 \\ 1.1248 & 0.2588 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.** Determinant (verifying non-singular):

$$
\det \mathbf{J} = (-1.4659)(0.2588) - (-0.9659)(1.1248) = -0.3794 + 1.0863 = 0.7069 \neq 0
$$

**Step 4.** Compute $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$:

$$
\mathbf{J}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.7069} \begin{bmatrix} 0.2588 & 0.9659 \\ -1.1248 & -1.4659 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3661 & 1.3663 \\ -1.5911 & -2.0734 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5.** Apply $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \mathbf{v}$ with $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$
\dot{q}_1 = (0.3661)(0.5) + (1.3663)(0.2) = 0.1831 + 0.2733 = 0.4563 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\dot{q}_2 = (-1.5911)(0.5) + (-2.0734)(0.2) = -0.7956 - 0.4147 = -1.2103 \; \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{q}_1 \approx 0.456 \; \text{rad/s}, \quad \dot{q}_2 \approx -1.210 \; \text{rad/s}}
$$

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP Module 1] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A **3-DOF planar manipulator** with link lengths $l_1 = l_2 = l_3 = 1$ m is given. Derive the $3 \times 3$ Jacobian matrix in terms of joint angles $q_1, q_2, q_3$. Discuss the singularity conditions of this manipulator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **redundant 7-DOF manipulator** (e.g., a 7R anthropomorphic arm), write the general solution for joint velocities in terms of the end-effector twist $\boldsymbol{\xi}$. Explain the role of the **null-space projector** in optimizing secondary objectives such as obstacle avoidance. Use the **pseudoinverse formulation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):** *(7 marks)*

The direct kinematics:

$$
x = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) + l_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3)
$$

$$
y = l_1 \sin q_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) + l_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3)
$$

The end-effector orientation:

$$
\phi = q_1 + q_2 + q_3
$$

The velocity vector is $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = [\dot{x}, \dot{y}, \dot{\phi}]^{\top}$. Computing partial derivatives:

$$
\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} - l_3 s_{123} & -l_2 s_{12} - l_3 s_{123} & -l_3 s_{123} \\ l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} + l_3 c_{123} & l_2 c_{12} + l_3 c_{123} & l_3 c_{123} \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $s_1 = \sin q_1$, $s_{12} = \sin(q_1+q_2)$, $s_{123} = \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3)$, and similarly for cosines.

**Singularity condition:** $\det \mathbf{J} = 0$. For the 3R planar arm, this determinant simplifies to $l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$ (the orientation column contributes only via the last row, which is independent of $s_1, s_{12}, s_{123}$ in the determinant expansion). Hence:

$$
\boxed{\det \mathbf{J} = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2 = 0 \iff q_2 = 0 \text{ or } \pi}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Singularity at $q_2 = 0$ (arm fully stretched — boundary singularity) and $q_2 = \pi$ (elbow folded back — interior singularity). The third joint $q_3$ does **not** affect the singularity condition. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

**Part (b):** *(7 marks)*

For a **7-DOF redundant manipulator**, $\mathbf{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 7}$ is **tall**. The general solution to $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}} + \left( \mathbf{I}_7 - \mathbf{J}^{+} \mathbf{J} \right) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

where $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1}$ is the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^7$ is an arbitrary velocity vector.

**Null-Space Projector Role:**

The matrix $\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I}_7 - \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J}$ is the **null-space projector** of $\mathbf{J}$, with two key properties:

1. **Idempotence:** $\mathbf{N}^2 = \mathbf{N}$ — repeated projection does nothing new.
2. **Null-space mapping:** $\mathbf{J} \mathbf{N} = \mathbf{0}$ — the projected velocity produces **zero end-effector motion**.

This means the second term $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J})\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ can be used to **reconfigure the internal posture** of the arm (e.g., bend the elbow to avoid an obstacle) **without disturbing the end-effector trajectory**.

**Typical secondary objective:** Maximize the manipulability index $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})}$ by setting:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = k_0 \left( \frac{\partial w}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \right)^{\top}
$$

or minimize distance from a desired "comfort" configuration $\mathbf{q}^*$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = k_0 (\mathbf{q}^* - \mathbf{q})
$$

The complete closed-loop scheme is known as the **Extended Jacobian Method** or **Task-Priority Formulation** (Nakamura & Hanafusa, 1987). **(7 marks awarded)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Inverse Differential Kinematics:**
> 1. **Forgetting the singularity check** — writing $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ without verifying $\det \mathbf{J} \neq 0$ loses **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing joint and task spaces** — students often write $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ (forwards!) instead of $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ — **3 mark penalty**.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical problems** — always report rad/s for revolute, m/s for prismatic.
> 4. **Incorrect pseudoinverse formula** — $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1}$ is for $m < n$ (tall). For $m > n$ (wide), the correct form is $\mathbf{J}^{+} = (\mathbf{J}^{\top}\mathbf{J})^{-1}\mathbf{J}^{\top}$. Mixing these is a **common 2-mark error**.
> 5. **Ignoring null-space in redundant manipulators** — for $n > m$ the solution is non-unique. State the **minimum-norm** choice explicitly.
> 6. **No diagram for a kinematic problem** — KTU evaluators award marks for **schematic** of the manipulator with frame assignments.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Inverse Differential Kinematics** solves for joint velocities given end-effector twist: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{x}}$.
- ☐ The **Jacobian** $\mathbf{J} = \partial \mathbf{f}/\partial \mathbf{q}$ is the first-order bridge between joint and task spaces.
- ☐ **Three structural cases:** Square (unique if non-singular), Tall/Redundant (infinite solutions, use pseudoinverse), Wide/Under-constrained (least-squares solution only).
- ☐ **Singularities** occur at $\det \mathbf{J} = 0$ — joint velocities become infinite, manipulator loses DOFs.
- ☐ **Pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{\top}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})^{-1}$ gives the **minimum-norm** velocity for redundant arms.
- ☐ **Null-space projector** $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{J})$ allows reconfiguration of internal joint motion without affecting the end-effector.
- ☐ **Damped Least Squares (DLS)** with damping factor $\lambda$ stabilizes the solution near singularities at the cost of small tracking error.
- ☐ **Manipulability index** $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top})}$ measures distance from singularity ($w = 0$ at singularity, $w$ maximum at optimal configurations).
- ☐ For a **2R planar arm**: $\det \mathbf{J} = l_1 l_2 \sin q_2$, singularities at $q_2 = 0, \pi$.
- ☐ For a **3R planar arm**: same singularity condition as 2R (the third joint doesn't affect it).
- ☐ **Resolved-rate control** (Whitney, 1969) is the canonical real-time IDK-based control loop.
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** explicitly: $\det \mathbf{J} \neq 0$, manipulator is in a non-singular configuration, joints are within limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Statics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statics in Mobile Robotics** is the branch of rigid body mechanics that analyzes the equilibrium of robotic systems (manipulators, mobile platforms, and wheeled/-legged robots) under the action of forces and moments, **without considering motion** (acceleration = 0). It forms the foundational pillar for understanding manipulator loading, payload capacity, base reactions, and tip-force analysis in stationary or slowly-moving robotic configurations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme context, statics for mobile robotics encompasses:
- **Force analysis** at joints and end-effectors
- **Moment (torque) balance** about reference points
- **Static equilibrium conditions**: $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ and $\sum \vec{M} = 0$
- **Center of gravity (CG)** determination
- **Stability criteria** for mobile platforms

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Robotic Arm Holding a Glass of Water**

Imagine a robotic arm mounted on a kitchen counter holding a glass of water. While the water is still (no sloshing), the arm is essentially in a **static condition**. The forces at play are:
- **Gravity** pulling the glass and arm links downward
- **Joint motor torques** keeping the arm rigid in position
- **Reaction forces** at the base where the arm is bolted to the counter

Just like you instinctively know how to hold a heavy bag without tipping over, a robot must be designed so that its motors and structure can statically balance all these forces. This is exactly what **Statics in Robotics** deals with — calculating these "invisible" forces and moments so the robot doesn't collapse, tip over, or drop the payload.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Equilibrium force sum**: $\sum \vec{F} = 0 \text{ N}$
- **Equilibrium moment sum**: $\sum \vec{M} = 0 \text{ N·m}$
- **Degrees of static indeterminacy** for a planar robot base: $D_s = 3n - r - 3$ (where $n$ = number of bodies, $r$ = number of reaction components)
- **Static stability factor (SSF)** for mobile robots: typically $\text{SSF} \geq 1.0$ for stable operation

## 1.4 Why Statics Matters in Mobile Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Statics is the **pre-requisite** for dynamics (Module 2/3), control system design, and payload optimization. Every motor sizing problem in robotics begins with a static force/torque calculation.

Without proper static analysis, a mobile robot may:
1. **Tip over** during payload manipulation
2. **Exceed motor torque ratings** causing burnout
3. **Experience structural deformation** under load
4. **Lose traction** due to improper weight distribution
5. **Fail grasping tasks** due to insufficient grip force calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Statics

The entire framework of statics in mobile robotics rests on **four foundational pillars** derived from Newtonian mechanics:

### Pillar 1: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia)
A body at rest remains at rest unless acted upon by an external force. In robotics, this means a stationary manipulator **must have all forces and moments balanced**.

### Pillar 2: Force Equilibrium
The vector sum of all forces acting on a robotic link or the entire robot must equal zero:

$$\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$$

For a 2D planar analysis, this decomposes into:
$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0$$

For a 3D spatial analysis:
$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum F_z = 0$$

### Pillar 3: Moment Equilibrium
The vector sum of all moments (torques) about **any** point must equal zero:

$$\sum \vec{M} = \vec{0}$$

This is the **rotational counterpart** to force equilibrium. For 2D:
$$\sum M_z = 0$$

### Pillar 4: Action-Reaction Principle
For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. In a robotic joint, the torque applied by the motor is reacted by an equal torque from the link.

## 2.2 Free Body Diagram (FBD) Methodology

The **Free Body Diagram** is the single most important analytical tool in KTU statics problems. It involves:

1. **Isolating the body** of interest from its surroundings
2. **Drawing all external forces** (weight, applied loads, joint reactions, friction, normal forces)
3. **Marking all moments** acting on the body
4. **Establishing a coordinate system** (typically at the joint or base)
5. **Applying equilibrium equations** to solve for unknowns

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** FBDs are used universally in robotic arm design, vehicle stability analysis, legged robot locomotion, gripper design, and payload capacity determination. Every commercial robot datasheet includes static load capacity derived from such analysis.

## 2.3 Force Types in Mobile Robotics

| Force Type | Symbol | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight (Gravity)** | $W = mg$ | Body's gravitational pull | Arm link weight |
| **Normal Force** | $N$ | Perpendicular contact reaction | Wheel on ground |
| **Friction Force** | $f = \mu N$ | Tangential contact resistance | Tire-road grip |
| **Tension/Compression** | $T, C$ | Axial force in link | Hydraulic actuator |
| **Applied Load** | $F_a$ | External payload force | Object being lifted |
| **Joint Reaction** | $R_x, R_y$ | Force from adjacent link | Motor bearing load |
| **Buoyancy** | $F_b = \rho g V$ | Fluid displacement force | Underwater robot |

## 2.4 Moment of a Force

The **moment** of a force $\vec{F}$ about a point $O$ is defined as the cross product of the position vector $\vec{r}$ (from $O$ to the point of application of $\vec{F}$) and the force vector:

$$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

**Magnitude:**
$$\vert M_O \vert = \vert r \vert \cdot \vert F \vert \cdot \sin(\theta) = F \cdot d_{\perp}$$

where $d_{\perp}$ is the **perpendicular distance** (moment arm) from $O$ to the line of action of $\vec{F}$.

**Varignon's Theorem (Principle of Moments):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The moment of a force about a point is equal to the **algebraic sum of the moments** of its components about the same point. This simplifies the analysis of forces with multiple components.

## 2.5 Center of Gravity (CG) and Center of Mass (CM)

For a robotic link of mass $m_i$ located at position $\vec{r}_i$, the center of gravity is:

$$\vec{r}_{CG} = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$$

For a continuous body:
$$\vec{r}_{CG} = \frac{\int \vec{r} \, dm}{\int dm}$$

The **total weight** of the robot acts at the CG, which is critical for stability and moment calculations.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMINATIONS**

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | Horizontal force equilibrium | Base reaction in x |
| 2 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | Vertical force equilibrium | Weight support |
| 3 | $\sum M_O = 0$ | Moment equilibrium about O | Joint torque calc |
| 4 | $W = mg$ | Weight calculation | Link loading |
| 5 | $M = F \cdot d$ | Moment = Force × perpendicular distance | Torque arm analysis |
| 6 | $\tau = r \times F$ | Vector moment | 3D torque analysis |
| 7 | $\vec{r}_{CG} = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Center of gravity | Stability analysis |
| 8 | $f_s \leq \mu_s N$ | Static friction limit | Wheel grip, gripper |
| 9 | $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$ | Mass moment of inertia | (Bridge to dynamics) |
| 10 | $F_{tip} \cdot L_1 = W \cdot L_2$ | Lever principle in arm | Payload capacity |
| 11 | $\sum F_z = 0$ (3D) | Out-of-plane force eq. | Spatial manipulator |
| 12 | $\sum M_x = \sum M_y = 0$ (3D) | Spatial moment eq. | Spatial base loads |

> **Note on Table Symbols:** All absolute value notations use standard math syntax. The vertical pipe is preserved only in formulas where mathematically essential.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered here are directly applied in:

1. **Industrial Manipulator Design** — ABB, KUKA, FANUC robots use static analysis to determine maximum payload
2. **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** — Amazon Kiva robots use CG analysis to prevent tipping while carrying shelves
3. **Humanoid Robots** — Boston Dynamics' Atlas uses real-time static balance computation
4. **Drone (UAV) Design** — Quadcopter thrust calculations rely on force equilibrium
5. **Surgical Robots** — Da Vinci system uses precise static force feedback
6. **Mars Rovers** — Curiosity's CG shifts during arm extension analyzed statically

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Link Planar Manipulator Static Analysis

Consider a **two-link planar robotic arm** holding a payload, the most common KTU examination problem.

### Problem Setup

- Link 1 (shoulder): length $L_1$, mass $m_1$, angle $\theta_1$ from horizontal
- Link 2 (elbow): length $L_2$, mass $m_2$, angle $\theta_2$ from horizontal
- Payload mass: $m_p$ at the end-effector
- Joint 1 (shoulder) at origin $O$
- Joint 2 (elbow) at position $(L_1 \cos\theta_1, L_1 \sin\theta_1)$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify all forces acting on Link 2 (forearm)**

Forces acting on Link 2:
- Weight of link 2: $W_2 = m_2 g$ (acting downward at CG of link 2, i.e., at $L_2/2$ from elbow)
- Payload weight: $W_p = m_p g$ (acting downward at end-effector, i.e., at $L_2$ from elbow)
- Reaction force at joint 2 (elbow): $R_{2x}$ (horizontal) and $R_{2y}$ (vertical)
- Motor torque at joint 2: $\tau_2$

**Step 2: Apply equilibrium equations to Link 2**

Horizontal force equilibrium:
$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies R_{2x} = 0$$

(If no external horizontal force is applied at the end-effector)

Vertical force equilibrium:
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_{2y} - W_2 - W_p = 0$$
$$R_{2y} = W_2 + W_p = m_2 g + m_p g$$

Moment equilibrium about joint 2 (elbow):
$$\sum M_2 = 0$$
$$\tau_2 - W_2 \cdot \frac{L_2}{2}\cos\theta_2 - W_p \cdot L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0$$

Solving for $\tau_2$:
$$\tau_2 = W_2 \cdot \frac{L_2}{2}\cos\theta_2 + W_p \cdot L_2 \cos\theta_2$$
$$\tau_2 = \left(\frac{m_2 g L_2}{2} + m_p g L_2\right)\cos\theta_2$$
$$\tau_2 = g L_2 \cos\theta_2 \left(\frac{m_2}{2} + m_p\right)$$

**Step 3: Analyze Link 1 (upper arm)**

Forces on Link 1:
- Weight: $W_1 = m_1 g$ at its CG ($L_1/2$ from shoulder)
- Reaction from Link 2 at elbow: $R_{2x}$, $R_{2y}$ (Newton's 3rd Law — opposite direction)
- Base reaction at shoulder: $R_{1x}$, $R_{1y}$
- Shoulder motor torque: $\tau_1$

**Step 4: Apply equilibrium to Link 1**

Horizontal equilibrium:
$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies R_{1x} - R_{2x} = 0 \implies R_{1x} = 0$$

Vertical equilibrium:
$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_{1y} - W_1 - R_{2y} = 0$$
$$R_{1y} = W_1 + R_{2y} = m_1 g + m_2 g + m_p g$$
$$R_{1y} = (m_1 + m_2 + m_p) g$$

Moment equilibrium about joint 1 (shoulder):
$$\sum M_1 = 0$$

The moment contribution of $W_1$ about shoulder:
$$M_{W_1} = -W_1 \cdot \frac{L_1}{2}\cos\theta_1$$

The moment contribution of $R_{2y}$ about shoulder (since $R_{2x} = 0$):
$$M_{R_{2y}} = -R_{2y} \cdot L_1 \cos\theta_1$$

Wait — careful: $R_{2y}$ acts at the elbow, and creates a moment about the shoulder. The horizontal distance from shoulder to elbow is $L_1 \cos\theta_1$. So:
$$M_{R_{2y}} = R_{2y} \cdot L_1 \cos\theta_1$$

(Note: $R_{2y}$ is the upward reaction at elbow transmitted to link 1 as downward force on link 1, but by Newton's 3rd law, the link 1 experiences a downward force $R_{2y}$ at the elbow position from link 2.)

Let me re-derive with care. The force $R_{2y}$ that link 2 exerts on link 1 at the elbow is **downward** (since link 2 pushes down on link 1 with the weight of link 2 + payload). So the moment about shoulder is:

$$M_{R_{2y, \text{on 1}}} = R_{2y} \cdot L_1 \cos\theta_1 \quad \text{(clockwise, hence negative)}$$

Therefore:
$$\tau_1 - W_1 \cdot \frac{L_1}{2}\cos\theta_1 - R_{2y} \cdot L_1 \cos\theta_1 = 0$$

$$\tau_1 = W_1 \cdot \frac{L_1}{2}\cos\theta_1 + R_{2y} \cdot L_1 \cos\theta_1$$

Substituting $W_1 = m_1 g$ and $R_{2y} = (m_2 + m_p)g$:

$$\tau_1 = m_1 g \cdot \frac{L_1}{2}\cos\theta_1 + (m_2 + m_p)g \cdot L_1 \cos\theta_1$$

$$\tau_1 = g L_1 \cos\theta_1 \left(\frac{m_1}{2} + m_2 + m_p\right)$$

**Step 5: Final Compact Result**

$$\boxed{\tau_1 = g L_1 \cos\theta_1 \left(\frac{m_1}{2} + m_2 + m_p\right)}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_2 = g L_2 \cos\theta_2 \left(\frac{m_2}{2} + m_p\right)}$$

These two equations are the **fundamental static torque equations** for a 2-link planar manipulator and are extremely high-yield for KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Typical KTU Statics Problems

| Problem Type | Key Equations | Unknowns Solved | Common Twist |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single link with payload | $\tau = gL\cos\theta (m/2 + m_p)$ | Joint torque | Angle variation |
| Two-link manipulator | $\tau_1, \tau_2$ formulas above | Both joint torques | $\theta_1, \theta_2$ |
| Mobile robot on incline | $\sum F_x = 0, \sum M = 0$ | Required friction | Slope angle |
| Gripper holding object | $F_{grip} \geq \frac{W}{2\mu}$ | Grip force | Coefficient of friction |
| Suspended payload (crane) | $T = W / \sin\alpha$ | Cable tension | Cable angle |
| Ladder against wall | Multi-force FBD | Friction, reactions | Wall reaction |
| Wheeled vehicle tipping | $\tan\theta_{tip} = \frac{t}{2h}$ | Tipping angle | Track width, CG height |

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Tipping Condition for a Mobile Robot

Consider a four-wheeled mobile robot on an inclined surface:

- Track width (distance between left and right wheels): $t$
- Height of CG above ground: $h$
