- Total weight: $W = mg$ acting at CG

When the robot is on a slope of angle $\alpha$, the **gravity force** $W$ acts vertically downward through the CG. The components are:
- Parallel to slope: $W \sin\alpha$ (causing tendency to slide)
- Perpendicular to slope: $W \cos\alpha$ (causing tendency to tip)

**Tipping occurs** when the line of action of the weight passes outside the wheel-base support polygon.

Taking moments about the **downhill wheel contact point** (the pivot about which tipping would occur):

The perpendicular distance from the CG to the tipping edge is:
$$d_{\perp} = \frac{t}{2}\cos\alpha - h\sin\alpha$$

**Tipping condition:** The moment of $W$ about the downhill wheel becomes zero (or reverses):

$$\sum M_{downhill} = 0$$
$$W \cos\alpha \cdot \frac{t}{2} - W \sin\alpha \cdot h = 0$$

Dividing by $W \cos\alpha$ (assuming $\alpha < 90°$):
$$\frac{t}{2} = h \tan\alpha_{tip}$$

$$\boxed{\tan\alpha_{tip} = \frac{t}{2h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** A mobile robot is **more stable** (resists tipping) when:
> - Track width $t$ is **larger** (wider base)
> - CG height $h$ is **lower** (CG closer to ground)
>
> This is why sports cars corner faster without tipping, and why industrial forklifts have low, wide bases.

## 3.4 Derivation: Gripper Force for Static Grasping

A simple two-finger gripper holds a cylindrical object:

- Object weight: $W = mg$
- Coefficient of friction between gripper pads and object: $\mu$
- Gripper applies normal force $N$ on each side

**Vertical equilibrium of the object:**
$$2f = W$$
$$2\mu N = W$$
$$N = \frac{W}{2\mu}$$

**Safety factor of 2** is typically applied in real designs:
$$N_{design} = \frac{W}{\mu} \quad \text{(Factor of Safety = 2)}$$

$$\boxed{F_{grip,min} = \frac{mg}{\mu}}$$

This is the **minimum grip force** required to hold the object without slipping. Real grippers use force feedback sensors to apply this force precisely.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Free Body Diagram: Two-Link Robotic Manipulator

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Base Origin O] -->|Shoulder Joint 1| B[Link 1 L1]
    B -->|Elbow Joint 2| C[Link 2 L2]
    C -->|Wrist| D[Payload mp]
    
    B -.->|W1 = m1g| B
    C -.->|W2 = m2g| C
    D -.->|Wp = mpg| D
    
    A -->|R1x, R1y, τ1| A
    B -->|R2x, R2y, τ2| B
```

**Description:** The diagram shows a two-link planar manipulator. The base (Origin O) supports Link 1 (length $L_1$, mass $m_1$) at the shoulder joint with reaction forces $R_{1x}$, $R_{1y}$ and motor torque $\tau_1$. Link 1 supports Link 2 (length $L_2$, mass $m_2$) at the elbow joint with reaction forces $R_{2x}$, $R_{2y}$ and motor torque $\tau_2$. The payload of mass $m_p$ hangs at the end-effector. Each link's weight acts downward at its respective center of gravity.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Static Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1[Step 1: Identify Robot Configuration] --> step2[Step 2: Draw Free Body Diagram]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: List All External Forces and Moments]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Establish Coordinate System]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Apply Force Equilibrium ΣF = 0]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Apply Moment Equilibrium ΣM = 0]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Solve Simultaneous Equations]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Validate Physical Plausibility]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Report Joint Torques and Base Reactions]
    
    style step1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style step2 fill:#fff4e1
    style step3 fill:#fff4e1
    style step4 fill:#fff4e1
    style step5 fill:#e8f5e9
    style step6 fill:#e8f5e9
    style step7 fill:#fce4ec
    style step8 fill:#f3e5f5
    style step9 fill:#c8e6c9
```

**Description:** This block diagram represents the **standard KTU methodology** for solving any static analysis problem in mobile robotics. Each step must be performed sequentially. The color coding groups the steps into phases: Blue (problem setup), Yellow (FBD construction), Green (equilibrium application), Pink (computation), Purple (verification), and Dark Green (final output).

## 4.3 Force Decomposition on Inclined Mobile Robot

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Mobile Robot on Slope] -->|Weight W vertical| B[CG Point]
    B -->|Component| C[W sinα parallel to slope]
    B -->|Component| D[W cosα perpendicular to slope]
    
    C --> E[Causes Sliding Tendency]
    C --> F[Opposed by Friction f = μN]
    
    D --> G[Creates Normal Force on Wheels]
    D --> H[Creates Tipping Moment about Downhill Wheel]
    
    H -->|If tanα > t/2h| I[ROBOT TIPS OVER]
    H -->|If tanα < t/2h| J[ROBOT REMAINS STABLE]
    
    style I fill:#ffcdd2
    style J fill:#c8e6c9
```

**Description:** The flow diagram illustrates how a mobile robot's weight $W$ on an inclined surface decomposes into two components — one parallel to the slope (causing sliding, opposed by friction) and one perpendicular (creating tipping moment). The decision boundary for stability is governed by the relation $\tan\alpha_{tip} = t/(2h)$, where $t$ is track width and $h$ is CG height. Red indicates failure; green indicates stable operation.

## 4.4 Support and Reaction Diagram: Mobile Robot Base

```mermaid
graph TD
    Base[Robot Base / Chassis] -->|Down Force| FL[Front Left Wheel Reaction NFL]
    Base -->|Down Force| FR[Front Right Wheel Reaction NFR]
    Base -->|Down Force| RL[Rear Left Wheel Reaction NRL]
    Base -->|Down Force| RR[Rear Right Wheel Reaction NRR]
    
    Base -->|Applied Load| Arm[Manipulator Weight W_arm]
    Arm -->|Payload| Pay[Payload W_p]
    
    FL --> Ground[Ground Surface]
    FR --> Ground
    RL --> Ground
    RR --> Ground
    
    Ground -->|Friction μN| Fric[Available Traction Force]
```

**Description:** A four-wheeled mobile robot platform showing how the total weight is distributed among four wheel contact points. The manipulator arm and its payload contribute additional downward force. The ground provides normal reactions at each wheel and friction forces (proportional to the normal reactions) that enable locomotion and prevent slipping. This is the basis for static stability analysis of mobile robotic platforms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Concept of Static Equilibrium `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define static equilibrium as applied to a robotic manipulator. State the two fundamental conditions that must be satisfied.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Static equilibrium** of a robotic manipulator refers to a state in which the manipulator is at rest (or moving with constant velocity) and **all forces and moments acting on it are balanced**.

The **two fundamental conditions** of static equilibrium are:

1. **Force Equilibrium:** The vector sum of all external forces acting on the robot (or any of its links) must be zero:
   $$\sum \vec{F} = \vec{0}$$

2. **Moment Equilibrium:** The vector sum of all moments (torques) about any point must be zero:
   $$\sum \vec{M} = \vec{0}$$

These conditions ensure that the manipulator does not undergo linear or angular acceleration, which is essential for predictable position control and safe payload handling.

---

### Question 2: Free Body Diagram `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a Free Body Diagram (FBD)? List any four quantities that are represented in an FBD of a robotic link.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is a simplified sketch of a body isolated from its surroundings, showing **all external forces and moments** acting on it. It is a critical tool for applying equilibrium equations.

Four quantities represented in an FBD of a robotic link are:
1. **Weight** of the link ($W = mg$) acting at its center of gravity
2. **Joint reaction forces** ($R_x$, $R_y$) at the connecting joints
3. **Applied external loads** (e.g., payload forces at end-effector)
4. **Motor torque** ($\tau$) at the actuated joint

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A robotic manipulator consists of a single rigid link of length $L = 0.5$ m and mass $m = 4$ kg, hinged at one end. The link is held horizontally and carries a payload of mass $m_p = 2$ kg at its free end. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².**

**a)** Draw the Free Body Diagram of the link and identify all forces and moments acting on it. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Determine the reaction forces at the hinge and the torque required at the hinge to maintain static equilibrium. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Solution — Free Body Diagram (7 marks)

**FBD Description:**

```
        ↑ Ry
        |
        |
    O---+---------------> x
        |\
        | \
        |  \  τ (motor torque, counter-clockwise)
        |   \
   Ry↑  |    \
        |     \  Link (length L, horizontal)
   Rx→  |      \
        |       \
        |        \
        ↓         ● (payload at end)
        Wy = mg   |
        (at L/2)  ↓ Wp = mpg
                   (at L)
```

**Forces and Moments Identified:**

1. **Weight of the link:** $W = mg = 4 \times 9.81 = 39.24$ N, acting downward at the midpoint ($L/2 = 0.25$ m from hinge)

2. **Payload weight:** $W_p = m_p g = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$ N, acting downward at the free end ($L = 0.5$ m from hinge)

3. **Hinge reaction forces:** $R_x$ (horizontal) and $R_y$ (vertical) at the hinge point $O$

4. **Motor torque at hinge:** $\tau$ (assumed counter-clockwise positive) to balance the gravitational moments

#### Part (b) Solution — Equilibrium Equations (7 marks)

**Step 1: Apply horizontal force equilibrium**

$$\sum F_x = 0$$
$$R_x = 0 \text{ N}$$

(No horizontal forces other than the hinge reaction)

**Step 2: Apply vertical force equilibrium**

$$\sum F_y = 0$$
$$R_y - W - W_p = 0$$
$$R_y = W + W_p = 39.24 + 19.62 = 58.86 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Apply moment equilibrium about the hinge $O$**

$$\sum M_O = 0$$
$$\tau - W \cdot \frac{L}{2} - W_p \cdot L = 0$$
$$\tau = W \cdot \frac{L}{2} + W_p \cdot L$$
$$\tau = 39.24 \times 0.25 + 19.62 \times 0.5$$
$$\tau = 9.81 + 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Results:**

$$\boxed{R_x = 0 \text{ N}, \quad R_y = 58.86 \text{ N} \text{ (upward)}, \quad \tau = 19.62 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A two-link planar robotic manipulator has the following specifications:**
- **Link 1 (shoulder to elbow):** Length $L_1 = 0.6$ m, mass $m_1 = 3$ kg, oriented at $\theta_1 = 60°$ from horizontal
- **Link 2 (elbow to end-effector):** Length $L_2 = 0.4$ m, mass $m_2 = 2$ kg, oriented at $\theta_2 = 30°$ from horizontal
- **Payload mass:** $m_p = 1.5$ kg at the end-effector
- **Take** $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**a)** Derive the expression for the static torque required at joint 2 (elbow) to hold the manipulator in equilibrium. Calculate its value. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the expression for the static torque required at joint 1 (shoulder) to hold the manipulator in equilibrium. Calculate its value. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Solution — Torque at Joint 2 (7 marks)

**Step 1: Isolate Link 2 and draw its FBD**

Forces on Link 2:
- Weight $W_2 = m_2 g = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$ N at distance $L_2/2 = 0.2$ m from elbow
- Payload $W_p = m_p g = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.715$ N at distance $L_2 = 0.4$ m from elbow
- Reaction $R_{2y}$ at elbow (vertical)
- Motor torque $\tau_2$ at elbow

**Step 2: Apply vertical equilibrium to find $R_{2y}$**

$$\sum F_y = 0$$
$$R_{2y} = W_2 + W_p = 19.62 + 14.715 = 34.335 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Apply moment equilibrium about joint 2**

Taking moments about joint 2 (elbow) — the moment arm for vertical forces is the horizontal distance, which is $L \cos\theta$:

$$\sum M_2 = 0$$
$$\tau_2 - W_2 \cdot \frac{L_2}{2}\cos\theta_2 - W_p \cdot L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0$$
$$\tau_2 = \left(\frac{W_2}{2} + W_p\right) L_2 \cos\theta_2$$
$$\tau_2 = \left(\frac{19.62}{2} + 14.715\right) \times 0.4 \times \cos 30°$$
$$\tau_2 = (9.81 + 14.715) \times 0.4 \times 0.866$$
$$\tau_2 = 24.525 \times 0.4 \times 0.866$$
$$\tau_2 = 8.495 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Answer (Part a):**
$$\boxed{\tau_2 \approx 8.50 \text{ N·m}}$$

#### Part (b) Solution — Torque at Joint 1 (7 marks)

**Step 1: Isolate Link 1 and identify forces**

Forces on Link 1:
- Weight $W_1 = m_1 g = 3 \times 9.81 = 29.43$ N at distance $L_1/2 = 0.3$ m from shoulder
- Reaction from Link 2 at elbow: $R_{2y} = 34.335$ N (downward on Link 1 by Newton's 3rd law)
- Base reactions $R_{1x}$, $R_{1y}$ at shoulder
- Motor torque $\tau_1$ at shoulder

**Step 2: Apply vertical equilibrium to find $R_{1y}$**

$$\sum F_y = 0$$
$$R_{1y} = W_1 + R_{2y} = 29.43 + 34.335 = 63.765 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Apply moment equilibrium about joint 1 (shoulder)**

$$\sum M_1 = 0$$
$$\tau_1 - W_1 \cdot \frac{L_1}{2}\cos\theta_1 - R_{2y} \cdot L_1 \cos\theta_1 = 0$$
$$\tau_1 = \left(\frac{W_1}{2} + R_{2y}\right) L_1 \cos\theta_1$$
$$\tau_1 = \left(\frac{29.43}{2} + 34.335\right) \times 0.6 \times \cos 60°$$
$$\tau_1 = (14.715 + 34.335) \times 0.6 \times 0.5$$
$$\tau_1 = 49.05 \times 0.6 \times 0.5$$
$$\tau_1 = 14.715 \text{ N·m}$$

**Final Answer (Part b):**
$$\boxed{\tau_1 \approx 14.72 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the weight of the link itself:** Many students only consider the payload and ignore $W_1, W_2$. This causes **3-4 mark deduction** in part-(a)/(b) problems. **Always include the self-weight of every link.**
>
> 2. **Using wrong moment arm:** The moment arm for a vertical force is the **horizontal distance** ($L\cos\theta$), not the link length $L$. Using $L$ instead of $L\cos\theta$ is a classic error worth **2 marks lost**.
>
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** Moments causing clockwise rotation must be assigned a consistent sign. Mixing signs gives a torque with wrong magnitude/direction. KTU examiners specifically check this.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the FBD:** Skipping the FBD (even a rough sketch) leads to **2 marks deducted** in the "draw and identify" sub-questions, even if the math is correct.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always state final answers with **units (N, N·m)**. Numerical answers without units are penalized **0.5 to 1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Confusing weight with mass:** $W = mg$, not $W = m$. Using $m$ directly in Newton calculations is a common physics-error trap.
>
> 7. **Not applying Newton's 3rd Law correctly:** When isolating Link 2, the reaction at the elbow on Link 1 is **equal and opposite** to that on Link 2. Forgetting to reverse direction causes sign errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — STATICS IN MOBILE ROBOTICS**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Statics = Study of forces/moments when robot is **at rest or in equilibrium**
- ✅ Two fundamental equations: $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ AND $\sum \vec{M} = 0$
- ✅ FBD is the **most critical** analytical tool — always draw it first
- ✅ Always include **self-weight of links** in addition to payload
- ✅ Moment arm for vertical force = **horizontal distance** ($L\cos\theta$)
- ✅ Newton's 3rd Law applies at every joint connection

### High-Yield Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ $W = mg$ (weight, $g = 9.81$ m/s²)
- ✅ $\tau = F \cdot d$ (moment of a force)
- ✅ $\tau_2 = g L_2 \cos\theta_2 \left(\frac{m_2}{2} + m_p\right)$ (two-link elbow torque)
- ✅ $\tau_1 = g L_1 \cos\theta_1 \left(\frac{m_1}{2} + m_2 + m_p\right)$ (two-link shoulder torque)
- ✅ $\tan\alpha_{tip} = \frac{t}{2h}$ (mobile robot tipping condition)
- ✅ $F_{grip,min} = \frac{mg}{\mu}$ (minimum gripper force)

### Stability Insights
- ✅ **Wider base** ($t$ larger) → More stable against tipping
- ✅ **Lower CG** ($h$ smaller) → More stable
- ✅ **Higher payload** → Larger joint torques required
- ✅ **Larger link angles from horizontal** → Smaller torque requirement (cosine factor)

### Common KTU Question Patterns
- ✅ Single link with payload (basic 7-mark problem)
- ✅ Two-link manipulator with payload (standard 14-mark problem)
- ✅ Mobile robot on inclined surface (stability-based)
- ✅ Gripper force calculation (friction-based)
- ✅ Multi-body systems with multiple unknowns

### Engineering Connections
- ✅ Motor sizing → From joint torque calculations
- ✅ Structural design → From reaction force calculations
- ✅ Safety factor → Typically 2× to 3× the calculated minimum
- ✅ Real-time control → Static torques are the baseline; dynamics adds corrections

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, statics questions often appear in **Part A (3 marks)** as direct definitions, and in **Part B (14 marks)** as full multi-link problems. Practice drawing FBDs first, then systematically apply the three equilibrium equations. Always box your final answer with proper units!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kineto-Static Duality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kineto-Static Duality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kineto-Static Duality (Kinetostatic Duality)** is a fundamental principle in mobile robotics and mechanism theory that establishes a **direct mathematical correspondence between the velocity constraints** at the contact points of a robot (kinematic side) and the **force/torque constraints** (static side) at the same contact points. It exploits the fact that the **same geometric Jacobian matrix** that propagates velocities from the robot's body to its contact points, when transposed, propagates contact forces back to produce the net wrench on the robot body.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **OERAT724 – Basics of Mobile Robotics**, Kineto-Static Duality is the theoretical bridge used to:
- Determine the **mobility** (degrees of freedom) of a wheeled mobile robot.
- Identify the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** / **Instantaneous Center of Zero Velocity (ICZV)**.
- Compute the net **wrench** (force + moment) exerted on the chassis by the ground through multiple wheel contacts.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine pushing a heavy office chair with caster wheels on a smooth floor.**
> - **Kinematic side (velocity):** When you push the chair forward, you can *observe* that each wheel contact point either rolls without slipping (so the velocity of the contact point along the rolling direction is zero) or it slides (slip occurs).
> - **Static side (force):** At the same instant, each wheel can only *push back* on the floor in directions where it is **not allowed to slide** — a free wheel can resist side-slip but not longitudinal slip, while a fixed wheel resists slip in the lateral direction.
> - **The Duality:** The set of *allowed motions* at each contact and the set of *allowed reaction forces* at that contact are *mathematical mirrors* of one another. What cannot move can take a force; what can move freely cannot take a force in that direction.

This mirror-image relationship is what we call **Kineto-Static Duality**.

## Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Wrench** $\xi$ | A generalized force combining linear force and moment acting on the rigid body. |
| **Twist** $\nu$ | A generalized velocity combining linear and angular velocity of the rigid body. |
| **Contact Point $p_i$** | The geometric point where the *i*-th wheel touches the ground. |
| **Jacobian $J_i$** | The matrix mapping robot twist to the velocity of contact point $p_i$. |
| **ICR / ICZV** | **Instantaneous Center of Rotation** — the point on the ground having zero velocity for the current twist. |
| **Legibility** | Whether a robot's wheel configuration is holonomic or non-holonomic. |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Symbol Convention used throughout these notes:**
> - $v$ = linear velocity (m/s), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
> - $f$ = force (N), $\tau$ = torque (N·m)
> - Bold lowercase $\mathbf{v}$ = vectors, capital $J$ = matrices

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Sides of a Wheel Contact

Every wheel-ground contact imposes **two simultaneous constraints** on the robot:

### Side A — The Velocity (Kinematic) Constraint
At the contact point $p_i$, the velocity of that material point on the wheel (as a rigid body attached to the chassis) must satisfy the **no-slip condition** in the rolling direction.

For a planar mobile robot with chassis twist $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^T$ in the world frame, the velocity of a contact point located at $(x_i, y_i)$ from the **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** is:

$$\mathbf{v}_{p_i} = \begin{pmatrix} v_x - \omega \, y_i \\ v_y + \omega \, x_i \end{pmatrix}$$

The **non-slipping constraint matrix** for the *i*-th contact is:

$$C_i(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi} = 0$$

where $C_i$ is a $1 \times 3$ row vector that projects the twist onto the slipping direction.

### Side B — The Force (Static) Constraint
A passive wheel (without a motor/brake in that direction) **cannot sustain** a force component in the direction it is free to roll. The reaction force $\mathbf{F}_i$ at the *i*-th contact must lie in the **null space** of the slipping direction (i.e., in the rolling-direction's orthogonal complement).

$$C_i \cdot \mathbf{F}_i = 0 \quad \text{(in the local wheel frame)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The kinematic constraint $C_i \boldsymbol{\xi}=0$ and the static constraint $C_i \mathbf{F}_i = 0$ use the **same matrix** $C_i$. This is the heart of **Kineto-Static Duality**.

## 2.2 The Duality Theorem (Statement)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinetostatic Duality Theorem:**
> If the no-slip constraint at contact $i$ is $C_i \boldsymbol{\xi} = 0$, then the only forces a passive wheel can transmit to the chassis are those lying in the **range space of $C_i^T$**, i.e., forces of the form $\mathbf{F}_i = C_i^T \lambda_i$, where $\lambda_i$ is a scalar Lagrange multiplier.
>
> Equivalently: **The directions in which the wheel cannot move are exactly the directions in which it can exert a force.**

This is why a **fixed (driven) wheel** resists motion sideways (and hence can exert a **lateral friction force**), while a **caster (passive) wheel** cannot resist lateral motion and exerts only a **vertical normal force**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chassis twist (planar) | $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (v_x, v_y, \omega)^T$ | Generalized velocity, $3 \times 1$ |
| 2 | Velocity at contact $p_i$ | $\mathbf{v}_{p_i} = \begin{pmatrix} v_x - \omega y_i \\ v_y + \omega x_i \end{pmatrix}$ | Velocity of point at $(x_i, y_i)$ w.r.t. ICR |
| 3 | Rolling constraint | $C_i \boldsymbol{\xi} = 0$ | $C_i$ is the **velocity constraint matrix** at contact *i* |
| 4 | Force constraint | $C_i \mathbf{F}_i = 0$ | Same $C_i$ used for force admissibility |
| 5 | Admissible contact force | $\mathbf{F}_i = C_i^T \lambda_i$ | Range-space representation (Duality) |
| 6 | Total wrench on chassis | $\mathbf{W} = \sum_i \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{F}_i \\ (p_i \times \mathbf{F}_i) \end{pmatrix}$ | Net force + moment from ground |
| 7 | ICR location | $(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR}) = \big(-\tfrac{v_y}{\omega},\; \tfrac{v_x}{\omega}\big)$ | Valid only if $\omega \neq 0$ |
| 8 | Degree of mobility | $\delta_m = 3 - \text{rank}(C_{all})$ | Number of independent twists allowed |
| 9 | Degree of steerability | $\delta_s = 3 - \text{rank}(C_s)$ | Number of independently steerable wheels |
| 10 | Maneuverability | $M = \delta_m + \delta_s$ | Total DOF (mobility + steering) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical convention note for tables:** Vertical bars inside math expressions have been written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\lvert v_x \rvert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Kinetostatic duality is the **theoretical foundation** used by KTU examiners to test:

- **Wheel-configuration analysis** — Given 3 wheels with their geometric properties, find the ICR location.
- **Mobility count** — How many DOFs does the robot chassis possess?
- **Force analysis** — Can a particular wheel arrangement climb a given slope?
- **Design of holonomic vs non-holonomic robots** — Duality reveals why a unicycle (1 fixed wheel + 1 castor) is non-holonomic.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Twist to Contact-Point Velocity

### Step 1 — Express the chassis motion as a twist
For a planar rigid body (the mobile robot chassis), the general velocity state is the 3-dimensional twist:

$$\boldsymbol{\xi} = \begin{pmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega \end{pmatrix}$$

where $v_x, v_y$ are linear velocities of the chassis reference point in the world frame, and $\omega$ is the angular velocity about the vertical axis.

### Step 2 — Compute velocity of an arbitrary contact point $p_i$
Let the contact point $p_i$ have coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ in the chassis frame. The velocity of this point (rigid-body kinematics) is the sum of the linear velocity of the reference point and the rotational contribution:

$$\mathbf{v}_{p_i} = \mathbf{v}_{ref} + \boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_i$$

Substituting $\boldsymbol{\omega} = (0, 0, \omega)$ and $\mathbf{r}_i = (x_i, y_i, 0)$:

$$\mathbf{v}_{p_i} = \begin{pmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \omega \end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix} x_i \\ y_i \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Evaluating the cross product (only the $z$-axis produces non-zero planar components):

$$\boldsymbol{\omega} \times \mathbf{r}_i = \begin{pmatrix} -\omega \, y_i \\ \omega \, x_i \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Therefore, the 2D planar velocity of contact point *i* is:

$$\mathbf{v}_{p_i} = \begin{pmatrix} v_x - \omega \, y_i \\ v_y + \omega \, x_i \end{pmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Each wheel contact point "rotates around" the ICR. The closer a point is to the ICR, the smaller its velocity magnitude. Points on the ICR itself have zero velocity — this is the foundation for graphical ICR construction in exam problems.

### Step 3 — Apply the no-slip rolling constraint
For a wheel aligned with rolling direction given by the unit vector $\mathbf{u}_i = (\cos\alpha_i, \sin\alpha_i)$ (where $\alpha_i$ is the wheel's orientation angle in the chassis frame), the velocity component *along* the rolling direction must vanish (pure rolling, no slipping):

$$\mathbf{u}_i^T \, \mathbf{v}_{p_i} = 0$$

Substituting the expression from Step 2:

$$(\cos\alpha_i)(v_x - \omega y_i) + (\sin\alpha_i)(v_y + \omega x_i) = 0$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange into standard constraint-matrix form
Collect the terms involving $v_x, v_y, \omega$:

$$\cos\alpha_i \cdot v_x + \sin\alpha_i \cdot v_y + (x_i \sin\alpha_i - y_i \cos\alpha_i)\omega = 0$$

This is a single linear equation in the three unknowns $(v_x, v_y, \omega)$, which we can write compactly as:

$$\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} \cos\alpha_i & \sin\alpha_i & (x_i \sin\alpha_i - y_i \cos\alpha_i) \end{pmatrix}}_{C_i} \begin{pmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega \end{pmatrix} = 0$$

$$\boxed{C_i(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi} = 0}$$

where $C_i$ is the **$1 \times 3$ velocity-constraint matrix** at contact *i*.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Static (Force) Duality

### Step 5 — Identify the admissible force directions
The non-slip constraint is a *single scalar equation*. The space of all twists that satisfy it is a 2-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$. The **orthogonal complement** of this subspace is a 1-dimensional subspace spanned by the row vector $C_i$ itself. By the Fundamental Theorem of Linear Algebra (Range-Null duality), the admissible contact forces — those that the wheel *can* transmit without violating the no-slip assumption — must lie in the **range space of $C_i^T$**:

$$\mathbf{F}_i = C_i^T \lambda_i = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\alpha_i \\ \sin\alpha_i \\ x_i \sin\alpha_i - y_i \cos\alpha_i \end{pmatrix} \lambda_i$$

where $\lambda_i \in \mathbb{R}$ is a Lagrange multiplier (the scalar magnitude of the constraint force).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial insight (the duality):** Step 4 gave us $C_i \boldsymbol{\xi} = 0$ (no motion in the rolling direction). Step 5 gave us $\mathbf{F}_i \in \text{Range}(C_i^T)$ (forces only in the rolling direction). *The same row vector $C_i$ controls both — that is the essence of Kineto-Static Duality.*

### Step 6 — Compute the total wrench on the chassis
Summing forces and moments from all *n* contact points:

$$\mathbf{F}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{F}_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i^T \lambda_i$$

$$\boldsymbol{\tau}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i F_{i,y} - y_i F_{i,x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \sin\alpha_i - y_i \cos\alpha_i)\lambda_i$$

The combined wrench vector (concatenating force and moment in 3D planar case) becomes:

$$\mathbf{W} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} \cos\alpha_i & \sin\alpha_i \\ -(x_i \sin\alpha_i - y_i \cos\alpha_i) & (x_i \cos\alpha_i + y_i \sin\alpha_i) \end{pmatrix}}_{J_i^T} \lambda_i$$

Wait — for the planar case with a 3-D twist, the moment arm is correctly captured. The general structure **$J_i^T$** appears: the *transpose of the kinematic Jacobian* mapping contact-point velocities becomes the **force propagator**.

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** A differential-drive robot has two fixed wheels of radius $r$, separated by distance $L$ along the $y$-axis at positions $(-L/2, 0)$ and $(L/2, 0)$. Both wheels are aligned with the $x$-axis ($\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = 0$). The robot also has a single free castor wheel at position $(0, l)$ whose constraint direction is also along $x$. Determine (a) the ICR for pure forward motion, (b) the rank of the combined constraint matrix.

**Solution:**

**(a)** For pure forward motion, $\omega = 0$ and $v_y = 0$. The ICR formula gives:

$$\omega = 0 \implies \text{ICR at infinity along } x\text{-axis (straight-line motion)}$$

If $\omega \neq 0$, the ICR is:

$$\big(x_{ICR}, y_{ICR}\big) = \big(-\tfrac{v_y}{\omega},\; \tfrac{v_x}{\omega}\big) = \big(0,\; \tfrac{v_x}{\omega}\big)$$

For differential drive with equal wheel speeds $\dot{\phi}_1 = \dot{\phi}_2$, we have $v_x = r\dot{\phi}$, $\omega = 0$, so the robot moves straight. With $\dot{\phi}_1 \neq \dot{\phi}_2$, $\omega = \frac{r(\dot{\phi}_1 - \dot{\phi}_2)}{L}$, producing a finite ICR at:

$$y_{ICR} = \frac{v_x}{\omega} = \frac{r\dot{\phi}_L + r\dot{\phi}_R}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{r(\dot{\phi}_L - \dot{\phi}_R)} = \frac{L(\dot{\phi}_L + \dot{\phi}_R)}{2(\dot{\phi}_L - \dot{\phi}_R)}$$

**(b)** Constraint matrix for each wheel (with $\alpha = 0$):

$$C_i = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & -y_i \end{pmatrix}$$

For the two fixed wheels: $C_1 = (1, 0, 0)$ and $C_2 = (1, 0, 0)$. These are **linearly dependent** (rank contribution = 1).
For the castor: $C_3 = (1, 0, -l)$. This is **independent** of $C_1$.

Total stacked matrix:

$$C_{all} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & -l \end{pmatrix} \implies \text{rank} = 2$$

Therefore, **mobility** $\delta_m = 3 - 2 = 1$ — the robot has **1 DOF** (forward/backward), consistent with a non-holonomic differential drive. ✓

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Duality Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **architecture of the Kineto-Static Duality pipeline** as a student should understand it for KTU 2024 Scheme questions:

```mermaid

    A["Robot Chassis Twist xi = vx vy omega"] --> B["Geometric Jacobian J_i per contact"]
    B --> C["Velocity of contact point v_pi"]
    C --> D["Rolling constraint C_i dot xi = 0"]
    D --> E["Constraint matrix C_i built"]
    E --> F{"Same C_i used twice"}
    F --> G["Side 1 KINEMATIC: admissible twists"]
    F --> H["Side 2 STATIC: F_i = C_i transpose times lambda"]
    G --> I["ICR Instantaneous Center of Rotation"]
    G --> J["Mobility delta_m = 3 minus rank C"]
    H --> K["Total wrench W on chassis"]
    H --> L["Force in directions where motion is forbidden"]
    I --> M["Steering design decision"]
    J --> M
    K --> N["Motor torque sizing"]
    L --> N
```

