\theta_4)}{r_3 \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} \\ &\quad - \omega_4 \cdot \frac{r_4 \sin(\theta_4 - \theta_3)}{r_3 \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)} \end{aligned} $$ For a parallel-coupler design ($\omega_3 \approx 0$), this simplifies dramatically: $$\omega_2 \cdot r_2 \sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4) + \omega_4 \cdot r_4 \sin(\theta_4 - \theta_3) = 0$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Gripping Force Using Principle of Virtual Work By the **Principle of Virtual Work**, the input work equals the output work for an ideal (frictionless) mechanism: $$T_{in} \cdot \delta\theta_{in} = F_{grip} \cdot \delta x_{jaw}$$ Solving for the gripping force: $$F_{grip} = T_{in} \cdot \frac{\partial \theta_{in}}{\partial x_{jaw}}$$ **Step 1:** Jaw position $x_{jaw}$ is a function of the input angle $\theta_{in}$ via the closed-loop geometry. **Step 2:** For a small virtual rotation $\delta\theta_{in}$, the corresponding jaw displacement is: $$\delta x_{jaw} = \frac{\partial x_{jaw}}{\partial \theta_{in}} \delta\theta_{in}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting back: $$F_{grip} = \frac{T_{in}}{\dfrac{\partial x_{jaw}}{\partial \theta_{in}}}$$ **Step 4:** The **Mechanical Advantage** at any instant is therefore: $$MA(\theta_{in}) = \frac{1}{\left\vert \dfrac{\partial x_{jaw}}{\partial \theta_{in}} \right\vert}$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Gold:** A small $\partial x_{jaw} / \partial \theta_{in}$ (jaw moves slowly per input rotation) = **high force amplification** = **good gripping region**. This is exactly where the linkage is *mechanically locked* — a key concept for **self-locking grippers** in mobile robotics. ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard) **Problem:** A 4-bar parallel gripper has link lengths $L_1 = 50$ mm, $L_2 = 20$ mm, $L_3 = 50$ mm, $L_4 = 20$ mm. Actuator torque $T_{in} = 1.5$ N·m. Find (a) Grashof classification, (b) Required gripping force at $\theta_2 = 90°$. **Solution (a):** S = 20, L = 50, P = 50, Q = 20. So $S+L = 70$ and $P+Q = 70$. **Equality holds** → Change-point / parallel-coupler mechanism. **Solution (b):** Jaw position derivative at $\theta_2 = 90°$ for symmetric design gives $\partial x_{jaw}/\partial\theta_{in} = 0.012$ m/rad. Therefore: $$F_{grip} = \frac{1.5}{0.012} = 125 \text{ N}$$ > [!NOTE] > The gripper amplifies a **1.5 N·m torque** into a **125 N gripping force** — a mechanical advantage of over **80×** in compact form, which is why this architecture is preferred in mobile robotics. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architecture of the 4-Bar Linkage Parallel Gripper ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Ground Link L1
Fixed to Robot Wrist] -->|Pivot A| nodeB[Input Crank L2
Driven by Actuator] nodeB -->|Pivot B| nodeC[Coupler Link L3
Carries Jaw 1] nodeC -->|Pivot C| nodeD[Output Rocker L4
Carries Jaw 2] nodeD -->|Pivot D| nodeA nodeE[Servo Motor / Pneumatic Cylinder] -->|Torque T_in| nodeB nodeF[Payload Object] -->|Reaction F_grip| nodeC nodeF -->|Reaction F_grip| nodeD nodeC -->|Parallel Translation| nodeG[Jaw 1
Linear Path] nodeD -->|Parallel Translation| nodeH[Jaw 2
Linear Path] nodeG -.Mirrored Motion.-> nodeH ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart[Control Signal
from Mobile Robot CPU] --> nodeActuator[Actuator
Servo or Pneumatic] nodeActuator -->|Torque T_in| nodeInput[Input Link L2
Rotates about Pivot A] nodeInput -->|Rotation θ2| nodeLoop[Four-Bar Closed Loop
Constraint Propagation] nodeLoop -->|Translation| nodeJaw1[Jaw 1 Path
Linear] nodeLoop -->|Translation| nodeJaw2[Jaw 2 Path
Linear] nodeJaw1 --> nodeGrasp[Object Grasp Point
F_grip] nodeJaw2 --> nodeGrasp nodeGrasp -->|Feedback| nodeSensors[Force / Tactile Sensors] nodeSensors -->|Closed-Loop Signal| nodeStart ``` ### 4.3 Free-Body / Kinematic State Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeState1[State 1: Jaws Open
θ2 = 0 deg] -->|Actuator Rotates| nodeState2[State 2: Approaching
θ2 = 45 deg] nodeState2 -->|Object Contact| nodeState3[State 3: Grasp
θ2 = 90 deg] nodeState3 -->|Hold / Transport| nodeState4[State 4: Release
θ2 returns to 0] nodeState3 -->|Force Analysis| nodeFA[Grip Force F_grip
= T_in / dx_dθ] nodeFA --> nodeSensors[Tactile Feedback
to Robot CPU] ``` ### 4.4 Force & Motion Summary Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeM[Mobile Robot Base] --> nodeW[Robot Wrist / Mount] nodeW --> nodeG[Gripper Housing
Contains Four-Bar] nodeG --> nodeP[Pivot A and Pivot D
Ground Link] nodeG --> nodeR[Input Crank
L2] nodeG --> nodeC[Coupler and Rocker
L3 and L4] nodeC --> nodeJ1[Jaw 1] nodeC --> nodeJ2[Jaw 2] nodeJ1 --> nodeOBJ[Object to Grasp] nodeJ2 --> nodeOBJ ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Safety Compliance Check:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `style`, `graph` used as node names). All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italic tags or HTML tables embedded inside node label strings. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a 4-bar linkage parallel gripper. State any two advantages of using it as an end-effector in mobile robots.* **Model Answer:** A 4-bar linkage parallel gripper is a planar, single-degree-of-freedom mechanism consisting of four rigid links connected by four revolute joints in a closed loop, used to convert a single actuator input into symmetric, parallel closing motion of two jaws. **Two advantages:** 1. The parallel jaw motion preserves the orientation of the grasped object, critical for transporting cups, tools, or sensors without slip. 2. The mechanism provides high mechanical advantage in a compact envelope, making it ideal for lightweight mobile robots with limited actuator power. *(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for advantages)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State Grashof's Law. Why is it important in the design of a 4-bar parallel gripper?* **Model Answer:** Grashof's Law states that for a four-bar linkage with link lengths classified as shortest $S$, longest $L$, and the other two $P$ and $Q$: $$S + L \leq P + Q$$ **Importance:** It determines whether the gripper mechanism can achieve continuous full-stroke jaw closure, a partial stroke, or be at a special change-point. For a parallel-coupler gripper, the design is typically at the *equality* boundary, which guarantees smooth, full-range parallel motion. *(1.5 marks for law + 1.5 marks for importance)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **forget the loop-closure vector equation** and try to solve kinematics by inspection — this leads to a **2-mark penalty** for missing first principles. > - Confusing the **coupler link (L3)** with the **output rocker (L4)** is a **frequent labelling error**, deducting **1 mark** per occurrence. > - Failing to mention **Grashof's classification** in any design question results in a guaranteed **2-mark cut** under KTU 2024 strict CO mapping. > - Units (N, N·m, m/s, mm) **must** accompany every numerical answer — missing units cost **0.5 mark per quantity**. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the **loop-closure equation** of a planar four-bar linkage used in a parallel gripper. Define all variables clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-bar parallel gripper has the following dimensions: $L_1 = 60$ mm, $L_2 = 25$ mm, $L_3 = 60$ mm, $L_4 = 25$ mm. The actuator applies a torque of $T_{in} = 2$ N·m. Compute (i) the Grashof classification and (ii) the gripping force if $\partial x_{jaw}/\partial\theta_{in} = 0.01$ m/rad at the configuration of interest. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** *Sketch:* Draw a planar quadrilateral ABCD with A, B, C, D as revolute joints; mark $r_1$ (ground), $r_2$ (input), $r_3$ (coupler), $r_4$ (output), with angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \theta_4$ measured from the global x-axis. *Derivation:* Starting at A and traversing the loop: $$r_2 e^{i\theta_2} + r_3 e^{i\theta_3} = r_1 e^{i\theta_1} + r_4 e^{i\theta_4}$$ Real and imaginary parts yield the **Freudenstein equation**: $$r_3^2 = r_1^2 + r_2^2 + r_4^2 - 2 r_1 r_2 \cos(\theta_1 - \theta_2) + 2 r_1 r_4 \cos(\theta_1 - \theta_4) - 2 r_2 r_4 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$ *(2 marks for sketch, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for Freudenstein form)* **Model Solution (b):** **(i)** S = 25 mm, L = 60 mm, P = 60 mm, Q = 25 mm. $$S + L = 25 + 60 = 85 \text{ mm}, \quad P + Q = 60 + 25 = 85 \text{ mm}$$ Equality holds → **Change-point (parallel-coupler) mechanism.** *(3 marks)* **(ii)** By the principle of virtual work: $$F_{grip} = \frac{T_{in}}{\partial x_{jaw}/\partial \theta_{in}} = \frac{2}{0.01} = 200 \text{ N}$$ *(3 marks for substitution, 1 mark for unit)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **kinematic architecture** of a 4-bar parallel gripper. With a labelled diagram, identify the four links, four revolute joints, and the geometric condition for parallel jaw motion. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **velocity ratio** between the input crank and the output jaw for a parallel-coupler 4-bar gripper. A gripper with $L_2 = 30$ mm, $L_4 = 30$ mm, and instantaneous input angular velocity $\omega_2 = 2$ rad/s is observed at $\theta_2 = 60°$, $\theta_4 = 120°$. Find the linear jaw velocity. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** The gripper consists of: - **Ground link (L1)** fixed to the robot wrist. - **Input crank (L2)** driven by actuator about pivot A. - **Coupler (L3)** carrying Jaw 1, floating. - **Output rocker (L4)** carrying Jaw 2, rotating about pivot D. **Parallel motion condition:** $L_1 + L_4 = L_2 + L_3$ (parallel-coupler geometry). The coupler remains geometrically parallel to the ground link, hence the jaws translate along parallel lines. *(2 marks diagram, 3 marks labelling, 2 marks condition)* **Model Solution (b):** For a parallel-coupler design, the jaw linear velocity magnitude is: $$\vert v_{jaw} \vert = \vert r_2 \omega_2 \cdot \sin(\theta_2 - \phi) \vert$$ where $\phi$ is the orientation of the jaw path. For the symmetric case, this reduces to: $$\vert v_{jaw} \vert = L_2 \cdot \omega_2 \cdot \sin(\theta_2 - \theta_4)$$ **Substitution:** $$\vert v_{jaw} \vert = 0.030 \times 2 \times \sin(60° - 120°) = 0.060 \times \sin(-60°) = 0.060 \times 0.866 = 0.052 \text{ m/s}$$ *(3 marks derivation, 3 marks substitution, 1 mark unit)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** A 4-bar linkage parallel gripper uses a closed four-bar mechanism to produce symmetric, parallel jaw motion. - **Key Links:** Ground (L1), Input (L2), Coupler (L3), Rocker (L4). - **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ — boundary equality gives parallel-coupler design. - **Parallel-coupler condition:** $L_1 + L_4 = L_2 + L_3$. - **Loop-closure equation:** $r_2 e^{i\theta_2} + r_3 e^{i\theta_3} = r_1 e^{i\theta_1} + r_4 e^{i\theta_4}$. - **Freudenstein equation** is the squared scalar form used for position analysis. - **Velocity analysis** uses time-derivative of the loop-closure equation. - **Force analysis** uses the Principle of Virtual Work: $F_{grip} = T_{in} / (\partial x_{jaw}/\partial \theta_{in})$. - **Mechanical advantage** is large near the change-point configuration. - **KTU-Favorite Triggers:** Grashof classification, loop-closure, gripping force calculation, parallel-coupler geometry. - **Units Checklist:** Always include **N, N·m, m, m/s, rad/s** with numerical answers. - **Diagram Rule:** Always draw **labelled sketches** showing all four links, joint labels (A, B, C, D), and link length symbols.

topics.title: Multi-Fingered Hands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multi-Fingered Hands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-Fingered Robotic Hand:** A multi-fingered hand is an articulated end-effector composed of multiple rigid links connected by joints, where each finger possesses its own kinematic chain and the entire system acts cooperatively to grasp, stabilize, and dexterously manipulate objects within a defined workspace.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context (Course: OERAT724 – Basics of Mobile Robotics, Module 2), multi-fingered hands are studied as the **terminal manipulation device** that extends the dexterity of a mobile manipulator. They transform a mobile base's gross mobility into fine, contact-rich manipulation capability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a multi-fingered robotic hand as the **"mechanical equivalent of a human hand"** that you might see mounted at the end of a robotic arm. Imagine trying to pick up a raw egg with a single rigid hook (like a coat hanger) — you can lift it, but you cannot rotate it gently, you cannot roll it, and any slip drops it. Now imagine using your own hand: the thumb opposes the fingers, the fingers wrap around the shape, and you can re-orient the egg without letting go. A multi-fingered hand gives a robot this same "opposable" capability.

The **thumb** (or the opposing finger) is the most critical design element — it is what separates a gripper from a *hand*.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

The following metrics are universally used in the KTU syllabus and standard texts to characterise a multi-fingered hand:

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent joints, typically **3 DOF per finger** (proximal, middle, distal — analogous to human MCP, PIP, DIP joints).
- **Number of Fingers:** Usually **3 to 5** (3-finger, 4-finger, or 5-finger anthropomorphic designs).
- **Workspace:** The 3D volume swept by the fingertip.
- **Payload:** The maximum force/weight the hand can hold statically, typically **5 N to 50 N** in research hands, **up to 100 N** in industrial grippers.
- **Grasp Force:** The contact force exerted at each fingertip, measured in **Newtons (N)**.
- **Dextrous Workspace:** The sub-region where the fingertip can reach with full orientability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2, the focus is on the *kinematic and static force analysis* of multi-fingered hands, NOT on their fabrication, actuation, or sensing. Memorise the relationship between **finger DOF**, **hand DOF**, and **grasp stability**.

### 1.4 Why Multi-Fingered Hands Matter in Mobile Robotics

A mobile robot (wheeled or legged) carries its manipulator over long distances. The multi-fingered hand at the tip performs:

1. **Grasping** — picking up tools, door handles, debris.
2. **Dexterous manipulation** — in-hand reorientation, button pressing, object handoff.
3. **Stabilising** — holding a workpiece against gravity or disturbance while the mobile base moves.

Without multi-fingered hands, a mobile robot reduces to a *mobile platform with a simple two-jaw gripper*, severely limiting the range of real-world tasks it can perform (rescue, healthcare, space servicing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomical Decomposition of a Multi-Fingered Hand

A multi-fingered hand is built from three structural layers:

**Layer 1 — Palm (Base link):**
The palm is the static mounting plate fixed to the robot's wrist. It is **non-moving** in most designs (no DOF).

**Layer 2 — Fingers (Articulated kinematic chains):**
Each finger is a serial chain of rigid links connected by revolute joints.

- **Proximal link** → attached to the palm, driven by joint 1.
- **Middle link** → driven by joint 2.
- **Distal link** → the terminal segment ending in the **fingertip**.

A standard 3-jointed finger has **3 DOF** (one per joint), making it a planar serial manipulator. With 4 such fingers mounted on the palm, the hand has **12 DOF** total (excluding palm motion).

**Layer 3 — Fingertip (Contact point):**
The fingertip is a rigid body in 3D space with **6 degrees of freedom in pose** (3 position + 3 orientation). The hand's task is to bring the fingertips to specified contact points on an object and apply controlled forces.

### 2.2 Grasp Taxonomy (KTU High-Yield Classification)

Grasps are classified along two fundamental axes:

**A. By Function (Cutkosky's Taxonomy – simplified for KTU):**

| Grasp Type | Description | Example Task |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Grasp** | Envelops the object with large contact area; maximises force | Holding a hammer |
| **Precision Grasp** | Fingertip-only contact; maximises dexterity | Holding a pen |
| **Hook Grasp** | Fingers curl passively; no active thumb needed | Carrying a briefcase |
| **Pinch / Tripod** | Thumb + two fingers form a stable 3-point contact | Picking up a coin |

**B. By Contact Geometry (Mishra–Schwartz–Sharir classification):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most-asked classification in KTU university exams. Memorise it.

| Contact Type | Symbol | Friction Conditions | DOF Constrained |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Point contact with friction** | PCWF | Friction cone non-empty | 3 force + 3 moment (limit) |
| **Point contact without friction** | PCWoF | μ = 0 | 3 force only |
| **Soft-finger contact** | SFC | μ > 0 + line friction | 3 force + 1 moment |
| **Line contact** | LC | Friction along line | Distributed |
| **Surface contact** | SC | Full constraint | 6 (rigid attachment) |

### 2.3 Grasp Matrix and Force Closure

The **grasp matrix G** maps fingertip forces to the resultant wrench (force + moment) on the object:

$$w = G \cdot f$$

Where:
- $f \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the vector of fingertip contact forces (m = total scalar force components across all fingers).
- $w \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ is the resultant wrench on the object.
- $G \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times m}$ is the grasp matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Force Closure Condition:** A grasp achieves force closure if the columns of G positively span $\mathbb{R}^{6}$, i.e., the origin lies in the interior of the convex hull of the columns of G. Physically: the fingers can generate any wrench on the object using only *non-negative* (pushing) contact forces within their friction cones.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOF of one finger (n-joint planar) | $n$ (revolute joints) | dimensionless |
| 2 | Total hand DOF (k fingers, n joints each) | $k \cdot n$ | excluding wrist |
| 3 | Fingertip position (2D finger) | $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | metres (m) |
| 4 | Fingertip position (y-component) | $y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ | metres (m) |
| 5 | Finger Jacobian (2-link planar) | $J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$ | 2×2, $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$, $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ |
| 6 | Grasp matrix definition | $w = G f$ | w: 6×1, f: m×1 |
| 7 | Force closure (point contact with friction) | $\exists\, \alpha_i \geq 0,\ f_i \in \mathcal{F}_i : \sum \alpha_i G_i = 0$ and $\sum \alpha_i \mathbf{c}_i = 0$ | where $\mathcal{F}_i$ is the friction cone of finger i |
| 8 | Friction cone half-angle | $\tan\phi = \mu$ (Coulomb) | μ = coefficient of friction |
| 9 | Grasp stability (potential energy) | $\Delta V = V_{after} - V_{before} \geq 0$ for all perturbations | V = potential energy |
| 10 | Equivalent point contact (CoP) | Wrench equivalence at the centre of pressure | $c_{op}$ on contact patch |

> [!NOTE]
> Always use **\vert** or **\mid** in mathematical expressions inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table layout. The friction cone condition uses inequality constraints, NOT equalities.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multi-fingered hands are deployed in:

- **Space robotics (NASA's Robonaut 2):** Dexterous EVA tasks in zero-gravity.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci):** Pinch and precision grasp of tissue.
- **Search and rescue (DRC HUBO):** Manipulating debris, turning valves.
- **Prosthetics (DEKA LUKE Arm):** Restoring human grasp functions.
- **Warehouse automation (Amazon Picking Challenge):** Picking diverse SKUs from bins.

The **grasp matrix formulation** is the foundational tool that allows engineers to design controllers that maintain stable grasps even when the object slips or the mobile base accelerates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Kinematics of a 2-Link Planar Finger

**Setup:** Consider one finger in the vertical plane, consisting of two rigid links of lengths $L_1$ (proximal) and $L_2$ (middle/distal combined for simplicity), connected by revolute joints at angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ measured from the horizontal.

**Step 1:** Express the position of joint 2 (knuckle) in the palm frame.

The knuckle position is the tip of link 1, located at:

$$x_1 = L_1 \cos\theta_1$$

$$y_1 = L_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 2:** Express the orientation of link 2 relative to the world frame.

Link 2 is rotated by the sum $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ from the horizontal, because link 1 is at $\theta_1$ and link 2 is at $\theta_2$ *relative to* link 1.

**Step 3:** Express the fingertip position by vector addition.

Adding the contribution of link 2 in the world frame:

$$x_2 = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y_2 = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

This is the **forward kinematic map** of a 2-link planar finger.

**Step 4:** Vector form for compactness.

Let $p \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$ be the fingertip position. Then:

$$p = \begin{bmatrix} L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Finger Jacobian (Velocity Kinematics)

**Step 1:** Differentiate the forward kinematic equations with respect to time.

Differentiating the x-component:

$$\dot{x}_2 = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \cdot (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$

**Step 2:** Group the $\dot{\theta}_1$ and $\dot{\theta}_2$ terms.

$$\dot{x}_2 = \left[-L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_1 + \left[-L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_2$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate the y-component similarly.

$$\dot{y}_2 = \left[L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_1 + \left[L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right]\dot{\theta}_2$$

**Step 4:** Write in matrix form.

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_2 \\ \dot{y}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Here, $s_1 = \sin\theta_1$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, and similarly for c.

**Step 5:** Identify the Jacobian.

$$J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

This Jacobian maps joint velocities to fingertip Cartesian velocities. It is essential for:
- Velocity control of the finger.
- Computing finger manipulability (singularities when $\det(J) = 0$).
- Transforming contact forces from fingertip frame to joint torque frame via the transpose.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Grasp Matrix for a 2-Finger Planar Grasp

**Setup:** Two point contacts with friction grasp a circular object of radius $R$ at contact points $C_1$ and $C_2$, located at angles $\alpha$ and $-\alpha$ on the object (measured from the centre).

**Step 1:** Define the contact normal directions.

For contact 1, the inward normal (pointing into the object) at angle $\alpha$ on the circle is:

$$\mathbf{n}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha \\ \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

For contact 2, located at $-\alpha$:

$$\mathbf{n}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha \\ -\sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Define the contact positions relative to the object centre.

$$\mathbf{c}_1 = R \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha \\ \sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{c}_2 = R \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha \\ -\sin\alpha \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Build the contribution of each contact to the wrench.

For a planar grasp, a contact force $f_i$ at point $c_i$ exerts a wrench:

$$w_i = \begin{bmatrix} f_i \\ c_i \times f_i \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_i n_{i,x} \\ f_i n_{i,y} \\ c_{i,x} f_i n_{i,y} - c_{i,y} f_i n_{i,x} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Assemble the grasp matrix G (size 3×2 for 2 planar contacts).

$$G = \begin{bmatrix} n_{1,x} & n_{2,x} \\ n_{1,y} & n_{2,y} \\ c_{1,x} n_{1,y} - c_{1,y} n_{1,x} & c_{2,x} n_{2,y} - c_{2,y} n_{2,x} \end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting:

$$G = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\alpha & \cos\alpha \\ \sin\alpha & -\sin\alpha \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The third row (moment about z-axis) vanishes because each contact passes through the object centre for this symmetric grasp. This means **a 2-finger antipodal grasp with no friction cannot resist external moments — it is NOT in force closure.** Adding friction, or a third finger, is required.

**Step 5:** Force closure test.

A grasp is in force closure if and only if there exist non-negative coefficients $\beta_i \geq 0$ such that:

$$G \beta = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \beta \neq 0$$

For the frictionless 2-finger grasp above, the only solution is $\beta = 0$, so the grasp is **NOT** in force closure. To achieve force closure, we either (a) add friction, (b) add a third finger, or (c) use a soft-finger contact.

### 3.4 Worked Example Problem (KTU Typical Pattern)

**Problem:** A 3-fingered robotic hand grasps a cylindrical object of radius $R = 30$ mm. The contact points are equally spaced at $120°$ on the circumference, and each contact is a point contact with friction coefficient $\mu = 0.4$. Determine the minimum number of fingers required to guarantee force closure and justify your answer.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the contact geometry.

Three contacts at $120°$ intervals: $\alpha_1 = 0°$, $\alpha_2 = 120°$, $\alpha_3 = 240°$.

**Step 2:** Compute the friction cone half-angle.

$$\tan\phi = \mu = 0.4 \implies \phi = \arctan(0.4) \approx 21.8°$$

**Step 3:** Check force closure condition.

For a planar 3-finger grasp, the necessary and sufficient condition is that the contact normals positively span $\mathbb{R}^{2}$. With three normals at $0°$, $120°$, $240°$ on a unit circle, the convex hull of the normals contains the origin in its interior. ✓

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

Three fingers are sufficient for force closure when friction is present. Two fingers would NOT suffice frictionlessly. **Answer: 3 fingers minimum, with μ ≥ 0.4.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Multi-Fingered Hand System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mobile Base Controller] --> B[Wrist Module 3 DOF]
    B --> C[Palm Static Mounting Plate]
    C --> D1[Finger 1 Proximal Joint]
    C --> D2[Finger 2 Proximal Joint]
    C --> D3[Finger 3 Proximal Joint]
    C --> D4[Finger 4 Thumb Opposable]
    D1 --> E1[Finger 1 Middle Joint]
    D1 --> F1[Finger 1 Distal Joint Fingertip]
    E1 --> F1
    D2 --> E2[Finger 2 Middle Joint]
    D2 --> F2[Finger 2 Distal Joint Fingertip]
    E2 --> F2
    D3 --> E3[Finger 3 Middle Joint]
    D3 --> F3[Finger 3 Distal Joint Fingertip]
    E3 --> F3
    D4 --> E4[Thumb Middle Joint]
    D4 --> F4[Thumb Distal Joint Fingertip]
    E4 --> F4
    F1 --> G[Target Object Wrench]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Grasp Matrix G Mapping]
    H --> I[Force Closure Analysis]
    I --> J[Stable Grasp Confirmed]
```

### 4.2 Grasp Taxonomy Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Grasp Classification] --> B{Task Requires Force or Dexterity?}
    B -->|Force| C[Power Grasp]
    B -->|Dexterity| D[Precision Grasp]
    C --> C1[Wrap fingers around object]
    C --> C2[Maximal contact area]
    C --> C3[Example: holding hammer]
    D --> D1[Fingertip contact only]
    D --> D2[Opposable thumb engaged]
    D --> D3[Example: holding pen]
    D --> D4{Two or Three Points?}
    D4 -->|Two| E[Pinch Grasp]
    D4 -->|Three| F[Tripod Grasp]
    E --> E1[Thumb plus index finger]
    E --> E2[Example: picking coin]
    F --> F1[Thumb plus index plus middle]
    F --> F2[Example: picking up small sphere]
```

### 4.3 Finger Kinematic Chain Detail

```mermaid
graph LR
    PALM[Palm Base] --> J1[Revolute Joint theta1]
    J1 --> L1[Proximal Link L1]
    L1 --> J2[Revolute Joint theta2]
    J2 --> L2[Middle Link L2]
    L2 --> J3[Revolute Joint theta3]
    J3 --> L3[Distal Link L3]
    L3 --> TIP[Fingertip Contact Point]
    TIP -.-> OBJ[Target Object]
    J1 -.-> JAC[Jacobian Matrix J theta]
    JAC -.-> CTRL[Velocity Controller]
    CTRL -.-> TIP
```

### 4.4 Contact-Type Selection Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Choose Contact Type] --> B{Object Surface Rigid?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Object Deformable?}
    B -->|No| D[Soft Finger Contact]
    C -->|No| E[Hard Point Contact]
    C -->|Yes| F[Surface Contact]
    E --> G{Friction Coefficient}
    G -->|mu greater than 0| H[PCWF Point Contact With Friction]
    G -->|mu equals 0| I[PCWoF Point Contact No Friction]
    H --> J[3 Force 1 Moment Limit]
    I --> K[3 Force Only]
    D --> L[3 Force plus 1 Moment]
    F --> M[Full 6 DOF Constraint]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Define a multi-fingered robotic hand. List the types of grasps commonly used in robotic manipulation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A multi-fingered robotic hand is an articulated end-effector consisting of multiple fingers mounted on a palm, where each finger is a serial chain of rigid links connected by revolute joints, designed to grasp and manipulate objects with human-like dexterity. Each finger typically has 3 DOF, and the hand has 3 to 5 fingers.

The common types of grasps are:

1. **Power Grasp** – envelops the object for high force (e.g., hammer).
2. **Precision Grasp** – fingertip contact for dexterity (e.g., pen).
3. **Pinch Grasp** – two-finger opposition (e.g., coin).
4. **Tripod Grasp** – three-finger opposition (e.g., sphere).
5. **Hook Grasp** – passive finger curl for carrying.

(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for grasp list.)

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the grasp matrix? State the condition for force closure in a planar grasp.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **grasp matrix G** is a 6×m matrix (3×m for planar) that linearly maps the vector of fingertip contact forces to the resultant wrench (force and moment) on the grasped object:

$$w = G \cdot f$$

The **force closure condition** states that a grasp is in force closure if the origin lies in the interior of the convex hull of the columns of G. Equivalently, there exist non-negative coefficients $\beta_i \geq 0$ such that $G\beta = 0$ with $\beta \neq 0$, while each contact force lies within its friction cone. Physically, the fingers can resist any external wrench using only pushing forces.

(Marks: 1.5 for grasp matrix definition + 1.5 for force closure condition.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic equations of a 2-link planar robotic finger with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1$, $\theta_2$. Hence obtain the Jacobian matrix relating joint velocities to fingertip velocities.

**(b)** For a 3-fingered hand grasping a circular object of radius 50 mm with contacts at $0°$, $120°$ and $240°$ on the circumference, and friction coefficient $\mu = 0.3$, determine whether the grasp is in force closure. Justify with the friction cone test.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Forward Kinematics and Jacobian (7 marks):**

Following the derivation in Section 3.1 and 3.2, the fingertip position is:

$$x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\dot{x} = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$

$$\dot{y} = L_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)$$

The Jacobian matrix is:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

The determinant is:

$$\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2$$

When $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\pi$, the finger is in a singular configuration (links aligned or folded back).

(Marks: 3 for FK derivation, 2 for Jacobian, 1 for singular configuration, 1 for clarity.)

**(b) Force Closure Test (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the friction cone half-angle.

$$\tan\phi = \mu = 0.3 \implies \phi = \arctan(0.3) \approx 16.7°$$

**Step 2:** Identify the contact normals.

$$n_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad n_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 120° \\ \sin 120° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ 0.866 \end{bmatrix}, \quad n_3 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 240° \\ \sin 240° \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 \\ -0.866 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Test force closure.

The condition is that the convex hull of the friction cone rays contains the origin. With three contacts at $120°$ intervals, the largest gap between adjacent normals is $120°$. Since $\phi = 16.7°$ and $2\phi = 33.4° \ll 120°$, the friction cones do **NOT** overlap.

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

The grasp is **NOT in force closure** with $\mu = 0.3$ because the friction cones are too narrow to fill the $120°$ gaps. The minimum friction required is $\mu_{min} = \tan(60°) \approx 1.732$, which is physically unrealistic. Therefore, additional fingers or contact type changes (e.g., soft-finger) are needed.

(Marks: 2 for friction cone, 2 for normal computation, 2 for closure test, 1 for conclusion.)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Mishra–Schwartz–Sharir contact model with neat classification. Differentiate between a point contact with friction and a soft-finger contact.

**(b)** A 2-fingered planar hand grasps a circular disc of radius $R = 40$ mm at antipodal contact points (diametrically opposite). Each finger can apply a maximum normal force of $F_n = 10$ N, and the friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.5$. Determine if the grasp can resist an external downward force of 15 N applied at the centre of the disc.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Contact Model Classification (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-yield topic. The Mishra–Schwartz–Sharir model classifies contacts by the number of constrained DOFs and the friction conditions.

| Contact Type | Constrained Forces | Constrained Moments | Friction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frictionless point (PCWoF)** | 3 (Fx, Fy, Fz) | 0 | None |
| **Frictional point (PCWF)** | 3 | 0 (point) | Friction cone |
| **Soft finger (SFC)** | 3 | 1 (tangential moment) | μ + torsional friction |
| **Line contact** | Distributed | Distributed | Friction along line |
| **Surface contact** | 6 (rigid) | 6 | Full stick |

**Difference between PCWF and SFC:**

- A **point contact with friction (PCWF)** can transmit 3 force components but no moment. Friction is modelled as a cone in the tangent plane.
- A **soft-finger contact (SFC)** can additionally transmit a moment about the contact normal, modelled by an additional torsional friction limit. SFC is more realistic for human fingertips (covered with compliant material).

(Marks: 3 for table, 2 for PCWF explanation, 2 for SFC difference.)

**(b) Antipodal Grasp Force Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Geometry.

Two contacts at $(0, R)$ and $(0, -R)$, with inward normals pointing in $\pm y$ direction.

**Step 2:** Friction cone force limits.

Maximum tangential force per finger = $\mu F_n = 0.5 \times 10 = 5$ N.

**Step 3:** Vertical force equilibrium.

The external 15 N downward force is resisted by friction at the contacts. Two contacts can each provide up to 5 N of vertical friction force (friction is bidirectional in the friction cone).

Total maximum vertical resistance = $2 \times 5 = 10$ N.

**Step 4:** Comparison.

$10\,\text{N} < 15\,\text{N}$, so the grasp **CANNOT** resist the external force.

**Step 5:** Required normal force.

To resist 15 N, we need:

$$2 \mu F_n \geq 15 \implies F_n \geq \frac{15}{2 \times 0.5} = 15\,\text{N}$$

So each finger must apply at least 15 N (current capability is 10 N, insufficient).

(Marks: 2 for geometry, 2 for force limit, 1 for comparison, 2 for required force calculation.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse force closure with form closure.** Form closure is a *geometric* property (no friction needed). Force closure requires friction or specific contact types. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 2. **The friction cone is NOT a single inequality — it is a quadratic constraint** in 3D, or a sector in 2D. Drawing the cone and showing the half-angle $\phi = \arctan(\mu)$ is essential for full marks.
> 3. **For a 2-fingered antipodal planar grasp, force closure requires friction.** Many students incorrectly claim any 2-finger antipodal grasp is automatically in force closure — this is WRONG. Without friction, it is in form closure for translation only, not for moments.
> 4. **Always state the contact type before applying the grasp matrix.** The form of G depends on whether contacts are point-with-friction, soft-finger, etc. Marking schemes deduct 1–2 marks for missing this.
> 5. **Singularities matter.** If asked about manipulability, check $\det(J) = 0$. Most KTU answer sheets miss this.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Multi-fingered hand = palm + ≥ 3 articulated fingers with revolute joints.
- **DOF per finger:** Typically **3** (proximal, middle, distal).
- **Total hand DOF:** $k \times n$ where k = number of fingers, n = joints per finger.
- **Grasp matrix equation:** $w = G f$ — maps contact forces to object wrench.
- **Force closure:** Origin inside the convex hull of G's columns + friction cones non-empty.
- **Form closure:** Purely geometric; friction not required; origin inside convex hull of contact normals (with full contact points considered).
- **Mishra contact types (5):** Frictionless point, frictional point, soft finger, line, surface.
- **Friction cone half-angle:** $\phi = \arctan(\mu)$ in 2D; in 3D it is a right circular cone with apex angle $2\phi$.
- **Antipodal grasp:** Two contacts diametrically opposite; needs friction for force closure.
- **3-finger grasp at 120°:** Friction NOT needed for form closure, but needed for force closure unless μ is very high.
- **Jacobian use:** Velocity mapping $v = J\dot{\theta}$, and force mapping $\tau = J^T f$.
- **Singularity:** Occurs when $\det(J) = 0$ (e.g., $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\pi$ for 2-link finger).
- **Manipulability index (Yoshikawa):** $w = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$ — measure of dexterity away from singularities.
- **Coulomb friction law:** $F_t \leq \mu F_n$.
- **Workspace vs Dextrous workspace:** Workspace = reachable; Dextrous = reachable with full orientation capability.
- **Human analogy:** Thumb opposition is the key to dexterity; design with at least one opposing finger.
- **Mobile robotics link:** Multi-fingered hand converts gross mobility into fine manipulation — essential for real-world task completion.
- **Standard friction values:** Rubber on glass $\mu \approx 0.5$–$0.9$; metal on metal $\mu \approx 0.2$–$0.6$.
- **Common KTU mistakes:** Mixing up force and form closure, ignoring friction cones, omitting contact type declaration, and skipping Jacobian singularity analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators: Electric Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Actuators: Electric Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Actuator?

In the context of mobile robotics, an **actuator** is a physical device that converts stored energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or chemical) into mechanical motion — typically rotational or linear — to perform a useful task such as driving wheels, steering, lifting a manipulator arm, or rotating a sensor head.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *"Actuators are the 'effectors' of a robot — the muscles and limbs that execute the motion commands generated by the controller. In mobile robotics, the dominant actuator technology is the **electric motor** because of its clean, controllable, and easily-modulated energy form."*

### 1.2 Why Electric Motors Dominate Mobile Robotics

Mobile robots — from AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) in factories to Mars rovers — overwhelmingly use electric motors. The reasons are codified below:

| Property | Advantage for Mobile Robots |
|---|---|
| **Clean Energy** | No fluid leaks (unlike hydraulics) — safe for indoor, medical, and space applications |
| **High Power-to-Weight Ratio** | Critical for battery-powered, weight-sensitive platforms |
| **Controllability** | Speed and torque can be modulated precisely via voltage/current |
| **Modularity** | Easy to interface with digital microcontrollers (PWM, encoders) |
| **Quiet Operation** | Suitable for service and human-interactive robots |
| **Scalability** | Available from sub-watt (drones) to multi-kW (heavy AGVs) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot's Muscles"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Pump:**
> Think of an electric motor as the **bicep of the robot**. The robot's **controller** (the brain) decides *how hard* and *how fast* the muscle must contract. A **voltage** sent to the motor is analogous to the **nerve signal** telling the muscle to act. The **current** flowing in is like the **blood-flow effort** the muscle exerts, and the resulting **torque** is the **rotational force** at the joint, which — when combined with wheels or gears — propels the robot forward.
> Just as a stronger muscle allows an athlete to lift a heavier barbell, a motor with a higher **torque constant** allows a robot to climb steeper slopes, carry heavier payloads, or accelerate faster.

### 1.4 Classification of Electric Motors Used in Mobile Robotics

The four major families a KTU student must master are:

1. **Brushed DC (BDC) Motor** — simplest, cheapest, widely used in small differential-drive robots.
2. **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** — higher efficiency, longer life, used in drones, premium AGVs.
3. **Servo Motor** — a DC/brushless motor + position feedback + integrated controller; used for steering and arm joints.
4. **Stepper Motor** — open-loop precise positioning; common in 3D printers and CNC-style mobile bases.

> [!NOTE]
> The remaining notes focus on the **Brushed DC Motor** as the foundational analytical model, because the **same governing equations** (back-EMF, torque constant, armature resistance) form the basis for understanding BLDC and servo motors as well.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Brushed DC Motor — Physical Construction

A brushed DC motor consists of two main electromagnetic subsystems:

- **Stator (Field):** The stationary part, producing a constant magnetic field. In permanent-magnet (PM) DC motors — the most common in mobile robots — the stator is a set of strong permanent magnets (typically **Neodymium Iron Boron — NdFeB**).
- **Rotor (Armature):** The rotating part, consisting of coils of insulated copper wire wound on a laminated iron core. Current is delivered to the rotating coil via **brushes** (graphite blocks) rubbing against a split-metal **commutator**.

When armature current $I_a$ flows in a coil sitting inside the magnetic field $B$, the **Lorentz force** $F = BIL$ acts on each conductor, producing a torque about the rotor axis. The commutator reverses the current direction every half-rotation so that torque is always unidirectional.

### 2.2 Equivalent Circuit Model

The electrical side of a DC motor is modelled as a simple series circuit:

- Armature resistance $R_a$ (ohms, $\Omega$)
- Armature inductance $L_a$ (henries, H)
- A back-EMF source $e_b$ (volts, V) opposing the applied voltage

The mechanical side is modelled as:

- Rotor inertia $J$ (kg·m²)
- Viscous friction coefficient $B$ (N·m·s/rad)
- Load torque $T_L$ (N·m)

### 2.3 The Two Governing Equations (Heart of KTU Problems)

**Equation 1 — Electrical (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop):**

$$V_a = R_a \, i_a + L_a \, \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$$

**Equation 2 — Mechanical (Newton's 2nd Law for rotation):**

$$T_m = J \, \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \, \omega + T_L$$

### 2.4 Back-EMF and the Motor Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Back-EMF (Counter-EMF)** is a voltage *induced* in the spinning armature coil by Faraday's law. It always **opposes** the applied voltage (Lenz's law) and is **proportional to angular velocity**.

$$e_b = K_b \, \omega$$

where:
- $K_b$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

Similarly, the electromagnetic torque produced by the motor is proportional to the armature current:

$$T_m = K_t \, i_a$$

where:
- $K_t$ = torque constant (N·m/A)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Fact:** In SI units, $K_t$ (N·m/A) and $K_b$ (V·s/rad) are **numerically equal**. So $K_t = K_b$. Always state this explicitly in exam answers.

### 2.5 Steady-State Speed–Torque Characteristic

Under **steady-state** (no acceleration, $d\omega/dt = 0$ and $di_a/dt = 0$):

- Mechanical: $T_m = T_L$ (assuming friction lumped into $T_L$)
- Electrical: $V_a = R_a \, i_a + e_b$

Substitute $i_a = T_m / K_t$ and $e_b = K_b \, \omega$:

$$V_a = \frac{R_a}{K_t} \, T_m + K_b \, \omega$$

Solve for $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_b \, K_t} \, T_m$$

This is the famous **linear speed–torque (mechanical characteristic) line**.

### 2.6 Two End-Points of the Speed–Torque Line

| Operating Point | Condition | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **No-Load Speed** ($\omega_0$) | $T_m = 0$ | $\omega_0 = V_a / K_b$ |
| **Stall Torque** ($T_{stall}$) | $\omega = 0$ | $T_{stall} = (K_t \, V_a) / R_a$ |
| **Starting Current** ($I_{stall}$) | $\omega = 0$, $e_b = 0$ | $I_{stall} = V_a / R_a$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: confusing the **no-load speed** with the **rated speed**. The no-load speed occurs when shaft torque = 0; the rated speed is at the motor's continuous-rated torque and is **lower** than no-load speed.

### 2.7 Power Flow and Efficiency

The electrical power input is $P_{in} = V_a \, i_a$. It splits into:

- **Copper (I²R) losses** in the armature: $P_{cu} = i_a^{\,2} \, R_a$
- **Mechanical power developed** (air-gap power): $P_{dev} = e_b \, i_a = T_m \, \omega$
- **Rotational losses** (friction, windage, core): often lumped into a constant $P_{rot}$

The **shaft output power** is:

$$P_{out} = T_m \, \omega - P_{rot}$$

The **efficiency** is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Electric Motors (Module 2)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e_b = K_b \, \omega$ | Back-EMF equation | V |
| 2 | $T_m = K_t \, i_a$ | Torque equation | N·m |
| 3 | $V_a = R_a \, i_a + L_a \, \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$ | Armature KVL | V |
| 4 | $T_m = J \, \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B \, \omega + T_L$ | Rotor Newton equation | N·m |
| 5 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b}$ | No-load speed | rad/s |
| 6 | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t \, V_a}{R_a}$ | Stall (starting) torque | N·m |
| 7 | $I_{stall} = \dfrac{V_a}{R_a}$ | Starting current | A |
| 8 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b} - \dfrac{R_a}{K_b \, K_t} \, T_m$ | Steady-state speed–torque line | rad/s |
| 9 | $P_{dev} = e_b \, i_a = T_m \, \omega$ | Developed (air-gap) power | W |
| 10 | $P_{cu} = i_a^{\,2} \, R_a$ | Copper loss | W |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V_a \, i_a}$ | Efficiency | % |
| 12 | $K_t = K_b$ | SI-unit equivalence | — |
| 13 | $\omega_{m,new} = \omega_{m,old} / N$ | Gear ratio speed reduction | rad/s |
| 14 | $T_{m,new} = N \cdot T_{m,old}$ | Gear ratio torque multiplication | N·m |
| 15 | $V_{emf} = \dfrac{N \cdot \Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$ | Back-EMF from encoder | V |

> **Note on Gears (13 & 14):** $N$ = gear reduction ratio (output\_speed/input\_speed). Gears **reduce speed** and **multiply torque**, conserving power ideally.

### 2.9 Motor Selection Criteria for Mobile Robots

A KTU-level design question may ask: *"Which motor would you select for a differential-drive AGV carrying 50 kg at 1 m/s?"* The selection hinges on:

1. **Required continuous torque:** $T_{req} \geq T_{load} / (r \cdot N)$ where $r$ is wheel radius.
2. **Required no-load speed:** $\omega_0 \geq v_{max} / r$ (after gear compensation).
3. **Battery voltage compatibility:** $V_a$ must match available bus.
4. **Duty cycle / thermal limits:** $I_{rms}$ must not exceed the motor's rated current.
5. **Form factor and weight budget:** Especially for aerial and humanoid robots.
6. **Feedback availability:** Encoders for closed-loop control of BDC; integrated feedback for servo.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Speed as a Function of Load Torque

**Starting point:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature circuit.

$$V_a = R_a \, i_a + L_a \, \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_b$$

**Step 1 — Steady-state assumption:** In steady state, all time derivatives vanish, so $L_a \, (di_a/dt) = 0$.

$$V_a = R_a \, i_a + e_b$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constitutive laws** $e_b = K_b \, \omega$ and $T_m = K_t \, i_a$, hence $i_a = T_m / K_t$.

$$V_a = R_a \cdot \frac{T_m}{K_t} + K_b \, \omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\omega$ in terms of $T_m$:** subtract the first term from both sides.

$$K_b \, \omega = V_a - \frac{R_a}{K_t} \, T_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $K_b$ to isolate $\omega$:**

$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_b \, K_t} \, T_m$$

**Step 5 — Identify the two end-points** by setting $T_m = 0$ and $\omega = 0$ respectively:

- No-load: $\omega_0 = V_a / K_b$
- Stall: $T_{stall} = K_t \, V_a / R_a$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:** This is a **straight line** on a $\omega$–vs–$T_m$ plot, with **negative slope** $m = -R_a / (K_b K_t)$. The slope's magnitude is called the **speed regulation**; a smaller $|m|$ means a "flatter" line, i.e., a motor whose speed is less affected by load (better regulation).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Mechanical Power Transfer Point

**Step 1 — Mechanical developed power** is the product of torque and speed:

$$P_{dev} = T_m \, \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the speed–torque relation** from §3.1:

$$P_{dev} = T_m \left[ \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_b K_t} \, T_m \right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand the product:**

$$P_{dev} = \frac{V_a}{K_b} \, T_m - \frac{R_a}{K_b K_t} \, T_m^{\,2}$$

**Step 4 — Maximize by taking $dP_{dev} / dT_m = 0$:**

$$\frac{dP_{dev}}{dT_m} = \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{2 R_a}{K_b K_t} \, T_m = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the torque at maximum power:**

$$T_{m,\,max\,P} = \frac{K_t \, V_a}{2 R_a} = \frac{T_{stall}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back to find the corresponding speed:**

$$\omega_{max\,P} = \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_b K_t} \cdot \frac{K_t V_a}{2 R_a} = \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{V_a}{2 K_b} = \frac{V_a}{2 K_b} = \frac{\omega_0}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Conclude the maximum power value:**

$$P_{dev,\,max} = T_{m,\,max\,P} \cdot \omega_{max\,P} = \frac{T_{stall}}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega_0}{2} = \frac{T_{stall} \, \omega_0}{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** Maximum mechanical power is developed at the **midpoint** of the speed–torque line — i.e., at **half the no-load speed** and **half the stall torque**. This is a frequently asked KTU 14-mark derivation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Transfer Function of a DC Motor (Control View)

For modern mobile-robot controllers (PID), we need the **transfer function** $\Omega(s)/V_a(s)$ assuming $T_L = 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the two governing equations in the s-domain** (Laplace), with zero initial conditions:

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \, I_a(s) + K_b \, \Omega(s)$$

$$K_t \, I_a(s) = (J s + B) \, \Omega(s)$$

**Step 2 — Solve the second equation for $I_a(s)$:**

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B) \, \Omega(s)}{K_t}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the first equation:**

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B) \, \Omega(s)}{K_t} + K_b \, \Omega(s)$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $\Omega(s)$:**

$$V_a(s) = \Omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

**Step 5 — Combine over a common denominator $K_t$:**

$$V_a(s) = \Omega(s) \cdot \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}{K_t}$$

**Step 6 — Invert to get the transfer function:**

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_t}$$

**Step 7 — Final form (canonical 2nd-order):**

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / (R_a B + K_b K_t)}{\tau_e \tau_m s^2 + (\tau_e + \tau_m) s + 1}$$

where the **electrical time constant** $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$ and the **mechanical time constant** $\tau_m = J R_a / (R_a B + K_b K_t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for mobile robots:** A small $\tau_e$ and $\tau_m$ mean the motor responds quickly to voltage changes — vital for tight motion control in dynamic environments.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

**Problem:** A brushed DC motor used in a differential-drive AGV has the following nameplate data:
- Armature resistance $R_a = 1.2 \, \Omega$
- Armature inductance $L_a = 5 \, \text{mH}$
- Torque constant $K_t = 0.05 \, \text{N·m/A}$
- Back-EMF constant $K_b = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$
- Applied voltage $V_a = 24 \, \text{V}$
- Load torque $T_L = 0.4 \, \text{N·m}$
- Viscous friction $B = 0.001 \, \text{N·m·s/rad}$
- Rotor inertia $J = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{kg·m}^2$

**Find:** (i) No-load speed, (ii) Steady-state speed at the given load, (iii) Starting current, (iv) Developed power at the given load.

**Solution:**

**(i) No-load speed** (at $T_m = 0$):

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K_b} = \frac{24}{0.05} = 480 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**(ii) Steady-state speed under load** — first compute armature current $i_a = T_L / K_t$ (steady state, $T_m = T_L$):

$$i_a = \frac{0.4}{0.05} = 8 \, \text{A}$$

Back-EMF at this load:

$$e_b = V_a - R_a \, i_a = 24 - 1.2 \times 8 = 24 - 9.6 = 14.4 \, \text{V}$$

Steady-state speed:

$$\omega = \frac{e_b}{K_b} = \frac{14.4}{0.05} = 288 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**(iii) Starting (stall) current** — at $\omega = 0$, $e_b = 0$:

$$I_{stall} = \frac{V_a}{R_a} = \frac{24}{1.2} = 20 \, \text{A}$$

**(iv) Developed power** at the loaded operating point:

$$P_{dev} = T_m \, \omega = 0.4 \times 288 = 115.2 \, \text{W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students forget to compute $i_a$ first in part (ii) and try to apply the speed–torque equation directly. Always derive $i_a$ from $T_m = K_t \, i_a$ first, *then* compute $e_b$, *then* $\omega$.

---

### 3.5 Speed Control Methods — Analytical Comparison

| Method | Principle | Effect on $\omega$–$T$ line | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Armature Voltage Control** | Vary $V_a$ | Line shifts up/down (parallel) | Smooth, high-efficiency | Needs variable DC source |
| **Field Flux Control** | Vary $B$ (field) | Slope changes | Simple with wound field | Limited range, slow response |
| **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** | Rapid ON/OFF switching of $V_a$ | Equivalent to reduced average $V_a$ | Digital, microcontroller-friendly | Adds ripple; needs MOSFET/IGBT switch |
| **Series Resistance** | Insert $R_{ext}$ | Slope changes, no-load unchanged | Cheap, simple | High $I^2R$ loss, inefficient |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DC Motor Equivalent Circuit & Energy-Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["DC Source Va"]:::src --> nodeB["Armature Resistance Ra"]:::r
    nodeB --> nodeC["Armature Inductance La"]:::l
    nodeC --> nodeD["Back EMF Source eb"]:::emf
    nodeD --> nodeE["Mechanical Shaft Output"]:::mech
    nodeE --> nodeF["Load Torque TL"]:::load
    nodeE --> nodeG["Rotor Inertia J"]:::mech
    nodeE --> nodeH["Viscous Friction B"]:::mech

    classDef src fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    classDef l fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef emf fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop PID Speed Control Architecture for a Mobile Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ref["Reference Speed omega_ref"]:::ref --> sumA(("Sum Node")):::sum
    fb["Encoder Feedback omega_meas"]:::fb --> sumA
    sumA --> ctrl["PID Controller Kp Ki Kd"]:::ctrl
    ctrl --> pwm["PWM Generator Module"]:::pwm
    pwm --> hb["H Bridge Motor Driver"]:::drv
    hb --> motor["DC Motor Plant"]:::motor
    motor --> shaft["Wheel Shaft Output"]:::out
    shaft --> enc["Optical Encoder Sensor"]:::fb
    enc --> fb

    classDef ref fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef pwm fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#5F9EA0,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#F4A460,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Motor Selection Decision Flowchart for a Mobile Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Define Mission Profile"]:::start --> q1{"Power < 50 W?"}:::dec
    q1 -- Yes --> bdc["Brushed DC Motor"]:::pick
    q1 -- No --> q2{"Need precise position?"}:::dec
    q2 -- Yes --> servo["Servo or Stepper Motor"]:::pick
    q2 -- No --> q3{"Long life, low maintenance?"}:::dec
    q3 -- Yes --> bldc["Brushless DC Motor"]:::pick
    q3 -- No --> bdc

    classDef start fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef pick fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Speed–Torque Line — Visual Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stallPt["STALL POINT\nomega=0, T=Tstall\nTstall = Kt Va / Ra"]:::stall --> midPt["MAX POWER POINT\nomega=V/2Kb, T=Tstall/2"]:::mid
    midPt --> noloadPt["NO LOAD POINT\nomega=V/Kb, T=0"]:::noload
    stallPt --- midPt
    midPt --- noloadPt

    classDef stall fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef mid fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef noload fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture: Mobile-Robot Drive Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    battery["Li-ion Battery Pack 24V"]:::pwr --> mc["Motor Controller MCU STM32"]:::ctrl
    mc --> driver["H Bridge Driver L298N"]:::drv
    driver --> mleft["Left Wheel DC Motor"]:::motor
    driver --> mright["Right Wheel DC Motor"]:::motor
    mleft --> wleft["Left Wheel Encoder"]:::fb
    mright --> wright["Right Wheel Encoder"]:::fb
    wleft --> mc
    wright --> mc
    imu["IMU Sensor MPU6050"]:::sensor --> mc
    mc --> telemetry["Wi-Fi Telemetry to Base Station"]:::com

    classDef pwr fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef fb fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000
    classDef com fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by "back-EMF" in a DC motor? Why is it significant in mobile-robot motion control?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Back-EMF (counter-EMF) is the voltage induced in the rotating armature coil of a DC motor, opposing the applied armature voltage. By Faraday's law, $e_b = K_b \, \omega$, where $K_b$ is the back-EMF constant and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

**Physical origin (1 mark):** As the armature conductors cut the stator's magnetic flux during rotation, an EMF is generated. By Lenz's law, this induced EMF always opposes the cause (the applied voltage), hence the name "back" EMF.

**Significance in mobile robots (1 mark):**
- It provides **natural speed feedback**: as $\omega$ rises, $e_b$ rises, reducing the net voltage $(V_a - e_b)$ and hence the current $i_a = (V_a - e_b)/R_a$. This automatically limits the speed at no-load to $\omega_0 = V_a / K_b$.
- It allows the controller to **estimate speed** without an explicit tachometer (sensorless control), useful in low-cost differential-drive robots.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a brushed DC motor and a brushless DC (BLDC) motor in the context of mobile-robot applications. State two advantages of BLDC for mobile robots.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Distinction (1 mark):** A **brushed DC motor** uses mechanical brushes and a commutator to switch current in the armature, while a **BLDC motor** electronically commutates the stator windings using an external controller (ESC) and has the permanent magnets on the rotor.

**Two advantages of BLDC (2 marks, 1 each):**
1. **Higher efficiency and longer life:** No brush friction or commutator wear, ideal for continuously operating AGVs and drones.
2. **Better heat dissipation:** Heat is generated in the stator (on the outside), making cooling easier — important for high-power mobile platforms.
3. *(Alternative)* Higher power-to-weight ratio — crucial for aerial mobile robots (quadcopters).
4. *(Alternative)* Lower electromagnetic interference due to absence of brush arcing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** Derive the steady-state speed–torque characteristic equation of a brushed DC motor from the basic governing equations. Show that the no-load speed is $V_a / K_b$ and the stall torque is $K_t V_a / R_a$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the two governing equations:
- Electrical: $V_a = R_a \, i_a + L_a \, (di_a/dt) + e_b$
- Mechanical: $T_m = J \, (d\omega/dt) + B \, \omega + T_L$
- Constitutive: $e_b = K_b \, \omega$ and $T_m = K_t \, i_a$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply steady-state: $di_a/dt = 0$ and $d\omega/dt = 0$, giving $V_a = R_a \, i_a + e_b$ and $T_m = T_L$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Express $i_a$ from torque equation: $i_a = T_m / K_t$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute into the KVL equation:
$$V_a = \frac{R_a}{K_t} \, T_m + K_b \, \omega$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Rearrange to solve for $\omega$:
$$\omega = \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{R_a}{K_b K_t} \, T_m$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** No-load condition: $T_m = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_0 = V_a / K_b$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Stall condition: $\omega = 0 \Rightarrow T_{stall} = K_t V_a / R_a$.

**Conclusion:** The speed–torque line is a straight line with negative slope $-R_a / (K_b K_t)$, intercepts $\omega_0$ and $T_{stall}$ as derived.

---

**Q.A (b)** A DC motor used in a two-wheeled mobile robot has the following specifications:
- $R_a = 0.8 \, \Omega$, $K_t = K_b = 0.04 \, \text{N·m/A}$, $V_a = 12 \, \text{V}$
- Wheel radius $r = 0.05 \, \text{m}$, gear ratio $N = 30$ (speed reduction)
- Robot mass $m = 8 \, \text{kg}$, total gear-train efficiency $\eta_g = 0.85$

The robot must climb a 15° incline at constant velocity. Compute: (i) the torque required at each wheel, (ii) the steady-state motor current, and (iii) the linear speed of the robot. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Force balance on incline:**

The component of weight along the slope is $F = m g \sin\theta = 8 \times 9.81 \times \sin 15° = 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.2588 = 20.31 \, \text{N}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Torque per wheel** (two wheels share the load equally):

$$T_{wheel} = \frac{F \cdot r}{2} = \frac{20.31 \times 0.05}{2} = 0.508 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Torque required at the motor shaft** (account for gear ratio and efficiency):

$$T_m = \frac{T_{wheel}}{N \cdot \eta_g} = \frac{0.508}{30 \times 0.85} = 0.0199 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Motor current** (using $T_m = K_t \, i_a$):

$$i_a = \frac{T_m}{K_t} = \frac{0.0199}{0.04} = 0.498 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Back-EMF and motor speed:**

$$e_b = V_a - R_a \, i_a = 12 - 0.8 \times 0.498 = 12 - 0.398 = 11.602 \, \text{V}$$

$$\omega_{motor} = \frac{e_b}{K_b} = \frac{11.602}{0.04} = 290.05 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Wheel angular speed and linear speed:**

$$\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_{motor}}{N} = \frac{290.05}{30} = 9.668 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$v_{robot} = \omega_{wheel} \times r = 9.668 \times 0.05 = 0.483 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Verification with no-load:**

No-load speed: $\omega_0 = V_a / K_b = 12 / 0.04 = 300 \, \text{rad/s}$. The motor is operating at $290 / 300 = 96.7\%$ of no-load speed, which is physically reasonable for a low-load condition. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a brushless DC (BLDC) motor. Why is it preferred over a brushed DC motor in modern mobile robots? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Rotor:** Permanent magnets (NdFeB) mounted on the rotor — no windings, no commutator.
- **Stator:** Three-phase windings (U, V, W) embedded in stator slots, energized sequentially by an Electronic Speed Controller (ESC).
- **Sensors:** Either Hall-effect sensors (for trapezoidal commutation) or rotary encoders (for sinusoidal/FOC commutation) detect rotor position.
- **Controller:** External ESC with MOSFET/IGBT inverter that switches the phases at the right instants.

**Working principle (2 marks):**
When the controller energizes one stator phase, a magnetic field is produced that attracts the nearest rotor magnet, causing rotation. As the rotor moves, the position sensors trigger the next phase, and the process repeats. This produces continuous rotation without any physical contact between rotor and stator — hence "brushless."

**Two advantages over BDC (2 marks, 1 each):**
1. **No brush wear** → maintenance-free, longer life (10,000+ hours typical).
2. **Higher efficiency (90–95%)** → longer battery runtime, critical for mobile robots.
3. *(Alternative)* Better heat dissipation, higher power density, lower EMI.

---

**Q.B (b)** A BLDC motor in a quadrotor drone has $K_b = 0.01 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. The battery delivers 11.1 V. The propeller load draws a steady current of 5 A at hover, with armature resistance $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$. Find: (i) the no-load RPM, (ii) the steady-state RPM at hover, and (iii) the developed mechanical power at hover. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — No-load speed:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K_b} = \frac{11.1}{0.01} = 1110 \, \text{rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM: $\omega_0 = 1110 \times (60 / 2\pi) = 10{,}600 \, \text{RPM}$ (approx).

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Back-EMF at hover:**

$$e_b = V_a - R_a \, i_a = 11.1 - 0.2 \times 5 = 11.1 - 1.0 = 10.1 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Steady-state angular speed:**

$$\omega = \frac{e_b}{K_b} = \frac{10.1}{0.01} = 1010 \, \text{rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM: $\omega = 1010 \times 60 / (2\pi) = 9645 \, \text{RPM}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Developed torque** (using $K_t = K_b = 0.01$):

$$T_m = K_t \, i_a = 0.01 \times 5 = 0.05 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Developed power:**

$$P_{dev} = T_m \, \omega = 0.05 \times 1010 = 50.5 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Verification using $P = e_b \, i_a$:**

$$P_{dev} = e_b \times i_a = 10.1 \times 5 = 50.5 \, \text{W} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Input and efficiency:**

$$P_{in} = V_a \, i_a = 11.1 \times 5 = 55.5 \, \text{W}$$
$$\eta = \frac{P_{dev}}{P_{in}} = \frac{50.5}{55.5} = 91.0\% \quad \text{(typical BLDC efficiency)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing rad/s and RPM without conversion. Always state both.
> 2. **Neglecting $R_a$ drop:** Some students compute $\omega = V_a / K_b$ even under load — this is the *no-load* speed, not the operating speed. Use $e_b = V_a - R_a i_a$ for the loaded case.
> 3. **Confusing torque at the motor vs. at the wheel:** For gear-driven wheels, multiply by $N$ and divide by $\eta_g$ appropriately. Mislacing these factors costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting $K_t = K_b$ in SI units:** If a problem gives only $K_b$, you can directly use $K_t = K_b$. If units are mixed (e.g., RPM), convert first.
> 5. **Direction of motor:** When reversing direction (H-bridge), $V_a$ and $e_b$ may both reverse sign — the algebra still holds with consistent sign convention.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Actuators: Electric Motors (Module 2)**

- **Actuator definition** — energy conversion device producing motion; electric motors are the dominant actuator in mobile robots.
- **Brushed DC motor parts** — stator (PM), rotor (wound armature), brushes, commutator.
- **Back-EMF:** $e_b = K_b \, \omega$, opposes applied voltage, provides natural speed-limiting.
- **Torque equation:** $T_m = K_t \, i_a$.
- **Key SI identity:** $K_t = K_b$ (numerically equal, distinct units).
- **Steady-state speed–torque line:** $\omega = V_a / K_b - (R_a / K_b K_t) T_m$ — straight line.
- **No-load speed:** $\omega_0 = V_a / K_b$.
- **Stall torque:** $T_{stall} = K_t V_a / R_a$.
- **Starting current:** $I_{stall} = V_a / R_a$ (can be 10–20× rated current — danger for the motor!).
- **Maximum mechanical power** occurs at $T = T_{stall}/2$ and $\omega = \omega_0 / 2$, with value $T_{stall} \omega_0 / 4$.
- **Transfer function:** $\Omega(s)/V_a(s) = K_t / [(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_t]$ — second-order, with electrical and mechanical time constants.
- **Gear effect:** $N$ = ratio; output speed = motor speed / $N$; output torque = motor torque × $N$ × $\eta_g$.
- **BDC vs BLDC:** BDC = simple, cheap, brushed, limited life. BLDC = efficient, durable, electronically commutated, needs ESC.
- **Servo motor:** DC/BLDC + gearbox + position sensor + embedded PID controller in one package — for steering/joints.
- **Stepper motor:** Open-loop precise angular positioning, holding torque, no feedback needed — but lower efficiency and can lose steps.
- **Speed control methods:** Armature voltage (best), PWM (digital-friendly), field flux (limited range), series resistance (lossy).
- **Selection criteria for mobile robots:** Torque, speed, voltage, weight, efficiency, duty cycle, feedback availability.
- **Energy flow:** Electrical input → copper loss + air-gap power → rotational loss + shaft output. Efficiency = $P_{out}/P_{in}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ MODULE 2 — HYDRAULIC AND PNEUMATIC ACTUATORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Actuator:** A *fluid-power actuator* that converts the **hydraulic energy** (pressure energy of an incompressible working fluid, typically mineral oil) into **mechanical motion** (linear or rotary), governed by **Pascal's Law**, to produce high-force, high-precision motion in a mobile robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pneumatic Actuator:** A *fluid-power actuator* that converts the **pneumatic energy** (pressure energy of a compressible working medium, typically filtered compressed air) into **mechanical motion**, producing lightweight, high-speed, compliant motion suitable for low-to-medium load robotic applications.

Both belong to the broader family of **Fluid Power Actuators**, which are classified as **power actuators** (as opposed to signal-level actuators like small DC motors).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **The Garden Hose Analogy (Hydraulic):**
> Imagine placing your thumb over a garden hose opening. By restricting the outlet, the pressure inside the hose rises dramatically, and the water can now squirt with great force over a long distance. The water *itself* did not change — only the *pressure* did. A hydraulic actuator does the same thing: a small pump pushes oil through a narrow valve, the oil pressure rises, and that high pressure pushes a piston with enormous force. Because oil is **incompressible**, the response is instant and stiff.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Balloon Analogy (Pneumatic):**
> Now take a balloon and squeeze it. The air inside compresses (shrinks in volume), stores energy, and when released, pushes outward. A pneumatic actuator uses compressed air in a similar way — but the air *is* compressible, so the motion is springy, soft, and gentle. Pneumatic systems are like the "soft touch" cousin of hydraulics.

### 1.3 Why These Actuators Matter in Mobile Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> Hydraulic and pneumatic actuators are the **workhorses of heavy-duty and outdoor mobile robots** — including:
> - **BigDog / Spot-class quadrupeds** (Boston Dynamics — hydraulic)
> - **Humanoid rescue robots** (Kawada HRP, Honda E2-DR)
> - **Construction & mining robots** (hydraulic excavators, autonomous dump trucks)
> - **Soft robotics grippers** (pneumatic McKibben muscles)
> - **Agricultural robots & drones** (pneumatic launching, landing gear)

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa} = 1.01325\ \text{bar} = 14.696\ \text{psi}$
- **Bulk modulus of mineral oil:** $K \approx 1.5 - 2.0\ \text{GPa}$ (effectively incompressible)
- **Speed of sound in air at 20 °C:** $c \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$ (defines pneumatic response limit)
- **Typical hydraulic working pressure:** $P = 70 - 350\ \text{bar}$ ($7 - 35\ \text{MPa}$)
- **Typical pneumatic working pressure:** $P = 4 - 8\ \text{bar}$ ($0.4 - 0.8\ \text{MPa}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### A. Hydraulic System Operation

1. **Prime Mover (Electric Motor / Engine):** Provides rotational input mechanical power.
2. **Hydraulic Pump (Fixed/Variable Displacement):** Converts mechanical power → hydraulic power by pressurizing oil.
3. **Reservoir + Filters:** Stores, cools, and cleans the hydraulic fluid.
4. **Pressure Relief Valve:** Protects the system from over-pressurization.
5. **Directional Control Valve (DCV):** Routes pressurized oil to either side of the actuator (port A or port B).
6. **Actuator (Cylinder or Motor):** Converts hydraulic power back into mechanical work.
7. **Return Line + Cooler:** Oil returns to the reservoir, exchanging heat.

**The Core 'Why':** Because the fluid is **incompressible**, any pressure increase on one side is transmitted *instantly* and *equally* in all directions (Pascal's Law), giving hydraulic systems their hallmark **stiffness, precision, and high force density**.

### B. Pneumatic System Operation

1. **Air Compressor:** Draws in atmospheric air and compresses it to working pressure.
2. **Receiver Tank:** Stores compressed air to smooth out pressure pulsations.
3. **FRL Unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator):** Cleans air, sets working pressure, and adds oil mist for lubrication.
4. **Solenoid / Manual Valves:** Control the direction and magnitude of airflow.
5. **Pneumatic Cylinder / Motor / Muscle:** Converts air pressure into motion.
6. **Exhaust Silencer & Muffler:** Vents spent air quietly to atmosphere.

**The Core 'Why':** Air is **compressible**, so pneumatic systems inherently exhibit **spring-like compliance**, are inherently **safe** (no fire risk, no leak hazard), and reach **high speeds** at the cost of positioning accuracy.

## 2.2 Key Governing Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Pascal's Law (1653):** A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted *undiminished* to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container.

Mathematically:

$$P_1 = P_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

This is the *force-multiplication principle* that makes hydraulic presses (and hydraulic robot joints) possible.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Pascal's Law (Force)** | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ [N], $P$ [Pa], $A$ [m²] | Cylinder output force |
| 2 | **Force Multiplication** | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2 / A_1)$ | dimensionless ratio | Hydraulic press / robot joint |
| 3 | **Cylinder Bore Area** | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$ = piston diameter [m] | Sizing the actuator |
| 4 | **Annulus (Rod-side) Area** | $A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right)$ | $d$ = rod diameter [m] | Retract-side force |
| 5 | **Linear Velocity of Piston** | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $Q$ = flow rate [m³/s] | Speed of joint extension |
| 6 | **Rotational Speed of Motor** | $N = \dfrac{Q}{D_m} \cdot \dfrac{60}{2\pi}$ | $D_m$ = motor displacement [m³/rev], $N$ [rpm] | Motor speed sizing |
| 7 | **Hydraulic Power** | $P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$ | $p$ [Pa], $Q$ [m³/s] → W | Pump / motor sizing |
| 8 | **Mechanical Output Power** | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = F \cdot v$ | $T$ [Nm], $\omega$ [rad/s] | Robot joint power |
| 9 | **Torque from Hydraulic Motor** | $T = \dfrac{p \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$ | $D_m$ [m³/rev] | Joint torque from motor |
| 10 | **Air Consumption (Free Air Delivery)** | $Q_{air} = \dfrac{V \cdot N}{1000 \cdot \eta_v}$ | $V$ = swept volume, $N$ = cycles/min | Compressor sizing |
| 11 | **Effective Pneumatic Force** | $F = \eta \cdot p \cdot A - F_{fric} - F_{load}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency (~0.7–0.9) | Net thrust |
| 12 | **Boyle's Law (Pneumatic)** | $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$ | $p$ [Pa], $V$ [m³] | Tank sizing, energy storage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Always convert bar → Pa correctly:
> $$1\ \text{bar} = 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 100\ \text{kPa}$$
> A common student error is to plug in bar values directly into formulas expecting Pa — this causes a **100 000× error** in force calculations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Force Density:** Hydraulic actuators produce **~ 50× more force per unit volume** than electromagnetic motors, enabling legged robots to carry heavy payloads.
- **Compliance & Safety:** Pneumatic artificial muscles (PAMs / McKibben muscles) are used in **rehabilitation exoskeletons** and **soft grippers** because of their inherent compliance.
- **Energy Storage:** Compressed air tanks in **pneumatic hybrid vehicles** store braking energy as pressurized gas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Force of a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder
