| Quantified by | Absolute or relative error | Standard deviation ($\sigma$) |
| Affected by | Systematic errors (bias, miscalibration) | Random errors (noise) |
| Example | A thermometer reads 99.8 °C when true is 100.0 °C → highly accurate | Same thermometer gives 99.8, 99.7, 99.9, 99.8 °C in 4 readings → highly precise |

> A sensor can be precise without being accurate, but high accuracy usually implies reasonable precision. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define (i) Sensitivity, (ii) Resolution, and (iii) Dynamic Range of a sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(i) Sensitivity:** The ratio of the change in sensor output to the change in the input that caused it: $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$. High sensitivity means a small input change produces a large output change. **(1 mark)**
- **(ii) Resolution:** The smallest increment in the input that the sensor can reliably detect and distinguish. For an n-bit ADC, resolution = Range / ($2^{n} - 1$). **(1 mark)**
- **(iii) Dynamic Range:** The ratio between the maximum and minimum measurable input, usually expressed in decibels: $\text{DR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(x_{max}/x_{min})$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** write only definitions for 14-mark questions. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates *numerical solving* and *real-world application*. A pure theory answer will be capped at 7 of 14 marks.
> - **Always** include a labeled diagram wherever the question mentions a sensor type, transducer, or signal flow. Missing diagram = −2 marks.
> - **Always** state units with numerical answers. Missing unit = −1 mark per instance.
> - **Do not** confuse "range" and "dynamic range" — this is a 2023–2024 KTU trend question and is graded strictly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Internal Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the static and dynamic characteristics of a sensor in detail. List any **six** static and **four** dynamic characteristics with definitions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A load cell used in a robotic arm force-feedback system is calibrated using standard weights. The following data is obtained:

| Applied Force $F$ (N) | Output Voltage $V$ (mV) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 |
| 50 | 5.2 |
| 100 | 10.1 |
| 150 | 14.8 |
| 200 | 19.7 |
| 250 | 24.5 |

Determine:
  (i) The sensitivity of the load cell in mV/N. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The offset (zero-load output). **(1 mark)**
  (iii) The maximum non-linearity as a percentage of Full-Scale (FS). **(3 marks)**
  (iv) If the ADC uses 12 bits over a 0–5 V range, what is the smallest force change detectable? **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Static and Dynamic Characteristics (7 marks):**

**Static Characteristics (any 6 × 1 mark each):**
1. **Accuracy** — closeness of measurement to true value.
2. **Precision** — repeatability of measurements.
3. **Resolution** — smallest detectable input change.
4. **Sensitivity** — $\Delta y / \Delta x$.
5. **Linearity** — straightness of output vs input.
6. **Range / Span** — min to max measurable input.
7. **Threshold** — minimum input producing a detectable output.
8. **Saturation** — maximum input beyond which output stops increasing.
9. **Dead band** — input range producing no output change.
10. **Hysteresis** — different output for increasing vs decreasing input.
11. **Drift** — slow change in output over time at constant input.

**Dynamic Characteristics (any 4 × 0.5 mark each):**
1. **Bandwidth** — frequency range of accurate response.
2. **Response time** — time to reach 63.2% of final value.
3. **Rise time** — time from 10% to 90% of final value.
4. **Settling time** — time to settle within ±2% of final value.
5. **Update rate** — readings per second.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Sensitivity (2 marks):**
Using the endpoints (best-fit method may also be accepted):

$$
S = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{F_{max} - F_{min}} = \dfrac{24.5 - 0.5}{250 - 0} = \dfrac{24.0}{250} = 0.096 \text{ mV/N}
$$

**(ii) Offset (1 mark):** Output at zero input.

$$
V_{offset} = 0.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**Calibration equation:**

$$
V = 0.096\,F + 0.5 \text{ mV}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Non-Linearity (3 marks):**

Ideal line equation: $V_{ideal} = 0.096\,F + 0.5$.

| $F$ (N) | $V_{actual}$ (mV) | $V_{ideal}$ (mV) | $\vert e \vert$ (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 | 0.50 | 0.00 |
| 50 | 5.2 | 5.30 | 0.10 |
| 100 | 10.1 | 10.10 | 0.00 |
| 150 | 14.8 | 14.90 | 0.10 |
| 200 | 19.7 | 19.70 | 0.00 |
| 250 | 24.5 | 24.50 | 0.00 |

Full-Scale output: $V_{FS} = 24.5 - 0.5 = 24.0$ mV.

Maximum error = 0.10 mV.

$$
\text{Non-linearity} = \dfrac{0.10}{24.0} \times 100\% = 0.417\% \text{ FS}
$$

**(iv) Smallest detectable force (1 mark):**

ADC resolution:

$$
\Delta V_{ADC} = \dfrac{5.0 \text{ V}}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{5.0}{4095} = 0.001221 \text{ V} = 1.221 \text{ mV}
$$

Smallest force change:

$$
\Delta F = \dfrac{\Delta V_{ADC}}{S} = \dfrac{1.221}{0.096} = 12.72 \text{ N}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 12-bit ADC is the *bottleneck* — it limits the system resolution to **~12.72 N**, even though the load cell itself is much more precise. This is a common real-world engineering insight in KTU answers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Internal Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional signal-flow path from physical stimulus to robot decision. Discuss how each stage affects overall sensor performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two distance-measuring sensors are evaluated on a mobile robot:
- **Sensor A:** Has an accuracy of ±0.5 mm and a precision (standard deviation) of ±0.1 mm.
- **Sensor B:** Has an accuracy of ±0.2 mm and a precision of ±0.4 mm.

If the robot needs to measure distances between **0.5 m and 5.0 m**, and the **true distance** to a wall is 2.000 m, calculate:
  (i) The maximum possible measured value for each sensor. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) The percentage relative error of each sensor's worst-case reading. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) Which sensor would you recommend for a SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) application, and why? Justify using the terminology studied. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Stage-wise Analysis (7 marks):**

```
Physical Stimulus → Sensing Element → Signal Conditioning → ADC 
→ Microcontroller → Perception Algorithm → Robot Decision
```

- **Sensing element (transducer):** Defines the *intrinsic* range, sensitivity, linearity, and noise floor. Errors here cannot be fixed later. **(2 marks)**
- **Signal conditioning (amplifier + filter):** Amplifies weak signals to ADC range and removes high-frequency noise. Must have high CMRR. Bad design adds **systematic offset**. **(1 mark)**
- **ADC:** Introduces **quantization error** = ±½ LSB. Resolution is limited by bit-depth. **(1 mark)**
- **Microcontroller:** Sampling rate must satisfy the **Nyquist criterion** ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$). Latency here affects real-time response. **(1 mark)**
- **Perception algorithm:** Uses sensor data for interpretation. Poor calibration here causes **drift** in long-term estimates. **(1 mark)**
- **Robot decision (actuator command):** Closing the loop — final system accuracy depends on the *worst* stage, not the best. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Comparison (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum possible measured value (2 marks):**

For Sensor A: $\text{Accuracy} = \pm 0.5$ mm and $\sigma = \pm 0.1$ mm.

$$
V_{A,max} = 2000.0 + 0.5 + (3 \times 0.1) = 2000.8 \text{ mm}
$$

(We use $3\sigma$ for 99.7% confidence under normal distribution.)

For Sensor B: $\text{Accuracy} = \pm 0.2$ mm and $\sigma = \pm 0.4$ mm.

$$
V_{B,max} = 2000.0 + 0.2 + (3 \times 0.4) = 2001.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Relative error of worst-case (2 marks):**

$$
e_{A} = \dfrac{0.8}{2000.0} \times 100\% = 0.040\%
$$

$$
e_{B} = \dfrac{1.4}{2000.0} \times 100\% = 0.070\%
$$

**(iii) Recommendation (3 marks):**

**Sensor A is recommended for SLAM**, even though its nominal accuracy is *worse* (±0.5 mm vs ±0.2 mm), because:

- **Higher precision** ($\sigma = 0.1$ mm vs $0.4$ mm) → SLAM relies on **relative** consistency between consecutive scans. A precise sensor can be calibrated to remove bias, but a noisy sensor cannot be made precise by calibration.
- **Lower worst-case error** (0.8 mm vs 1.4 mm) → reduces map distortion.
- **Better repeatability** → less jitter in feature matching, fewer loop-closure failures.

> **Key Insight:** *Accuracy* can be improved by calibration; *Precision* is an intrinsic property. For algorithms that depend on **relative** measurements (like SLAM, odometry, visual odometry), **precision dominates accuracy**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Sensor = Transducer** that converts a physical stimulus into an electrical signal.
- [x] **Proprioceptive** = internal state (encoders, IMU); **Exteroceptive** = environment (LIDAR, camera).
- [x] **Accuracy** = closeness to *true value*; **Precision** = closeness between *repeated* readings.
- [x] **Sensitivity** = $\Delta y / \Delta x$; **Resolution** = smallest detectable input step.
- [x] **Range** = min–max input; **Dynamic Range** = ratio of max/min (in dB).
- [x] **SNR** = $20 \log_{10}(A_s / A_n)$ in dB; **6 dB ≈ 1 bit** of effective resolution.
- [x] **Linearity** = straightness; **Hysteresis** = up-vs-down path difference; **Drift** = slow change over time.
- [x] **Systematic error** (bias) is removable by calibration; **Random error** (noise) is reduced by averaging.
- [x] **Static characteristics** are evaluated at constant input; **Dynamic characteristics** (bandwidth, response time) depend on frequency/time.
- [x] **KTU Pitfall:** Always write **units**, draw **diagrams**, and use **formulas** with the exact term from this terminology list.
- [x] **For SLAM/odometry:** Precision matters more than absolute accuracy.
- [x] **For absolute localization (GPS):** Accuracy dominates over precision.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra (2024 Scheme):** *"Define the term → State the formula → Solve a number → Apply to a robot scenario → Draw a diagram."* If your answer follows this 5-step pattern for any 7-mark question, you are virtually guaranteed a full-score valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors that Measure the Robot's Joint Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors that Measure the Robot's Joint Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of KTU 2024 Scheme robotics syllabus, **Joint Configuration Sensors** (also termed *proprioceptive position sensors* or *internal state sensors*) are defined as the class of transducers mounted directly on the kinematic joints of a robot (revolute or prismatic) whose primary function is to convert the mechanical state — namely **angular displacement, angular velocity, or linear displacement** — of the joint into a quantifiable electrical signal (analog voltage, digital pulses, or a coded word) suitable for real-time feedback control and state estimation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Joint configuration sensors are universally classified as **proprioceptive sensors** because they sense the *internal* state of the robot, in contrast to **exteroceptive sensors** (cameras, LiDAR, sonar) that sense the *external* environment. For mobile robots, these sensors are indispensable for **dead reckoning, odometry, and closed-loop joint/speed control**.

### 1.2 Sensor Classification (KTU Taxonomy)

| Class | Sub-class | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Proprioceptive | **Joint Configuration** | Encoders, Potentiometers, Resolvers, Hall sensors |
| Proprioceptive | Velocity | Tachometers, Gyroscopes |
| Exteroceptive | Range | Sonar, LiDAR, IR |
| Exteroceptive | Vision | Cameras |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Speedometer of a Car"**
> Imagine driving a car with your eyes closed. You still need to know three things to navigate: *how far you've turned the steering wheel*, *how much the wheels have rotated*, and *how fast they are spinning*. **Joint configuration sensors do exactly this for a robot**: the *steering wheel* is a revolute joint, the *pedals* are prismatic joints, and the *speedometer* is a velocity sensor. The sensor on each joint is the robot's "self-awareness tool" — it tells the controller what each limb is doing without needing to "see" the world.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Resolution (R)** — smallest measurable increment; for an N-slot encoder, **R = 360°/N**.
- **Accuracy** — closeness of measured value to true value (%).
- **Linearity** — deviation of output from ideal straight-line transfer function.
- **Repeatability** — ability to reproduce the same reading under identical conditions.
- **Bandwidth** — maximum rate of change the sensor can track (Hz).
- **Hysteresis** — difference in output for increasing vs. decreasing input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorical Breakdown of Joint Configuration Sensors

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups these sensors into **five principal families**:

1. **Optical Encoders** (Incremental and Absolute)
2. **Potentiometers** (Linear and Rotary)
3. **Resolvers** and **Synchros**
4. **Hall Effect Sensors**
5. **Magnetostrictive / Inductive Sensors** (briefly)

---

### 2.2 Optical Encoders — Operational Logic

An **optical encoder** consists of a light source (LED), a **coded disk** (glass or metal with transparent/opaque slots), and a **photodetector** array. As the disk rotates with the joint shaft, light pulses are generated.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Disk with **N slots** rotates by angle θ.
- Number of pulses counted = $N_{pulse}$.
- Angular displacement: $\theta = \dfrac{N_{pulse}}{N} \times 360°$.
- **Resolution** (smallest detectable angle): $R = \dfrac{360°}{N}$.
- **Quadrature decoding**: Two photodetectors offset by 90° produce channels A and B, allowing **direction sensing** and **4× multiplication** of resolution.

**Types:**
- **Incremental Encoder**: Outputs pulses; absolute position is *lost* on power-off (requires homing).
- **Absolute Encoder**: Uses a unique Gray-code pattern per angle; position is *retained* on power-up.

---

### 2.3 Potentiometers

A **potentiometer** is a resistive element with a sliding wiper. Output voltage is proportional to wiper position (voltage divider principle).

**Operating equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_w}{R_{total}}$$

where $R_w$ is the resistance from one end to the wiper, and $R_{total}$ is full-scale resistance.

- Resolution is **theoretically infinite** (analog device) but practically limited by wiper contact noise.
- Subject to **wear and tear**, hence unsuitable for high-cycle industrial joints.

---

### 2.4 Resolvers and Synchros

**Resolvers** are rugged electromagnetic transducers consisting of:
- A **rotor winding** (excited by AC reference, typically 400 Hz to 10 kHz).
- Two **stator windings** placed 90° apart (sine and cosine).

**Output equations:**

$$V_{sin} = V_{ref} \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$
$$V_{cos} = V_{ref} \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

The angle θ is recovered by computing:

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{sin}}{V_{cos}}\right)$$

- **Synchros** are similar but have a three-stator configuration used historically in servo systems.
- Resolvers tolerate **shock, vibration, and temperature** far better than optical encoders, making them ideal for aerospace, military, and heavy-industry robots.

---

### 2.5 Hall Effect Sensors

A **Hall sensor** detects the magnetic field perpendicular to a current-carrying conductor. When a magnet mounted on the rotating shaft passes the sensor, it produces a voltage pulse.

**Hall voltage equation:**

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $I$ is the current, $B$ is the magnetic flux density, and $t$ is the conductor thickness.

- Used in **BLDC motor commutation** and low-cost wheel encoders.
- Contactless, hence **no mechanical wear**.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Optical encoder resolution | N = number of slots |
| 2 | $\theta = \dfrac{N_{pulse}}{N} \times 360°$ | Angle from pulse count | Incremental encoder |
| 3 | $d = r \cdot \theta$ | Linear distance from wheel rotation | r = wheel radius, θ in rad |
| 4 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{V_{sin}}{V_{cos}}\right)$ | Resolver angle recovery | After demodulation |
| 5 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_w}{R_{total}}$ | Potentiometer output | Voltage divider |
| 6 | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ | Hall voltage | B in Tesla |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$ | Angular velocity (numerical) | rad/s |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$ | Pulse frequency from encoder | Hz |
| 9 | $v = r \cdot \omega$ | Linear wheel speed | m/s |
| 10 | $R_{quad} = \dfrac{90°}{N}$ | Quadrature resolution (4×) | Half of step |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Utility:** In mobile robotics, **wheel encoders** (a form of optical encoder coupled to drive wheels) are the cornerstone of **odometry** — the process of estimating the robot's pose (x, y, θ) by integrating wheel rotations. The accuracy of dead-reckoning navigation is **fundamentally limited** by encoder resolution.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial manipulators** (e.g., KUKA, ABB arms): Resolvers and absolute encoders for high-precision servo control.
- **Mobile robots (AGVs)**: Incremental encoders on drive wheels for odometry.
- **Humanoid robots** (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas): Harmonic-drive absolute encoders at every joint.
- **Drones / Quadrotors**: Hall sensors in BLDC motors for RPM feedback.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)**: Optical absolute encoders for sub-millimeter precision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Encoder Resolution from Number of Slots

**Given:** A rotary optical encoder disk has **N** transparent slots equally spaced around a full 360° circumference.

**To derive:** The angular resolution **R** of the encoder (in degrees per pulse).

**Step 1 — Define the problem:**
The total angular sweep of one full revolution is $360°$, and this is divided equally into N slots.

**Step 2 — Apply uniform distribution principle:**

$$\text{Angle per slot} = \frac{\text{Total angle}}{\text{Number of slots}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$R = \frac{360°}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Unit analysis:** degrees ÷ (dimensionless) = **degrees**. ✓

**Step 5 — Example computation:** For N = 360 slots:

$$R = \frac{360°}{360} = 1° \text{ per pulse}$$

For N = 1000 slots:

$$R = \frac{360°}{1000} = 0.36° \text{ per pulse}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wheel Odometry from Encoder Pulses

**Given:** A differential-drive mobile robot with wheel radius **r = 0.05 m**, encoder resolution **N = 500 pulses/rev**, and measured pulse count **$N_{pulse}$ = 250** during a sampling interval **Δt = 0.1 s**.

**Step 1 — Convert pulses to angle:**

$$\theta = \frac{N_{pulse}}{N} \times 2\pi = \frac{250}{500} \times 2\pi = \pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Convert angle to linear distance traveled by the wheel:**

$$d = r \cdot \theta = 0.05 \times \pi = 0.1571 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute linear velocity:**

$$v = \frac{d}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.1571}{0.1} = 1.571 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute angular velocity of the wheel:**

$$\omega = \frac{\theta}{\Delta t} = \frac{\pi}{0.1} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RPM:**

$$\text{RPM} = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 31.416 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 300 \text{ RPM}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resolver Angle Recovery (Analytical Detail)

**Given:** Resolver stator outputs after synchronous demodulation:

$$V_{sin} = 0.866 \text{ V}, \quad V_{cos} = 0.500 \text{ V}$$

**Step 1 — Recall demodulated outputs:**

$$V_{sin} = K \sin(\theta), \quad V_{cos} = K \cos(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio to eliminate K:**

$$\frac{V_{sin}}{V_{cos}} = \frac{\sin(\theta)}{\cos(\theta)} = \tan(\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{0.866}{0.500} = 1.732$$

**Step 4 — Solve for θ:**

$$\theta = \arctan(1.732) = 60°$$

**Step 5 — Verify quadrant correctness:** Since both $V_{sin}$ and $V_{cos}$ are positive, θ is in the **first quadrant (0°–90°)**. ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Property | Optical Encoder | Potentiometer | Resolver | Hall Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Digital pulses | Analog voltage | Analog (sine/cosine) | Digital pulse |
| Resolution | High (up to 26-bit) | Theoretically infinite | High (14–16 bit) | Moderate |
| Absolute position | Only absolute type | Yes | Yes | No (usually incremental) |
| Wear & tear | Low (contactless) | High (wiper contact) | None | None |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | High | Low |
| Environmental tolerance | Dust-sensitive | Humidity-sensitive | Excellent | Good |
| Bandwidth | High | Low | High | Moderate |
| Typical application | Wheel odometry, arms | Low-cost joints | Aerospace, military | BLDC motors |

---

### 3.5 Quadrature Decoding — Resolution Multiplication

**Given:** Incremental encoder with N = 1000 slots and quadrature (A, B) channels.

**Step 1 — Base resolution:**

$$R_{base} = \frac{360°}{N} = \frac{360°}{1000} = 0.36°$$

**Step 2 — With quadrature, every edge (rising and falling) of both A and B yields a count.** The 4× factor arises from the four edges (A↑, A↓, B↑, B↓) per slot cycle.

**Step 3 — Quadrature resolution:**

$$R_{quad} = \frac{R_{base}}{4} = \frac{0.36°}{4} = 0.09°$$

**Step 4 — Effective counts per revolution:**

$$N_{eff} = 4 \times N = 4000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

This is the principle used by all **modern digital quadrature counter ICs** (e.g., LS7366, AMT103).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Optical Incremental Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LED Light Source] --> B[Slotted Code Disk]
    B --> C[Photodetector A]
    B --> D[Photodetector B]
    C --> E[Signal Conditioning A]
    D --> F[Signal Conditioning B]
    E --> G[Quadrature Decoder]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Motor Shaft / Joint]
    G --> I[Digital Position Output]
    G --> J[Controller / Microprocessor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The slotted disk is mechanically coupled to the joint shaft. As the joint rotates, slots interrupt the LED light beam, producing pulses on channels A and B (90° phase-shifted). The quadrature decoder extracts position and direction.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Resolver Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Rotor Excitation AC] --> B[Resolver Rotor]
    B --> C[Stator Sine Winding]
    B --> D[Stator Cosine Winding]
    C --> E[Demodulator 1]
    D --> F[Demodulator 2]
    A --> E
    A --> F
    E --> G[ADC Input 1]
    F --> H[ADC Input 2]
    G --> I[Microprocessor]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Arctan Computation]
    J --> K[Digital Angle Output]
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mobile Robot Odometry Using Joint Encoders

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Left Wheel Encoder] --> D[Encoder Interface]
    B[Right Wheel Encoder] --> D
    C[Steering Joint Encoder] --> D
    D --> E[Microcontroller]
    E --> F[Pose Update Algorithm]
    F --> G[Robot Position x y theta]
    F --> H[Velocity v omega]
    G --> I[Navigation Stack]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Motion Controller]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block-level architecture shows how **joint configuration sensors** form the *front-end* of the robot's proprioceptive feedback loop. The encoder counts are converted into linear and angular displacements, integrated over time to yield pose estimates, and fed back for closed-loop motion control.

---

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Joint Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Select Joint Sensor] --> Q1{Need absolute position?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Harsh environment?}
    Q1 -- No --> Inc[Incremental Optical Encoder]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Res[Resolver]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{High precision required?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> AEnc[Absolute Optical Encoder]
    Q3 -- No --> Pot[Potentiometer]
    Inc --> Hall{Hall sensor feasible?}
    Hall -- Yes --> HallS[Hall Effect Sensor]
    Hall -- No --> Inc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define proprioceptive sensors and give two examples of joint configuration sensors.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Proprioceptive sensors are those that measure the **internal state** of a robot — its joint angles, wheel rotations, battery voltage, motor speed — without depending on external environmental cues. They form the basis of self-awareness in a mobile robot. (2 marks)
Two examples:
1. **Optical incremental encoder** mounted on a drive wheel for odometry. (0.5 mark)
2. **Potentiometer** on a revolute joint for angle measurement. (0.5 mark)

*(Allotted: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for classification, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between incremental and absolute encoders in 4 points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Incremental | Absolute |
|---|---|---|
| Position info on power-up | Lost (needs homing) | Retained |
| Output | Pulses | Unique coded word per angle |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Use case | Odometry, high-speed | Precision arms, safety-critical |

*(1.5 marks for ≥3 valid distinctions, 1 mark for tabular clarity, 0.5 mark for relevant examples.)*

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of an optical incremental encoder** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for its resolution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
An optical incremental encoder consists of:
- **Light source** (LED, often infrared).
- **Code disk** — a glass or metal disk with N equally-spaced radial slots.
- **Photodetectors** — typically two, placed such that their outputs are 90° out of phase (channels A and B).
- **Signal conditioning electronics** — amplifiers, Schmitt triggers.
- **Output stage** — TTL/CMOS-compatible digital pulses.

**Working (3 marks):**
As the disk rotates with the joint shaft, the slots alternately transmit and block light. The photodetector outputs a train of electrical pulses whose **frequency** is proportional to shaft speed and whose **count** is proportional to angular displacement. Channel B lagging A by 90° enables **direction detection** (clockwise vs. counter-clockwise). Quadrature decoding (counting all four edges) multiplies the base resolution by 4.

**Derivation of resolution (2 marks):**
Total angular sweep per revolution = $360°$. Number of slots = N.

$$R = \frac{360°}{N}$$

For N = 1000 slots, $R = 0.36°$ per pulse, and with 4× quadrature, $R_{quad} = 0.09°$.

---

**(b)** A **differential-drive mobile robot** has wheel radius r = 0.08 m. The left and right wheel encoders record 1200 and 1500 pulses respectively in a sampling interval of 0.2 s. Both encoders have a resolution of 500 pulses/rev. Compute the linear and angular velocities of the robot. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert pulses to wheel angles (1.5 marks):**

$$\theta_L = \frac{1200}{500} \times 2\pi = 4.8\pi \text{ rad}$$
$$\theta_R = \frac{1500}{500} \times 2\pi = 6.0\pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Compute wheel angular velocities (1.5 marks):**

$$\omega_L = \frac{4.8\pi}{0.2} = 24\pi \approx 75.40 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\omega_R = \frac{6.0\pi}{0.2} = 30\pi \approx 94.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute linear velocities at the wheels (1.5 marks):**

$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L = 0.08 \times 75.40 = 6.032 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R = 0.08 \times 94.25 = 7.540 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute robot's linear and angular velocities (2.5 marks):**

Let wheelbase (distance between wheels) = $L = 0.30$ m (assumed; students should state assumption).

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{7.540 + 6.032}{2} = 6.786 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{7.540 - 6.032}{0.30} = 5.027 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $v = 6.786$ m/s, $\omega = 5.027$ rad/s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a **circuit diagram and output equations**, explain the working of a **resolver** as a joint configuration sensor. Why is it preferred in aerospace applications? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A resolver is an electromagnetic transducer with:
- **Rotor winding** — single coil on rotating shaft, excited by AC reference $V_{ref} \sin(\omega t)$.
- **Two stator windings** — physically displaced by 90°, called the **sine** and **cosine** windings.

**Output equations (2.5 marks):**
The mutual inductance between rotor and each stator winding varies sinusoidally with shaft angle θ:

$$V_{sin}(t) = V_{ref} \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$
$$V_{cos}(t) = V_{ref} \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

After **synchronous demodulation** (multiplying by $\sin(\omega t)$ and low-pass filtering):

$$V_{sin}^{DC} = K \sin(\theta), \quad V_{cos}^{DC} = K \cos(\theta)$$

The angle is recovered by:

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{sin}^{DC}}{V_{cos}^{DC}}\right)$$

**Why preferred in aerospace (2.5 marks):**
- **No optical components** — immune to dust, oil, vibration.
- **Wide temperature range** (–55°C to +155°C typical).
- **Shock and vibration tolerant** — used in jet engine actuators, missile fin control.
- **Long operational life** — fully contactless electromagnetic coupling.
- **High accuracy** (5–10 arc-minutes) suitable for flight control surfaces.

---

**(b)** A robot joint uses a **rotary potentiometer** with total resistance 10 kΩ, supplied with $V_{ref}$ = 5 V. The wiper is at 35% of the total resistance. Find the output voltage and the equivalent angular position if the potentiometer spans 270°. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute wiper resistance (1.5 marks):**

$$R_w = 0.35 \times R_{total} = 0.35 \times 10{,}000 = 3{,}500 \;\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Apply voltage divider (2 marks):**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_w}{R_{total}} = 5 \times \frac{3500}{10{,}000} = 1.75 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to angular position (2.5 marks):**

Since the pot spans 270°, the angle per unit resistance is:

$$\frac{270°}{10{,}000} = 0.027°/\Omega$$

For 3500 Ω:

$$\theta = 0.027 \times 3500 = 94.5°$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$\theta = 0.35 \times 270° = 94.5° \;\; ✓$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{out} = 1.75$ V; $\theta = 94.5°$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the radian-degree conversion** when computing $\theta$ from pulses: $N_{pulses}/N$ gives *fractions of a revolution*; multiply by $2\pi$ for **radians** or by $360°$ for **degrees** depending on the question's requirement.
> 2. **Mixing up the angle convention** in differential-drive kinematics: $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$ — if the sign convention is reversed, marks are deducted.
> 3. **Omitting assumptions** (e.g., wheelbase L, no slip) — always state the assumed value of L in odometry problems.
> 4. **Confusing the two resolver windings** — sine and cosine are offset by 90° in *space*, not in *time*. The AC excitation is the *carrier*; the angular info is in the *envelope*.
> 5. **Wrong quadrature factor** — students often write $2\times$ instead of $4\times$ for quadrature resolution. KTU examiners specifically look for the four-edge (A↑, A↓, B↑, B↓) justification.
> 6. **In Part A questions**, students write essays instead of crisp points. KTU awards 3 marks only for **structured** answers with diagrams and bullet points.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Joint configuration sensors** are *proprioceptive* — they sense the robot's *internal* state.
- Five major families: **Optical encoders, Potentiometers, Resolvers, Hall sensors, Magnetostrictive sensors**.
- **Optical encoders** dominate mobile robotics due to high resolution, low cost, and digital output.
- **Resolution formula**: $R = 360°/N$ (or $2\pi/N$ in radians); **quadrupled** in quadrature mode.
- **Resolver** is the gold standard for harsh environments — uses sine/cosine demodulation and arctangent recovery.
- **Potentiometer** is the simplest, cheapest, but suffers from mechanical wear; suitable for low-cycle applications.
- **Hall sensors** are contactless and use magnetic fields; used in BLDC motor commutation.
- **Odometry** (the most important KTU exam application): $d = r\theta$, $v = r\omega$, pose update via integration.
- **Incremental** encoders lose position on power-off; **Absolute** encoders retain it (use Gray code).
- **Quadrature decoding** provides 4× resolution enhancement and **direction sensing**.
- For **differential-drive robots**: $v = (v_R + v_L)/2$ and $\omega = (v_R - v_L)/L$.
- **Resolver equation**: $\theta = \arctan(V_{sin}/V_{cos})$ after synchronous demodulation.
- **Hall voltage**: $V_H = R_H \cdot I \cdot B / t$.
- **Always state assumptions** in numerical answers — KTU examiners reward clarity.
- **Sensor selection** depends on: precision, environment, cost, bandwidth, and whether absolute position is needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain any one joint configuration sensor" in 7 marks, always include: (1) labelled diagram (2 marks), (2) working principle (2 marks), (3) one key equation (1 mark), (4) one real-world application (1 mark), (5) one advantage/limitation (1 mark). This is the **standard KTU valuation key pattern** for 7-mark sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors that Measure Ego-Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors that Measure Ego-Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Ego-Motion

In mobile robotics, **Ego-Motion** refers to the estimation of a robot's own motion (its position, velocity, orientation, and acceleration) relative to a global or local reference frame, using sensors that are physically mounted on the robot itself. These sensors are classified under the broader category of **proprioceptive sensors** — sensors that measure internal states of the system rather than external environmental features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standardized Terminology):**
> *Ego-motion is the process of determining a mobile robot's self-motion parameters — linear velocity, angular velocity, heading, and traversed distance — by measuring internal mechanical, rotational, inertial, or magnetic quantities without relying on external landmarks.*

The principal proprioceptive sensors used to measure ego-motion are:

| Sensor Class | Measured Quantity | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheel Encoders (Optical/Magnetic)** | Wheel rotation (revolutions / ticks) | Distance travelled, linear velocity |
| **Gyroscope** | Angular velocity about an axis | Heading change (yaw rate) |
| **Accelerometer** | Specific force (proper acceleration) | Linear acceleration → velocity, displacement |
| **Magnetometer (Digital Compass)** | Earth's magnetic field vector | Absolute heading (yaw angle) |
| **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)** | Combined angular rate + linear acceleration | Full 6-DoF ego-motion state |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ego"?**
> The Latin word *ego* ("I") is used because the sensor perceives the *robot's own* motion, not the motion of objects in the world. This is why cameras and LIDAR are **exteroceptive** (they sense the world), while encoders and gyros are **proprioceptive** (they sense the self).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to walk 10 metres straight ahead and then turn 90° right. You cannot see the room, so you must rely on your body's *internal* senses:

- **Your feet** count each step (like a **wheel encoder** counting ticks).
- **The fluid in your inner ear (vestibular system)** tilts when you turn (like a **gyroscope** measuring angular velocity).
- **The tiny hairs in your semicircular canals** sense acceleration (like an **accelerometer**).
- **Your skin and muscle memory** give rough distance estimates (like **odometry drift**).

These internal senses combine to give you a fuzzy but real-time sense of where you are — this is exactly what ego-motion sensors do for a robot. However, just like you would slowly drift off-course without vision, ego-motion estimates accumulate error over time — a phenomenon called **drift**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Ego-motion sensors (proprioceptive):** Encoders, IMU, gyroscopes, accelerometers, compass.
> - **Exteroceptive sensors:** Cameras, LIDAR, SONAR, GPS (sometimes).

---

### 1.3 Coordinate Frames Used in Ego-Motion

A solid grasp of reference frames is essential before any derivation.

$$
\mathcal{F}_W : \{x_W, y_W, z_W\} \quad \text{(World / Global / Inertial frame)}
$$

$$
\mathcal{F}_B : \{x_B, y_B, z_B\} \quad \text{(Body frame, fixed to robot)}
$$

$$
x_B \rightarrow \text{forward (heading)}, \quad y_B \rightarrow \text{left side}, \quad z_B \rightarrow \text{upward}
$$

The **transformation** from body-frame velocity to world-frame velocity is given by the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$ where $\theta$ is the heading angle:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x}_W \\ \dot{y}_W \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_B \\ \omega_B \end{bmatrix}
$$

Here, $v_B$ is the body-frame linear velocity and $\omega_B$ is the body-frame angular velocity — both of which are **directly produced by ego-motion sensors**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wheel Encoder Odometry

#### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

A **wheel encoder** is a rotary sensor (typically optical or magnetic) mounted on the motor shaft or wheel hub. It produces $N$ pulses per revolution (PPR). Each pulse corresponds to a measurable angular increment:

$$
\Delta\phi_{\text{tick}} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \text{ [radians per tick]}
$$

The linear distance travelled by the wheel for $k$ accumulated ticks is:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{2\pi k r}{N}
$$

where $r$ is the wheel radius.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why encoders are the workhorse of mobile robotics:**
> They are cheap (a few hundred rupees), extremely high resolution (up to 10000 PPR), have very low latency, and require no external infrastructure. Almost every KTU lab differential-drive robot uses them.

---

#### 2.1.2 Differential-Drive Odometry Model

For a robot with two independently driven wheels of radius $r$, separated by axle length $L$, with encoder tick counts $\Delta N_L$ and $\Delta N_R$:

**Step 1 — Compute individual wheel displacements:**
$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi r \cdot \Delta N_L}{N}, \qquad \Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi r \cdot \Delta N_R}{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute robot's linear and angular displacements:**
$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}, \qquad \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Update global pose** $(x, y, \theta)$:
$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})
$$

$$
y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})
$$

$$
\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The midpoint orientation $\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}$ is used (instead of $\theta_k$) to reduce integration error — this is the **Euler midpoint / improved Euler method** used universally in KTU-validated odometry.

---

### 2.2 Gyroscope — Measuring Angular Velocity

#### 2.2.1 Working Principle

A gyroscope measures angular velocity $\omega$ about its sensitive axis. There are three principal families:

1. **Mechanical / Spinning-mass gyros** — based on conservation of angular momentum (classical, large, used in aircraft).
2. **Optical gyros (Ring Laser Gyro / Fibre Optic Gyro)** — based on Sagnac effect; extremely precise; used in ships, submarines, aircraft.
3. **MEMS gyros** — micro-electro-mechanical vibrating structures; cheap, small, used in smartphones and robots.

#### 2.2.2 Gyroscope Model

The measured angular rate $y_{\text{gyro}}$ is modelled as:

$$
y_{\text{gyro}}(t) = \omega(t) + b_g + n_g(t)
$$

where:

- $\omega(t)$ = true angular rate
- $b_g$ = bias (constant offset in rad/s)
- $n_g(t)$ = white Gaussian noise (zero mean)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Gyro Bias:**
> Even when the robot is *stationary*, a MEMS gyroscope outputs a non-zero value (typically $0.5°/\text{s}$ to $5°/\text{s}$). Over time, integrating this bias causes unbounded heading drift. This is the **#1 ego-motion error source** and a guaranteed KTU exam question.

---

### 2.3 Accelerometer — Measuring Linear Acceleration

#### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

An accelerometer measures the **specific force** $a_{\text{specific}}$ acting on a proof mass, which equals:

$$
a_{\text{specific}} = a_{\text{true}} - g
$$

where $g$ is the gravitational acceleration vector ($\approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A perfectly stationary accelerometer lying flat outputs $+g$ along its vertical axis (it is measuring the *reaction* to gravity). This is why accelerometers alone cannot separate motion from tilt without a complementary filter.

#### 2.3.2 Accelerometer Model

$$
y_{\text{acc}}(t) = a(t) + b_a + n_a(t)
$$

where $b_a$ is bias, $n_a$ is measurement noise.

To extract velocity and displacement (called **inertial navigation**):

$$
v(t) = v(0) + \int_{0}^{t} y_{\text{acc}}(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

$$
s(t) = s(0) + \int_{0}^{t} v(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

---

### 2.4 Magnetometer (Digital Compass)

A magnetometer measures the Earth's magnetic field vector $\vec{B}_E$. With the magnetic inclination/declination known, the heading angle $\psi$ is:

$$
\psi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{-B_y}{B_x}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Magnetometers provide **absolute** heading — unlike gyroscopes which are **relative**. This makes them invaluable for correcting gyro drift in real-world systems (e.g., through a Kalman filter).

---

### 2.5 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

An **IMU** is a chip-level integration of (typically) a 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer (± sometimes a 3-axis magnetometer, in which case it is called a **MARG** or **9-DoF IMU**).

$$
\text{IMU output at time } t = \left\{ \omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z, a_x, a_y, a_z \right\}
$$

The **strapdown inertial navigation equations** propagate pose, velocity, and position:

$$
\dot{q} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, q \otimes \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\dot{v}^W = R(q)\, a_{\text{specific}} + g^W
$$

$$
\dot{p}^W = v^W
$$

where $q$ is the orientation quaternion, $R(q)$ the rotation matrix, and $g^W$ the gravity vector in world frame.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Equation | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel linear displacement | $\Delta s = \dfrac{2\pi r k}{N}$ | metres |
| 2 | Encoder angular increment | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | rad/tick |
| 3 | Differential-drive linear disp. | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$ | m |
| 4 | Differential-drive angular disp. | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}$ | rad |
| 5 | Pose update (midpoint Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$ | m |
| 6 | Pose update | $y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin(\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2})$ | m |
| 7 | Heading update | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta$ | rad |
| 8 | Gyro model | $y_{\text{gyro}} = \omega + b_g + n_g$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Accelerometer model | $y_{\text{acc}} = a + b_a + n_a$ | m/s² |
| 10 | Velocity by integration | $v(t) = v(0) + \int y_{\text{acc}}\, dt$ | m/s |
| 11 | Heading from magnetometer | $\psi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{-B_y}{B_x}\right)$ | rad |
| 12 | Quaternion kinematics | $\dot{q} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, q \otimes [0,\omega]^T$ | rad/s |
| 13 | World velocity update | $\dot{v}^W = R(q)\,a + g^W$ | m/s² |
| 14 | World position update | $\dot{p}^W = v^W$ | m/s |
| 15 | Body-to-world rotation (2D) | $\begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Wheel odometry** powers warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva), AGVs in factories, and differential-drive research platforms (TurtleBot).
> - **IMU + GPS fusion** drives autonomous cars (Apollo, Waymo).
> - **Optical gyros** guide commercial airliners and submarines.
> - **MEMS IMUs** stabilize drones and image gimbals (DJI).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Differential-Drive Odometry Update

**Given:** A differential-drive robot with wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, encoder resolution $N = 500$ pulses/rev, axle length $L = 0.30$ m. In one sampling interval, left wheel gives $\Delta N_L = 220$ ticks and right wheel gives $\Delta N_R = 280$ ticks. The robot starts at pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$.

**Find:** New pose $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1)$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Convert ticks to wheel arc lengths:**

$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi r \Delta N_L}{N}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 220}{500}
$$

$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{0.069115}{1} = 0.06911 \text{ m}
$$

Similarly:

$$
\Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 280}{500}
$$

$$
\Delta s_R = 0.08796 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute robot's linear and angular displacements:**

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2} = \frac{0.08796 + 0.06911}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta s = 0.07853 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L} = \frac{0.08796 - 0.06911}{0.30}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{0.01885}{0.30} = 0.06283 \text{ rad} \approx 3.6^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Compute midpoint orientation:**

$$
\theta_{\text{mid}} = \theta_0 + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = 0 + \frac{0.06283}{2} = 0.03142 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Update global pose:**

$$
x_1 = x_0 + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{\text{mid}})
$$

$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.07853 \times \cos(0.03142)
$$

$$
x_1 = 0.07853 \times 0.99951 = 0.07849 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_1 = y_0 + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{\text{mid}})
$$

$$
y_1 = 0 + 0.07853 \times \sin(0.03142)
$$

$$
y_1 = 0.07853 \times 0.03141 = 0.002467 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \Delta\theta = 0 + 0.06283 = 0.06283 \text{ rad}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{(x_1,\, y_1,\, \theta_1) = (0.0785 \text{ m},\ 0.00247 \text{ m},\ 0.0628 \text{ rad})}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Strapdown Inertial Heading Integration

**Given:** A 1-axis gyroscope produces $N=200$ measurements over a $10$ s interval. Sample values are stored in array $y[k]$ (rad/s). Bias $b_g = 0.01$ rad/s.

**Find:** Total heading change $\theta(10)$ after bias correction.

**Derivation:**

The discrete-time heading update with bias correction is:

$$
\theta[k] = \theta[k-1] + (y[k] - b_g)\,\Delta t
$$

where $\Delta t = 10/200 = 0.05$ s.

**Worked Example with 5 sample values:** Suppose $y = [0.55, 0.48, 0.60, 0.52, 0.58]$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Subtract bias from each sample:**

$$
\tilde{y}[1] = 0.55 - 0.01 = 0.54 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\tilde{y}[2] = 0.48 - 0.01 = 0.47 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\tilde{y}[3] = 0.60 - 0.01 = 0.59 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\tilde{y}[4] = 0.52 - 0.01 = 0.51 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\tilde{y}[5] = 0.58 - 0.01 = 0.57 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all samples and multiply by $\Delta t$:**

$$
\theta(10) = \Delta t \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{N} \tilde{y}[k]
$$

$$
\theta(10) = 0.05 \times (0.54 + 0.47 + 0.59 + 0.51 + 0.57)
$$

$$
\theta(10) = 0.05 \times 2.68
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta(10) = 0.134 \text{ rad} \approx 7.68^\circ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that even with a "small" bias of 0.01 rad/s, after just 10 seconds the bias contribution alone would be $0.01 \times 10 = 0.1$ rad $\approx 5.7°$ — nearly as large as the actual signal. This single fact is why **inertial-only navigation drifts**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Error Propagation in Wheel Odometry

**Given:** Encoder resolution uncertainty $\sigma_N = 1$ tick, wheel radius uncertainty $\sigma_r = 0.001$ m.

**Find:** Uncertainty in measured distance per single tick.

**Derivation:**

The distance per tick is:

$$
s = \frac{2\pi r}{N}
$$

Using first-order error propagation (linearisation of $s = f(r, N)$):

$$
\sigma_s^2 \approx \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial r}\right)^2 \sigma_r^2 + \left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial N}\right)^2 \sigma_N^2
$$

Compute partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial s}{\partial r} = \frac{2\pi}{N}, \qquad \frac{\partial s}{\partial N} = -\frac{2\pi r}{N^2}
$$

For $N = 500$, $r = 0.05$ m, $\sigma_N = 1$, $\sigma_r = 0.001$:

$$
\frac{\partial s}{\partial r} = \frac{2\pi}{500} = 0.01257 \text{ m/m}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial s}{\partial N} = -\frac{2\pi \times 0.05}{500^2} = -1.257 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/tick}
$$

Plug in:

$$
\sigma_s^2 = (0.01257)^2 (0.001)^2 + (-1.257\times 10^{-6})^2 (1)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_s^2 = 1.580\times 10^{-10} + 1.580\times 10^{-12}
$$

$$
\sigma_s \approx 1.26 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/tick} = 0.0126 \text{ mm/tick}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each encoder tick is precise to ~0.01 mm, but with millions of ticks per kilometre, even this tiny error compounds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Ego-Motion Sensor Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph phys[Physical Robot Platform]
        wheelL[Left Wheel Rotation]
        wheelR[Right Wheel Rotation]
        bodyIMU[3-Axis IMU Module]
        mag[3-Axis Magnetometer]
    end

    subgraph enc[Encoder Signal Conditioning]
        encL[Optical Encoder L - 500 PPR]
        encR[Optical Encoder R - 500 PPR]
        qdr[Quadrature Decoder]
    end

    subgraph imuProc[IMU Signal Processing]
        bias[Subtract Bias b]
        tempComp[Temperature Compensation]
        cal[Allan Variance Calibration]
    end

    subgraph fusion[Sensor Fusion Layer]
        odom[Differential Drive Odometry]
        gyroInt[Gyro Integration - Heading]
        magCorr[Magnetometer Heading Correction]
        kalman[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    end

    subgraph out[Ego Motion Output State]
        pos[World Position x y]
        vel[Linear Velocity v]
        head[Heading Theta]
        ori[Orientation Quaternion q]
    end

    wheelL --> encL --> qdr
    wheelR --> encR --> qdr
    bodyIMU --> bias --> tempComp --> cal
    mag --> magCorr

    qdr --> odom
    cal --> gyroInt
    magCorr --> kalman
    odom --> kalman
    gyroInt --> kalman

    kalman --> pos
    kalman --> vel
    kalman --> head
    kalman --> ori
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Raw wheel ticks and raw IMU data pass through low-level conditioning (debiasing, temperature compensation, calibration), then enter the **sensor fusion layer**. The fusion block is typically an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) that optimally combines wheel odometry (short-term accurate) with IMU (smooth orientation) and magnetometer (absolute heading). The output is the full ego-motion state.

---

### 4.2 IMU Strapdown Navigation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Gyro Output omega plus bias bg]) --> intg1[Integrate omega to obtain attitude q]
    attOut([Attitude q and Rotation R q]) --> rot[Rotate accelerometer specific force into world frame]
    start2([Accelerometer Output a minus g]) --> rot
    rot --> nav1[Subtract gravity to obtain true acceleration a true]
    nav1 --> intg2[Integrate to obtain world velocity v world]
    intg2 --> outV([World Velocity v])
    intg2 --> intg3[Integrate velocity to obtain world position p]
    intg3 --> outP([World Position p])
    intg1 --> outQ([Orientation q])
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the canonical **strapdown inertial navigation algorithm** used in every aircraft INS, ship gyrocompass system, and smartphone AR/VR pipeline. KTU examiners frequently ask students to label or trace this exact flow.

---

### 4.3 Error Drift Mechanism (Why Ego-Motion Drifts)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[True Angular Rate omega] -->|+ bias b g| b[Measured Rate]
    b -->|integrate over time| c[Heading Theta t]
    c -->|error grows linearly with t| d[Unbounded Heading Drift]
    a2[True Acceleration a] -->|+ bias b a| b2[Measured Acc]
    b2 -->|double integration| c2[Position Estimate]
    c2 -->|error grows as t squared| d2[Quadratic Position Error]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:**
> "Why does accelerometer error grow as $t^2$ while gyroscope error grows as $t$?"
> **Answer:** Gyro measures angular velocity $\omega$ — one integration gives angle. A bias $b_g$ integrated for time $t$ gives heading error $b_g \cdot t$ (linear growth). Accelerometer measures acceleration $a$ — double integration gives position. A bias $b_a$ double-integrated gives position error $\tfrac{1}{2} b_a t^2$ (quadratic growth — much worse!).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define ego-motion. List any four proprioceptive sensors used in mobile robots to measure ego-motion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Ego-motion is the estimation of a mobile robot's own motion parameters (linear velocity, angular velocity, heading, position) relative to a reference frame, using sensors mounted on the robot.

**Sensors (2 marks — ½ mark each, any 4):**
1. Wheel encoders (optical / magnetic)
2. Gyroscope (mechanical, optical, or MEMS)
3. Accelerometer
4. Magnetometer (digital compass)
5. IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain why an accelerometer alone cannot provide accurate position information over long durations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An accelerometer measures specific force $a_{\text{specific}} = a_{\text{true}} - g$. To obtain position, two successive integrations of the measured acceleration must be performed:

$$
v(t) = \int a\, dt, \qquad p(t) = \int v\, dt
$$

Any bias error $b_a$ in the accelerometer integrates twice, producing a position error proportional to $t^2$. Furthermore, the accelerometer cannot distinguish between gravity and true linear acceleration in the tilt direction, leading to unbounded drift. Hence, accelerometers must be combined with gyroscopes (or other heading sensors) for usable position estimates.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the differential-drive odometry update equations. Clearly state all symbols and explain each step of the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential-drive robot has wheel radius $r = 0.08$ m, encoder resolution $N = 1000$ pulses/rev, axle length $L = 0.40$ m. In one interval, left encoder gives $\Delta N_L = 150$ ticks and right encoder gives $\Delta N_R = 250$ ticks. Starting pose $(0, 0, 0)$, compute the new pose using the midpoint Euler method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define encoder-to-distance conversion.
A wheel encoder produces $N$ pulses per full revolution. Each tick corresponds to angular increment $\Delta\phi = 2\pi/N$. For $k$ accumulated ticks, the wheel arc length is:

$$
\Delta s = r \cdot k \cdot \Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi r k}{N}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply to left and right wheels.

$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi r \Delta N_L}{N}, \qquad \Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi r \Delta N_R}{N}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute robot motion in body frame. The average of left and right displacements gives linear travel; their difference divided by axle length gives rotation:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}, \qquad \Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Project into world frame using midpoint heading $\theta_{\text{mid}} = \theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}$:

$$
x_{k+1} = x_k + \Delta s \cos(\theta_{\text{mid}}), \quad y_{k+1} = y_k + \Delta s \sin(\theta_{\text{mid}}), \quad \theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \Delta\theta
$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute arc lengths.

$$
\Delta s_L = \frac{2\pi \times 0.08 \times 150}{1000} = 0.07540 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta s_R = \frac{2\pi \times 0.08 \times 250}{1000} = 0.12566 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute robot motion.

$$
\Delta s = \frac{0.12566 + 0.07540}{2} = 0.10053 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{0.12566 - 0.07540}{0.40} = 0.12566 \text{ rad} \; (\approx 7.2^\circ)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Midpoint heading.

$$
\theta_{\text{mid}} = 0 + \frac{0.12566}{2} = 0.06283 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Pose update.

$$
x_1 = 0 + 0.10053 \cos(0.06283) = 0.10053 \times 0.99803 = 0.10033 \text{ m}
$$

$$
y_1 = 0 + 0.10053 \sin(0.06283) = 0.10053 \times 0.06279 = 0.006313 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Heading update.

$$
\theta_1 = 0 + 0.12566 = 0.12566 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final boxed answer.

$$
\boxed{(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) = (0.1003 \text{ m},\ 0.00631 \text{ m},\ 0.1257 \text{ rad})}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the strapdown inertial navigation system (INS) algorithm. State the kinematic equations that propagate position, velocity, and orientation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEMS gyroscope has a constant bias $b_g = 0.02$ rad/s. Estimate the heading error after (i) 10 s and (ii) 100 s of integration. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define strapdown INS — a system in which inertial sensors (gyros + accelerometers) are rigidly mounted to the vehicle body (as opposed to a gimbaled platform).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Block diagram — see §4.2 of these notes. Provide the verbal trace: gyro → attitude update → rotate accelerometer → subtract gravity → integrate → velocity → integrate → position.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Kinematic equations.

Orientation update (quaternion form):

$$
\dot{q} = \frac{1}{2}\, q \otimes \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}
$$

Velocity update:

$$
\dot{v}^W = R(q)\, a_{\text{specific}} + g^W
$$

Position update:

$$
\dot{p}^W = v^W
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Note that $R(q)$ is the rotation matrix derived from quaternion $q$, $g^W \approx [0, 0, -9.81]^T$ m/s² in NED frame.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Heading from gyro integration.

$$
\theta(t) = \int_{0}^{t} (\omega_{\text{true}} + b_g)\, d\tau = \omega_{\text{true}} t + b_g t
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Heading error due to bias.

$$
\Delta\theta(t) = b_g \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Plug in numbers.

For $t = 10$ s:

$$
\Delta\theta(10) = 0.02 \times 10 = 0.2 \text{ rad} \approx 11.46^\circ
$$

For $t = 100$ s:

$$
\Delta\theta(100) = 0.02 \times 100 = 2.0 \text{ rad} \approx 114.6^\circ
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Comment.

$$
\boxed{\text{Heading error grows linearly with time. A bias of just 0.02 rad/s causes 11.5° error in 10 s and over 114° in 100 s.}}
$$

This proves that inertial-only navigation is unsuitable for long-duration operation without external aiding (e.g., GPS, magnetometer, or visual odometry).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the midpoint term** $\theta_k + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}$ — costs ~2 marks. Examiners specifically test whether you used the **improved Euler** (midpoint) or naive Euler.
> 2. **Mixing up units** (ticks vs. revolutions, mm vs. m, deg vs. rad) — common ½-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing wheel *radius* $r$ with wheel *diameter* $d$** — formula uses radius.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** (or vice-versa) in final answer.
> 5. **Stating that accelerometers measure gravity zero when stationary** — they actually measure $+g$ (the specific force reaction).
> 6. **Skipping the bias-correction step** in gyro integration problems — typically 1 full mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ego-Motion Sensors**

- ✅ **Ego-motion** = self-motion estimation; **proprioceptive** sensors only.
- ✅ **Wheel encoder formula:** $\Delta s = \dfrac{2\pi r k}{N}$ — the single most-tested formula.
- ✅ **Differential-drive equations:** $\Delta s = \tfrac{\Delta s_R + \Delta s_L}{2}$, $\Delta\theta = \tfrac{\Delta s_R - \Delta s_L}{L}$.
- ✅ **Midpoint (improved) Euler** is the KTU-accepted integration method for odometry.
- ✅ **Gyro model:** $y = \omega + b_g + n_g$ — bias is the dominant error.
- ✅ **Accelerometer model:** $y = a + b_a + n_a$ — double integration amplifies bias quadratically.
- ✅ **Specific force** = true acceleration minus gravity — accelerators sense this, not pure $a$.
- ✅ **Magnetometer** gives absolute heading; can correct gyro drift.
- ✅ **IMU** = 3-axis gyro + 3-axis accel (± magnetometer → 9-DoF).
- ✅ **Strapdown INS** kinematic equations: quaternion update + world velocity + world position.
- ✅ **Gyro error grows as $t$**, **accelerometer error grows as $t^2$** — high-yield KTU comparison.
- ✅ **Drift** is inherent to all proprioceptive sensors; external aiding (GPS, vision, LIDAR) is needed for long-term accuracy.
- ✅ Real-world systems: warehouse AGVs (encoders), drones (IMU), autonomous cars (IMU+GPS), submarines (FOG).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors to Measure Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors to Measure Force

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Force Sensor** (or force transducer) is a transducer that converts an input mechanical force — including tension, compression, pressure, torque, shear, or contact load — into a measurable, quantifiable electrical output signal (voltage, current, frequency, or resistance change). In the context of **mobile robotics**, force sensors form the **proprioceptive-exteroceptive bridge** that allows a robot to perceive, regulate, and react to physical interactions with its environment, its payload, and its own body.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, force sensors belong to the broader class of **Physical Contact Transducers (PCTs)** and are sub-classified as:
- **Direct Force Sensors** — measure force magnitude directly (e.g., load cells, piezoelectric sensors).
- **Indirect Force Sensors** — infer force from a measurable deformation (e.g., strain gauge on a deflecting beam).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Force sensors are explicitly listed under *Sensors used in Mobile Robots* alongside position, velocity, and proximity sensors. Examiners expect students to know **principle of operation, governing equation, and one application per sensor type.**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are pressing your thumb against a digital kitchen weighing scale. The scale does **not** directly read "force" — it reads the **tiny deformation** of a metallic plate underneath. The plate has tiny etched patterns (a **strain gauge**) that change their electrical resistance when bent. The electronics convert that tiny resistance change into a kilogram reading.

> **In a mobile robot, the same principle is replicated hundreds of times**:
> - The robot's **wheels** use force sensors to detect whether they are slipping on the ground.
> - The **gripper** uses force sensors to know when it has grasped an egg without crushing it.
> - The **bumper** uses force sensors to detect collisions.
> - The **joint motor** uses a torque sensor to regulate how hard the arm is pushing.

**The intuition is simple:** *Force cannot be measured directly because it is invisible. We measure its effects — strain, displacement, or charge generation — and back-calculate the force using physics.*

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical quantities and constants are **mandatory for KTU answers**:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force | $F$ | **Newton (N)** | SI base derived unit |
| Pressure | $P$ | **Pascal (Pa)** = N/m² | Often used in fluid contact |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | **Dimensionless (m/m)** | Always unitless |
| Stress | $\sigma$ | **Pa or N/m²** | Internal resisting force/area |
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **GPa (typical for metals ~ 200 GPa)** | Material property |
| Gauge Factor | $GF$ | **Dimensionless** (typically 2.0 for constantan) | Strain gauge sensitivity |
| Piezoelectric Constant | $d_{33}$ | **C/N or pC/N** | Material property |
| Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | For weight calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** $F = m \cdot a$ (dynamic, Newton's 2nd law) versus $W = m \cdot g$ (static weight). Examiners frequently test this difference.

---

## 1.4 Major Classification of Force Sensors

Force sensors used in mobile robotics can be taxonomized as follows:

1. **Strain-Gauge Based Force Sensors** (most common — load cells, tactile sensors)
2. **Piezoelectric Force Sensors** (high-frequency, dynamic force measurement)
3. **Capacitive Force Sensors** (used in MEMS, electronic skin)
4. **Optical Force Sensors** (fiber Bragg gratings, used in harsh environments)
5. **Piezoresistive Force Sensors** (semiconductor-based, high sensitivity)
6. **Magnetic / Hall-Effect Force Sensors** (non-contact, used in joints)

Each of these will be analyzed in the next section under the **operational principle** and **governing formula**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Strain-Gauge Based Force Sensor

**Principle (Why it works):** A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil arranged in a **zig-zag (serpentine) pattern** bonded to a deformable substrate. When the substrate deforms under load, the foil stretches or compresses, changing its electrical resistance. Since $R = \rho L / A$, any change in length $L$ or cross-section $A$ alters $R$.

**How it measures force:**
1. An applied force $F$ creates internal stress $\sigma$ in a structural member.
2. Stress produces strain $\varepsilon$ in the material.
3. Strain deforms the bonded gauge, changing its resistance by $\Delta R$.
4. A **Wheatstone bridge** converts the tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable voltage $V_{out}$.

**The Critical Chain:** $F \rightarrow \sigma \rightarrow \varepsilon \rightarrow \Delta R / R \rightarrow V_{out}$

> [!NOTE]
> The serpentine pattern is used because it **maximizes the effective length $L$** along the strain axis, amplifying $\Delta R$ for a given small strain (often $\varepsilon \sim 10^{-6}$).

---

### 2.1.2 Piezoelectric Force Sensor

**Principle (Why it works):** Certain crystals (Quartz, PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate, PVDF polymer) generate an electrical charge $q$ on their surfaces when mechanically stressed. This is the **direct piezoelectric effect**, discovered by the Curie brothers in 1880.

**How it measures force:**
1. An applied force $F$ produces stress $\sigma$ in the crystal.
2. Stress displaces the dipole structure, generating surface charge $q = d \cdot F$.
3. The charge is collected by electrodes and converted to voltage by a charge amplifier.
4. The voltage is proportional to the applied force.

**Critical Limitation:** Piezoelectric sensors **cannot measure static (DC) force** because the generated charge slowly leaks away. They excel at **dynamic, high-frequency** force measurement (e.g., impact, vibration).

---

### 2.1.3 Capacitive Force Sensor

**Principle (Why it works):** A parallel-plate capacitor has $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. When force deforms the structure, the gap $d$ changes, altering $C$. Measuring $C$ reveals the force.

**Why it is used in robotics:** Highly suited for **MEMS and electronic skin (e-skin)** applications because of low power consumption, high sensitivity, and CMOS compatibility.

---

### 2.1.4 Optical Force Sensor (Fiber Bragg Grating — FBG)

**Principle (Why it works):** An FBG reflects a specific wavelength $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$, where $\Lambda$ is the grating period. When force deforms the fiber, $\Lambda$ changes, shifting $\lambda_B$. Measuring the wavelength shift reveals the strain and hence the force.

**Why it is used in robotics:** Immune to **electromagnetic interference (EMI)**, safe in explosive/medical environments, and small in size.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for force-sensor problems.

| # | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Application / Sensor Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F / A$ | $\sigma$ = stress [Pa], $F$ = force [N], $A$ = area [m²] | All force sensors (basic definition) |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ (Hooke's Law) | $\varepsilon$ = strain [–], $E$ = Young's modulus [Pa] | Strain gauge derivation |
| 3 | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | $GF$ = gauge factor [–] (≈ 2.0 for constantan) | Strain gauge |
| 4 | $V_{out} = \left( \Delta R / R \right) \cdot \left( V_{ex} / 4 \right)$ | $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage [V] | Wheatstone bridge output |
| 5 | $q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | $d_{33}$ = piezoelectric coeff. [C/N], $q$ = charge [C] | Piezoelectric sensor |
| 6 | $V = q / C_f$ | $C_f$ = feedback capacitance [F] | Charge amplifier output |
| 7 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $d$ = gap [m] | Capacitive sensor |
| 8 | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$ | $\lambda_B$ = Bragg wavelength [m], $\Lambda$ = grating period [m] | Fiber Bragg Grating |
| 9 | $\delta L = F L^3 / (3 E I)$ (cantilever tip) | $I$ = 2nd moment of area [m⁴] | Cantilever beam force |
| 10 | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | $\tau$ = torque [N·m], $r$ = lever arm [m] | Torque sensor (joint) |
| 11 | $W = m \cdot g$ | $W$ = weight [N], $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Weight = static force |
| 12 | $F = m \cdot a$ | $a$ = acceleration [m/s²] | Dynamic force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Equation 3 ($\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$) and Equation 4 (Wheatstone bridge output) are the **two most frequently tested** formulas. Memorize them along with Equation 1 and Equation 2.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor Type | Real-World Robotic Application |
|---|---|
| Strain Gauge Load Cell | Wheel payload monitoring in AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) |
| Piezoelectric Sensor | Collision/impact detection on mobile robot bumpers |
| Capacitive Tactile Sensor | Dexterous robotic hand gripper (e-skin) |
| FBG Sensor | Force feedback in surgical / underwater robots |
| Piezoresistive Sensor | Low-cost humanoid robot foot pressure mapping |
| Hall-Effect Force Sensor | Non-contact torque sensing in robot joints |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Strain-Gauge Output on a Cantilever Beam Force Sensor

**Problem Setup (KTU Standard):** A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$ is fixed at one end. A strain gauge is bonded on the **top surface** near the fixed end. A force $F$ is applied at the free end (perpendicular to the beam). Derive an expression for the Wheatstone bridge output voltage $V_{out}$ in terms of $F$.

---

### Step 1: Establish the Bending Moment

The bending moment at the fixed end (where the gauge is located) due to force $F$ at the free end is:

$$
M = F \cdot L
$$

This is the **maximum bending moment** along the beam and is the reason we place the gauge at the fixed end — maximum sensitivity.

---

### Step 2: Compute the Bending Stress

For a rectangular cross-section, the bending stress at the outer fiber (top or bottom surface) is given by the **flexure formula**:

$$
\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}
$$

where $c = t / 2$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the outer surface, and $I$ is the second moment of area. For a rectangular section:

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma = \frac{M \cdot (t/2)}{b t^3 / 12} = \frac{6 M}{b t^2}
$$

---

### Step 3: Apply Hooke's Law to Get Strain

Using $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{6 M}{E \, b \, t^2}
$$

Substituting $M = F \cdot L$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{6 F L}{E \, b \, t^2}
$$

This is the **surface strain** at the fixed end of the cantilever.

---

### Step 4: Convert Strain to Resistance Change

Using the strain gauge equation $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \frac{6 F L}{E \, b \, t^2}
$$

---

### Step 5: Convert Resistance Change to Bridge Output Voltage

A quarter-bridge Wheatstone bridge with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ gives:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

Substituting Step 4:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{6 F L}{E \, b \, t^2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{3 \, V_{ex} \, GF \, L}{2 \, E \, b \, t^2} \cdot F}
$$

---

### Step 6: Identify the Sensitivity

The **sensitivity** $S$ of the force sensor (V/N) is:

$$
S = \frac{V_{out}}{F} = \frac{3 \, V_{ex} \, GF \, L}{2 \, E \, b \, t^2}
$$

The final derived expression shows that **sensitivity increases with** $V_{ex}$, $GF$, and $L$, and **decreases with** $E$, $b$, and $t^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight (frequently asked in KTU):** To increase sensor sensitivity, prefer a **longer, thinner beam** with a **low Young's modulus substrate** and a **higher gauge factor** material. However, this trades off against the **linear range** and **mechanical robustness** of the sensor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Piezoelectric Sensor Output Through Charge Amplifier

**Step 1:** The applied force $F$ generates a charge via the direct piezoelectric effect:

$$
q = d_{33} \cdot F
$$

**Step 2:** The charge amplifier (an op-amp with feedback capacitor $C_f$) converts charge to voltage using the principle of virtual ground:

$$
V_{out} = -\frac{q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}
$$

**Step 3:** The sensitivity of the piezoelectric force sensor is:

$$
S_p = \frac{V_{out}}{F} = -\frac{d_{33}}{C_f}
$$

The negative sign indicates a 180° phase shift introduced by the inverting amplifier configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $C_f$ matters:** By selecting a smaller $C_f$, the sensitivity increases linearly. However, $C_f$ also sets the **low-frequency cutoff** $f_c = 1 / (2 \pi R_f C_f)$, so reducing it raises the cutoff and makes the sensor **blind to slow (low-frequency) forces** — a key reason piezoelectric sensors are unsuitable for static force measurement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Strain Gauge | Piezoelectric | Capacitive | FBG Optical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Measures Static Force? | **Yes** | **No** (drift) | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Measures Dynamic Force? | Yes | **Excellent** | Yes | Yes |
| Sensitivity | Medium | **Very High** | High | Medium |
| Linearity | High | Moderate | High | High |
| Temperature Sensitivity | Moderate | **High** | Low | Low |
| EMI Immunity | Low | Low | Low | **Very High** |
| Cost | **Low** | Medium | Medium | **High** |
| Typical Use in Mobile Robots | Load cell, foot sensor | Collision detector | Tactile / e-skin | Surgical robot |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU):** Students often claim piezoelectric sensors "measure all forces". They do not — they **fail to measure static (DC) force** due to charge leakage. Examiners specifically test this.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Strain-Gauge Force Sensor Signal Chain

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete signal path from physical force to digital readout, including signal conditioning stages typically found in a mobile robot's force-sensing module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Force F] --> B[Deformable Elastic Element: Cantilever Beam]
    B --> C[Mechanical Strain epsilon on Gauge]
    C --> D[Resistance Change deltaR in Gauge Foil]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge with Excitation Vex]
    E --> F[Differential Output Voltage Vout mV range]
    F --> G[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    G --> H[Low-Pass Anti Aliasing Filter]
    H --> I[ADC: 12 to 16 bit]
    I --> J[Microcontroller DSP or FPGA]
    J --> K[Robot Control Loop: Motor / Gripper / Wheel]
    K -. Feedback .-> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Top-to-bottom flow: physical force → mechanical deformation → electrical signal → conditioning → digital processing → actuation.
- The dashed feedback arrow (`.->`) represents the **closed-loop control** typical in robotic force regulation.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Piezoelectric Force Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dynamic Force F impact or vibration] --> B[Piezo Crystal: Quartz or PZT or PVDF]
    B --> C[Surface Charge q d33 times F]
    C --> D[Charge Amplifier Op Amp with C feedback]
    D --> E[High Pass Filter blocks DC drift]
    E --> F[ADC: High Sampling Rate >= 10 kHz]
    F --> G[Microcontroller: Time Domain or FFT Analysis]
    G --> H[Robot Reaction: Collision Stop or Vibration Damping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **High-Pass Filter** stage — this is what makes piezoelectric systems intrinsically blind to static loads, a key KTU concept.

---

## 4.3 System-Level Architecture: Force Sensor Placement on a Mobile Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MobileRobot[Mobile Robot Platform]
        A1[Wheel 1: Force Sensor in Hub]
        A2[Wheel 2: Force Sensor in Hub]
        A3[Wheel 3: Force Sensor in Hub]
        A4[Wheel 4: Force Sensor in Hub]
        B1[Chassis: Center of Mass Load Cell]
        C1[Bumper: Piezoelectric Impact Sensor]
        D1[Arm Joint 1: Torque Sensor]
        D2[Arm Joint 2: Torque Sensor]
        E1[Gripper Tip: Tactile Sensor Array]
    end
    F1[Central Sensor Hub: Multiplexed ADC]
    MobileRobot --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Robot Main Controller: Trajectory and Force Planner]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice (KTU expects awareness):** In a real mobile manipulator, force sensors are placed at **multiple hierarchical levels** — wheels (locomotion), chassis (stability), joints (manipulation), and fingertips (interaction). This multi-level placement enables fault detection and graceful degradation.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Force Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Measure Force] --> B{Static or Dynamic}
    B -- Static DC --> C{Environment EMI}
    C -- Clean Lab --> D[Strain Gauge Load Cell]
    C -- Harsh EMI --> E[Fiber Bragg Grating Sensor]
```
