```mermaid
    B -- Dynamic Impact or Vibration --> F[Frequency Range]
    F -- Low to Medium --> G[Piezoelectric Sensor with Charge Amplifier]
    F -- High Frequency >= 1 MHz --> H[PVDF Thin Film Sensor]
    A --> I{Need Spatial Mapping Tactile}
    I -- Yes --> J[Capacitive Tactile Array e-skin]
    I -- No --> K[Single Point Load Cell or Strain Gauge]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Gauge Factor (GF) of a strain gauge. Mention the typical value of GF for a constantan strain gauge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Gauge Factor (GF)** is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance $\Delta R / R$ to the fractional change in length (strain) $\varepsilon$ produced in the gauge:

$$
GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}
$$

Mathematically: $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$

For **constantan** (a copper-nickel alloy, Cu 55%, Ni 45%), the typical value of GF is **2.0 to 2.1**.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition (1.5 marks) ✓
- Equation (1 mark) ✓
- Typical value (0.5 mark) ✓

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why cannot a piezoelectric force sensor be used to measure a constant (static) force? Justify with one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A piezoelectric sensor generates charge $q = d_{33} \cdot F$ only when the force is **changing with time** (i.e., during dynamic loading). Under a constant static force, the generated charge **leaks away** through the sensor's internal resistance, the cable, and the input impedance of the charge amplifier, causing the output to drift to zero. Therefore, piezoelectric sensors are inherently suited only for **dynamic force measurement** (impact, vibration, pressure transients), and not for static loads. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern Note]**
> Each Part B question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.
> Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Wheatstone bridge-based strain-gauge force sensor used in mobile robots. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of the applied force $F$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Description (3 marks):**

A strain-gauge force sensor consists of:
- A **deformable elastic element** (cantilever beam, diaphragm, or proving ring) that deflects when force is applied.
- A **strain gauge** — a thin metallic foil in a serpentine pattern — bonded to the surface of the elastic element using a specialized adhesive.
- A **Wheatstone bridge** circuit with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ that converts the resistance change $\Delta R$ of the gauge into a differential voltage $V_{out}$.
- **Signal conditioning** electronics: instrumentation amplifier, filter, ADC.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**

When force $F$ is applied, the elastic element deforms, producing strain $\varepsilon$ on its surface. The bonded strain gauge experiences the same strain. The gauge factor relates the resulting fractional resistance change to strain: $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$. The Wheatstone bridge converts this into a voltage output.

**Step 3 — Derivation of $V_{out}$ (2 marks):**

For a quarter-bridge configuration with one active gauge:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon
$$

For a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$ with Young's modulus $E$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{6 F L}{E b t^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{3 \, V_{ex} \, GF \, L}{2 \, E \, b \, t^2} \cdot F}
$$

---

**(b) Compare strain-gauge and piezoelectric force sensors on the basis of: (i) ability to measure static force, (ii) sensitivity, (iii) frequency response, and (iv) typical robotic application. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected):**

| Parameter | Strain Gauge | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Static Force Measurement | **Yes**, suitable for static loads | **No**, charge leaks away under constant force |
| (ii) Sensitivity | Moderate ($\Delta R / R \sim 10^{-3}$ at full scale) | **Very High** (suitable for micro-Newton forces) |
| (iii) Frequency Response | DC to a few kHz | **Excellent**, from a few Hz to MHz |
| (iv) Typical Robotic Application | Wheel load cells, foot pressure sensors, payload monitoring | Collision detection on bumpers, vibration analysis, impact sensing in manipulators |

**Mark Distribution (1.75 marks per parameter × 4 = 7 marks).**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Describe the working principle of a piezoelectric force sensor. Derive the output voltage of a charge amplifier connected to it. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Piezoelectric Effect (2 marks):**

The **direct piezoelectric effect**, discovered in 1880, is the generation of an electrical charge on the surface of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (quartz, PZT, PVDF) when subjected to mechanical stress.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**

A force $F$ applied to the crystal produces stress $\sigma = F / A$. This stress generates a surface charge $q$ given by:

$$
q = d_{33} \cdot F
$$

where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (in C/N). Electrodes collect this charge, and a charge amplifier converts it to a usable voltage.

**Step 3 — Charge Amplifier Output (3 marks):**

A charge amplifier uses an op-amp with a feedback capacitor $C_f$. The virtual ground at the inverting input forces all charge $q$ to flow into $C_f$, producing:

$$
V_{out} = -\frac{q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}
$$

The sensitivity is $S_p = -d_{33} / C_f$ (in V/N). The negative sign indicates an inverting configuration.

---

**(b) Explain the working of a Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) based force sensor. Mention two advantages of FBG sensors for use in mobile robots operating in harsh environments. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — FBG Working Principle (4 marks):**

A Fiber Bragg Grating is a periodic variation in the refractive index of the core of an optical fiber. It acts as a wavelength-selective mirror, reflecting a specific Bragg wavelength:

$$
\lambda_B = 2 \, n_{eff} \, \Lambda
$$

where $n_{eff}$ is the effective refractive index of the fiber core and $\Lambda$ is the grating period.

When an external force deforms the fiber, the grating period $\Lambda$ changes, shifting the reflected Bragg wavelength $\lambda_B$. By measuring the wavelength shift $\Delta \lambda_B$, the strain $\varepsilon$ in the fiber is determined:

$$
\Delta \lambda_B / \lambda_B = (1 - p_e) \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where $p_e$ is the photo-elastic coefficient (≈ 0.22 for silica).

Using Hooke's law, the force $F$ producing this strain is calculated.

**Step 2 — Advantages for Harsh-Environment Mobile Robots (3 marks):**

1. **Immunity to Electromagnetic Interference (EMI):** Because FBG is an optical sensor, it can operate reliably near high-voltage motors, welding equipment, and in strong RF fields — common in industrial mobile robots.
2. **Intrinsic Safety:** No electrical current flows at the sensing point, making FBG safe for use in explosive atmospheres (e.g., mining robots, petrochemical inspection robots) and intrinsically safe in medical robots (MRI environment).
3. **Multiplexing:** Multiple FBGs at different wavelengths can be inscribed on a single fiber, allowing distributed force sensing along robot limbs with a single interrogation unit.

(Mark distribution: 4 marks for working, 3 marks for advantages; 2 advantages required for full marks.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Force Sensor Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer** — "$V_{out} = 1.5 F$" is **wrong**; the correct form is "$V_{out} = 1.5 \text{ mV/N} \times F$" with explicit units. *(–1 mark per occurrence)*
> 2. **Mixing Up Gauge Factor and Poisson's Ratio** — Gauge Factor GF (~2) is **not** Poisson's ratio $\nu$ (~0.3). They are completely different quantities. Examiners specifically test this confusion. *(–1 mark)*
> 3. **Stating Piezoelectric Sensors "Measure All Forces"** — They **cannot** measure static (DC) force due to charge leakage. This is a guaranteed **–2 mark** deduction if missed.
> 4. **Omitting the Derivation Steps in 14-mark Questions** — KTU valuation key requires **explicit intermediate steps**. Jumping from given to final equation without showing $\sigma = F/A$, $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$, and $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ will cost **up to 4 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing $F = m \cdot a$ and $W = m \cdot g$** — These are not interchangeable. Force in dynamic systems uses $F = m \cdot a$; static weight uses $W = mg$. Examiners test this in 3-mark questions.
> 6. **Sketching a Diagram Without Labels** — A Wheatstone bridge with unmarked resistors loses marks. Always label $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ and indicate $V_{ex}$ and $V_{out}$ clearly. *(–1 to 2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A force sensor converts mechanical force into an electrical signal; it cannot measure force directly — it measures the **effects** of force (strain, charge, displacement).
- **Primary Equation Chain:** $F \rightarrow \sigma = F/A \rightarrow \varepsilon = \sigma / E \rightarrow \Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon \rightarrow V_{out}$.
- **Gauge Factor** of constantan strain gauge ≈ **2.0 to 2.1** (dimensionless).
- **Piezoelectric equation:** $q = d_{33} \cdot F$; charge amplifier output $V_{out} = -q / C_f = -d_{33} F / C_f$.
- **Piezoelectric sensors CANNOT measure static force** — they only respond to dynamic (time-varying) forces. *Most-tested KTU fact.*
- **Cantilever beam sensitivity:** $S = 3 V_{ex} GF L / (2 E b t^2)$. Higher $L$, lower $E$, lower $t$ → higher sensitivity.
- **Capacitive sensor equation:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. Force changes $d$.
- **FBG equation:** $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$. Used in EMI-harsh environments.
- **Torque sensor equation:** $\tau = F \cdot r$ (force times moment arm).
- **Static weight:** $W = m g$, with $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- **Young's modulus of steel:** ~**200 GPa**; aluminum ~**70 GPa**.
- **Classification remember-order:** Strain Gauge → Piezoelectric → Capacitive → Optical (FBG) → Piezoresistive → Hall-Effect.
- **Sensor placement on a mobile robot:** Wheels (locomotion force), Bumper (impact), Joints (torque), Gripper (tactile), Chassis (load).
- **Advantages of optical FBG sensors in mobile robots:** EMI immunity, intrinsic safety, multiplexing, small size, corrosion resistance.
- **Bridge output formula to memorize:** $V_{out} = (V_{ex} / 4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ for a quarter-bridge.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** In the 14-mark questions, always draw a **neat labeled diagram first**, then state the governing equations, and finally present the **substituted numerical/algebraic derivation**. Examiners award 2–3 marks for a clean diagram alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors to Measure Distance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors to Measure Distance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensors to Measure Distance** are non-contact (or contact-based) transducers employed in mobile robotic systems to quantify the spatial separation between the robot and objects, obstacles, terrain features, or landmarks in its environment. These sensors form the perceptual front-end of the **perception–localization–navigation pipeline**, converting physical phenomena (acoustic, optical, electromagnetic) into measurable electrical signals proportional to range.

In mobile robotics, distance measurement sensors are broadly classified into two categories:

| Classification | Operating Principle | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Sensors** | Emit energy and measure reflection/return | Ultrasonic (SONAR), LiDAR, ToF, Infrared (active) |
| **Passive Sensors** | Receive ambient energy emitted by environment | Stereo Cameras, Monocular Vision |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are standing in a dark room with a flashlight. To know how far the wall is, you could:
> 1. **Shout and listen for the echo** → This is exactly how **ultrasonic sensors** work (sound instead of light, sonar principle).
> 2. **Shine the flashlight and time how long it takes to see the reflection** → This is the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle used in LiDAR and radar.
> 3. **Shine a laser dot and see where it lands on the wall, then triangulate** → This is the **triangulation principle** used in IR and some laser sensors.
> 4. **Use two eyes to judge depth (parallax)** → This is **stereo vision**.

For a mobile robot navigating a warehouse, distance sensors act as the robot's "eyes and ears," continuously reporting "how far is the next obstacle?" so the robot's control system can plan collision-free motion.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following physical constants are essential for range sensor calculations:
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$
> - **Speed of sound in air (at 20°C):** $v_{sound} = 343 \, \text{m/s}$
> - **Standard reference range for indoor robotics:** **0.1 m to 30 m**
> - **Typical accuracy benchmarks:** **±1 cm (LiDAR)**, **±3 cm (Ultrasonic)**, **±5 mm (IR Triangulation)**
> - **Refresh rate (sensor update frequency):** **10 Hz to 100 kHz** depending on sensor class

## 1.4 Classification Overview (KTU 2024 Module 2 Scope)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (OERAT724 – Basics of Mobile Robotics) focuses on the following range sensor families:

1. **Ultrasonic Sensors (SONAR)** – Time-of-Flight using acoustic waves
2. **Infrared (IR) Distance Sensors** – Triangulation and intensity-based
3. **Laser Range Finders / LiDAR** – Optical Time-of-Flight and Phase-Shift
4. **Time-of-Flight (ToF) Cameras** – Array-based optical ranging
5. **Stereo Vision Systems** – Passive triangulation via disparity
6. **Tactile / Whisker Sensors** – Contact-based binary distance=0 detection

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ultrasonic Sensors (SONAR Principle)

### Operating Logic Steps

1. The transmitter emits a **high-frequency acoustic pulse** (typically **40 kHz**, above human hearing threshold of 20 kHz).
2. The pulse propagates through air at the speed of sound, $v_{sound}$.
3. Upon striking an object, a portion of the acoustic energy is **reflected** back as an echo.
4. The receiver detects the echo, and a microcontroller measures the **round-trip time** $t$.
5. The distance is computed using the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** equation.

### Key Formulas

- **One-way distance:** $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$ (the factor of 2 accounts for round trip)
- **Speed of sound with temperature compensation:** $v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \, T$ (where T is in °C, result in m/s)
- **Beam divergence angle:** typical **15° to 30°** cone (causes "crosstalk" in multi-sensor arrays)
- **Minimum range:** $\approx 0.02 \, \text{m}$ (ringing time of transducer)
- **Maximum range:** $\approx 10 \, \text{m}$ (attenuation of acoustic energy in air)

### Why and How

> [!NOTE]
> **Why factor of 2?** The pulse travels to the object AND back. If total round-trip time is 6 ms and speed is 340 m/s, total path = 2.04 m, so one-way distance = 1.02 m.
>
> **Why temperature compensation matters:** Sound speed in air varies with temperature. At 0°C it is 331 m/s, at 40°C it is 355 m/s — an error of ~7% which directly translates to distance error.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotic vacuum cleaners** (e.g., Roomba's early generations) used ultrasonic sensors for cliff detection and obstacle avoidance.
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)** in warehouses use SONAR for low-cost proximity detection.
- **Drawback:** Specular reflection (at oblique angles) can deflect echoes away from receiver — fails on smooth, angled surfaces.

---

## 2.2 Infrared (IR) Distance Sensors

### 2.2.1 Triangulation-Based IR Sensors (e.g., Sharp GP2Y0A21)

**Logic Steps:**
1. An IR LED emits a **modulated infrared beam** (typically 850 nm wavelength).
2. The beam strikes the target and scatters.
3. A **position-sensitive detector (PSD)** or linear CCD array receives the reflected light.
4. Based on the **angle of the reflected ray** to the PSD, the geometry yields the distance.

**Triangulation formula:**

Let $b$ = baseline distance between emitter and detector, $\theta$ = angle of reflected ray on the PSD. Then:

$$d = \dfrac{b \cdot f}{x_{PSD}}$$

where $f$ is the focal length of the receiving lens and $x_{PSD}$ is the spot position on the PSD.

### 2.2.2 Intensity-Based IR Sensors

The returned IR intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance:

$$I_{received} \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$$

This is highly **non-linear and reflectance-dependent**, hence less accurate.

---

## 2.3 Laser Range Finders and LiDAR

### 2.3.1 Pulsed Time-of-Flight LiDAR

**Logic:**
1. Emits a **short laser pulse** (typically 905 nm or 1550 nm infrared).
2. Measures the time for the pulse to return: $t = \dfrac{2d}{c}$.
3. Distance: $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$.

**Maximum range:** Up to **200 m** for automotive LiDAR.

### 2.3.2 Phase-Shift LiDAR

1. Emits a **continuously modulated** laser beam at frequency $f_{mod}$.
2. Measures the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ between emitted and received signal.
3. Distance: $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase-shift method is more accurate at short ranges** (sub-mm) but has an ambiguity range of $\dfrac{c}{2f_{mod}}$.

---

## 2.4 Time-of-Flight (ToF) Cameras

ToF cameras illuminate the entire scene with **modulated IR light** and measure the phase shift at every pixel simultaneously, producing a **depth map** at video frame rates (30–60 fps).

$$d(x,y) = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi(x,y)}{4\pi f_{mod}}$$

---

## 2.5 Stereo Vision (Passive Triangulation)

### Logic Steps

1. Two cameras (left $C_L$ and right $C_R$) separated by baseline $B$ capture the same scene from different viewpoints.
2. A corresponding point $P$ in the real world has image coordinates $(x_L, y)$ and $(x_R, y)$.
3. **Disparity:** $\delta = x_L - x_R$
4. Depth: $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{\delta}$

where $f$ is the focal length of the cameras.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** As distance increases, disparity decreases. This means stereo vision is **accurate only at close-to-medium ranges** and degrades for far objects.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU exams for this module.**

| Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Symbol Meanings | Typical Range | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic (SONAR) | $d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$ | $v_{sound}$ = sound speed, $t$ = round-trip time | 0.02–10 m | ±3 cm |
| Speed of sound (T-comp) | $v = 331 + 0.6T$ | $T$ in °C | — | — |
| IR Triangulation | $d = \dfrac{b \cdot f}{x_{PSD}}$ | $b$ = baseline, $f$ = focal length, $x$ = PSD position | 0.04–5 m | ±5 mm |
| Pulsed LiDAR (ToF) | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c$ = light speed, $t$ = round-trip time | 0.1–200 m | ±1 cm |
| Phase-Shift LiDAR | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ | $\Delta\phi$ = phase shift, $f_{mod}$ = modulation freq | 0.01–100 m | ±1 mm |
| ToF Camera | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi(x,y)}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ | per-pixel phase shift | 0.1–10 m | ±1 cm |
| Stereo Vision | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{\delta}$ | $f$ = focal length, $B$ = baseline, $\delta$ = disparity | 0.5–50 m | Sub-cm to dm |
| Intensity-based IR | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$ | inverse-square law | <1 m | Highly variable |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **factor of 2** in time-of-flight equations. The pulse travels to the target **AND back**, so the total path is $2d$, not $d$.

---

## 2.7 Sensor Selection Trade-off Table

| Parameter | Ultrasonic | IR Triangulation | LiDAR | Stereo Camera | ToF Camera |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Very Low | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| Range | Short | Short-Medium | Long | Medium | Medium |
| Accuracy | Low | Medium | High | Medium | Medium |
| Works in dark | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Works in sunlight | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| 2D/3D | 1D point | 1D point | 2D/3D scan | 3D dense | 3D dense |
| Update rate | 20–50 Hz | 25–100 Hz | 10–100 Hz | 30 fps | 30–60 fps |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ultrasonic Distance from Echo Time

We will derive the fundamental equation used in **HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor**, the most common sensor in KTU lab courses.

**Given:**
- Round-trip time of acoustic pulse: $t$ (in seconds)
- Speed of sound in air: $v_{sound}$ (in m/s)
- Distance to object: $d$ (in m)

**Step 1 — Define total path length traveled by the pulse**

The pulse travels from the transmitter to the object, then back to the receiver. If $d$ is the one-way distance, the total path length $L_{total}$ is:

$$L_{total} = d + d = 2d$$

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental kinematic equation**

For constant velocity:

$$L_{total} = v_{sound} \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for total path length**

$$v_{sound} \cdot t = 2d$$

**Step 4 — Solve for distance $d$**

$$d = \dfrac{v_{sound} \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Incorporate temperature compensation**

Substitute the temperature-dependent speed of sound:

$$v_{sound}(T) = 331 + 0.6 \, T$$

Therefore:

$$d = \dfrac{(331 + 0.6T) \cdot t}{2}$$

**Numerical Example:**

> An HC-SR04 sensor reports an echo time of $t = 5.85 \, \text{ms}$ at room temperature $T = 25°\text{C}$. Find the distance.

**Solution:**

$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331 + 15 = 346 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$d = \dfrac{346 \times 5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \dfrac{2.0241}{2} = 1.012 \, \text{m}$$

The object is approximately **1.01 m** away.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stereo Vision Depth from Disparity

**Setup:** Two cameras with parallel optical axes, separated by baseline $B$. Both have focal length $f$. A point $P$ at world depth $Z$ projects to image points with horizontal coordinates $x_L$ (left image) and $x_R$ (right image).

**Step 1 — Apply similar triangles (left camera)**

From the pinhole camera model:

$$\dfrac{x_L}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Apply similar triangles (right camera)**

$$\dfrac{x_R}{f} = \dfrac{X - B}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the two equations**

$$\dfrac{x_L - x_R}{f} = \dfrac{X - (X - B)}{Z} = \dfrac{B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Define disparity $\delta = x_L - x_R$**

$$\dfrac{\delta}{f} = \dfrac{B}{Z}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for depth Z**

$$Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{\delta}$$

**Step 6 — Derive depth error as a function of disparity error**

Assuming disparity estimation has uncertainty $\Delta\delta$, the derivative gives:

$$\dfrac{\partial Z}{\partial \delta} = -\dfrac{f \cdot B}{\delta^{2}}$$

So the depth uncertainty is:

$$\Delta Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{\delta^{2}} \cdot \Delta\delta = \dfrac{Z^{2}}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta\delta$$

**Numerical Example:**

> A stereo camera rig has baseline $B = 0.12 \, \text{m}$, focal length $f = 800 \, \text{pixels}$, and disparity $\delta = 40 \, \text{pixels}$. Find the depth $Z$.

**Solution:**

$$Z = \dfrac{800 \times 0.12}{40} = \dfrac{96}{40} = 2.4 \, \text{m}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Phase-Shift LiDAR Distance

**Given:** A continuous-wave laser modulated at frequency $f_{mod}$, returning with a phase delay $\Delta\phi$.

**Step 1 — Relate phase delay to time delay**

The phase delay is proportional to the time delay:

$$\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_{mod} \cdot \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the one-way time delay (NOT round trip, because the modulation envelope is what is delayed).

**Step 2 — Relate time delay to distance**

$$\tau = \dfrac{d}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Combine**

$$\Delta\phi = 2\pi f_{mod} \cdot \dfrac{d}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for distance**

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{2\pi f_{mod}}$$

But because phase can only be measured in $[0, 2\pi)$, the actual one-way distance may be a multiple of the wavelength. The unambiguous range is:

$$d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2f_{mod}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the factor of 2 vs 4:** The factor of 2 (not 4) appears here because phase shift measures the **one-way** path when applied to the modulation envelope. The 4 appears in some texts because they include additional system-related round-trip considerations. The KTU syllabus standard form is $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$ for **two-way** phase-shift ranging. Students should verify with their textbook's convention.

**Using the KTU syllabus standard form (two-way):**

$$d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_{mod}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | SONAR | IR | LiDAR | Stereo Vision | Tactile |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Wave | Acoustic | Optical (IR) | Optical (Laser) | Visible light | Mechanical |
| Speed of Wave | 343 m/s | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s | $3 \times 10^8$ m/s | N/A (passive) | N/A (contact) |
| Working in dark | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| Working in sunlight | Yes | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| Affected by humidity | Yes | No | Slightly | No | No |
| Affected by material color | No | Yes | Slightly | Yes | No |
| Field of view | Wide (cone) | Narrow | Very narrow | Wide | Point |
| Data rate | Low | Medium | High (scanning) | High | On-event |
| Cost | Lowest | Low | Highest | Medium | Lowest |

---

## 3.5 Error Source Analysis (Analytical)

### Ultrasonic Errors

- **Temperature error:** $1°C$ change → $0.6 \, \text{m/s}$ change → $0.17\%$ distance error
- **Crosstalk error:** When multiple sensors interfere, false short-range readings occur
- **Specular reflection error:** At angles > 30° from normal, echo is lost

### LiDAR Errors

- **Multipath error:** Light bouncing off multiple surfaces causes ghost points
- **Rain/fog attenuation:** Water droplets scatter the laser beam
- **Eye-safety limit:** 905 nm limited to lower power; 1550 nm allows higher power and longer range

### Stereo Vision Errors

- **Textureless surfaces** (e.g., blank walls) cause stereo matching failure
- **Baseline–range tradeoff:** Larger baseline gives better accuracy but requires overlapping field of view

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Mobile Robot Perception Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    envA[Environment and Obstacles] --> emA[Energy Emission Stage]
    emA --> propA[Wave Propagation Stage]
    propA --> refA[Reflection or Scattering]
    refA --> recA[Receiver Stage]
    recA --> sigA[Signal Conditioning]
    sigA --> procA[Time or Phase Measurement]
    procA --> calcA[Distance Calculation Module]
    calcA --> outA[Digital Range Output]
    outA --> ctrlA[Robot Controller and Navigation]
    ctrlA --> actA[Motor and Actuator Commands]
    actA --> mobA[Robot Motion Update]
    mobA --> envA
```

## 4.2 Sensor Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA[Distance Measurement Sensors]
    rootA --> actA[Active Sensors]
    rootA --> pasA[Passive Sensors]
    actA --> usA[Ultrasonic SONAR]
    actA --> irA[Infrared IR]
    actA --> liA[LiDAR Laser]
    actA --> tfA[Time of Flight Camera]
    pasA --> svA[Stereo Vision]
    pasA --> moA[Monocular Vision]
    pasA --> taA[Tactile Whisker]
    usA --> tA1[Time of Flight Principle]
    irA --> tA2[Triangulation Principle]
    irA --> tA3[Intensity Principle]
    liA --> tA4[Pulsed ToF]
    liA --> tA5[Phase Shift]
    tfA --> tA6[Modulated Light Array]
    svA --> tA7[Disparity Calculation]
```

## 4.3 Ultrasonic Sensor Operational Flow (HC-SR04 Example)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as Robot Microcontroller
    participant TRIG as Trigger Pin
    participant USND as Ultrasonic Transducer
    participant OBJ as Target Object
    participant ECHO as Echo Pin
    MCU->>TRIG: Send 10us pulse
    TRIG->>USND: Emit 8 cycle 40kHz burst
    USND->>OBJ: Acoustic wave travels
    OBJ->>USND: Echo returns
    USND->>ECHO: Set HIGH for duration t
    ECHO->>MCU: Measure pulse width t
    MCU->>MCU: Compute distance d equals v times t divided by 2
```

## 4.4 Stereo Vision Triangulation Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    camL[L Camera at origin] -->|focal length f| imgL[Image Plane Left]
    camR[R Camera at baseline B] -->|focal length f| imgR[Image Plane Right]
    ptP[World Point P at depth Z] --> imgL
    ptP --> imgR
    imgL -->|xL| disp[Disparity Computation]
    imgR -->|xR| disp
    disp --> depth[Depth Z equals f times B over delta]
```

## 4.5 LiDAR Phase Shift Measurement Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    modA[Modulation Oscillator] --> emitA[Laser Diode Emitter]
    emitA --> tgtA[Target Object]
    tgtA --> detA[Photodetector Receiver]
    modA --> refA[Reference Signal]
    detA --> mixA[Phase Detector Mixer]
    refA --> mixA
    mixA --> phA[Phase Delta Output]
    phA --> dA[Distance Calculator]
    dA --> outA[Digital Range Value]
```

## 4.6 Sensor Selection Decision Tree for Mobile Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA[Need distance measurement?] --> qB[Indoor or outdoor?]
    qB -->|Indoor| qC[Budget constraint?]
    qB -->|Outdoor| qD[Long range needed?]
    qC -->|Low cost| qE[Simple object detection?]
    qC -->|Medium| qF[Need 2D mapping?]
    qC -->|High| qG[Autonomous navigation?]
    qE --> selA[Use IR or Ultrasonic]
    qF --> selB[Use 2D LiDAR]
    qG --> selC[Use 3D LiDAR and Camera]
    qD -->|Yes| selD[Use automotive LiDAR]
    qD -->|No| selE[Use short range LiDAR or ToF]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define Time-of-Flight (ToF) principle in distance measurement. List two sensors that use this principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Time-of-Flight (ToF) principle measures distance by calculating the time taken for a wave (acoustic or electromagnetic) to travel from the sensor to the target and back. The fundamental equation is:

$$d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where $v$ is the wave propagation speed and $t$ is the round-trip time.

Two sensors using ToF principle: **(1)** Ultrasonic sensor (SONAR), **(2)** Pulsed LiDAR (Laser Range Finder).

*(Award 2 marks for definition with equation, 1 mark for listing two sensors.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between active and passive distance sensors with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Sensors | Passive Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Emits its own energy | Receives ambient energy |
| Works in dark | Yes | No |
| Example | Ultrasonic sensor, LiDAR | Stereo camera, monocular camera |
| Power consumption | Higher | Lower |

*(Award 1.5 marks per row × 2 rows = 3 marks. Any equivalent valid differentiation accepted.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **ultrasonic distance sensor**. Derive the distance equation and explain the effect of temperature on the measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare ultrasonic sensors and LiDAR in terms of range, accuracy, working environment, and cost. Discuss one application of each in mobile robotics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Trigger Circuit] → [Ultrasonic Transmitter] → [Air Medium]
                                                        ↓
[Microcontroller] ← [Echo Detector] ← [Ultrasonic Receiver] ← (Echo from object)
```

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

The ultrasonic sensor consists of a transmitter and receiver pair. The transmitter emits a high-frequency acoustic pulse (typically 40 kHz) into the air. This pulse travels at the speed of sound, strikes an object, and reflects back as an echo. The receiver detects this echo, and the time elapsed is measured by a microcontroller.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Let $d$ = distance, $t$ = round-trip time, $v$ = speed of sound.

The total path of the pulse is $2d$, and using $distance = speed \times time$:

$$2d = v \cdot t \implies d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

**Effect of Temperature (extra for completeness):**

The speed of sound depends on air temperature:

$$v = 331 + 0.6T \quad (\text{T in °C})$$

So the distance equation becomes:

$$d = \dfrac{(331 + 0.6T) \cdot t}{2}$$

A 10°C change in temperature changes $v$ by 6 m/s, causing approximately 1.7% distance error — non-negligible for precision robotics.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Ultrasonic | LiDAR |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 0.02 – 10 m | 0.1 – 200 m |
| Accuracy | ±3 cm | ±1 cm |
| Sunlight performance | Excellent | Excellent |
| Dark performance | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost | ₹200 – ₹500 | ₹5,000 – ₹5,00,000 |
| Beam width | Wide (~30° cone) | Very narrow (<1°) |
| Affected by fog/rain | Slightly | Significantly |
| Update rate | 20–50 Hz | 10–100 Hz (scanning) |

**Applications (2 marks):**
- **Ultrasonic:** Robotic vacuum cleaners (cliff detection), low-cost AGV obstacle detection.
- **LiDAR:** Autonomous vehicles (Google/Waymo), SLAM in service robots, 3D terrain mapping for agricultural robots.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **stereo vision principle** for distance measurement in mobile robots. Derive the depth equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An autonomous mobile robot uses a LiDAR sensor to detect an obstacle. The sensor emits a laser pulse and receives the reflected pulse after 0.66 microseconds. Calculate the distance to the obstacle. If the same obstacle is detected by an ultrasonic sensor (at 25°C) with round-trip time 5.85 ms, calculate the distance. Compare the two measurements. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Principle (3 marks):**

Stereo vision uses two cameras (left and right) separated by a known baseline distance $B$. Both cameras capture the same scene. A point in 3D space appears at different horizontal positions in the two images — this horizontal shift is called **disparity**. From disparity, depth can be computed.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Using the pinhole camera model and similar triangles:

From left camera: $\dfrac{x_L}{f} = \dfrac{X}{Z}$

From right camera: $\dfrac{x_R}{f} = \dfrac{X - B}{Z}$

Subtracting: $\dfrac{x_L - x_R}{f} = \dfrac{B}{Z}$

Let $\delta = x_L - x_R$ (disparity). Then:

$$Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{\delta}$$

where:
- $Z$ = depth (distance from camera plane)
- $f$ = focal length
- $B$ = baseline distance between cameras
- $\delta$ = disparity in pixels

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**LiDAR Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $t_{LiDAR} = 0.66 \, \mu s = 0.66 \times 10^{-6} \, s$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$

$$d_{LiDAR} = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 0.66 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \dfrac{198}{2} = 99 \, \text{m}$$

**Ultrasonic Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $t_{US} = 5.85 \, \text{ms} = 5.85 \times 10^{-3} \, s$, $T = 25°C$

$$v_{sound} = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 346 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$d_{US} = \dfrac{346 \times 5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \dfrac{2.0241}{2} \approx 1.012 \, \text{m}$$

**Comparison (1 mark):**

The LiDAR detects the obstacle at **99 m**, while the ultrasonic sensor only detects it at **1.01 m**. This shows that LiDAR is suitable for **long-range detection** (e.g., highway driving scenarios), while ultrasonic sensors are limited to **short-range proximity** applications (e.g., parking assist, indoor obstacle detection). The vast difference in distances also reflects the difference in operating range, not an error in measurement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes (Module 2 – Distance Sensors):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ToF equations** — This is the #1 mistake. The wave travels there and back. Examiner deducts **1 to 2 marks** for this.
>
> 2. **Writing $v = 340 \, \text{m/s}$ without specifying temperature** — For precise answers, include the temperature or use the formula $v = 331 + 0.6T$. The 340 m/s approximation is acceptable only if you state "at 15°C" or similar.
>
> 3. **Confusing IR intensity and IR triangulation** — These are two **different** IR sensing principles. Intensity is reflectance-dependent; triangulation is geometry-based.
>
> 4. **In stereo vision, writing disparity = $x_L + x_R$ instead of $x_L - x_R$** — Disparity is a difference, not a sum. This costs 1 mark minimum.
>
> 5. **Writing "LiDAR uses sound waves"** — LiDAR uses **light** (laser). This is a basic conceptual error worth 2+ marks.
>
> 6. **Not drawing block diagrams in working-principle questions** — KTU examiners expect a block diagram for any "explain the working principle" question. Skipping it costs 1.5–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Distance Measurement Sensors**

- ✅ **Master the ToF equation** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ — applicable to both SONAR and LiDAR.
- ✅ **Speed of sound at 25°C** = 346 m/s; use $v = 331 + 0.6T$ for accuracy.
- ✅ **Speed of light** = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Stereo vision depth** $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{\delta}$ — disparity is the horizontal pixel shift.
- ✅ **IR triangulation** is geometry-based and **independent of surface reflectance**; IR intensity is reflectance-dependent.
- ✅ **LiDAR** = Light Detection And Ranging; uses **laser pulses** (NOT sound).
- ✅ **SONAR** = Sound Navigation And Ranging; uses **acoustic pulses** (40 kHz).
- ✅ **HC-SR04** is the standard lab sensor — emits 8-cycle 40 kHz burst triggered by a 10 µs pulse.
- ✅ **Phase-shift LiDAR** is more accurate at short range; pulsed LiDAR is better for long range.
- ✅ **ToF cameras** produce per-pixel depth — used in gesture recognition, indoor robots.
- ✅ **Active sensors** work in dark; **passive sensors** (cameras) need ambient light.
- ✅ **Crosstalk** is a problem in multi-ultrasonic-sensor arrays — use firing sequencing.
- ✅ **Specular reflection** causes ultrasonic failure on smooth angled surfaces.
- ✅ **Stereo vision fails on textureless surfaces** (blank walls) — combine with other sensors.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion** (LiDAR + Camera + IMU) is the modern robotics standard — no single sensor is perfect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conversion Units for Exam:**
> - $1 \, \mu s = 10^{-6} \, s$
> - $1 \, ms = 10^{-3} \, s$
> - $1 \, ns = 10^{-9} \, s$
> - For light: in 1 ns, light travels ~30 cm round-trip (~15 cm one-way)
> - For sound: in 1 ms, sound travels ~34 cm round-trip (~17 cm one-way)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors to Measure Global Pose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors to Measure Global Pose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Global Pose

In mobile robotics, **Global Pose** is defined as the complete state of a robot expressed in a fixed, world-coordinate reference frame (typically denoted as the *inertial frame* or *global frame*). The global pose is mathematically represented as a 6-DOF (Degrees of Freedom) vector:

$$
P_{global} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T
$$

Where:
- $x, y, z$ represent the **global position** (in meters) of the robot along the axes of the world frame.
- $\phi$ (roll), $\theta$ (pitch), $\psi$ (yaw) represent the **global orientation** (in radians or degrees) of the robot with respect to the world frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike *local pose* (measured relative to a starting point or odometry frame), *global pose* is absolute and remains valid even if the robot is restarted. The term "global" implies the measurement is referenced to the Earth or a fixed inertial coordinate system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in an unfamiliar city and given a task: *Find your way to the library*. A local sensor (like counting your footsteps) tells you that you have walked 500 meters from where you started — but if someone spun you around while blindfolded, you wouldn't know *which direction* the library is anymore. This is **dead reckoning (local pose)**.

Now imagine you have a smartphone with GPS and a compass. Even if you are spun, dropped in a new place, or turned upside down, the phone tells you:
- "You are at **latitude 10.0261° N, longitude 76.3125° E**" → this is your **global position**.
- "You are facing **North-East (45° from magnetic north)**" → this is your **global heading**.

Sensors that measure global pose do exactly this — they give the robot an *absolute* awareness of where it is on the Earth and which way it is pointing, without relying on its previous motion history.

## 1.3 Categories of Global Pose Sensors

Global pose sensors can be classified based on which component of the pose they measure:

| Component Measured | Sensor Type | Key Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Global Position $(x, y, z)$ | **Satellite-based** | GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou (GNSS family) |
| Global Position (terrain-referenced) | **Beacon-based / Landmark-based** | UWB beacons, Wi-Fi triangulation, Visual landmarks |
| Global Orientation $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ | **Field-sensing** | Magnetometer (compass), Gyroscope (drift-corrected) |
| Global Orientation (gravity-referenced) | **Inertial** | Accelerometer (for roll/pitch via gravity vector) |
| Combined pose | **Sensor Fusion Units** | IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit), AHRS (Attitude and Heading Reference System) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Examinations:** The Earth's magnetic field strength is approximately **$\vec{B}_{Earth} \approx 25$ to $65$ microteslas ($\mu T$)** depending on geographic location. The standard gravity is **$g = 9.80665 \, m/s^2$**. These constants are often used in numerical problems.

## 1.4 Why Global Pose Sensing is Hard

Measuring global pose is fundamentally difficult because most physical sensors (encoders, accelerometers, gyroscopes) measure *changes* (derivatives) of state rather than the state itself. This leads to **drift** in dead-reckoning. True global sensors must reference an *external* signal that is fixed to the world — such as:
- **Satellites** in orbit (for GPS),
- **The Earth's magnetic field** (for compass),
- **The gravity vector** (for tilt sensors),
- **Fixed beacons** in the environment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS / GPS)

The **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based navigation system consisting of a constellation of at least **24 satellites** orbiting the Earth at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km**. Each satellite continuously broadcasts a signal containing its position and the precise time of transmission.

### Operating Principle: Trilateration

GPS uses a technique called **trilateration** (NOT triangulation, since angles are not measured). A receiver on the ground measures the *time-of-flight* of signals from at least 4 satellites to compute its 3D position.

$$
d_i = c \cdot (t_{received,i} - t_{transmitted,i})
$$

Where:
- $d_i$ = distance (pseudorange) to the $i$-th satellite (meters)
- $c$ = speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$
- $t_{received}$ = time the signal was received by the GPS receiver
- $t_{transmitted}$ = time the signal was sent from the satellite

For each satellite $i$, the geometric constraint is:

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2 = d_i^2
$$

Solving the system of 4 such equations (from 4 satellites) yields the receiver's $(x, y, z)$ position and corrects the receiver's clock bias $\delta t$.

### Sources of GPS Error

| Error Source | Typical Magnitude | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionospheric delay** | 1–5 m | Signal slows in the ionosphere |
| **Tropospheric delay** | 0.5–2 m | Signal slows in the lower atmosphere |
| **Multipath** | 0.5–3 m | Signal reflects off buildings/ground |
| **Satellite clock/orbit error** | 1–2 m | Imperfect atomic clocks/e ephemeris |
| **Receiver noise** | ~0.5 m | Internal electronic noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total typical civilian GPS accuracy: ~2.5 to 10 meters** without corrections. With **DGPS (Differential GPS)** or **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)** corrections, accuracy can reach **centimeter level (1–10 cm)**.

## 2.2 Magnetometer (Digital Compass) for Global Heading

A **magnetometer** measures the strength and direction of the Earth's magnetic field vector. The horizontal component of this field always points toward **magnetic North**, providing an *absolute* heading reference.

The yaw (heading) angle $\psi$ measured by a magnetometer is computed as:

$$
\psi = \arctan\left(\frac{-B_y}{B_x}\right)
$$

Where $B_x$ and $B_y$ are the components of the measured magnetic field in the robot's local horizontal plane (after tilt compensation).

**Tilt compensation** is necessary because a magnetometer mounted on a tilted robot will misread the heading. The tilt-compensated heading formula is:

$$
\psi = \arctan\left(\frac{-B_y \cos\phi + B_z \sin\phi}{B_x \cos\theta + B_y \sin\theta \sin\phi + B_z \sin\theta \cos\phi}\right)
$$

Where $\phi$ and $\theta$ are roll and pitch obtained from an accelerometer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Magnetometers are highly susceptible to local magnetic disturbances** from motors, batteries, steel structures, and electronic components. They must be mounted away from sources of electromagnetic interference (EMI) and calibrated using the **hard-iron / soft-iron calibration** procedure.

## 2.3 Accelerometer for Global Tilt (Roll & Pitch)

An accelerometer at rest measures the **gravity vector** $\vec{g} = [0, 0, -g]$ in the world frame. When the accelerometer is tilted with the robot, the projection of gravity onto the sensor axes gives roll and pitch:

$$
\theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right) \quad \text{(pitch)}
$$

$$
\phi = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_y}{g}\right) \quad \text{(roll)}
$$

Where $a_x, a_y$ are the horizontal components of acceleration in the sensor frame.

> [!NOTE]
> The accelerometer **cannot measure yaw** because the gravity vector has no horizontal rotational symmetry — rotating about the vertical axis does not change the accelerometer reading. This is why yaw is measured using a magnetometer or gyroscope.

## 2.4 Gyroscope for Rate of Rotation

A **gyroscope** measures angular velocity $\omega$ (in rad/s) about its sensitive axis. The change in orientation over time is obtained by integration:

$$
\theta(t) = \theta(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \omega(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Critical Problem:** Integration of noisy signals leads to **drift** — the estimated angle slowly diverges from the true angle over time. This is why gyroscopes alone cannot measure *absolute* global orientation; they need to be initialized and periodically corrected by an absolute sensor (magnetometer, GPS, or visual landmarks).

For a 3-axis gyroscope, the orientation update over a small time step $\Delta t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{k+1} &= \phi_k + \omega_x \cdot \Delta t \\
\theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \omega_y \cdot \Delta t \\
\psi_{k+1} &= \psi_k + \omega_z \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) and Sensor Fusion

An **IMU** is a single module that combines a 3-axis accelerometer, a 3-axis gyroscope, and often a 3-axis magnetometer (in which case it is called a **9-DOF IMU** or **MARg Sensor**).

The IMU alone cannot give **absolute** global pose — it gives *relative* orientation (drifting) and *double-integrated* position (which drifts even faster). To convert an IMU into a global pose sensor, **sensor fusion** is required.

The most common fusion technique used in mobile robotics is the **Complementary Filter** or the **Kalman Filter / Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**.

### Complementary Filter for Orientation

$$
\hat{\theta}_{k} = \alpha \cdot (\hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega \cdot \Delta t) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = filter coefficient (typically 0.95 to 0.98)
- The first term is the **gyroscope integration** (good short-term accuracy, drifts long-term)
- The second term is the **accelerometer-based tilt** (good long-term accuracy, noisy short-term)
- The filter blends high-frequency gyroscope data with low-frequency accelerometer data.

### Kalman Filter for Position (GPS + IMU)

The Kalman filter maintains a state estimate $\hat{x}_k$ and uncertainty $P_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prediction:} \quad \hat{x}_{k|k-1} &= A \hat{x}_{k-1|k-1} + B u_k \\
P_{k|k-1} &= A P_{k-1|k-1} A^T + Q \\
\\
\text{Update:} \quad K_k &= P_{k|k-1} H^T (H P_{k|k-1} H^T + R)^{-1} \\
\hat{x}_{k|k} &= \hat{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_{k|k-1}) \\
P_{k|k} &= (I - K_k H) P_{k|k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $Q$ is process noise covariance and $R$ is measurement noise covariance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** No single sensor can robustly provide global pose. The state-of-the-art approach in mobile robotics is to **fuse GPS (for absolute position) + IMU (for high-rate motion) + Magnetometer (for absolute heading)** using a Kalman or complementary filter.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i$ | GPS pseudorange from time-of-flight | meters (m) |
| 2 | $\psi = \arctan(-B_y / B_x)$ | Magnetometer heading (tilt-compensated form in §2.2) | radians |
| 3 | $\theta = \arcsin(a_x / g)$ | Pitch from accelerometer at rest | radians |
| 4 | $\phi = \arcsin(a_y / g)$ | Roll from accelerometer at rest | radians |
| 5 | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \int \omega \, d\tau$ | Gyroscope integration for angle | radians |
| 6 | $\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}$ | Complementary filter fusion | dimensionless |
| 7 | $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | matrix |
| 8 | $P_{global} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ | 6-DOF global pose vector | m, rad |
| 9 | $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$ | Speed of light (used in GPS) | m/s |
| 10 | $g = 9.80665 \, m/s^2$ | Standard gravity | m/s² |
| 11 | $B_{Earth} \approx 25$–$65 \, \mu T$ | Earth's magnetic field strength | microtesla |
| 12 | GPS altitude $\approx 20{,}200$ km | GPS satellite orbit altitude | km |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Cars (e.g., Waymo, Tesla):** Combine **RTK-GPS + IMU + Lidar** for lane-level positioning.
- **Agricultural Robots:** Use **GPS + IMU** for precise row-following in fields.
- **Drones (UAVs):** Use **GPS + IMU + Magnetometer + Barometer** for stable flight and waypoint navigation.
- **Maritime Robotics:** Differential GPS is used for harbor navigation.
- **Search and Rescue Robots:** GPS + thermal cameras + IMU for indoor-outdoor transition.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: GPS Position from Pseudoranges

**Goal:** Derive the mathematical model that allows a GPS receiver to determine its 3D position from satellite signals.

### Step 1: Define the geometry
Let the receiver's unknown position be $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ in the Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed (ECEF) frame, and let the $i$-th satellite's known position be $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$. The true geometric distance is:

$$
\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_u - x_i)^2 + (y_u - y_i)^2 + (z_u - z_i)^2}
$$

### Step 2: Introduce the clock bias
Consumer GPS receivers do not have atomic clocks. They have a clock bias $\delta t$ (in seconds) which means every measured pseudorange $\tilde{\rho}_i$ is inflated by the amount $c \cdot \delta t$:

$$
\tilde{\rho}_i = c \cdot (\Delta t_{measured}) = \rho_i + c \cdot \delta t
$$

This is why we call it a *pseudo*range — it contains a bias from clock error.

### Step 3: Write the system of equations
For $n$ satellites, we have $n$ equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_u - x_1)^2 + (y_u - y_1)^2 + (z_u - z_1)^2 &= (\tilde{\rho}_1 - c \cdot \delta t)^2 \\
(x_u - x_2)^2 + (y_u - y_2)^2 + (z_u - z_2)^2 &= (\tilde{\rho}_2 - c \cdot \delta t)^2 \\
(x_u - x_3)^2 + (y_u - y_3)^2 + (z_u - z_3)^2 &= (\tilde{\rho}_3 - c \cdot \delta t)^2 \\
(x_u - x_4)^2 + (y_u - y_4)^2 + (z_u - z_4)^2 &= (\tilde{\rho}_4 - c \cdot \delta t)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Linearize using differences
Subtract equation 1 from equations 2, 3, 4 to eliminate the quadratic $x_u^2 + y_u^2 + z_u^2$ terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1 - x_2) x_u + (y_1 - y_2) y_u + (z_1 - z_2) z_u + c \cdot \delta t &= \frac{1}{2}\left[(\tilde{\rho}_2^2 - \tilde{\rho}_1^2) - (x_2^2 - x_1^2) - (y_2^2 - y_1^2) - (z_2^2 - z_1^2)\right] \\
(x_1 - x_3) x_u + (y_1 - y_3) y_u + (z_1 - z_3) z_u + c \cdot \delta t &= \frac{1}{2}\left[(\tilde{\rho}_3^2 - \tilde{\rho}_1^2) - (x_3^2 - x_1^2) - (y_3^2 - y_1^2) - (z_3^2 - z_1^2)\right] \\
(x_1 - x_4) x_u + (y_1 - y_4) y_u + (z_1 - z_4) z_u + c \cdot \delta t &= \frac{1}{2}\left[(\tilde{\rho}_4^2 - \tilde{\rho}_1^2) - (x_4^2 - x_1^2) - (y_4^2 - y_1^2) - (z_4^2 - z_1^2)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a system of 3 linear equations in 4 unknowns $(x_u, y_u, z_u, \delta t)$. To solve, we need **at least 4 satellites**, giving a unique solution via matrix inversion.

### Step 5: Solve the matrix equation
The system can be written compactly as:

$$
H \cdot \begin{bmatrix} x_u \\ y_u \\ z_u \\ c \cdot \delta t \end{bmatrix} = \vec{b}
$$

Where $H$ is a $3 \times 4$ matrix with rows $\begin{bmatrix} x_1 - x_i & y_1 - y_i & z_1 - z_i & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Using the **least-squares** pseudo-inverse:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_u \\ y_u \\ z_u \\ c \cdot \delta t \end{bmatrix} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \vec{b}
$$

### Step 6: Final solution
After solving, divide the last element by $c$ to recover the clock bias $\delta t$ in seconds, and convert $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ from ECEF to latitude/longitude/altitude using standard geodetic transformations (WGS-84 ellipsoid).

## 3.2 Derivation: Complementary Filter for IMU Orientation Fusion

**Goal:** Combine gyroscope (high-frequency, drifting) with accelerometer (low-frequency, absolute) to estimate roll/pitch.

### Step 1: Define the discrete-time update
Let $\theta_k$ be the true pitch angle at time step $k$, and let $\Delta t$ be the sampling interval. The gyroscope measures the angular rate $\omega_k$ at this step.

### Step 2: Gyroscope-based estimate
The angle change over the interval is $\omega_k \cdot \Delta t$, so the gyroscope-only update is:

$$
\theta_{gyro,k} = \theta_{k-1} + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t
$$

### Step 3: Accelerometer-based estimate
From the gravity projection, the accelerometer pitch is:

$$
\theta_{accel} = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Combine with complementary filter
The two estimates are fused as:

$$
\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro,k} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}
$$

Substituting the gyroscope update:

$$
\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot (\hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \arcsin\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation
- If $\alpha = 1$: the filter uses *only* the gyroscope → drifts over time.
- If $\alpha = 0$: the filter uses *only* the accelerometer → noisy but absolute.
- If $\alpha = 0.98$: 98% gyro + 2% accel → robust estimate (typical choice for robotics).

### Step 6: Transfer function (frequency domain)
Taking the Z-transform, the filter is a 1st-order IIR low-pass for accelerometer and high-pass for gyroscope:

$$
\hat{\Theta}(z) = \alpha z^{-1} \hat{\Theta}(z) + \alpha \Delta t \, \Omega(z) + (1 - \alpha) \Theta_{accel}(z)
$$

Solving:

$$
\hat{\Theta}(z) = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}} \Omega(z) + \frac{1 - \alpha}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}} \Theta_{accel}(z)
$$

This confirms: gyroscope passes through a high-pass filter (blocks DC drift), accelerometer passes through a low-pass filter (blocks high-frequency noise). The cutoff frequency depends on $\alpha$ and $\Delta t$.

## 3.3 Numerical Example Problem

> **Problem:** A robot at rest measures the following accelerometer readings: $a_x = 0.5 \, m/s^2$, $a_y = 0.0 \, m/s^2$, $a_z = -9.51 \, m/s^2$. Compute the roll and pitch angles. Take $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Solution:**

Pitch $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right) = \arcsin\left(\frac{0.5}{9.81}\right) = \arcsin(0.05097) \approx 0.05099 \, rad \approx 2.92^\circ
$$

Roll $\phi$:

$$
\phi = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_y}{g}\right) = \arcsin(0) = 0^\circ
$$

Verification: The magnitude should equal $g$:

$$
\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0 + 90.44} = \sqrt{90.69} \approx 9.523 \, m/s^2
$$

This is close to $g = 9.81$, with the small discrepancy due to accelerometer noise and measurement rounding.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Performance Comparison

| Sensor | Measures | Global/Local | Drift? | Update Rate | Range | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GPS | $(x, y, z)$ | **Global** | No | 1–10 Hz | Worldwide | 2.5–10 m (civilian) |
| RTK-GPS | $(x, y, z)$ | **Global** | No | 1–20 Hz | Worldwide | 1–10 cm |
| Magnetometer | Heading $\psi$ | **Global** | No | 10–100 Hz | Worldwide | 1–5° (after calibration) |
| Accelerometer | Tilt $(\phi, \theta)$ | **Global** (at rest) | No | 50–1000 Hz | n/a | 0.1–1° |
| Gyroscope | Angular rate $\dot{\psi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\phi}$ | **Local** | **Yes** | 50–1000 Hz | ±2000°/s typical | drifts ~1°/min |
| IMU (fused) | Full 6-DOF (relative) | **Local** | **Yes** | 100–1000 Hz | n/a | drifts over time |
| GPS + IMU + Mag | Full 6-DOF | **Global** | Minimal | 50–200 Hz | Worldwide | ~1 m position, ~1° heading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The only way to achieve *true global pose* in a practical mobile robot is to **fuse an absolute sensor (GPS / Magnetometer) with a relative sensor (IMU / Encoders)**. This is the central design principle of modern mobile robot navigation stacks such as **ROS (Robot Operating System)** with the `robot_localization` package.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 1: Define global pose in mobile robotics. How is it different from local pose?

**Model Answer:**
Global pose is the complete state — position $(x, y, z)$ and orientation $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ — of a mobile robot expressed in a fixed, world-coordinate reference frame. It is **absolute**, meaning it does not depend on the robot's previous motion history. In contrast, local pose is measured **relative** to a starting point or the robot's own odometry frame. Local pose suffers from drift (errors accumulate over time), while global pose is drift-free because it is referenced to external signals such as satellites (GPS), the Earth's magnetic field (compass), or fixed beacons. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 2: Why can't a gyroscope alone measure global pose?

**Model Answer:**
A gyroscope measures **angular velocity** (rate of rotation), not absolute orientation. To obtain orientation, its output must be **integrated** over time. Because the measured angular velocity contains small biases and noise, integration causes the orientation estimate to **drift** away from the true value. The longer the gyroscope runs, the larger the accumulated error. Since it has no reference to an external, fixed signal (like Earth's magnetic field or gravity), a gyroscope cannot determine *absolute* orientation — it can only measure *changes* in orientation. (3 marks)

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the operating principle of the GPS system. With a neat block diagram, describe how a GPS receiver determines its 3D position using trilateration. What is the minimum number of satellites required, and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A robot is fitted with a 3-axis accelerometer and 3-axis gyroscope. The accelerometer gives $a_x = 2.0 \, m/s^2$, $a_y = 1.5 \, m/s^2$, $a_z = -9.2 \, m/s^2$ at rest. Compute the roll and pitch. If the gyroscope gives $\omega_x = 0.05 \, rad/s$, $\omega_y = 0.02 \, rad/s$ at the same instant, write the complementary filter update equation and find the next estimate of pitch using $\alpha = 0.96$ and $\Delta t = 0.01 \, s$. (Assume initial pitch = 0).

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — GPS Principle

**Principle:**
The Global Positioning System (GPS) uses a constellation of at least **24 satellites** in medium Earth orbit (altitude ~20,200 km). Each satellite broadcasts a signal containing:
1. The satellite's position (ephemeris data).
2. The precise time of transmission (from an atomic clock).
3. Almanac data of other satellites.

A GPS receiver on the robot measures the **time-of-flight** of each signal:

$$
\Delta t_i = t_{received,i} - t_{transmitted,i}
$$

The pseudorange is $d_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$.

**Trilateration** is then used: from the known satellite positions and measured distances, the receiver solves for its own 3D position. The geometry is:

$$
(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2 = d_i^2
$$

**Why 4 satellites minimum?**
- 3 satellites are needed to solve for the 3 unknown position coordinates $(x, y, z)$.
- A 4th satellite is required because the receiver's clock is not synchronized with the satellite's atomic clock — the receiver has a clock bias $\delta t$. The 4th equation provides the extra unknown needed to solve for $\delta t$ along with $(x, y, z)$.

**Block diagram (described in text for the answer sheet):**

```
[Satellite 1, 2, 3, 4] --signals--> [GPS Antenna] --RF--> [Receiver Front-End]
                                                                    |
                                                                    v
                                                       [Time-of-Flight Measurement]
                                                                    |
                                                                    v
                                                       [Pseudorange Calculator]
                                                                    |
                                                                    v
                                                       [Trilateration Solver]
                                                       (solves 4 equations, 4 unknowns)
                                                                    |
                                                                    v
                                                          [Position x, y, z + delta t]
```

**Sources of Error:** Ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, multipath, satellite clock/orbit error, receiver noise. (Total typical error: 2.5–10 m for civilian GPS). (7 marks distributed: 2 marks principle, 2 marks trilateration math, 1 mark block diagram, 2 marks 4-satellite reasoning)

---

#### Part (b) — Accelerometer and Complementary Filter Calculation

**Step 1: Compute roll from accelerometer**

$$
\phi = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_y}{g}\right) = \arcsin\left(\frac{1.5}{9.81}\right) = \arcsin(0.1529) \approx 0.1535 \, rad \approx 8.80^\circ
$$

**Step 2: Compute pitch from accelerometer**

$$
\theta_{accel} = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right) = \arcsin\left(\frac{2.0}{9.81}\right) = \arcsin(0.2039) \approx 0.2054 \, rad \approx 11.77^\circ
$$

**Step 3: Compute gyroscope integration for pitch**

$$
\theta_{gyro} = \theta_{prev} + \omega_y \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.02 \times 0.01 = 0.0002 \, rad
$$

**Step 4: Apply complementary filter**

$$
\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\hat{\theta}_k = 0.96 \times 0.0002 + 0.04 \times 0.2054
$$

$$
\hat{\theta}_k = 0.000192 + 0.008216
$$

$$
\hat{\theta}_k \approx 0.008408 \, rad \approx 0.482^\circ
$$

**Interpretation:** The complementary filter heavily weights the accelerometer (4%) because the gyroscope has only integrated for one short timestep. After many steps, the gyroscope contribution builds up. (7 marks: 2 marks roll, 2 marks pitch, 3 marks complementary filter)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the working of a 3-axis magnetometer as a heading sensor. Derive the expression for tilt-compensated heading. What are the major sources of error in magnetometer-based heading?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical sensor fusion system that combines GPS, IMU, and magnetometer to produce a global pose estimate for a mobile robot. Mention the role of the Kalman filter in this system.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Magnetometer for Heading

A 3-axis magnetometer measures the **Earth's magnetic field vector** $\vec{B}_{Earth} = [B_x, B_y, B_z]$ in the sensor's local frame. The horizontal projection of this vector always points toward **magnetic North**, providing an absolute heading reference.

In an ideal, tilt-free sensor, the heading (yaw) is:

$$
\psi = \arctan\left(\frac{-B_y}{B_x}\right)
$$

**Tilt-Compensated Derivation:**

When the sensor is tilted, the gravity vector in the sensor frame is $\vec{a} = [a_x, a_y, a_z]$. We first compute the roll $\phi$ and pitch $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_x}{g}\right) \quad , \quad \phi = \arcsin\left(\frac{a_y}{g}\right)
$$

We then rotate the magnetic field vector back into the horizontal plane using the rotation matrix $R(\phi, \theta)$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} B_x^h \\ B_y^h \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\phi \sin\theta & \cos\phi \sin\theta \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} B_x \\ B_y \\ B_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

The tilt-compensated heading is then:

$$
\psi = \arctan\left(\frac{-B_y^h}{B_x^h}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{-B_y \cos\phi + B_z \sin\phi}{B_x \cos\theta + B_y \sin\theta \sin\phi + B_z \sin\theta \cos\phi}\right)
$$

**Sources of Error:**
1. **Hard-iron distortion** — permanent magnets and DC currents in the robot add a constant offset to the field.
2. **Soft-iron distortion** — ferrous materials deform the field lines.
3. **Magnetic declination** — the difference between magnetic North and true North (varies by geography, ~−1° to +25° depending on location).
4. **Electromagnetic interference** from motors, power lines, etc.
5. **Inclination error** — the field is not horizontal except at the magnetic equator. (7 marks: 2 marks basic heading, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks errors)

---

#### Part (b) — Sensor Fusion Architecture

**Block Diagram (described):**

```
[GPS Receiver]      --(x_GPS, y_GPS, z_GPS)--> [Kalman Filter: Prediction + Update]
[Magnetometer]      --(psi_mag)-------------> [Kalman Filter]
[IMU: Accel + Gyro] --(a_x, a_y, a_z, w_x, w_y, w_z)--> [Strapdown Integration]
                                              [Kalman Filter] --> (x, y, z, phi, theta, psi)
```

**Role of the Kalman Filter:**
- **Prediction step:** Uses the IMU's high-rate accelerometer and gyroscope data to *predict* how the state (position, velocity, orientation) has changed during the last timestep.
- **Update step:** When a GPS position or magnetometer heading arrives (at a lower rate, e.g., 1–10 Hz), the Kalman filter *corrects* the IMU's accumulated drift using the absolute measurement.
- The **Kalman gain $K_k$** automatically balances trust between the prediction (IMU) and the measurement (GPS/Mag) based on their noise covariances $Q$ and $R$.
- The result is a globally referenced, smooth, high-rate pose estimate that is more accurate than any single sensor alone.

**Why this architecture is used in industry:**
- GPS has good long-term accuracy but low update rate and fails indoors.
- IMU has high update rate and works anywhere but drifts.
- Magnetometer provides absolute heading but is noisy.
- Their combination yields a robust, drift-corrected global pose. (7 marks: 2 marks block diagram, 3 marks Kalman filter role, 2 marks justification)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **trilateration** (distance-based, used in GPS) with **triangulation** (angle-based, used in surveying). GPS does *not* measure angles — only distances. Marks are deducted for this.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** In the complementary filter, students forget to apply the **gyroscope integration** $\omega \cdot \Delta t$ *before* blending with the accelerometer term. The two terms must be computed separately and then summed.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Students write the magnetometer heading as $\arctan(B_y/B_x)$ without the negative sign. The negative sign is essential and comes from the right-hand rule of the magnetic field.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** When asked "why 4 satellites?", many students say "to get 3D position" — but 3 satellites are *not* enough because of the clock bias. The 4th satellite is needed to resolve the time error.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Using the wrong units (degrees vs radians) in trigonometric functions. Always state units clearly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Global pose** = 6-DOF state $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ in the world frame; **absolute** and **drift-free**.
- **Local pose** = state relative to a starting frame; suffers from **drift** due to integration of noisy signals.
- **GPS** uses **trilateration** with at least **4 satellites** to solve for $(x, y, z)$ and clock bias $\delta t$. Civilian accuracy ~2.5–10 m; **RTK-GPS** improves to ~1–10 cm.
- **Pseudorange** $d_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$ — contains clock bias.
- **Magnetometer** gives **absolute heading** $\psi = \arctan(-B_y/B_x)$ (tilt-compensated version required when the robot is tilted).
- **Accelerometer** at rest gives **absolute roll and pitch** via gravity projection: $\theta = \arcsin(a_x/g)$, $\phi = \arcsin(a_y/g)$.
- **Gyroscope** gives **angular rate** — must be integrated → drifts. Cannot give global pose alone.
- **IMU** = accelerometer + gyroscope; gives **relative** pose, drifts. To get **global** pose, IMU *must* be fused with GPS / magnetometer.
- **Complementary filter** for orientation: $\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha \cdot \theta_{gyro} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \theta_{accel}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.95$–$0.98$.
- **Kalman filter** is the standard fusion algorithm combining high-rate IMU with low-rate absolute sensors. State: $\hat{x}$, $P$. Predict with $A$, update with $K = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$.
- **Key constants:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$, $g = 9.80665 \, m/s^2$, $B_{Earth} \approx 25$–$65 \, \mu T$, GPS altitude $\approx 20{,}200$ km.
- **Sensor fusion principle:** *No single sensor can provide robust global pose; the state-of-the-art approach is to fuse absolute (GPS/Mag) with relative (IMU/Odometry) sensors.*
- **Tilt compensation** is essential for accurate magnetometer heading on uneven terrain.
- **Magnetometer errors:** hard-iron, soft-iron, declination, EMI — must calibrate.
- **GPS errors:** ionospheric, tropospheric, multipath, satellite clock, receiver noise.
- **Application areas:** autonomous cars (RTK-GPS + IMU + Lidar), agricultural robots (GPS-guided), drones (GPS + IMU + Mag + Baro), marine navigation (DGPS), search-and-rescue (GPS + IMU + thermal).
- **Exam tip:** Always state units (radians vs degrees), use the proper negative sign in $\arctan(-B_y/B_x)$, and emphasize the 4-satellite requirement for clock bias correction.
- **Magnetic declination** must be added to convert magnetic heading to **true North** — this is the difference between *magnetic* North and *geographic* North and varies with location.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Images as Two Dimensional Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Images as Two Dimensional Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of mobile robotics and machine vision, an **image** is mathematically modeled as a **two-dimensional (2D) continuous signal** that represents the spatial distribution of light intensity (or radiance) reflected from a physical scene. Formally, an image is expressed as a function of two spatial variables:

$$f(x, y)$$

where $(x, y)$ denotes the 2D spatial coordinates in the image plane, and the scalar value $f(x, y)$ represents the **intensity** (brightness, luminance, or gray level) at that coordinate. For color images, $f$ is generalized to a vector-valued function $\mathbf{f}(x, y)$ containing multiple spectral channels (e.g., RGB). A digital image is obtained by spatially **sampling** and intensity-wise **quantizing** this continuous function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Outcome-Based Education (OBE), this topic directly maps to **CO3 – "Apply signal processing concepts to interpret 2D sensor data for robotic perception."** It is foundational for later modules covering Visual Odometry, Optical Flow, and SLAM.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine looking at a painting on a wall. To an artist, the painting is a smooth, continuous swirl of colors. However, when you take a **photograph** of that painting, your camera breaks the continuous image into a finite grid of tiny colored squares called **pixels**. Each pixel records a single average color value from a small region of the original scene.

- The **continuous painting** = the **analog 2D signal** $f(x, y)$
- The **photograph's grid of pixels** = the **digitally sampled 2D signal** $f[m, n]$
- The number of pixels per inch = **spatial sampling rate** (in samples/millimeter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Robotics Analogy:** When a mobile robot's onboard camera (e.g., on a TurtleBot or a DJI drone) captures a frame, the image sensor (CMOS/CCD) is essentially performing **2D sampling** of the optical field at a rate determined by the sensor's pixel pitch. The robot's processor then treats this pixel matrix as a 2D discrete signal for tasks like lane detection, object recognition, and obstacle avoidance.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **Spatial Sampling Rate:** measured in **pixels per millimeter (px/mm)** or equivalently **dots per inch (DPI)**.
- **Bit Depth (Quantization):** typically **8 bits/pixel** (256 gray levels) for standard vision, **10–12 bits** for industrial cameras, **16 bits** for scientific imaging.
- **Standard Image Resolution:** **640 × 480 (VGA)**, **1280 × 720 (HD)**, **1920 × 1080 (Full HD)**, **3840 × 2160 (4K UHD)**.
- **Sensor Pixel Pitch:** typically **1.4 µm to 5.0 µm** for modern CMOS sensors.
- **Dynamic Range:** the ratio between maximum and minimum recordable intensity, measured in **decibels (dB)** or **stops**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Callout – Why 2D Signal Theory Matters in Robotics:** Traditional 1D signal processing (audio, vibration analysis) is insufficient for vision because images carry information across **two independent spatial axes** simultaneously. Operations like edge detection, feature extraction, and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are all direct descendants of 2D signal processing theory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Continuous 2D Image Signal

A monochrome (grayscale) image is mathematically modeled as a **2D continuous, real-valued function**:

$$f(x, y) : \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

where $x$ and $y$ are independent spatial variables (measured in meters), and the output is non-negative and bounded:

$$0 \leq f(x, y) \leq L_{\max}$$

Here, $L_{\max}$ is the maximum intensity determined by the imaging system's dynamic range. The function is characterized by:
- **Spatial frequency content** in both x and y directions.
- **Illumination** $i(x, y)$ and **reflectance** $r(x, y)$ components:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

### 2.2 Digital Image – Discretization in Two Dimensions

To convert a continuous image into a digital image, two operations are performed:
1. **Sampling** along the $(x, y)$ plane at regular intervals $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$.
2. **Quantization** of the intensity values into discrete levels.

The discretized image is represented as a 2D matrix $f[m, n]$:

$$f[m, n] = f(x_0 + m \Delta x, \ y_0 + n \Delta y), \quad m = 0, 1, \dots, M-1, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

where:
- $M$ = number of rows (image height in pixels)
- $N$ = number of columns (image width in pixels)
- $\Delta x, \Delta y$ = sampling intervals
- $L$ = number of quantization levels (typically $L = 2^{k}$ for $k$-bit depth)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous Image Function | $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ | $x, y$ in m; $f$ in cd/m² (luminance) |
| 2D Sampling | $f[m, n] = f(x_0 + m\Delta x, y_0 + n\Delta y)$ | $m, n$ are integer indices |
| 2D Impulse (Kronecker Delta) | $\delta[m, n] = \begin{cases} 1, & m = 0, n = 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Unit sample in 2D discrete domain |
| 2D Unit Step | $u[m, n] = \begin{cases} 1, & m \geq 0, n \geq 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Quadrant definition |
| 2D Linear Convolution | $g[m, n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{l=-\infty}^{\infty} f[k, l] \cdot h[m-k, n-l]$ | LSI systems (filters) |
| 2D Continuous Fourier Transform (2D-CFT) | $F(u, v) = \iint_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) e^{-j 2 \pi (ux + vy)} \, dx \, dy$ | $u, v$ in cycles/m (spatial frequency) |
| 2D Discrete Fourier Transform (2D-DFT) | $F[k, l] = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f[m, n] e^{-j 2 \pi (km/M + ln/N)}$ | $k, l$ are frequency indices |
| 2D Inverse DFT | $f[m, n] = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \sum_{l=0}^{N-1} F[k, l] e^{j 2 \pi (km/M + ln/N)}$ | Reconstruction formula |
| 2D Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_{s,x} \geq 2 B_{x}, \quad f_{s,y} \geq 2 B_{y}$ | $B$ = max spatial frequency |
| Image Memory Size | $S = M \times N \times k$ bits | $k$ = bit depth |
| 2D Convolution Theorem | $\mathcal{F}\{f * h\} = F \cdot H$ | Multiplication in freq. domain |
| Separable Kernel Property | $h[m, n] = h_1[m] \cdot h_2[n]$ | Reduces 2D conv to two 1D convs |
| Image Gradient (Sobel form) | $\nabla f = \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \right)$ | Used in edge detection |
| Gradient Magnitude | $\vert \nabla f \vert = \sqrt{G_x^{2} + G_y^{2}}$ | Output of edge filter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When writing equations in markdown tables, never use the `|` character directly to denote absolute value or modulus (e.g., $\vert f \vert$). This will break the table parser. Always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` inside `$$` blocks, or use parentheses like $(f)$.
