> 5. **Using a step size $\alpha$ that is too large in gradient descent** without justification. Mention *line search* (Armijo rule) or *trust region* to be safe. (−1 mark)
> 6. **Conflating *optimal control* and *optimal path*.** A path is a *geometric* object; a trajectory is *time-parameterized*. The objective function differs! (−1 mark)

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **objective function** $J$ maps a robot's state/control to a single scalar cost; **minimize** it subject to constraints.
- ✅ The four problem components: **decision variables, objective, constraints, boundary conditions**.
- ✅ The **gradient** $\nabla J$ points in the direction of *steepest increase*; $-\nabla J$ is the descent direction.
- ✅ **Necessary optimality:** $\nabla J = \mathbf{0}$ (unconstrained) or KKT (constrained).
- ✅ **Sufficient optimality:** Hessian $\nabla^2 J \succ 0$ (unconstrained) or second-order KKT (constrained).
- ✅ The **shortest path** in a flat plane is a **straight line** (Euler–Lagrange result).
- ✅ **Lagrange multipliers** $\lambda$ are *shadow prices* — the rate of change of optimal cost with respect to a unit relaxation of a constraint.
- ✅ **Complementary slackness** $\lambda g = 0$ is the signature of KKT — either the constraint is binding, or its multiplier is zero, never both nonzero.
- ✅ **Quadratic cost with positive-definite $Q$** has a unique global minimum at $\mathbf{x}^\star = -Q^{-1}\mathbf{c}$.
- ✅ **Steepest-descent** update: $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - \alpha \nabla J(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$.
- ✅ **Newton's update** (faster but costlier): $\mathbf{x}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{x}^{(k)} - H^{-1}\nabla J$.
- ✅ **Pontryagin's Minimum Principle** reduces continuous optimal control to a two-point boundary-value problem.
- ✅ **Algorithm selection rule of thumb:** quadratic → Newton/LQR; graph → A*/D*; continuous no-gradient → GA/PSO/SA; continuous with gradient → BFGS; high-D sampling → RRT*/PRM*.
- ✅ In real robots, multi-objective cost = weighted sum of $J_{task}, J_{effort}, J_{safety}, J_{reg}$, with safety weight set highest.
- ✅ **LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)** is the workhorse of modern MPC; cost is $\int (x^\top Q x + u^\top R u)\,dt$ with $Q, R \succ 0$.
- ✅ Always specify the **integration variable** ($s$ for arc-length, $t$ for time) and the **norm** (Euclidean $\Vert \cdot \Vert$) when writing $J$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - "**G**radient **Z**ero, **H**essian **P**ositive ⇒ Minimum" — **GZHP**.
> - "**KKT = Stationarity + Feasibility + Slackness**" — **SFS**.
> - "**Lagrange = Cost + λ × Constraint**" — pure recipe.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Execution: Reactive Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Task Execution: Reactive Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactive Control** is a task-execution paradigm in mobile robotics in which the robot's immediate motor actions are generated as a direct, time-critical mapping from the current sensory input to actuator commands, **without the explicit construction, maintenance, or reasoning over an internal world model**.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme sense, Reactive Control belongs to the family of **behaviour-based control architectures** (popularized by Rodney Brooks, MIT AI Lab, 1986). It is characterized by a tight **Perception → Action coupling** where sensor data and actuator responses are linked within a single control cycle, typically operating at the robot's native sensor refresh rate (often **10 Hz to 100 Hz** depending on the platform).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Reactive Control sits at the lowest tier of the well-known **Three-Layer Robot Control Hierarchy**:
> 1. **Reactive Layer (lowest):** Instantaneous sensor-driven response.
> 2. **Executive / Sequencing Layer (middle):** Task planning and coordination.
> 3. **Deliberative Layer (highest):** Strategic planning using world models.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a dark, unfamiliar room. You do **not** stop to build a complete 3D map of every chair, table, and book on the floor. Instead, your foot **senses** the edge of a step → your brain **instantly commands** your leg to lift higher. Your hand brushes a wall → it reflexively **pulls back**. You are reacting directly to the world *as you sense it*, in the moment.

> A mobile robot using reactive control behaves exactly like a person navigating a dark room — it does not think, it **reacts**.

The reaction time of a biological reflex arc is roughly **30 to 50 milliseconds**. A well-tuned reactive controller on a mobile robot (e.g., a Roomba vacuum) can produce motor updates every **10 to 20 milliseconds**, often faster than human reflexes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Reactive control is the opposite of *Deliberative control*. A deliberative robot follows the classical **Sense → Plan → Act (SPA)** paradigm. A reactive robot follows **Sense → Act (SA)** — there is **no planning step**.

## 1.3 When is Reactive Control Used?

Reactive control is preferred when:
- The environment is **dynamic, unstructured, or unknown**.
- The task is **time-critical** (collision avoidance, obstacle following).
- Computational resources are **limited** (small embedded boards like Arduino, STM32).
- The robot must operate in **cluttered, human-shared spaces**.

**Standard Metric:** The control loop latency — the time between sensor read and motor command — is often targeted to be **below 50 ms** for indoor mobile robots.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Perception–Action Loop

Reactive control operates as a closed feedback loop. The architecture can be expressed conceptually as:

$$
\mathbf{u}(t) = f\bigl(\mathbf{s}(t)\bigr)
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{s}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **sensor vector** at time $t$ (e.g., range readings, camera features, IR intensities).
- $\mathbf{u}(t) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **actuator / velocity command** at time $t$.
- $f(\cdot)$ is the **reactive mapping** — typically a fixed, stateless, or minimally stateful function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A purely reactive system has **no internal state** (or only minimal state). The command at time $t$ depends *only* on the sensor input at time $t$. This is what makes the response instantaneous.

## 2.2 Subsumption Architecture (Rodney Brooks, 1986)

The most famous reactive framework is the **Subsumption Architecture**, which decomposes robot behaviour into **layers of competence** arranged hierarchically:

| Layer (Bottom = Highest Priority) | Behaviour | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 0 (Lowest) | Wander / Avoid Obstacles | Random walk with collision check |
| Layer 1 | Explore / Follow Walls | Wall-following behaviour |
| Layer 2 | Build Maps | (Often skipped in pure reactive) |
| Layer 3 | Identify Objects | Vision-based classification |
| Layer 4 (Highest) | Plan / Reason | Deliberative planner |

Each higher layer **subsumes** (overrides) the lower layers when it produces output. Lower layers run continuously and act as **safety reflexes**.

## 2.3 Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986) — The Mathematical Backbone

The Potential Field method is the canonical mathematical formulation of reactive control. The robot is treated as a charged particle moving in a **force field** generated by obstacles and goals.

### 2.3.1 Attractive Potential (Goal-Seeking)

The goal exerts an attractive force pulling the robot toward it:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{att}} \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q)
$$

where:
- $q = (x, y)$ is the robot's current position.
- $\rho_{\text{goal}}(q) = \Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert$ is the **Euclidean distance** from robot to goal.
- $k_{\text{att}}$ is the **attractive gain** (positive scalar, units: N/m or V/m).

The corresponding attractive **force** is the negative gradient:

$$
F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}}(q) = -k_{\text{att}} \bigl(q - q_{\text{goal}}\bigr)
$$

> **Conical vs Parabolic:** The simple quadratic form above produces a *parabolic* well. For large $\rho_{\text{goal}}$ a *conical* form $U_{\text{att}} = k_{\text{att}} \rho_{\text{goal}}$ is often used to prevent excessive force at distant goals.

### 2.3.2 Repulsive Potential (Obstacle Avoidance)

Each obstacle $i$ generates a repulsive potential active only within an influence distance $d_{\text{inf}}$:

$$
U_{\text{rep},i}(q) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{rep}} \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs},i}(q)} - \dfrac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho_{\text{obs},i}(q) \le d_{\text{inf}} \\[6pt]
0 & \text{if } \rho_{\text{obs},i}(q) > d_{\text{inf}}
\end{cases}
$$

The corresponding repulsive force:

$$
F_{\text{rep},i}(q) =
\begin{cases}
k_{\text{rep}} \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs},i}(q)} - \dfrac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs},i}^{2}(q)} \dfrac{q - q_{\text{obs},i}}{\rho_{\text{obs},i}(q)} & \text{if } \rho_{\text{obs},i} \le d_{\text{inf}} \\[6pt]
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.3.3 Total Force (Superposition Principle)

Because potentials are scalar fields, forces from multiple sources **superpose linearly**:

$$
F_{\text{total}}(q) = F_{\text{att}}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_{\text{rep},i}(q)
$$

The robot's velocity command is then proportional to this total force:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{cmd}} = v_{\max} \cdot \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{\Vert F_{\text{total}} \Vert + \varepsilon}
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is a small constant to avoid division by zero.

## 2.4 Braitenberg Vehicles (Historical Foundation)

Valentino Braitenberg's 1984 thought experiments describe simple vehicles whose sensors are **directly connected** to motors via excitatory or inhibitory links. They exhibit surprisingly life-like behaviours (fear, aggression, love) using only reactive wiring.

**Vehicle 2a (Fear):** Light sensor connects **inversely** to motor — robot flees from light.

**Vehicle 3a (Aggression):** Cross-coupled sensors to motors — robot chases light aggressively.

## 2.5 Motor Schemas (Arkin, 1987)

Ronald Arkin's **Motor Schema Theory** generalises reactive control using **vector fields** for each behaviour (move-to-goal, avoid-obstacle, follow, dock, wander). Output vectors are summed, with priority-based weighting:

$$
\mathbf{V}_{\text{output}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot \mathbf{V}_i
$$

where $w_i$ is a behaviour-specific weight (often dynamic, modulated by perceptual triggers).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{s}(t)$ | Sensor vector at time $t$ | V, m, rad | Sampled at $10$ to $100$ Hz |
| $\mathbf{u}(t)$ | Actuator / velocity command | m/s, rad/s | Clipped to $\pm v_{\max}$ |
| $f(\cdot)$ | Reactive mapping function | — | Often a fixed lookup or NN |
| $U_{\text{att}}$ | Attractive potential | J (or arb. units) | Grows quadratically with $\rho_{\text{goal}}$ |
| $U_{\text{rep}}$ | Repulsive potential | J | Zero when $\rho_{\text{obs}} > d_{\text{inf}}$ |
| $k_{\text{att}}$ | Attractive gain | N/m | Tune to dominate at goal |
| $k_{\text{rep}}$ | Repulsive gain | N/m | Tune to dominate near obstacles |
| $d_{\text{inf}}$ | Influence distance of obstacle | m | Typically $1$ to $3$ m for indoor robots |
| $\rho_{\text{goal}}$ | Distance to goal | m | $\rho_{\text{goal}} \geq 0$ |
| $\rho_{\text{obs},i}$ | Distance to obstacle $i$ | m | $\rho_{\text{obs},i} > 0$ |
| $v_{\max}$ | Maximum linear speed | m/s | $0.3$ to $1.0$ m/s for indoor |
| $w_i$ | Behaviour weight (schemas) | dimensionless | $0 \leq w_i \leq 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Engineering:** Potential fields are used today in **AGV fleets in Amazon warehouses**, **surgical robots** for needle steering, **Mars rovers** (Curiosity uses elements of reactive control in its autonomous navigation), and **autonomous vacuum cleaners** like Roomba. The same math underpins modern **Artificial Potential Field (APF) variants** such as VFH (Vector Field Histogram) and Dynamic Window Approach (DWA).

## 2.7 Local Minima Problem — The Critical Limitation

A pure gradient-descent on $F_{\text{total}}$ can cause the robot to get stuck at points where $F_{\text{total}} = 0$ but the goal is not reached. These are called **local minima** of the combined potential field.

**Symptoms of local minima:**
- Robot oscillates in a small region.
- Robot stops far from goal.
- Robot moves in a closed loop.

**Remedies used in practice:**
1. Add a **random walk** behaviour that triggers when velocity drops below a threshold.
2. Use a **virtual obstacle** / **tangential field** near the stuck point.
3. Escalate to a **deliberative layer** that replans the global path.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition, every gradient computation, and every numerical evaluation is shown explicitly. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: Attractive Force from the Attractive Potential

### Step 1 — Write the attractive potential

We begin with the standard quadratic attractive potential anchored at the goal point $q_{\text{goal}} = (x_g, y_g)$:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(x, y) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{att}} \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(x, y)
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the squared distance

The squared Euclidean distance from robot position $(x, y)$ to goal $(x_g, y_g)$ is:

$$
\rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(x, y) = (x - x_g)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2}
$$

Therefore the potential becomes:

$$
U_{\text{att}}(x, y) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{att}} \left[(x - x_g)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2}\right]
$$

### Step 3 — Take the partial derivative with respect to $x$

Using the chain rule, the partial derivative of $U_{\text{att}}$ with respect to $x$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{att}} \cdot 2 (x - x_g) = k_{\text{att}} (x - x_g)
$$

### Step 4 — Take the partial derivative with respect to $y$

By identical reasoning:

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y} = k_{\text{att}} (y - y_g)
$$

### Step 5 — Form the gradient vector

The gradient of the scalar field $U_{\text{att}}$ in 2D is:

$$
\nabla U_{\text{att}} = \left[\frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial x},\; \frac{\partial U_{\text{att}}}{\partial y}\right]^{\!\top} = k_{\text{att}} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_g \\ y - y_g \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the negative gradient to get the force

Force is the negative gradient of potential (objects move from high to low potential):

$$
F_{\text{att}} = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -k_{\text{att}} \begin{bmatrix} x - x_g \\ y - y_g \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 7 — Interpret the result

This force is a **restoring spring force** that linearly pulls the robot toward the goal. The magnitude is:

$$
\Vert F_{\text{att}} \Vert = k_{\text{att}} \, \rho_{\text{goal}}
$$

**Engineering insight:** The further the robot is from the goal, the stronger the pull — exactly like a virtual spring stretched between the robot and the goal.

## 3.2 Derivation: Repulsive Force from the Repulsive Potential

### Step 1 — State the repulsive potential for one obstacle

For a single obstacle at position $q_{\text{obs}} = (x_o, y_o)$:

$$
U_{\text{rep}}(x, y) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{\rho(x, y)} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right)^{2}
$$

where $\rho(x, y) = \sqrt{(x - x_o)^{2} + (y - y_g)^{2}}$ and the expression is active only when $\rho \le d_{\text{inf}}$.

### Step 2 — Introduce a short-hand

Let $r = \rho(x, y)$ for clarity. Then:

$$
U_{\text{rep}} = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{rep}} \left(r^{-1} - d_{\text{inf}}^{-1}\right)^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate the inner term with respect to $r$

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r^{-1} - d_{\text{inf}}^{-1}\right) = -r^{-2}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the chain rule for $\partial U_{\text{rep}} / \partial r$

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{rep}} \cdot 2 \left(r^{-1} - d_{\text{inf}}^{-1}\right) \cdot \left(-r^{-2}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial r} = -k_{\text{rep}} \left(r^{-1} - d_{\text{inf}}^{-1}\right) r^{-2}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to partial derivatives with respect to $x$ and $y$

We use the chain rule with $\partial r / \partial x = (x - x_o) / r$ and $\partial r / \partial y = (y - y_o) / r$:

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial r} \cdot \frac{\partial r}{\partial x} = -k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \frac{1}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{x - x_o}{r}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial y} = -k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \frac{1}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{y - y_o}{r}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the negative gradient to get the force

$$
F_{\text{rep},x} = -\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial x} = k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \frac{1}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{x - x_o}{r}
$$

$$
F_{\text{rep},y} = -\frac{\partial U_{\text{rep}}}{\partial y} = k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \frac{1}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{y - y_o}{r}
$$

### Step 7 — Vector form

$$
F_{\text{rep}} = k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \frac{1}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{(x - x_o, \; y - y_o)}{r}
$$

**Engineering insight:** The factor $\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}$ is zero when $r = d_{\text{inf}}$, so the repulsive force smoothly vanishes at the influence boundary — there is no discontinuity.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A differential-drive mobile robot is at position $q = (2, 3)$ m. The goal is at $q_{\text{goal}} = (10, 8)$ m. A single circular obstacle is centred at $q_{\text{obs}} = (4, 4)$ m with influence distance $d_{\text{inf}} = 2$ m. Given gains $k_{\text{att}} = 1.0$ N/m and $k_{\text{rep}} = 0.5$ N/m, compute the total force vector on the robot.

### Step 1 — Compute goal distance

$$
\rho_{\text{goal}} = \sqrt{(2 - 10)^{2} + (3 - 8)^{2}} = \sqrt{64 + 25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute attractive force

$$
F_{\text{att},x} = -k_{\text{att}} (x - x_g) = -1.0 \cdot (2 - 10) = 8.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{att},y} = -k_{\text{att}} (y - y_g) = -1.0 \cdot (3 - 8) = 5.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{att}} = (8.0, \; 5.0) \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute obstacle distance

$$
\rho_{\text{obs}} = \sqrt{(2 - 4)^{2} + (3 - 4)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Check influence condition

$$
\rho_{\text{obs}} = 2.236 \text{ m} > d_{\text{inf}} = 2.0 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{obstacle has NO effect at this instant}
$$

### Step 5 — Total force

$$
F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{att}} + F_{\text{rep}} = (8.0, \; 5.0) + (0, 0) = (8.0, \; 5.0) \text{ N}
$$

### Step 6 — Normalized velocity command

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{cmd}} = v_{\max} \cdot \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{\Vert F_{\text{total}} \Vert + \varepsilon}
$$

With $v_{\max} = 0.5$ m/s, $\varepsilon = 0.01$, and $\Vert F_{\text{total}} \Vert = \sqrt{64 + 25} = \sqrt{89} \approx 9.43$:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{cmd}} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{(8.0, 5.0)}{9.44} \approx (0.424, \; 0.265) \text{ m/s}
$$

**Interpretation:** The robot will move at approximately **0.42 m/s** along the $x$-axis and **0.27 m/s** along the $y$-axis, smoothly heading toward the goal.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reactive Control Loop (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the tight perception–action loop characteristic of reactive control, contrasted with the deliberative Sense-Plan-Act loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment / World] --> B[Sensors]
    B --> C[Sensor Pre-processing]
    C --> D{Reactive Mapping f of s}
    D --> E[Motor / Actuator Command]
    E --> F[Wheels / Motors]
    F --> A
    G[NO World Model] -.-> D
    H[NO Planner] -.-> D
    I[NO Internal Map] -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three dashed lines (G, H, I) emphasise the defining absence in reactive control: **no world model, no planner, no internal map** of the environment is maintained.

## 4.2 Subsumption Architecture Stack

This block diagram shows the layered structure of Brooks' Subsumption Architecture, where higher layers can suppress (subsume) the outputs of lower layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Layer 0 - Avoid Obstacles] --> M0[Suppression of L0]
    L1[Layer 1 - Wander Randomly] --> M0
    L2[Layer 2 - Explore Environment] --> M1[Suppression of L1]
    L3[Layer 3 - Map Building] --> M2[Suppression of L2]
    L4[Layer 4 - Plan and Reason] --> M3[Suppression of L3]
    M0 --> OUT[Final Motor Command]
    M1 --> OUT
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    L4 --> M3
    L3 --> M2
    L2 --> M1
    L1 --> M0
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Layer 0 is the most basic survival reflex. Layer 4 is the most intelligent planning behaviour. Each higher layer has the power to **suppress** the lower ones, but lower layers remain active when not suppressed — this gives reactive systems their trademark **robustness to upper-layer failure**.

## 4.3 Potential Field Functional Architecture

A block-level schematic of how the Potential Field method processes a single sensor cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Range Sensors] --> DEC[Compute Distances to Obstacles]
    DEC --> REP[Repulsive Potential Generator]
    GOAL[Goal Coordinates] --> ATT[Attractive Potential Generator]
    REP --> SUM[Vector Superposition]
    ATT --> SUM
    SUM --> NORM[Normalize to Max Velocity]
    NORM --> CMD[Velocity Command to Motors]
    GA[k_att Gain] -.-> ATT
    GR[k_rep Gain] -.-> REP
    DI[d_influence Parameter] -.-> REP
    VM[v_max Parameter] -.-> NORM
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the **gains** $k_{\text{att}}$, $k_{\text{rep}}$ and the parameter $d_{\text{inf}}$ are external tuning inputs — they are *not* derived from sensors. This is the key difference from a learning-based controller.

## 4.4 Three-Layer Robot Control Hierarchy (Contextual Architecture)

Reactive control does not stand alone; in modern KTU-style hybrid systems it forms the lowest layer of a three-tier hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HIGH[Deliberative Layer]
        D1[Global Path Planner]
        D2[World Model and Map]
    end
    subgraph MID[Executive / Sequencing Layer]
        E1[Task Coordinator]
        E2[Sub-goal Selector]
    end
    subgraph LOW[Reactive Layer]
        R1[Obstacle Avoidance]
        R2[Goal Seeking]
        R3[Wall Following]
    end
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> R1
    E2 --> R2
    E2 --> R3
    R1 --> ACT[Actuators]
    R2 --> ACT
    R3 --> ACT
    SENS[All Sensors] --> R1
    SENS --> R2
    SENS --> R3
    SENS --> D2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Information flows **downward** (plans become sub-goals become motor commands) and **sensory data flows upward** at all levels. The reactive layer is the only one that talks to the actuators in real time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]

**Q: Define Reactive Control in mobile robotics. List any two of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reactive Control** is a control paradigm in which the robot's actuator commands are generated as a direct function of the current sensory input, **without the construction or use of an internal world model** and without a separate planning step.

**Key Characteristics (any two):**
1. **Tight perception–action coupling** — sensor reading and motor response occur within the same control cycle (typically under 50 ms).
2. **No internal world model** — the robot does not maintain a global map; it reacts only to what is sensed at the current instant.
3. **Stateless or minimally stateful** — the command depends only on present input, not on memory of past inputs.
4. **Robust to dynamic environments** — since there is no model to become outdated, the system handles moving obstacles naturally.

> *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for two correct characteristics)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the Subsumption Architecture? Who proposed it and in which year?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Subsumption Architecture** is a reactive, behaviour-based robot control framework in which the overall robot behaviour is decomposed into a hierarchy of **layers of competence**. Each higher layer can **subsume** (override) the outputs of lower layers, while lower layers continue to run as safety reflexes.

- **Proposed by:** Rodney Brooks
- **Year:** 1986
- **Institution:** MIT Artificial Intelligence Laboratory

> *(1 mark each for definition, proposer, and year — total 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **Potential Field Method** of reactive obstacle avoidance with neat mathematical formulation. Derive the expressions for **attractive** and **repulsive** forces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot is at position $(1, 2)$ m. The goal is at $(7, 6)$ m. A circular obstacle is at $(3, 3)$ m with influence distance $d_{\text{inf}} = 1.5$ m. Gains are $k_{\text{att}} = 1.0$ and $k_{\text{rep}} = 0.8$. Compute the **total force** on the robot and the **unit vector direction** of its motion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Concept (2 marks):** The Potential Field Method, introduced by **Oussama Khatib in 1986**, treats the robot as a charged particle moving in a 2D force field. The **goal acts as an attractive pole**, while each **obstacle acts as a repulsive pole**. The robot follows the negative gradient of the combined potential.

2. **Attractive Potential and Force (2.5 marks):**

$$
U_{\text{att}}(q) = \frac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{att}} \, \rho_{\text{goal}}^{2}(q), \quad \rho_{\text{goal}} = \Vert q - q_{\text{goal}} \Vert
$$

Taking the negative gradient:

$$
F_{\text{att}}(q) = -\nabla U_{\text{att}} = -k_{\text{att}} \bigl(q - q_{\text{goal}}\bigr)
$$

This is a linear restoring (spring-like) force pointing from the robot toward the goal.

3. **Repulsive Potential and Force (2.5 marks):**

$$
U_{\text{rep},i}(q) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2} \, k_{\text{rep}} \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs},i}} - \dfrac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right)^{2} & \rho_{\text{obs},i} \le d_{\text{inf}} \\
0 & \rho_{\text{obs},i} > d_{\text{inf}}
\end{cases}
$$

The corresponding repulsive force on the robot is:

$$
F_{\text{rep},i}(q) = k_{\text{rep}} \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs},i}} - \dfrac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \dfrac{1}{\rho_{\text{obs},i}^{2}} \cdot \hat{n}_{i}
$$

where $\hat{n}_{i}$ is the unit vector pointing from the obstacle to the robot.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Goal distance:**

$$
\rho_{\text{goal}} = \sqrt{(1-7)^{2} + (2-6)^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.211 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Attractive force:**

$$
F_{\text{att},x} = -1.0 \cdot (1 - 7) = 6.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{att},y} = -1.0 \cdot (2 - 6) = 4.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Obstacle distance:**

$$
\rho_{\text{obs}} = \sqrt{(1-3)^{2} + (2-3)^{2}} = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Influence check:**

$$
\rho_{\text{obs}} = 2.236 \text{ m} > d_{\text{inf}} = 1.5 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{\text{rep}} = (0, 0)
$$

**Step 5 — Total force:**

$$
F_{\text{total}} = (6.0, \; 4.0) \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude and unit vector:**

$$
\Vert F_{\text{total}} \Vert = \sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.211 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\hat{u} = \frac{F_{\text{total}}}{\Vert F_{\text{total}} \Vert} = \left( \frac{6.0}{7.211}, \; \frac{4.0}{7.211} \right) \approx (0.832, \; 0.555)
$$

> **Result:** The robot moves in the direction $(0.832, \; 0.555)$ — i.e., toward the upper-right, exactly along the line to the goal.

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to A) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** Compare **Reactive Control** and **Deliberative Control** in mobile robotics. Highlight at least **four points of difference**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Subsumption Architecture** with a suitable diagram. Explain how higher layers **suppress** lower layers with one practical example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Aspect | Reactive Control | Deliberative Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Paradigm** | Sense → Act (SA) | Sense → Plan → Act (SPA) |
| **World Model** | None or minimal | Complete geometric / semantic map |
| **Computation** | Low, real-time | High, often off-board |
| **Response Time** | Milliseconds | Seconds to minutes |
| **Environment** | Dynamic, unknown | Known, static |
| **Robustness** | High (no model to fail) | Lower (model can be wrong) |
| **Optimality** | Local optima | Global optimum (when solvable) |
| **Examples** | Roomba, Braitenberg vehicles | GPS-route planners, A* on a map |

> *(1 mark for each of 4 valid comparison points + 3 marks for explanation/table — distribute as per KTU valuation key)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

The **Subsumption Architecture**, proposed by **Rodney Brooks (1986)** at MIT, organises robot control as a stack of **task-achieving layers of competence**. Each layer is a complete behaviour-producing module that maps sensors to actuators.

**Layering (from lowest priority to highest):**

| Layer | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| 0 | Avoid Obstacles (collision safety) |
| 1 | Wander (random exploration) |
| 2 | Follow Walls (structured exploration) |
| 3 | Identify Objects (perception-based reasoning) |
| 4 | Plan Routes (deliberative reasoning) |

**Suppression Mechanism:**

A higher layer can **inhibit** the output of a lower layer. For example, suppose the robot is wandering (Layer 1 active) and its vision system (Layer 3) identifies the kitchen as the target room. Layer 3 sends a **suppression signal** to Layer 1, telling it to halt. Layer 1's motor output is replaced by Layer 3's output. Meanwhile, Layer 0 (avoid obstacles) **continues to run** to ensure the robot does not bump into furniture on the way.

> **Key property:** Lower layers can never be permanently disabled by higher layers — they always run in the background as **safety reflexes**. This gives Subsumption its signature **fault-tolerance**.

**Diagram (text representation, as required by the question):**

```
Layer 4: Plan / Reason         ──► Suppresses Layer 3
Layer 3: Identify Objects      ──► Suppresses Layer 2
Layer 2: Follow Walls          ──► Suppresses Layer 1
Layer 1: Wander                ──► Suppresses Layer 0 (only when needed)
Layer 0: Avoid Obstacles       ──► ALWAYS ACTIVE (safety floor)
                                      ↓
                              [ Final Motor Command ]
```

> *(3.5 marks for diagram, 2 marks for suppression explanation, 1.5 marks for the practical example)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the influence-distance condition** (3 to 4 marks lost per question): The repulsive potential is **zero** when $\rho_{\text{obs}} > d_{\text{inf}}$. Many students wrongly compute a repulsive force even when the obstacle is outside the influence zone.
>
> 2. **Sign error in the attractive force** (1 to 2 marks): The attractive force is $-k_{\text{att}}(q - q_{\text{goal}})$, which **expands to** $+k_{\text{att}}(q_{\text{goal}} - q)$. Many students write it as $+k_{\text{att}}(q - q_{\text{goal}})$ and end up with a force pushing the robot *away* from the goal.
>
> 3. **Confusing reactive with hybrid control** (2 to 3 marks): Reactive control is **purely** Sense–Act. The moment you mention a planner, a map, or a global path, you have stepped into **hybrid** control territory. Examiners will deduct marks if the question asks for *reactive* and the answer describes a *deliberative* system.
>
> 4. **Omitting the units** (1 mark): Force in Newtons, distance in metres, gain in N/m. Forgetting units costs easy marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon$ regularisation** (1 mark): When normalising the velocity command, division by zero must be avoided using a small positive constant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reactive Control = Sense → Act.** No model, no planner, no map.
- **Subsumption Architecture (Brooks, 1986)** layers behaviours; higher layers **suppress** lower layers; **lowest layer is always active** for safety.
- **Potential Field Method (Khatib, 1986)** is the canonical mathematical formulation:
  - $F_{\text{att}} = -k_{\text{att}}(q - q_{\text{goal}})$
  - $F_{\text{rep},i} = k_{\text{rep}} \left(\frac{1}{\rho_i} - \frac{1}{d_{\text{inf}}}\right) \frac{1}{\rho_i^{2}} \hat{n}_{i}$ (active only when $\rho_i \le d_{\text{inf}}$)
- **Total force = superposition** of attractive and all repulsive forces.
- **Local minima** are the main limitation — solved by random walks, tangential fields, or escalation to a deliberative layer.
- **Motor Schemas (Arkin, 1987)** generalise to weighted vector summation: $\mathbf{V}_{\text{out}} = \sum_i w_i \mathbf{V}_i$.
- **Braitenberg Vehicles (1984)** are the historical foundation — direct sensor-to-motor wiring.
- **Three-layer hierarchy:** Deliberative (top) → Executive (middle) → Reactive (bottom).
- **Typical gains:** $k_{\text{att}} \in [0.5, 2.0]$, $k_{\text{rep}} \in [0.5, 2.0]$, $d_{\text{inf}} \in [1, 3]$ m for indoor robots.
- **Typical speeds:** Indoor mobile robots $v_{\max} \in [0.3, 1.0]$ m/s.
- **Use reactive control when:** environment is unknown, dynamic, cluttered, or time-critical.
- **Avoid pure reactive control when:** the task requires global optimality, multi-step reasoning, or symbolic planning.
- **Key people to remember for KTU viva:** **Rodney Brooks** (Subsumption), **Oussama Khatib** (Potential Fields), **Ronald Arkin** (Motor Schemas), **Valentino Braitenberg** (Vehicles).
- **Key years to remember:** **1984** (Braitenberg), **1986** (Brooks Subsumption, Khatib Potential Field), **1987** (Arkin Motor Schemas).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite State Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Finite State Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Finite State Machine (FSM)**, also known as a **Finite Automaton**, is a mathematical model of computation used to design sequential logic circuits and control systems. In the context of mobile robotics, an FSM is a **behavioral control architecture** that describes a robot's operation as a finite set of discrete *states*, *transitions* between those states, and a set of *actions* or *events* that trigger the transitions.

Formally, an FSM is defined as a 5-tuple:

$$\Sigma_{FSM} = \{S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F\}$$

Where:
- $S$ = A finite, non-empty set of **states**.
- $\Sigma$ = A finite, non-empty set of **input symbols (events/sensors)**.
- $\delta$ = The **transition function**, $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$.
- $s_0$ = The **initial state** ($s_0 \in S$).
- $F$ = A set of **accepting/final states** ($F \subseteq S$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In mobile robotics, FSMs are the foundational building block of **Behavior-Based Robotics (BBR)**. They allow a robot to switch between discrete behaviors (e.g., *Wander*, *Avoid Obstacle*, *Follow Wall*, *Dock*) based on real-time sensor inputs without requiring complex continuous control mathematics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **vending machine**. It can only be in one of a limited number of states at any given moment: `IDLE`, `COIN_INSERTED`, `DISPENSING`, or `OUT_OF_STOCK`. When you press a button or insert a coin, the machine moves from one state to another based on a strict set of rules. It does not "think" — it simply checks its current state and the input, then decides the next state.

A **mobile robot** behaves identically. Consider a simple floor-cleaning robot:
- If the battery is above 20% AND no obstacle is detected $\rightarrow$ **CLEANING_FORWARD** state.
- If the front bumper hits an object $\rightarrow$ **AVOID_OBSTACLE** state.
- If the battery drops below 20% $\rightarrow$ **RETURN_TO_DOCK** state.

The robot is always in exactly **one** state, and it switches between states based purely on sensor events. This is the essence of an FSM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** FSMs replace complex, monolithic "sense $\rightarrow$ plan $\rightarrow$ act" pipelines with reactive, discrete rules. They are computationally cheap, deterministic, and highly debuggable, which is why they dominate real-time mobile robot controllers like the Roomba and many autonomous warehouse robots (e.g., Kiva/Amazon robots).

### 1.3 Key Terminology
| Term | Description | Robotics Example |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **State** | A distinct behavioral mode | `WANDERING`, `CHARGING` |
| **Event / Input** | A trigger from the environment or internal clock | `bumper_pressed`, `IR_signal_lost` |
| **Transition** | The edge connecting two states | From `WANDERING` to `AVOIDING` |
| **Action** | Output executed when entering/leaving a state | `set_motor_speed(50)` |
| **Guard Condition** | A logical predicate that must be true for a transition | `battery < 20%` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of an FSM in Robotics — The "How" and "Why"

The power of an FSM lies in its absolute determinism. The robot's behavior is completely predictable, which is critical for safety-critical applications like autonomous vehicles and industrial AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles).

**Step 1: Define the States ($S$)**
The designer enumerates every distinct behavior the robot can exhibit. A common pitfall is to define too many states (state explosion). The "why" is to keep the model computationally tractable — the state space complexity is $O(N)$ where $N$ is the number of states.

**Step 2: Identify the Input Alphabet ($\Sigma$)**
These are the discrete symbols coming from sensors. Continuous signals (like a 0–5V analog voltage) must be *thresholded* into discrete events. For example, an IR distance sensor reading is converted to a binary event: `NEAR` (distance < 30 cm) or `FAR` (distance $\geq$ 30 cm).

**Step 3: Define the Transition Function ($\delta$)**
This is a complete mapping that answers: "Given my current state $S_i$ and current event $e_k$, what is my next state $S_j$?" The function must be **total** — every $(S_i, e_k)$ pair must have a defined output. Undefined transitions are the #1 cause of "frozen robot" bugs.

**Step 4: Specify the Initial State ($s_0$)**
The robot must always "boot" into a known state, typically `INIT` or `IDLE`. The "how" is by hardcoding the first state in the microcontroller's startup code.

**Step 5: (Optional) Mark Final States ($F$)**
In robotics, "final" states often represent mission completion (e.g., `DOCKED` or `TASK_DONE`).

### 2.2 Two Principal Topologies: Moore vs. Mealy Machines

In mobile robotics, this distinction is critical because it determines **when** the robot executes an action.

| Feature | **Moore Machine** | **Mealy Machine** |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Dependency** | Output depends **only on the current state** | Output depends on **current state AND current input** |
| **Action Timing** | Actions execute upon *entering* a state | Actions execute *during* the transition |
| **Reactivity** | Slower (one cycle latency) | Faster (immediate response) |
| **Robotics Use Case** | Mission sequencing, mode selection | Reflexive obstacle avoidance, motor cut-off |
| **Visual Notation** | Action written inside the state node | Action written on the transition arrow |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** If a question asks "design an FSM for obstacle avoidance," and you need the robot to stop motors the instant the bumper is hit, you are describing a **Mealy** machine. If the robot must perform a multi-step "spin left" sequence after entering `AVOIDING`, that is a **Moore** machine.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FSM Tuple | $\Sigma_{FSM} = \{S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F\}$ | Formal 5-element definition |
| 2 | Transition Function | $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ | Maps state + input to next state |
| 3 | Moore Output | $\lambda : S \rightarrow \Gamma$ | Output is a function of state only |
| 4 | Mealy Output | $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma$ | Output is a function of state and input |
| 5 | Number of States | $N = \vert S \vert$ | Cardinality of the state set |
| 6 | Total Transitions (Deterministic) | $T = N \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$ | Every state $\times$ every input must map somewhere |
| 7 | Reachability | $S_{reach} = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} \delta(s_0, w_i)$ | Set of states reachable from $s_0$ via input strings $w_i$ |
| 8 | Equivalence (Two FSMs) | $M_1 \equiv M_2$ | Identical output for all input sequences |
| 9 | Cycle Time (RTOS) | $t_c \leq t_{deadline}$ | FSM update loop must meet real-time constraints |
| 10 | Storage Complexity | $O(\log_2 N)$ bits | $n$ flip-flops store the state (for $N \le 2^n$ states) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** FSMs are the silent workhorses of the **AUTOSAR** automotive standard, **UML Statecharts** in embedded systems, and **ROS SMACH** (a Python library for robot task-level FSMs). In KTU lab examinations, FSMs are typically implemented on Arduino/ESP32 boards to control line-follower and grid-solving robots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem: Designing an FSM for a Two-Room Floor Cleaner

**Problem Statement:**
A differential-drive mobile robot must clean two rooms (Room A and Room B). It starts in Room A. It cleans until its battery is low (event $e_b$), at which point it returns to its charging dock. While cleaning, if the front bumper is pressed (event $e_p$), it backs up and turns, then continues cleaning. When charging is complete (event $e_c$), it resumes cleaning. Design a complete Moore FSM.

**Step 1: Enumerate the States**
We identify 4 distinct behavioral modes:
- $S_0$ = `IDLE` (waiting for start command, $s_0$)
- $S_1$ = `CLEANING`
- $S_2$ = `AVOIDING_OBSTACLE`
- $S_3$ = `RETURNING_TO_DOCK`
- $S_4$ = `CHARGING`

Thus, $S = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$, so $N = 5$.

**Step 2: Define the Input Alphabet**
- $e_s$ = start command pressed
- $e_p$ = bumper pressed
- $e_c$ = charging complete
- $e_b$ = battery low
- $\emptyset$ = no event (timeout, sensor update)

**Step 3: Construct the Transition Function $\delta$**

We define $\delta$ as a complete mapping. For brevity, we present the high-priority transitions:

$$\delta(S_0, e_s) = S_1 \quad \text{(start cleaning)}$$

$$\delta(S_1, e_p) = S_2 \quad \text{(hit obstacle, avoid)}$$

$$\delta(S_2, \emptyset) = S_1 \quad \text{(avoidance complete, resume)}$$

$$\delta(S_1, e_b) = S_3 \quad \text{(battery low, go home)}$$

$$\delta(S_3, \emptyset) = S_4 \quad \text{(docked, begin charging)}$$

$$\delta(S_4, e_c) = S_1 \quad \text{(full charge, clean again)}$$

**Step 4: State Transition Table (Complete)**

| Current State $\backslash$ Input | $e_s$ | $e_p$ | $e_c$ | $e_b$ | $\emptyset$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ (IDLE) | $S_1$ | $S_0$ | $S_0$ | $S_0$ | $S_0$ |
| $S_1$ (CLEANING) | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_3$ | $S_1$ |
| $S_2$ (AVOIDING) | $S_2$ | $S_2$ | $S_2$ | $S_2$ | $S_1$ |
| $S_3$ (RETURNING) | $S_3$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ |
| $S_4$ (CHARGING) | $S_4$ | $S_4$ | $S_1$ | $S_4$ | $S_4$ |

**Step 5: Verify Completeness**
For a deterministic FSM, the total number of defined transitions must be:

$$T = N \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert = 5 \text{ states} \times 5 \text{ inputs} = 25 \text{ transitions}$$

Counting the entries in the table: 5 rows $\times$ 5 columns = **25 cells**. ✓ The FSM is complete (no undefined transitions).

**Step 6: Calculate Storage Requirement**
The number of bits required to encode 5 states is:

$$n_{bits} = \lceil \log_2(N) \rceil = \lceil \log_2(5) \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}$$

This means a single 3-bit register in the microcontroller (e.g., 3 GPIO pins or a 3-bit variable) can fully encode the robot's state.

**Step 7: Reachability Analysis**
Starting from $s_0 = S_0$:

$$S_{reach} = \{S_0, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$$

All 5 states are reachable. This is a "fully connected" FSM in the sense that no state is dead code. If any state were unreachable, it would be a design error and should be removed to save memory.

### 3.2 Derivation: Why $N \le 2^n$ States in $n$ Bits

We derive the storage bound. With $n$ binary flip-flops (bits), the total number of unique patterns is:

$$P(n) = 2^n$$

Since each state must be represented by a unique pattern, the maximum number of states $N_{max}$ is:

$$N_{max} = 2^n$$

Solving for $n$ given $N$:

$$2^n \ge N$$

$$n \cdot \ln(2) \ge \ln(N)$$

$$n \ge \frac{\ln(N)}{\ln(2)} = \log_2(N)$$

Since $n$ must be an integer:

$$n = \lceil \log_2(N) \rceil$$

This is the same formula used in Step 6 above. For example, a 3-bit register can hold up to 8 states ($2^3 = 8$); if $N = 5$, we use 3 of those 8 codes (e.g., `000`, `001`, `010`, `011`, `100`) and leave `101`, `110`, `111` as unused — a 37.5% encoding inefficiency, but unavoidable in binary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FSM Controller in a Mobile Robot

The following diagram illustrates how an FSM controller interfaces with the physical robot. It shows the closed-loop flow from sensors, through the FSM logic, to actuators.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Environment] --> B[Sensor Layer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning and Thresholding]
    C --> D[Event Encoder - Sigma]
    D --> E[FSM Core - State S and delta Function]
    E --> F[Output Decoder - lambda]
    F --> G[Motor Driver and Actuator Layer]
    G --> H[Robot Chassis and Wheels]
    H --> A
    E --> I[State Memory - s0 and S]
    I --> E
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style C fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style D fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style G fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
    style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style I fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (Environment $\rightarrow$ Sensor $\rightarrow$ Chassis $\rightarrow$ Environment) is the physical world interaction.
- The **inner loop** (Thresholding $\rightarrow$ Encoder $\rightarrow$ FSM $\rightarrow$ Decoder $\rightarrow$ Motor) is the computational control loop.
- The **State Memory** block (bottom right) feeds back into the FSM core so the system "remembers" its current state across discrete time steps.

### 4.2 State Diagram for a Two-Room Cleaning Robot (Moore Machine)

This state diagram corresponds exactly to the analytical problem solved in Section 3.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0idle
    s0idle --> s1clean : es start button
    s1clean --> s2avoid : ep bumper pressed
    s1clean --> s3return : eb battery low
    s2avoid --> s1clean : avoidance complete
    s3return --> s4charge : docked
    s4charge --> s1clean : ec fully charged
    note right of s1clean
        Action set motor speed 50
    end note
    note right of s2avoid
        Action reverse and turn left
    end note
    note right of s4charge
        Action enable charger relay
    end note
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The `note right of` blocks in the state diagram indicate **Moore-style actions** (outputs tied to the state itself). If these were placed on the arrows instead, the diagram would represent a **Mealy machine**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Finite State Machine. List and briefly explain its five formal components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Finite State Machine (FSM) is a mathematical model of computation that describes a system using a finite number of discrete states, transitions between those states, and actions.

The five formal components are:
1. **$S$ (States):** A finite, non-empty set of distinct behavioral modes the robot can be in.
2. **$\Sigma$ (Input Alphabet):** A finite set of discrete input symbols or events generated by sensors.
3. **$\delta$ (Transition Function):** A function $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ that maps the current state and current input to the next state.
4. **$s_0$ (Initial State):** The starting state of the system, $s_0 \in S$.
5. **$F$ (Final/Accepting States):** A subset $F \subseteq S$ of states that represent the completion of the FSM's task.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a Moore machine and a Mealy machine with a suitable robotics example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Moore Machine | Mealy Machine |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Definition** | $\lambda : S \rightarrow \Gamma$ | $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma$ |
| **Dependency** | Output depends only on the **current state** | Output depends on **current state AND current input** |
| **Reactivity** | One-cycle latency | Immediate response |
| **Robotics Example** | A mission sequencer that runs "spin left for 2 seconds" the moment it enters the `AVOIDING` state | An emergency stop that cuts motor power the instant the bumper is pressed, regardless of state |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Design a complete Moore FSM for a mobile robot that performs **wall-following**. The robot has three states: `INIT`, `FOLLOW_WALL`, and `RECOVER`. It must transition to `RECOVER` if the wall is lost (event $e_l$), and return to `FOLLOW_WALL` once the wall is reacquired (event $e_r$). Provide the formal tuple, the state transition table, and draw the state diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Hierarchical Finite State Machine (HFSM)** concept. How does it address the **state explosion problem** in mobile robotics? Provide one real-world example. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1: Formal Tuple Definition**
$$M = (S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F)$$

Where:
- $S = \{S_0, S_1, S_2\}$ where $S_0$ = `INIT`, $S_1$ = `FOLLOW_WALL`, $S_2$ = `RECOVER`
- $\Sigma = \{e_s, e_l, e_r, \emptyset\}$ (start, wall lost, wall recovered, no event)
- $s_0 = S_0$
- $F = \{S_1\}$ (mission complete when wall is being followed)

**Step 2: Transition Function**

$$\delta(S_0, e_s) = S_1 \quad \text{(start command begins wall following)}$$

$$\delta(S_1, e_l) = S_2 \quad \text{(wall lost, enter recovery search)}$$

$$\delta(S_2, e_r) = S_1 \quad \text{(wall found, resume following)}$$

$$\delta(S_1, \emptyset) = S_1 \quad \text{(self-loop, keep following)}$$

**Step 3: State Transition Table**

| Current State | $e_s$ | $e_l$ | $e_r$ | $\emptyset$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ (INIT) | $S_1$ | $S_0$ | $S_0$ | $S_0$ |
| $S_1$ (FOLLOW_WALL) | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_1$ |
| $S_2$ (RECOVER) | $S_2$ | $S_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ |

**Step 4: State Diagram**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0init
    s0init --> s1follow : es start
    s1follow --> s2recover : el wall lost
    s2recover --> s1follow : er wall reacquired
    s1follow --> s1follow : no event self loop
```

**Step 5: Verification**
Total cells = 3 states $\times$ 4 inputs = 12 entries. All defined $\Rightarrow$ deterministic and complete. Storage requirement: $\lceil \log_2(3) \rceil = 2$ bits. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

A **Hierarchical Finite State Machine (HFSM)**, also called a **Statechart**, is an extension of the standard FSM in which states can themselves contain nested FSMs. The system has a "super-state" that delegates control to a sub-state machine, drastically reducing the number of explicit states.

**The State Explosion Problem:**
In a flat FSM, if a robot has $K$ independent sub-behaviors, the number of combined states grows exponentially as $N_{total} = N_1 \times N_2 \times \dots \times N_K$. For example, a robot with 4 independent modes, each having 3 substates, requires $3^4 = 81$ states in a flat FSM. An HFSM treats each mode as a separate child FSM, requiring only $3 \times 4 = 12$ explicit states plus 4 super-states — a 6.75$\times$ reduction.

**Real-World Example: Autonomous Vehicle Cruise Control**
- **Super-State:** `CRUISE_MODE`
  - **Sub-FSM 1:** `LANE_KEEPING` $\rightarrow$ `LANE_CHANGE_LEFT` $\rightarrow$ `LANE_CHANGE_RIGHT`
  - **Sub-FSM 2:** `SPEED_CONTROL` $\rightarrow$ `ACCELERATE` $\rightarrow$ `DECELERATE` $\rightarrow$ `MAINTAIN`
  - **Sub-FSM 3:** `OBSTACLE_RESPONSE` $\rightarrow$ `BRAKE` $\rightarrow$ `EVADE` $\rightarrow$ `RESUME`
- The driver manually changes which sub-FSM is active, and the super-state handles orthogonal (parallel) coordination.

**Other applications:** HFSMs are used in the **ROS SMACH** library, **UML Statecharts** for industrial automation, and **Unity game AI** for non-player character behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A line-follower robot uses **two IR sensors** (Left = $L$, Right = $R$) on a black track. It must execute the following logic: if both sensors are off the line, go forward; if only the left sensor is on the line, turn left; if only the right sensor is on the line, turn right; if both are on the line (intersection), stop. Design a **Mealy FSM** for this robot. Draw the state diagram and provide the transition table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of FSMs in mobile robotics. Compare FSMs with **Behavior Trees (BT)** as an alternative control architecture. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Step 1: Define Inputs**
The two IR sensors produce 4 possible binary combinations: $LL$, $LR$, $RL$, $RR$ where `1` = sensor on the black line.

$$\Sigma = \{00, 01, 10, 11\}$$

**Step 2: Define States**
For a simple line-follower, we can use a single state $S_0$ = `TRACK` (the robot is always tracking) plus a final state $S_1$ = `STOP`. Thus $N = 2$.

**Step 3: Define Mealy Outputs**
Outputs are motor commands: `FWD` (forward), `LEFT` (turn left), `RIGHT` (turn right), `STOP`.

**Step 4: Transition Function and Output (Mealy Form)**

| Current State | Input $LR$ | Next State | Output $\lambda$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ (TRACK) | $00$ | $S_0$ | `FWD` |
| $S_0$ (TRACK) | $01$ (Left ON) | $S_0$ | `LEFT` |
| $S_0$ (TRACK) | $10$ (Right ON) | $S_0$ | `RIGHT` |
| $S_0$ (TRACK) | $11$ (Intersection) | $S_1$ | `STOP` |
| $S_1$ (STOP) | any | $S_1$ | `STOP` |

**Step 5: Mealy State Diagram**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0track
    s0track --> s0track : L0 R0 forward
    s0track --> s0track : L1 R0 turn left
    s0track --> s0track : L0 R1 turn right
    s0track --> s1stop : L1 R1 intersection
    s1stop --> s1stop : any stop
```

**Note on Mealy vs Moore:** The actions (motor commands) are written on the *transitions* in this diagram, which is the defining visual signature of a Mealy machine. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Advantages of FSMs in Mobile Robotics (4 marks):**
1. **Determinism:** Given a state and input, the next state is uniquely defined. Critical for safety in industrial AGVs.
2. **Low computational overhead:** A single `switch-case` statement in C/C++ implements the entire FSM, requiring only $O(1)$ lookup time.
3. **Easy verification and testing:** State transition tables enable exhaustive testing via Model Checking (e.g., SPIN, NuSMV tools).
4. **Excellent hardware mapping:** FSMs map directly to flip-flops, enabling FPGA-based real-time controllers.
5. **Debuggability:** The current state can be exposed via a serial monitor or LED indicator for live debugging.

**Limitations of FSMs in Mobile Robotics (1.5 marks):**
1. **State explosion:** The number of states grows combinatorially with the number of independent behaviors.
2. **Poor scalability:** Adding a new sensor often requires re-engineering the entire transition table.
3. **No learning/adaptation:** Pure FSMs are reactive and do not learn from experience.
4. **Hard to express concurrency:** Parallel behaviors (e.g., "follow wall" AND "monitor battery") require interlinked states.

**Comparison: FSM vs. Behavior Tree (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | FSM | Behavior Tree (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Flat graph of states and transitions | Hierarchical tree of nodes (Sequence, Selector, Action) |
| **Reactivity** | Reactive only to defined events | Reactive at every tick; supports preemption |
| **Scalability** | Poor (state explosion) | High (modular subtrees) |
| **Concurrency** | Awkward (requires HFSM) | Native (parallel nodes) |
| **Robotics Use** | Simple reactive controllers, mission sequencers | Complex AI (NAO, Boston Dynamics, game NPCs) |
| **Readability** | Medium (state diagram) | High (graphical tree) |

**Conclusion:** Use **FSMs** for simple, hard real-time, safety-critical tasks (e.g., bumper-based emergency stop). Use **BTs** for complex, multi-layered AI behaviors requiring modularity and learning. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Incomplete transition tables:** Examiners deduct up to **3 marks** if a single $(S_i, e_k)$ cell is left blank. A deterministic FSM must be a *total* function.
> 2. **Confusing Moore and Mealy output placement:** If the question says "Moore," do NOT put actions on the arrows. This is a **2-mark** penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting the initial state:** Always explicitly state $s_0$. Examiners will not assume it.
> 4. **Wrong formula for Moore/Mealy:** Memorize the exact domains: $\lambda : S \rightarrow \Gamma$ vs. $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Gamma$. Mixing them up costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **No real-world example:** A purely theoretical answer without a robotics example (e.g., Roomba, line follower, AGV) loses **1 mark** for lacking application context.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **FSM Formal Tuple:** $\{S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F\}$ — memorize this 5-element definition verbatim.
- [x] **Transition Function:** $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ — the heart of every FSM.
- [x] **Moore vs. Mealy:** Moore = output from state only; Mealy = output from state + input. **Moore actions live inside the state node; Mealy actions live on the transition arrow.**
- [x] **Determinism:** A DFA must have exactly one transition per (state, input) pair. Total cells = $N \cdot \vert\Sigma\vert$.
- [x] **Storage:** Number of flip-flops needed = $\lceil\log_2 N\rceil$ bits.
- [x] **State Explosion:** Mitigated by **Hierarchical FSMs (HFSMs)** or by switching to **Behavior Trees**.
- [x] **Real-Time Constraint:** The FSM update loop period $t_c$ must be $\le$ the system deadline $t_{deadline}$.
- [x] **Reachability:** Always verify that every state is reachable from $s_0$. Unreachable states indicate design errors.
- [x] **Robotics Applications:** Behavior selection, wall-following, line-following, mission sequencers, emergency stops, battery management, and AGV traffic coordination.
- [x] **Tools to Know:** ROS SMACH (FSM library), UML Statecharts, MATLAB Stateflow, AUTOSAR (automotive standard), Arduino `switch-case` implementation.
- [x] **Common Exam Trap:** Examiners love asking "design an FSM for a ___" — always draw the diagram, the table, AND give the formal tuple. Missing any one costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Finite State Machine and Behaviour Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Finite State Machine and Behaviour Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hierarchical Finite State Machine (HFSM)

A **Hierarchical Finite State Machine (HFSM)**, also known as a **Statechart**, is a formal computational model that extends the classical Finite State Machine (FSM) by allowing states to be organized in a parent–child (nested) tree-like structure. In the HFSM, a *superstate* can encapsulate one or more *substates*, enabling the designer to express complex behaviour through decomposition. When a superstate is active, exactly one of its substates is automatically active, which drastically reduces state-explosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Compliant):** An HFSM is defined as a 6-tuple $(S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F, H)$ where $H \subseteq S \times S$ defines the hierarchy (parent–child relation) on the finite set of states $S$, $\Sigma$ is the input alphabet, $\delta$ is the transition function, $s_0$ is the root state, and $F \subseteq S$ is the set of final (accepting) states.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Building"**

Think of an HFSM as the floor plan of a multi-storey office building. Each floor (the **superstate**) is a high-level activity such as *"Operating Mode"*, *"Charging Mode"*, or *"Emergency Mode"*. Inside each floor, there are specific rooms (the **substates**) like *"Patrol"*, *"Dock Search"*, or *"Sleep Recovery"*. A worker (the robot) is always on exactly one floor at a time, but the moment they leave that floor, they do **not** need to re-check the rules of other floors — those rules are inherited. This drastically simplifies the overall control logic and makes the system modular.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Inheritance of behaviour from a superstate to its substates is the *single most important property* of an HFSM. The transition *ON_EXIT* of a child also implicitly triggers *ON_EXIT* of the parent when leaving the parent's scope.

---

## 1.2 Behaviour Tree (BT)

A **Behaviour Tree (BT)** is a tree-structured control architecture widely used in robotics and game AI to model decision-making and task execution. The tree is traversed by a discrete **tick** signal that flows from the root downwards, and each node returns one of three discrete statuses: **SUCCESS**, **FAILURE**, or **RUNNING**. The tree is composed of two major categories of nodes — **Control Nodes** (internal) and **Execution Nodes** (leaves) such as *Action* and *Condition*.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Nurse"**

Imagine a triage nurse in a hospital. The nurse follows a strict, written decision protocol represented as a tree. They start with a top-level question (the **root**) like *"Is the patient in critical condition?"*. If **YES**, they tick down the left branch (an **Action** node) and immediately call the emergency team (**SUCCESS**). If **NO**, they tick down the right branch and continue to ask further questions (**Sequence** of conditions). The nurse never hesitates — they always tick the tree and immediately return a *Yes/No/Working* answer. This ticking protocol is the essence of a Behaviour Tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tick Rate (Hz):** In mobile robotics, behaviour trees are typically evaluated at a fixed control frequency, commonly **10 Hz to 50 Hz**, depending on the sensors and computational budget of the embedded platform.

---

## 1.3 Why HFSM and BT for Mobile Robotics?

A classical flat FSM suffers from the **state-explosion problem** — for $n$ boolean internal flags, the number of states grows as $2^n$. HFSMs and BTs were introduced into robotics to overcome this by:

1. **Modularity** — behaviours are independent, reusable sub-trees/sub-machines.
2. **Readability** — graphical representation maps 1-to-1 with engineering intent.
3. **Reactivity** — BTs are highly reactive; the next tick can re-evaluate the whole tree in **O(log n)** for balanced trees.
4. **Composability** — sub-trees from different modules can be plugged together without rewriting logic.

| Property | HFSM | Behaviour Tree |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Topology | Directed graph (with hierarchy) | Tree |
| Trigger Mechanism | Event-driven | Tick-driven |
| Return Status | State identity | SUCCESS / FAILURE / RUNNING |
| Reactivity to External Events | Medium | Very High |
| Learning Curve | Moderate | Easy |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Definition of HFSM

An **HFSM** is formally defined as the tuple:

$$M_{HFSM} = (S, \; \Sigma, \; \delta, \; s_0, \; F, \; H)$$

where the parameters are:

- $S$ — finite non-empty set of states.
- $\Sigma$ — finite set of input events / triggers.
- $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ — transition function mapping (current state, event) to a next state.
- $s_0 \in S$ — the initial root state.
- $F \subseteq S$ — set of accepting / final states.
- $H \subseteq S \times S$ — the hierarchy relation, where $(s_p, s_c) \in H$ means $s_p$ is the **parent (superstate)** of child $s_c$.

The hierarchy $H$ must satisfy the **tree property**:

$$\forall s_c \in S, \; \vert \{ s_p \in S \mid (s_p, s_c) \in H \} \vert = 1 \quad \text{except for the root}$$

This guarantees that every non-root state has **exactly one parent**, giving a tree of states.

### Why HFSM Works (Operational Logic)

1. **Enter Root** — at start-up, the system enters $s_0$ (root) and recursively enters the default child.
2. **Dispatch Event** — when an event $\sigma \in \Sigma$ arrives, the engine walks up the active state hierarchy looking for a valid transition.
3. **Bubble Up Search** — if no local transition exists in the current state, the engine checks the parent, then grandparent, until a match is found.
4. **Exit Stack** — when leaving a superstate, all its substates are exited in **LIFO** order.
5. **Enter Stack** — when entering a superstate, its initial substate is recursively entered.

---

## 2.2 Behaviour Tree Node Taxonomy

A Behaviour Tree has **two broad categories** of nodes:

### 2.2.1 Control Flow Nodes (Internal Nodes)

| Node Type | Symbol | Logic | Returns |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sequence (→)** | $\rightarrow$ | Ticks children left-to-right; succeeds only if ALL succeed | SUCCESS / FAILURE / RUNNING |
| **Selector (?)** | $?$ | Ticks children left-to-right; succeeds on FIRST success | SUCCESS / FAILURE / RUNNING |
| **Parallel (⇉)** | $\parallel$ | Ticks all children simultaneously | Per configured policy M-of-N |
| **Decorator (◇)** | $\diamond$ | Wraps a single child; inverts / repeats / limits | Depends on decorator type |
| **Inverter** | $\neg$ | Negates child's result (SUCCESS ↔ FAILURE) | Negation |
| **Repeat (n)** | $\times n$ | Ticks child exactly $n$ times | Depends on child |

### 2.2.2 Execution Nodes (Leaf Nodes)

| Node Type | Symbol | Returns | Side Effect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Action (▶)** | $\text{ACT}$ | SUCCESS / FAILURE / RUNNING | Mutates world (move, grasp) |
| **Condition (◇ test)** | $\text{CND}$ | SUCCESS / FAILURE only (no RUNNING) | Read-only sensor query |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Condition** node must *never* return RUNNING — it is a pure predicate evaluation. A **Sequence** short-circuits on the first FAILURE; a **Selector** short-circuits on the first SUCCESS.

---

## 2.3 The Tick Algorithm

The tick propagates as a **depth-first, left-to-right** traversal. For a node $N$ at depth $d$ in a tree of branching factor $b$ and height $h$, the worst-case time per tick is:

$$T_{tick} = \mathcal{O}\!\left( b \cdot h \right)$$

For a perfectly balanced tree, $h = \log_b N$ where $N$ is the total number of nodes, yielding:

$$T_{tick} = \mathcal{O}\!\left( b \cdot \log_b N \right)$$

The maximum number of nodes visited per tick is bounded by the number of nodes in the tree, so the upper bound is $\mathcal{O}(N)$.

---

## 2.4 HFSM vs Behaviour Tree — Engineering Trade-Off

| Criterion | HFSM | Behaviour Tree |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **State Explosion** | Mitigated by hierarchy | N/A (stateless) |
| **Memory Footprint** | $\mathcal{O}(S)$ states stored | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ nodes stored |
| **CPU per cycle** | $\mathcal{O}(S)$ worst case | $\mathcal{O}(\log N)$ balanced |
| **Reusability of modules** | Limited (state-bound) | High (sub-trees pluggable) |
| **Priority override** | Needs explicit transitions | Natural via Selector |
| **Suitability for ROS 2** | Via SMACH / FlexBE | Via BehaviorTree.CPP |
| **Failure recovery** | Manual | Built-in via Fallback |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-Away:** Behaviour Trees are the **preferred architecture** in modern ROS 2 (Robot Operating System) navigation stacks such as *Nav2* and *BT-Explorer* because of their modularity and graceful failure handling.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| HFSM tuple | $M = (S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F, H)$ | Hierarchy $H$ is a tree |
| Tick complexity (balanced) | $T = \mathcal{O}(\log_b N)$ | $b$ = branching factor |
| Tick complexity (worst) | $T = \mathcal{O}(N)$ | All nodes visited |
| Sequence returns | $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} r_i$ | Logical AND of children |
| Selector returns | $\bigvee_{i=1}^{k} r_i$ | Logical OR of children |
| Number of leaves $L$ in a full $b$-ary tree of height $h$ | $L = b^{h}$ | Geometric series |
| Tick rate $f_{tick}$ | $f_{tick} \in [10, 50] \; \text{Hz}$ | Typical for mobile robots |
| Standard status set | $\mathcal{R} = \{\text{SUCCESS}, \text{FAILURE}, \text{RUNNING}\}$ | Cardinality = 3 |
| HFSM parent property | $\forall s_c \neq s_0,\; \mid H^{-1}(s_c) \mid = 1$ | Exactly one parent |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tick Complexity of a Balanced Behaviour Tree

**Problem:** Prove that the tick propagation time of a balanced $b$-ary behaviour tree with $N$ nodes is $\mathcal{O}(\log_b N)$.

**Step 1 — Height of a balanced $b$-ary tree.**

A $b$-ary tree of height $h$ has at most:

$$N_{\max} = \sum_{k=0}^{h} b^{k} = \frac{b^{h+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$

This is a finite geometric series. We can simplify it to:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{h} b^{k} = \frac{b^{h+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Invert the relation.**

For large $h$, the dominant term is $b^{h}$, so we write:

$$N \approx \frac{b^{h+1}}{b - 1}$$

Solving for $h$ by taking the base-$b$ logarithm:

$$\log_b \left( N (b - 1) \right) = h + 1$$

$$h = \log_b N + \log_b(b - 1) - 1$$

For asymptotic analysis, the constant terms vanish, giving:

$$h = \mathcal{O}(\log_b N)$$

**Step 3 — Tick traverses the path.**

The tick visits exactly one node per depth level along the path from the root to a leaf. In the worst case (Sequence node with all children RUNNING, or Selector visiting all children), the tick visits all children of an internal node.

Total nodes visited per tick in the balanced case:

$$T_{tick} = b \cdot h = b \cdot \mathcal{O}(\log_b N) = \mathcal{O}(\log_b N)$$

> **Conclusion:** $T_{tick} = \mathcal{O}(\log_b N)$. For a binary tree ($b=2$), this is simply $\mathcal{O}(\log_2 N)$, the well-known logarithmic tick time of BTs in Nav2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — State-Explosion Reduction in HFSM

**Problem:** A flat FSM has $n$ orthogonal boolean variables. Show how an HFSM reduces the state count.

**Step 1 — Flat FSM state count.**

A flat FSM that monitors $n$ independent boolean modes must enumerate every distinct combination, giving:

$$N_{flat} = 2^{n}$$

For $n=10$, $N_{flat} = 1024$ states — clearly intractable.

**Step 2 — HFSM grouping.**

Suppose the $n$ booleans are partitioned into $k$ groups of sizes $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k$ where:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i = n$$

Each group is implemented as a sub-HFSM with $2^{n_i}$ states. The HFSM composes them in parallel via **orthogonal (AND) regions**.

**Step 3 — Total states in HFSM.**

$$N_{HFSM} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} 2^{n_i} = 2^{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i} = 2^{n}$$

Wait — this gives the same number! The real saving comes when booleans are **mutually exclusive**. If only one group is active at a time, the active states are:

$$N_{HFSM,\,active} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} 2^{n_i}$$

**Step 4 — Reduction factor.**

$$\eta = \frac{N_{flat}}{N_{HFSM}} = \frac{2^n}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} 2^{n_i}}$$

For $k$ groups of equal size $n_i = n/k$:

$$\eta = \frac{2^n}{k \cdot 2^{n/k}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** Let $n=12$ boolean modes split into $k=4$ groups of 3.

$$N_{flat} = 2^{12} = 4096$$
$$N_{HFSM} = 4 \times 2^{3} = 32$$
$$\eta = \frac{4096}{32} = 128$$

> **Result:** A 12-mode controller reduces from 4096 flat states to just 32 HFSM states — a 128× reduction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Selector Node Probabilistic Success

**Problem:** A Selector has $k$ children with independent success probabilities $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_k$. Find the overall success probability.

**Step 1 — Selector semantics.**

A Selector stops at the **first child** that returns SUCCESS or RUNNING. It returns FAILURE only if all children return FAILURE. Thus:

$$P(\text{Failure}) = \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - p_i)$$

**Step 2 — Probability of Success or Running.**

$$P(\text{Success or Running}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1 - p_i)$$

**Step 3 — For equal probabilities $p_i = p$:**

$$P(\text{Success or Running}) = 1 - (1 - p)^{k}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** Let $k=3$ and $p=0.8$ per child.

$$P = 1 - (0.2)^{3} = 1 - 0.008 = 0.992$$

> **Conclusion:** A 3-child Selector with each child succeeding 80% of the time succeeds or continues running 99.2% of the time — a strong argument for **ordered fallbacks** in BT design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose HFSM vs BT

| Application Scenario | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mission state with strict ordering (Takeoff → Cruise → Land) | **HFSM** | Sequential phases map naturally to nested states |
| Reactive obstacle avoidance with priorities | **BT** | Selector gives clean priority fallback |
| Hybrid: ordered mission + reactive sub-tasks | **HFSM wrapping BT** | Each HFSM state contains a BT sub-tree |
| ROS 2 Nav2 navigation | **BT** | Nav2 ships with BT.CPP |
| Hard real-time safety interlocks | **HFSM** | Event-driven, predictable latency |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HFSM Diagram — Mobile Robot Mission Controller

The following diagram shows a Hierarchical FSM for a mobile delivery robot. The superstate `OPERATE` contains substates `PATROL`, `DELIVER`, and `DOCK`. A separate orthogonal superstate `SAFETY` runs in parallel.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sRoot
    sRoot --> sOperate
    sRoot --> sSafety
    sRoot --> sCharge

    state sOperate {
        [*] --> sPatrol
        sPatrol --> sDeliver : ORDER_RECEIVED
        sDeliver --> sPatrol : DELIVERY_DONE
        sDeliver --> sDock : LOW_BATTERY
        sPatrol --> sDock : LOW_BATTERY
    }

    state sSafety {
        [*] --> sNominal
        sNominal --> sEStop : BUMPER_HIT
        sEStop --> sRecover : TIMEOUT_5S
    }

    sOperate --> sCharge : BATTERY_CRITICAL
    sCharge --> sOperate : BATTERY_FULL
    sSafety --> [*] : SHUTDOWN
    sCharge --> [*] : SHUTDOWN
```

> **Reading the diagram:** `sOperate` is a **superstate** that owns its substates. The `sSafety` superstate is **orthogonal** — it runs independently of `sOperate`. Event `LOW_BATTERY` causes exit from `sOperate` and entry into `sCharge`.

---

## 4.2 Behaviour Tree Diagram — Reactive Obstacle & Goal Follower

The diagram below depicts a BT for a mobile robot that first checks safety, then either follows the global path or stops safely. The structure uses a **Sequence** at the root and a **Selector** for fallback between two action strategies.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootSeq["SEQUENCE Root"] --> safetySel["SELECTOR SafetyCheck"]
    rootSeq --> driveSel["SELECTOR DriveStrategy"]

    safetySel --> condSafe["CONDITION IsPathSafe"]
    safetySel --> actionStop["ACTION EmergencyStop"]

    driveSel --> actionFollow["ACTION FollowPath"]
    driveSel --> actionReplan["ACTION ReplanPath"]
    driveSel --> actionIdle["ACTION IdleWait"]

    classDef ctrl fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b83260,color:#000
    classDef act  fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#2a9d8f,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1d4e89,color:#000
    class rootSeq,driveSel,safetySel ctrl
    class actionStop,actionFollow,actionReplan,actionIdle act
    class condSafe cond
```

> **Tick interpretation:** Each tick (typically 10 Hz), the root Sequence first checks safety via the Selector. If the path is unsafe, the EmergencyStop action runs (SUCCESS once stopped). Otherwise the DriveStrategy Selector tries FollowPath; if it FAILS, ReplanPath is ticked; if that also FAILS, IdleWait succeeds.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — HFSM vs BT Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HFSM_BOX["HFSM CONTROL FLOW"]
        hEvt["Event Arrives"] --> hLookup["Lookup Current State"]
        hLookup --> hUp["Bubble Up to Parent"]
        hUp --> hMatch{"Transition Found?"}
        hMatch -- YES --> hExit["Exit Current States"]
        hMatch -- NO  --> hStay["Remain in State"]
        hExit --> hEnter["Enter New States"]
    end

    subgraph BT_BOX["BT CONTROL FLOW"]
        bTick["Tick Issued"] --> bRoot["Visit Root"]
        bRoot --> bType{"Node Type?"}
        bType -- Sequence --> bSeq["Tick children left-to-right"]
        bType -- Selector  --> bSel["Tick children until SUCCESS"]
        bType -- Action    --> bAct["Execute primitive"]
        bType -- Condition --> bCnd["Evaluate predicate"]
        bSeq --> bRet["Return Status"]
        bSel --> bRet
        bAct --> bRet
        bCnd --> bRet
    end

    classDef hFSMNode fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#e09f3e,color:#000
    classDef bTNode fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2a9d8f,color:#000
    class hEvt,hLookup,hUp,hMatch,hExit,hStay,hEnter hFSMNode
    class bTick,bRoot,bType,bSeq,bSel,bAct,bCnd,bRet bTNode
```

> **Key engineering observation:** The HFSM flow is **event-driven** (waits for an event), whereas the BT flow is **tick-driven** (probes the world periodically). This is why BTs are more robust in highly dynamic environments like crowded corridors.

---

## 4.4 Hybrid Architecture — HFSM Hosting a BT per State

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mTop["Mission HFSM"] --> s1["State: EXPLORE"]
    mTop --> s2["State: DELIVER"]
    mTop --> s3["State: RETURN"]

    s1 --> bt1["Behaviour Tree: Exploration"]
    s2 --> bt2["Behaviour Tree: Delivery"]
    s3 --> bt3["Behaviour Tree: Docking"]

    bt1 --> bt1a["Selector: Follow frontier / Random walk"]
    bt1 --> bt1b["Sequence: Map, Localize, Save"]

    bt2 --> bt2a["Selector: Navigate to goal"]
    bt2 --> bt2b["Action: Drop payload"]

    bt3 --> bt3a["Sequence: Find dock, Align, Plug in"]
```

> **Best Practice:** Use HFSM to model *phases* of a mission and BT to model *reactive decisions* within each phase. This is the architecture used in industrial service robots and is frequently asked in KTU design questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
