# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between a Finite State Machine (FSM) and a Hierarchical Finite State Machine (HFSM). Mention any two advantages of HFSM over flat FSM in mobile robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flat FSM | HFSM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Structure** | Single layer of states | States nested inside superstates |
| **Transitions** | All transitions explicit | Local transitions + inherited from superstate |
| **State Explosion** | Yes, combinatorial | Mitigated via hierarchy |
| **Reusability** | Low | High — sub-machines can be reused |

**Two advantages for mobile robotics:**

1. **State-Explosion Mitigation:** A 12-mode controller reduces from $2^{12} = 4096$ flat states to about 32 HFSM states (as derived in Section 3.2).
2. **Behavioural Inheritance:** The *PATROL* substate can inherit common event-handling logic from its superstate *OPERATE*, reducing code duplication.
3. **Cleaner Mission Phases:** A delivery robot's phases (*EXPLORE → DELIVER → RETURN*) map naturally to nested states.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

**List the four major node types of a Behaviour Tree and state the return value of each. Why must a Condition node never return RUNNING?**

**Model Answer:**

| Node Type | Function | Allowed Return Values |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sequence** | AND of children | SUCCESS / FAILURE / RUNNING |
| **Selector** | OR / priority fallback | SUCCESS / FAILURE / RUNNING |
| **Action** | Executes world mutation | SUCCESS / FAILURE / RUNNING |
| **Condition** | Pure sensor predicate | SUCCESS / FAILURE only |

**Why Condition cannot return RUNNING:**

A condition is a *pure* boolean evaluation of current sensor data. There is no asynchronous work, no motion command, and no actuator to wait for. If the predicate is true → SUCCESS, else → FAILURE. Returning RUNNING would imply "still evaluating", which has no meaning for an instantaneous predicate. The tick protocol of BTs relies on the strict RUNNING contract of Action nodes to pause long-running tasks; injecting RUNNING into a Condition would break the parent Sequence/Selector's traversal logic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Hierarchical Finite State Machine**. Define the hierarchy relation $H$ and show that it forms a tree. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a service robot with $n = 15$ boolean operating modes grouped into $k = 5$ orthogonal clusters of equal size, compute the state-reduction factor $\eta$ achieved by an HFSM. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture of HFSM:**

An HFSM is described by the 6-tuple:

$$M = (S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F, H)$$

where the **hierarchy relation** $H \subseteq S \times S$ specifies parent–child ownership of states. Formally, a state $s_p$ is a **parent** of $s_c$ iff $(s_p, s_c) \in H$.

**Proof that $H$ is a tree:**

A tree is a connected acyclic graph in which every non-root node has exactly one parent.

1. **Single root:** $s_0$ has no parent — defined as the entry point of the system.
2. **Unique parent:** $\forall s_c \in S \setminus \{s_0\}$, we have $\vert H^{-1}(s_c) \vert = 1$. That is, every non-root state has exactly one parent. This rules out multi-inheritance.
3. **Acyclicity:** $H$ is irreflexive (no state is its own parent) and contains no cycles. If a cycle existed, the system would have an infinite entry sequence, contradicting the finiteness of $S$.
4. **Connectivity:** Every $s \in S$ is reachable from $s_0$ by repeated application of the parent inverse.

Hence, the graph $(S, H)$ is a rooted tree.

**Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above)** showing `sOperate` superstate with substates `sPatrol`, `sDeliver`, `sDock`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *substates* with *parallel regions*. A substate is exclusive (only one active within a superstate at a time), whereas an orthogonal region is a parallel state — both can be active simultaneously. Drawing them the same way costs 2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $n = 15$ boolean modes, $k = 5$ equal clusters.

**Step 1 — Modes per cluster.**

$$n_i = \frac{n}{k} = \frac{15}{5} = 3$$

**Step 2 — Flat FSM state count.**

$$N_{flat} = 2^{n} = 2^{15} = 32{,}768 \text{ states}$$

**Step 3 — HFSM state count (active states via clustering).**

$$N_{HFSM} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} 2^{n_i} = 5 \cdot 2^{3} = 5 \cdot 8 = 40 \text{ states}$$

**Step 4 — Reduction factor.**

$$\eta = \frac{N_{flat}}{N_{HFSM}} = \frac{32{,}768}{40} = 819.2$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The HFSM achieves an **819-fold reduction** in the number of states that need to be enumerated, designed, and tested. This makes verification tractable and reduces transition-table memory by the same factor.

$$\boxed{\eta = 819.2 \approx 819}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the **Behaviour Tree** for a mobile robot that must: *(i)* check if a goal exists, *(ii)* if yes, follow a global path, *(iii)* if the path is blocked, replan, and *(iv)* on completion, idle. Use proper node labels and explain the tick flow. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Selector node in a BT has $k=4$ children with success probabilities $p_1 = 0.9$, $p_2 = 0.7$, $p_3 = 0.5$, $p_4 = 0.3$. Compute the probability that the Selector returns SUCCESS or RUNNING. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Behaviour Tree Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    seqRoot["SEQUENCE MissionTick"] --> selGoal["SELECTOR GoalCheck"]
    seqRoot --> selDrive["SELECTOR DriveFallback"]
    seqRoot --> actDone["ACTION SignalDone"]

    selGoal --> condGoal["CONDITION GoalExists"]
    selGoal --> actAbort["ACTION AbortMission"]

    selDrive --> actFollow["ACTION FollowPath"]
    selDrive --> actReplan["ACTION ReplanPath"]
    selDrive --> actIdle["ACTION IdleWait"]

    classDef ctrl fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b83260,color:#000
    classDef act  fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#2a9d8f,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1d4e89,color:#000
    class seqRoot,selGoal,selDrive ctrl
    class actAbort,actFollow,actReplan,actIdle,actDone act
    class condGoal cond
```

**Tick flow explanation (10 Hz loop):**

1. The root **Sequence** ticks its children in order: `GoalCheck` → `DriveFallback` → `SignalDone`.
2. The **Selector `GoalCheck`** first ticks `GoalExists`. If **SUCCESS** (a goal exists in the path planner), the Selector returns SUCCESS and the Sequence proceeds.
3. If `GoalExists` returns **FAILURE**, the Selector ticks `AbortMission`. If `AbortMission` returns SUCCESS, the Selector returns SUCCESS but the overall Sequence proceeds to the (now meaningless) DriveFallback — this is why the structure should be refined with an `Inverter`. For the KTU answer, we document the trade-off.
4. The **Selector `DriveFallback`** tries `FollowPath`. If it FAILS, it ticks `ReplanPath`. If that also FAILS, `IdleWait` (which always succeeds) is ticked. This guarantees the Selector never returns FAILURE unless all three fail.
5. Finally `SignalDone` (Action) returns SUCCESS once the goal is reached.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students label a Selector as a "Sequence" by mistake. Remember: **Selector = OR (Fallback)**, succeeds on the first child that does not fail; **Sequence = AND**, succeeds only when all children succeed. Mislabeling costs 2 marks immediately.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $k = 4$ children with $p_1=0.9$, $p_2=0.7$, $p_3=0.5$, $p_4=0.3$.

**Step 1 — Probability of failure of each child.**

$$q_1 = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1$$
$$q_2 = 1 - 0.7 = 0.3$$
$$q_3 = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$
$$q_4 = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7$$

**Step 2 — Probability all four children fail (Selector returns FAILURE).**

$$P(\text{FAIL}) = q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_3 \cdot q_4$$

Multiplying step by step:

$$P(\text{FAIL}) = 0.1 \times 0.3 = 0.03$$
$$P(\text{FAIL}) = 0.03 \times 0.5 = 0.015$$
$$P(\text{FAIL}) = 0.015 \times 0.7 = 0.0105$$

**Step 3 — Probability of SUCCESS or RUNNING.**

$$P(\text{SUCCESS or RUNNING}) = 1 - P(\text{FAIL}) = 1 - 0.0105 = 0.9895$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.9895 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 98.95\%}$$

> **Engineering insight:** Even with one child having only 30% success, the ordered Selector ensures the overall mission is achievable nearly 99% of the time, justifying the **fallback-first design** of behaviour trees in real robots.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HFSM & Behaviour Trees (KTU 2024)**

- ✅ **HFSM** is a 6-tuple $(S, \Sigma, \delta, s_0, F, H)$ where $H$ is the parent–child hierarchy forming a tree.
- ✅ **Superstate** encapsulates substates; only one substate is active at a time within a superstate.
- ✅ **Event-bubbling** lets an unmatched event "travel up" to the parent superstate for a transition.
- ✅ **Orthogonal (AND) regions** allow multiple superstates to run in parallel within the same parent.
- ✅ **State reduction** for $n$ modes split into $k$ clusters is $\eta = \dfrac{2^{n}}{k \cdot 2^{n/k}}$.
- ✅ A **Behaviour Tree** has two categories: **Control Nodes** (Sequence, Selector, Parallel, Decorator) and **Execution Nodes** (Action, Condition).
- ✅ Sequence = logical AND of children; Selector = logical OR with priority.
- ✅ Condition nodes return only SUCCESS or FAILURE — never RUNNING.
- ✅ Action nodes may return SUCCESS, FAILURE, or RUNNING (for long-running primitives).
- ✅ Tick complexity of a balanced binary BT is $\mathcal{O}(\log_2 N)$.
- ✅ Selector's overall success probability: $P = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1 - p_i)$.
- ✅ HFSM = event-driven; BT = tick-driven — choose BT for highly reactive mobile robots.
- ✅ **Hybrid** architecture: HFSM for *mission phases*, BT for *reactive sub-tasks* inside each phase.
- ✅ ROS 2 *Nav2* stack uses BT.CPP — BT is the *de facto* standard in modern mobile robotics.
- ✅ Avoid confusing substates (exclusive) with orthogonal regions (parallel) in diagrams — **2-mark trap** in KTU valuation.
- ✅ Typical BT tick rate for mobile robots: **10 Hz to 50 Hz**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mission Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mission Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Mission Planning* in mobile robotics is the **high-level cognitive process** by which an autonomous robot determines a sequence of actions and trajectories required to achieve a defined operational goal, while satisfying environmental constraints, kinematic limits, and dynamic uncertainties.

## 1.1 What Exactly Is Mission Planning?

In a broad sense, **Mission Planning** is the bridge between a robot's *intention* (the goal) and its *execution* (motor commands). It answers three fundamental questions:

1. **Where do I want to go?** → Goal specification
2. **How do I get there?** → Path / trajectory generation
3. **What could go wrong, and how do I react?** → Contingency and replanning

Mission Planning is typically decomposed into a hierarchy:

- **Strategic (Mission) Level:** Highest abstraction. Decides *what* tasks to perform (e.g., "Deliver package from A to B within 15 minutes").
- **Tactical (Path) Level:** Decides *how* to navigate globally (e.g., "Use Corridor 2, then turn left").
- **Execution (Reactive) Level:** Handles *real-time* obstacle avoidance and low-level control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes the **deliberative vs. reactive vs. hybrid paradigm**. Students must know which architecture suits which application.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The Google Maps Mental Model

Imagine you are driving a car from your home (Kochi) to your college in Thiruvananthapuram. Your **brain** performs mission planning continuously:

- **Pre-trip planning (Deliberative):** You open Google Maps, type the destination, and it shows the optimal route via NH-66.
- **Mid-trip replanning (Reactive):** You see a traffic jam — Maps instantly suggests an alternative route.
- **Local avoidance (Reflexive):** A pedestrian suddenly crosses — you brake immediately without thinking about the destination.

| Layer | Human Analogy | Robotic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Mission | "Reach office by 9 AM" | Goal specification, task scheduler |
| Path | "Take NH-66" | Global planner (A*, RRT) |
| Motion | "Steer slightly left" | Local planner (DWA, Potential Field) |
| Control | "Press accelerator" | PID / feedback controller |

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

A well-designed mission planner is judged by four metrics (all must be explicitly stated for full marks in KTU Part A):

- **Optimality:** Does it produce the shortest / lowest-cost path?
- **Completeness:** Will it find *a* solution if one exists?
- **Time Complexity:** How fast does it compute a plan?
- **Robustness:** Can it recover from dynamic disturbances?

> [!TIP]
> Standard reference values used in KTU problems: Robot radius $r$, wheel radius $R$, wheelbase $L$, map resolution $0.05$–$0.5$ m.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Planning Pipeline (Operational Logic)

A standard mission planning module operates in this sequence:

1. **Perception** — Build a map using sensors (LIDAR, camera, odometry).
2. **World Modeling** — Maintain an internal representation (occupancy grid, topological graph).
3. **Goal Specification** — Define start $q_{start}$ and goal $q_{goal}$ in configuration space.
4. **Global Planning** — Generate a coarse, optimal path through known obstacles.
5. **Local Planning** — Refine the path to handle dynamic obstacles and kinematic constraints.
6. **Replanning** — Trigger if the global plan becomes invalid.
7. **Execution** — Send velocity commands $(v, \omega)$ to the controller.

## 2.2 Configuration Space (C-Space) — The Heart of Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every KTU Module-4 planning question starts with C-Space.** Mastering this is non-negotiable.

**Definition:** The *Configuration Space* $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible configurations $q$ a robot can attain. For a planar mobile robot:
$$\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2 \quad \text{(position only)} \quad \text{or} \quad \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3 \quad \text{(position + orientation)}$$

- $\mathcal{C}_{free}$: collision-free configurations.
- $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$: configurations in collision.

$$\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_{free} \cup \mathcal{C}_{obs}, \quad \mathcal{C}_{free} \cap \mathcal{C}_{obs} = \emptyset$$

### Why C-Space?
A robot is **not a point**. To simplify planning, we *shrink* the robot to a point and *grow* the obstacles using the **Minkowski Sum**.

$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{q \in \mathcal{C} \;\vert\; R(q) \cap \mathcal{O} \neq \emptyset\}$$

Equivalently, for a translating robot of shape $A$ and obstacle $B$:
$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = B \oplus (-A) = \{b - a \;\vert\; b \in B,\; a \in A\}$$

## 2.3 Classical Planning Algorithms

### A) Roadmap Methods
Reduce continuous space to a 1-D graph.

- **Visibility Graph:** Connect $q_{start}$, $q_{goal}$, and all obstacle vertices with straight lines that don't intersect obstacles. Shortest path guaranteed but fragile to narrow passages.
- **Voronoi Diagram:** Connect points equidistant from obstacles. Maximizes clearance but paths are longer.

### B) Cell Decomposition
Partition the free space into simple cells.

- **Exact Cell Decomposition:** Trapezoidal decomposition. Cells are convex polygons. Complete and optimal.
- **Approximate Cell Decomposition:** Regular grid (e.g., 4-connected, 8-connected). Resolution-complete.

### C) Potential Field Method
Treat the robot as a particle under an artificial force field.

- **Attractive force** pulls toward the goal.
- **Repulsive force** pushes away from obstacles.

> [!WARNING]
> Potential fields suffer from **local minima** — a classic KTU exam pitfall. They are NOT complete.

### D) Sampling-Based Methods (Modern Standard)

- **PRM (Probabilistic Roadmap):** Multi-query. Builds a graph by sampling random configurations and connecting them. Probabilistically complete.
- **RRT (Rapidly-Exploring Random Tree):** Single-query. Grows a tree from $q_{start}$ toward random samples. Excellent for high-dimensional spaces. Probabilistically complete but NOT optimal (RRT* fixes this).

## 2.4 Search Algorithms on Discrete Graphs

For grid maps, planners reduce to graph search. The two you MUST know:

- **Dijkstra's Algorithm:** Optimal, but explores blindly in all directions. Cost $f(n) = g(n)$.
- **A* Search:** Optimal *if* the heuristic is admissible. Cost:
$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$
where $g(n)$ is the cost-so-far and $h(n)$ is the heuristic estimate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Admissible heuristic: $h(n) \leq \text{true cost from } n \text{ to goal}$. Common examples: Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{C}$ | Configuration Space | Set of all $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | dimensionless |
| $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | C-space obstacle | $B \oplus (-A)$ | dimensionless |
| $U_{att}$ | Attractive potential | $\frac{1}{2}\,k_{att}\,\rho_{goal}^{2}$ | J (joules) |
| $U_{rep}$ | Repulsive potential | $\frac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}$ if $\rho \leq \rho_0$, else $0$ | J |
| $\vec{F}_{att}$ | Attractive force | $-\nabla U_{att} = -k_{att}\,\rho_{goal}\,\hat{\rho}$ | N |
| $\vec{F}_{rep}$ | Repulsive force | $-\nabla U_{rep}$ | N |
| $f(n)$ | A* evaluation | $g(n) + h(n)$ | path cost units |
| $h(n)$ | Heuristic | $\leq$ true remaining cost | same as $g$ |
| $\rho_0$ | Influence distance | Threshold beyond which $U_{rep}=0$ | m |
| $k_{att}$ | Attractive gain | Tuning constant $\geq 1$ | N/m |
| $\eta$ | Repulsive gain | Tuning constant $\geq 1$ | N/m² |
| $L$ | Path length | $\sum_{i} \sqrt{\Delta x_i^{2} + \Delta y_i^{2}}$ | m |
| $T_{plan}$ | Planning time | Depends on algorithm complexity | s |

> Note: All pipe symbols above (such as $\vert$) are rendered using `\vert` to keep the table intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Automation (Amazon Kiva):** Uses A* variants on occupancy grids to route hundreds of robots.
- **Autonomous Cars (Waymo, Tesla):** Hybrid planner — RRT* for global, MPC / Potential Field for local.
- **Space Exploration (NASA Mars Rovers):** PRM-based planners for long-distance navigation with hazard maps.
- **Disaster Response (DARPA SubT):** Sampling-based planners in unknown, degraded environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minkowski Sum for C-Space Obstacle

**Setup:** A circular robot of radius $r$ moves in a 2D workspace containing a single point obstacle $P = (x_p, y_p)$.

**Goal:** Find the set of robot center positions that cause a collision.

**Step 1 — Geometric Insight.**
The robot of radius $r$ collides with point $P$ if and only if the distance between the robot's center $(x, y)$ and $P$ is less than $r$:
$$\sqrt{(x - x_p)^{2} + (y - y_p)^{2}} < r$$

**Step 2 — Square Both Sides.**
$$(x - x_p)^{2} + (y - y_p)^{2} < r^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Generalize for a Polygon Obstacle $B$.**
If the robot has shape $A$, then collision occurs when the translated robot intersects $B$:
$$A(q) \cap B \neq \emptyset$$

Solving for $q$:
$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{q \;\vert\; \exists\, a \in A,\; b \in B \text{ such that } q = b - a\}$$

**Step 4 — Express as Minkowski Sum.**
$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = B \oplus (-A) = \{b - a \;\vert\; b \in B,\; a \in A\}$$

**Conclusion:** Growing the obstacle by the robot's shape (Minkowski sum) reduces the planning problem to finding a path for a *point* robot through $\mathcal{C}_{free}$.

$$\boxed{\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \left(B \oplus (-A)\right)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Potential Field Force Equations

**Step 1 — Define the Attractive Potential.**
The most common form is a quadratic well:
$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q)$$

where $\rho_{goal}(q) = \lVert q - q_{goal} \rVert$ is the Euclidean distance to the goal.

**Step 2 — Compute the Attractive Force.**
The force is the negative gradient of the potential:
$$\vec{F}_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att}\, (q - q_{goal})$$

**Step 3 — Define the Repulsive Potential.**
For an obstacle at distance $\rho(q)$ with influence radius $\rho_0$:
$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}\, \eta\, \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\[6pt] 0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the Repulsive Potential.**
Let $u = \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}$. Then $U_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} \eta u^{2}$, so:
$$\frac{\partial U_{rep}}{\partial \rho} = \eta\, u \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\right) = -\frac{\eta}{\rho^{2}} \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)$$

Applying the chain rule with $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial q} = \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$:
$$\vec{F}_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}} \cdot \frac{q - q_{obs}}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Total Force on Robot.**
$$\vec{F}_{total}(q) = \vec{F}_{att}(q) + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{F}_{rep,\,i}(q)$$

**Step 6 — Robot Motion (Velocity Command).**
A simple kinematic unicycle model maps force to control:
$$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta, \quad \dot{y} = v \sin\theta, \quad \dot{\theta} = \omega$$
with $v = K_v \lVert \vec{F}_{total} \rVert$ and $\omega = K_\omega\,(\theta_{desired} - \theta_{current})$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: A* on a 4×4 Grid

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A robot must travel from $S=(0,0)$ to $G=(3,3)$ on a 4-connected grid. Cells $(1,2)$ and $(2,2)$ are blocked. Use $A^*$ with Manhattan distance heuristic and $g$-cost = 1 per step.

**Step 1 — Initialize Open List.**
Open = $\{(0,0)\}$, with $f(0,0) = 0 + 6 = 6$. Closed = $\emptyset$.

**Step 2 — Expand (0,0).**
Successors: $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$.
- $(1,0)$: $g=1$, $h=5$, $f=6$.
- $(0,1)$: $g=1$, $h=5$, $f=6$.

**Step 3 — Expand lowest-$f$ node, say (1,0).**
Successors: $(2,0)$, $(1,1)$, $(0,0)$ (skip — in Closed).
- $(2,0)$: $g=2$, $h=4$, $f=6$.
- $(1,1)$: $g=2$, $h=4$, $f=6$.

**Step 4 — Continue expansion...**
The algorithm expands cells in concentric "f-contours" around $S$, ensuring the first time $G$ is popped from Open, the path is optimal.

**Step 5 — Optimal Path Returned.**
$$S \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (3,3) = G$$
Total cost $= 6$ (vs. Manhattan distance $= 6$, optimal).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Visibility Graph | Yes (exact) | Yes | $O(n^{3})$ | $O(n^{2})$ | Sparse polygonal maps |
| Voronoi Diagram | Yes | No (longer paths) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | High-clearance requirements |
| Trapezoidal Decomposition | Yes | Yes (resolution-dependent) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Indoor polygonal spaces |
| Grid + BFS | Resolution-complete | Yes (uniform cost) | $O(V+E)$ | $O(V)$ | Small grids |
| A* | Yes (with admissible $h$) | Yes | Exponential worst-case | $O(V)$ | Heuristic-rich maps |
| Potential Field | No (local minima) | No | $O(1)$ per step | $O(N_{obs})$ | Real-time local avoidance |
| PRM | Probabilistically complete | No (PRM* = yes) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Multi-query, static |
| RRT | Probabilistically complete | No (RRT* = yes) | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | High-dimensional, single-query |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Mission Planning System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User / Mission Goal] --> B[Goal Specification Module]
    B --> C[World Model and Map Database]
    C --> D[Global Planner A star RRT PRM]
    D --> E[Local Planner DWA Potential Field]
    E --> F[Trajectory Tracker Controller]
    F --> G[Mobile Robot Hardware]
    G --> H[Sensor Feedback LIDAR IMU Camera]
    H --> C
    F --> I{Replan Trigger}
    I -->|Yes new obstacle| D
    I -->|No path valid| E
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Block Diagram:** Standard closed-loop mission planning architecture. The dashed feedback path represents sensor-driven map updates, while the dashed lateral path represents replanning triggers.

---

## 4.2 Configuration Space Transformation (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workspace W with shaped robot] --> B[Shrink robot to point]
    B --> C[Grow obstacles via Minkowski sum]
    C --> D[Configuration space C free]
    D --> E[Plan point robot path]
    E --> F[Transform back to workspace trajectory]
```

> **Figure 4.2 — C-Space Reduction Pipeline:** This is the canonical 5-step reduction taught in KTU Module 4. Marks are awarded in Board exams for explicitly stating "Minkowski sum" and "point-robot equivalence."

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical Planning Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission Planning] --> B{Environment Known?}
    B -->|Fully Known| C[Deliberative Planner]
    B -->|Partially Known| D[Hybrid Planner]
    B -->|Unknown| E[Reactive Planner]
    C --> F[A star Dijkstra RRT]
    D --> G[Global RRT plus Local DWA]
    E --> H[Potential Field Bug Algorithm]
    F --> I[Optimal Path Output]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Decision Tree:** Helps students select the correct planner class for an exam problem. Common KTU phrasing: *"Suggest a suitable planner for a warehouse with static racks but dynamic human workers."* → Answer: Hybrid (Global RRT + Local DWA).

---

## 4.4 A* Search Visualization (Grid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Start at 0,0] --> N1[Node 1,0 g1 h5 f6]
    N1 --> N2[Node 2,0 g2 h4 f6]
    N2 --> N3[Node 3,0 g3 h3 f6]
    N3 --> N4[Node 3,1 g4 h2 f6]
    N4 --> N5[Node 3,2 g5 h1 f6]
    N5 --> G[Goal 3,3 g6 h0 f6]
    N1 -.blocked.-> B1[Node 1,2 BLOCKED]
    N2 -.blocked.-> B2[Node 2,2 BLOCKED]
```

> **Figure 4.4 — A* Graph Expansion:** Solid arrows = traversed path; dashed arrows = pruned/blocked neighbors. This exact pattern appears in KTU Module-4 numerical questions worth 7 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Configuration Space (C-Space). Why is it important in mobile robot motion planning?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** C-Space $\mathcal{C}$ is the set of all possible configurations $q$ that a robot can attain. For a planar mobile robot, $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ (position) or $\mathbb{R}^3$ (position + orientation). It is partitioned into $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ (collision-free) and $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ (in collision).
- **Importance (1 mark):** C-Space reduces the motion-planning problem for a *shaped robot* in a *shaped workspace* to that of a *point robot* in an equivalent space — greatly simplifying algorithm design.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a Visibility Graph and a Voronoi Diagram as roadmap methods.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Visibility Graph | Voronoi Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Path characteristic | Shortest (optimal length) | Maximum clearance (safe) |
| Vertices used | Obstacle corners | Points equidistant from 2+ obstacles |
| Computational cost | $O(n^{3})$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Best for | Sparse, convex obstacles | Cluttered, safety-critical maps |
| Optimality guarantee | Yes (shortest path) | No (paths are longer) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Potential Field Method** for mobile robot path planning with suitable equations. Discuss its **advantages and limitations**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A circular mobile robot of radius $r = 0.3$ m must travel from start $S = (0, 0)$ to goal $G = (10, 0)$ in a workspace containing a single circular obstacle centered at $(5, 0)$ with radius $R_{obs} = 1$ m. Using the **Minkowski Sum** concept, determine the **Configuration Space obstacle** and whether a straight-line path exists. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Potential Field Method (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Potential Field Method treats the robot as a charged particle moving under the influence of an artificial potential field, where the goal acts as an attractor and obstacles act as repulsors.

**Attractive Potential & Force (2 marks):**
$$U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\, k_{att}\, \rho_{goal}^{2}(q), \qquad \vec{F}_{att}(q) = -k_{att}\,(q - q_{goal})$$

**Repulsive Potential & Force (2 marks):**
$$U_{rep}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\, \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_{0}}\right)^{2} \quad \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_0$$
$$\vec{F}_{rep}(q) = \eta \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right) \frac{1}{\rho^{2}}\,\hat{\rho}$$

**Total Force & Motion:**
$$\vec{F}_{total} = \vec{F}_{att} + \sum_{i} \vec{F}_{rep,\,i}$$

**Advantages (1 mark):** Real-time, computationally cheap ($O(N_{obs})$), smooth trajectories, easy to implement.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Suffers from *local minima* (robot can get stuck), oscillations in narrow passages, no global optimality guarantee.

---

#### **Part (b) — C-Space Obstacle Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the shapes (1 mark).**
- Robot: circle of radius $r = 0.3$ m.
- Obstacle: circle of radius $R_{obs} = 1$ m centered at $O = (5, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Apply Minkowski Sum (2 marks).**
For two circles, the Minkowski sum is a circle centered at the same point with radius equal to the sum of radii:
$$R_{\mathcal{C}_{obs}} = R_{obs} + r = 1 + 0.3 = 1.3 \text{ m}$$
$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{(x, y) \;\vert\; (x - 5)^{2} + (y - 0)^{2} \leq 1.3^{2}\}$$

**Step 3 — Distance Analysis (2 marks).**
The straight line from $S=(0,0)$ to $G=(10,0)$ passes through $(5,0)$, which is the center of $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$.
- Minimum distance from line to obstacle center = $0$ m.
- $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ radius = $1.3$ m.

Since $0 < 1.3$, the straight-line path **intersects** $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (2 marks).**
A collision-free straight-line path **does NOT exist**. The planner must detour — e.g., with a clearance $d > 1.3$ m above or below the $x$-axis. The minimum detour altitude is $\pm 1.3$ m, giving a path length of:
$$L = 2\sqrt{5^{2} + 1.3^{2}} \approx 2 \times 5.166 \approx 10.33 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_{detour} \approx 10.33 \text{ m vs. } L_{straight} = 10 \text{ m}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **A* search algorithm** for grid-based path planning. Derive its evaluation function and explain the role of the heuristic. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A robot in a 5×5 grid (4-connected, 8 in total free cells) must travel from $S=(0,0)$ to $G=(4,4)$. Cells $(2,2)$, $(2,3)$, and $(3,2)$ are blocked. Execute A* using **Euclidean distance heuristic** and show the **optimal path and its cost**. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — A* Algorithm Theory (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A* is a best-first graph search algorithm that finds the least-cost path from a start node to a goal node using a heuristic to guide expansion toward the goal.

**Evaluation Function (2 marks):**
$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$
where $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to $n$, and $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to goal.

**Heuristic Properties (2 marks):**
- *Admissible:* $h(n) \leq$ true remaining cost. Guarantees optimality.
- *Consistent (Monotonic):* $h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$. Guarantees no reopenings.
- Common heuristics: Manhattan ($h = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$) for 4-connected; Euclidean ($h = \sqrt{\Delta x^{2} + \Delta y^{2}}$) for 8-connected.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Initialize Open list with start node; Closed list empty.
2. Pop node $n$ with lowest $f(n)$ from Open.
3. If $n$ is goal, **reconstruct and return path**.
4. Add $n$ to Closed; expand neighbors.
5. For each neighbor, compute $g, h, f$; add/update in Open if better path found.
6. Repeat until goal found or Open empty (failure).

---

#### **Part (b) — A* Numerical Execution (7 marks)**

**Grid Setup:** 5×5, $S=(0,0)$, $G=(4,4)$, blocked: $\{(2,2),(2,3),(3,2)\}$. Movement: 4-connected, step cost = 1. Heuristic: Euclidean $h(n) = \sqrt{(x-4)^{2} + (y-4)^{2}}$.

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Initialize.**
Open = $\{(0,0)\}$, $f(0,0) = 0 + \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Expand frontier.**
After expanding $(0,0)$, neighbors $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$ enter Open with $f \approx 5.83$ and $f \approx 5.83$. Continue expanding along the diagonal (the Euclidean heuristic favors cells closer to $G$).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Avoid blocked cells.**
The path must detour around $\{(2,2),(2,3),(3,2)\}$. A valid detour goes:
$$(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (1,1) \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (1,4) \to (2,4) \to (3,4) \to (4,4)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Compute cost.**
Number of steps = 8. So $g(G) = 8$, $h(G) = 0$, $f(G) = 8$.

$$\boxed{\text{Optimal Path Length} = 8 \text{ units}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — A* Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mark Closed:** Many students re-expand the same node, leading to incorrect $f$-values. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using non-admissible heuristic:** If $h$ overestimates, A* may return a *non-optimal* path. Examiners specifically test admissibility. Loss: 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing 4-connectivity with 8-connectivity:** Diagonal moves cost $\sqrt{2}$ in 8-connected grids, not 1. State the connectivity at the start of your answer. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping heuristic justification:** Just writing $f = g + h$ without explaining *why* heuristic matters = 0 marks for that 2-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 4 Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Mission Planning** = (Goal Specification) + (Path Planning) + (Replanning).
- ☐ **Three architectures:** Deliberative (slow, optimal), Reactive (fast, myopic), Hybrid (best of both — *most exam-asked*).
- ☐ **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$** partitions into $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$.
- ☐ **Minkowski Sum** shrinks the robot to a point and grows the obstacle: $\mathcal{C}_{obs} = B \oplus (-A)$.
- ☐ **Roadmap methods:** Visibility Graph (shortest), Voronoi Diagram (max clearance).
- ☐ **Cell Decomposition:** Exact (trapezoidal) vs. Approximate (grid). A* runs on grids.
- ☐ **Potential Field:** Attractive + Repulsive. Suffers from **local minima** — incomplete.
- ☐ **A* formula:** $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Heuristic must be **admissible** for optimality.
- ☐ **Sampling-based:** PRM (multi-query), RRT (single-query). Both **probabilistically complete**.
- ☐ **Performance metrics:** Optimality, Completeness, Time complexity, Robustness.
- ☐ **Real-world examples to quote:** Amazon Kiva (A*), Waymo (RRT*+MPC), Mars rovers (PRM).
- ☐ **Latex must-use:** Always show $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ and the Minkowski Sum notation in any planning answer — these are the two most-likely 7-mark derivation questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Suggest a planner for X scenario"*, ALWAYS answer in this 4-step format:
> 1. Classify scenario (static vs. dynamic, known vs. unknown).
> 2. Choose planner class (deliberative / reactive / hybrid).
> 3. Name specific algorithm (A*, RRT, DWA, Potential Field).
> 4. Justify (cite complexity, completeness, optimality).
> Following this template almost guarantees full marks on 7-mark design questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping: Map Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mapping: Map Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In mobile robotics, **Mapping** is the process by which a robot constructs a spatial model of its environment using sensor measurements and pose estimates. A **Map Representation** is the formal data structure used to encode and store this spatial information in a form that the robot's perception, planning, and localization modules can interpret and update.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OERAT724, Module 4), map representations are broadly classified into three primary paradigms:

1. **Metric (Grid) Maps** – Continuous or discretized geometric representation of space.
2. **Topological Maps** – Graph-based abstraction of the environment using nodes and edges.
3. **Hybrid Maps** – Combined metric-topological representations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to differentiate between metric, topological, and hybrid maps, derive the update equations for occupancy grids, and analyze the computational complexity of each representation with respect to memory and time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist navigating an unfamiliar city:

- **Metric Map** = A paper road map with streets, buildings, and distances drawn to scale. Every centimeter on the map represents a fixed number of meters in reality. (Precise, but bulky and hard to update mentally.)
- **Topological Map** = A subway diagram. Only key stations (nodes) and connections (edges) matter. Distances and exact geography are abstracted away. (Compact, but lacks fine detail.)
- **Occupancy Grid** = A chessboard overlay on a video game. Each square is marked "free," "occupied," or "unknown." The robot slowly fills in the board as it explores. (Probabilistic and easy to update, but memory grows with area.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Standard):** A map is a function `m : S → L` that assigns a label `L` (e.g., occupancy, traversability, semantic class) to each location `S` in a chosen reference frame, where `S ⊂ ℝ²` or `S ⊂ ℝ³`.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Grid cell resolution | `r` | **0.05 m to 0.5 m** (typical) |
| Log-odds prior (unknown) | `l₀` | **0** (i.e., P = 0.5) |
| Log-odds of occupied | `l_occ` | **+0.85 to +1.4** |
| Log-odds of free | `l_free` | **−0.4 to −0.85** |
| Map update frequency | `f_map` | **1 Hz to 20 Hz** |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the unit of grid resolution and the role of the **log-odds value zero** (representing maximum uncertainty). Memorize these constants.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Map Representations

#### A. Metric / Grid-Based Maps

The environment is discretized into a 2D (or 3D) lattice of cells. Each cell stores a state variable.

- **Why:** Robots perceive the world as a continuous stream of points; discretization makes probabilistic reasoning tractable.
- **How:** A range sensor (LiDAR, sonar) emits a ray; cells along the ray are updated using the **inverse sensor model**.

**Sub-types:**
1. **Occupancy Grid Map (OGM):** Binary or probabilistic per cell.
2. **Counting / Evidence Grid:** Raw hit and miss counters per cell.
3. **Reflection Probability Map:** Models glass / mirror surfaces.
4. **Elevation / 2.5D Map:** Stores height for terrain traversal.

#### B. Topological Maps

The environment is represented as a graph `G = (V, E)`.
- **Vertices (V):** Distinct places (rooms, intersections, landmarks).
- **Edges (E):** Navigable connections (corridors, doors).

- **Why:** Highly compact; scales to large environments (entire buildings, cities).
- **How:** Place recognition (e.g., vision-based) creates vertices; odometry connects them.

> [!NOTE]
> Topological maps suffer from the **perceptual aliasing** problem — two distinct locations may look identical to the sensor, making vertex creation ambiguous.

#### C. Feature-Based Maps

A sparse set of geometric primitives (points, lines, planes) is extracted from sensor data.
- **Why:** Low memory, robust to dynamic clutter, ideal for SLAM front-ends.
- **How:** Feature extractors (e.g., SIFT, FAST corners, line segments from LiDAR) detect landmarks; an EKF or pose-graph stores their global coordinates.

#### D. Semantic Maps

Augment metric/topological maps with object class labels (door, chair, person).
- **Why:** Enables high-level task planning ("go to the kitchen").
- **How:** Deep learning classifiers (CNN, YOLO) label regions of a metric grid or vertices of a topological graph.

#### E. Hybrid Maps

Combine the geometric precision of metric maps with the structural abstraction of topological maps.
- **Why:** Best of both worlds — local precision + global scalability.
- **How:** A top-level topological graph governs navigation; each node contains a local metric sub-map.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variable Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Occupancy probability | `P(m_i)` | Probability that cell `i` is occupied | Dimensionless, range `[0, 1]` |
| 2 | Log-odds transform | `l(m_i) = ln [ P(m_i) / (1 − P(m_i)) ]` | Log-odds of cell `i` | Range `(−∞, +∞)` |
| 3 | Inverse log-odds | `P(m_i) = 1 / (1 + e^{−l(m_i)})` | Recover probability from log-odds | Logistic sigmoid |
| 4 | Log-odds update | `l_t(m_i) = l_{t−1}(m_i) + l_inv(m_i) − l₀` | Bayesian update additive form | Additive in log-odds |
| 5 | Prior (unknown) | `l₀ = 0` | P(m_i) = 0.5 | Maximum uncertainty |
| 6 | Inverse sensor model | `P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)` | Likelihood given measurement and pose | Conditional probability |
| 7 | Number of cells | `N = (L_x × L_y) / r²` | Total grid cells | L = map side length (m) |
| 8 | Memory size | `M = N × b` | Bytes required | `b` = bytes/cell (typ. 1) |
| 9 | Topological graph | `G = (V, E)` | V = places, E = connections | Sparse storage |
| 10 | Feature covariance | `Σ_f` (3×3) | Uncertainty of landmark | Units m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board exam tip: When asked to "derive the update rule for an occupancy grid," always start from **Bayes' rule** and end with the **additive log-odds form** (Equation 4). This is the single most-tested formula in this module.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla):** Use **2.5D metric grids** + **HD topological lane graphs** (hybrid).
- **Warehouse Robots (Amazon Kiva):** Rely on **2D occupancy grids** for obstacle avoidance and **topological node sequences** for route planning.
- **Vacuum Robots (Roomba):** Internally use **2D occupancy grids** at low resolution (~5 cm) to build coverage maps.
- **Mars Rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance):** Employ **feature-based landmark maps** with stereo vision for long-baseline navigation where GPS is unavailable.
- **Underwater AUVs:** Use **sonar occupancy grids** in 3D voxel form due to GPS-denied environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Occupancy Grid Update Rule (Bayesian Form)

**Goal:** Show how the log-odds update rule is derived from first principles of Bayesian probability.

**Step 1 — Problem Setup**

Let `m_i` be the occupancy state of grid cell `i` (either occupied or free). The robot receives a sequence of measurements `z_{1:t} = {z_1, z_2, …, z_t}` from poses `x_{1:t} = {x_1, x_2, …, x_t}`. We wish to compute the posterior:

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Markov Assumption (Recursive Bayesian Update)**

Assuming measurements are conditionally independent given the map and the current pose, the posterior can be written recursively:

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \, P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

The denominator `P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})` is a normalizing constant η that does not depend on `m_i`.

**Step 3 — Introduce the Log-Odds Ratio**

Define the log-odds `l(m_i)` as:

$$
l(m_i) = \ln \frac{P(m_i)}{1 - P(m_i)}
$$

Let `l_t(m_i)` denote the log-odds after incorporating measurement `z_t`, and `l_{t−1}(m_i)` denote the prior log-odds from time `t − 1`. We also define the inverse sensor model in log-odds form:

$$
l_{inv}(m_i) = \ln \frac{P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t)}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the Additive Recursion**

We can write the posterior as a ratio, eliminating the normalization constant:

$$
\frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})} = \frac{P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)}{1 - P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t)} \cdot \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides yields the celebrated **additive log-odds update**:

$$
l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{inv}(m_i) - l_0
$$

where `l₀ = 0` is the log-odds of a uniform prior `P(m_i) = 0.5`.

**Step 5 — Conversion Back to Probability**

To retrieve the occupancy probability for path planning and display:

$$
P(m_i) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \exp\!\big(l_t(m_i)\big)} = \frac{\exp\!\big(l_t(m_i)\big)}{1 + \exp\!\big(l_t(m_i)\big)}
$$

This is the standard **logistic sigmoid** function applied to the accumulated log-odds.

**Step 6 — Interpretation of `l_0`**

If the map is initialized with `l_0 = 0` (i.e., `P = 0.5`), then the unknown cells remain at probability 0.5 until the first measurement updates them. This guarantees an unbiased prior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why log-odds?** Multiplication of probabilities (Bayes' rule) becomes **addition** in log-odds space, which is computationally cheap and numerically stable. Floating-point underflow (probability → 0) is avoided because the log-odds range `(−∞, +∞)` is mapped to real numbers.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Sensor Model (Range Finder)

**Step 1 — Setup**

A range sensor at pose `x_t = (x, y, θ)` returns a distance measurement `z_t = d` along a beam. The cell `m_i` lies at distance `r_i` from the sensor along the beam, with thickness `Δr/2` on each side.

**Step 2 — Three Cases**

$$
P(m_i \mid z_t, x_t) =
\begin{cases}
P_{occ} & \text{if } 0 \leq r_i < d - \Delta r/2 \\[4pt]
P_{free} & \text{if } d - \Delta r/2 \leq r_i \leq d + \Delta r/2 \\[4pt]
0.5 & \text{if } r_i > d + \Delta r/2 \text{ (beyond the obstacle)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Typical Values**

- `P_{occ} = 0.9` (high confidence that the cell at the end of the beam is occupied).
- `P_{free} = 0.3` (mild confidence the cell is free — sensor may have hit an edge).
- `P = 0.5` (no information beyond the beam endpoint).

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners often ask: *"Why is the inverse sensor model discontinuous?"* Answer: Because the ray-tracing algorithm updates only the cells **along** the beam with `P_free` and the **endpoint** cell with `P_occ`; cells beyond are left untouched, creating a hard step.

### 3.3 Memory and Computational Complexity Analysis

**Metric Grid (2D, area A, resolution r):**

$$
N = \frac{A}{r^2} \quad \text{and} \quad M = N \cdot b
$$

For `A = 100 m²`, `r = 0.1 m`, `b = 1 byte/cell`:

$$
N = \frac{100}{0.01} = 10{,}000 \text{ cells} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M = 10 \text{ kB}
$$

**Topological Map (V vertices, E edges):**

$$
M = V \cdot (b_v + k \cdot b_e)
$$

where `k = degree`, `b_v` = bytes per vertex, `b_e` = bytes per edge. For `V = 50`, `b_v = 16`, `b_e = 8`:

$$
M = 50 \times (16 + 2 \times 8) = 1600 \text{ bytes} = 1.6 \text{ kB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Topological maps are ~6× more memory-efficient for the same environment, but lose geometric precision. This trade-off is a classic KTU 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Map Representations

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Map Representations] --> B[Metric Maps]
    A --> C[Topological Maps]
    A --> D[Hybrid Maps]
    A --> E[Semantic Maps]
    A --> F[Feature Based Maps]

    B --> B1[Occupancy Grid]
    B --> B2[Evidence Grid]
    B --> B3[2.5D Elevation Map]
    B --> B4[3D Voxel Grid]

    C --> C1[Graph Based]
    C --> C2[Voronoi Diagram]
    C --> C3[GVD Generalized Voronoi]

    D --> D1[Topological over Metric]
    D --> D2[Metric with Topological Index]

    F --> F1[Point Landmarks]
    F --> F2[Line Landmarks]
    F --> F3[Plane Landmarks]
```

### 4.2 Occupancy Grid Map — Functional Update Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sensor Reading z_t] --> B[Robot Pose x_t]
    B --> C[Ray Casting Algorithm]
    C --> D[Identify Cells Along Beam]
    D --> E1[Free Cells: l_free Update]
    D --> E2[Endpoint Cell: l_occ Update]
    E1 --> F[Log Odds Update l_t]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Convert to Probability P]
    G --> H[Updated Map m_t]
    H --> I[Path Planner / Display]
```

### 4.3 Topological Map — Graph Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[Vertex: Lobby] --- N2[Vertex: Corridor A]
    N1 --- N3[Vertex: Staircase]
    N2 --- N4[Vertex: Room 101]
    N2 --- N5[Vertex: Room 102]
    N3 --- N6[Vertex: Floor 2 Hall]
    N6 --- N7[Vertex: Lab 201]
    N4 -.Edge: 8m.-> N5
    N7 -.Edge: Door.-> N3
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each **vertex** in the topological graph stores a fingerprint (visual signature) for place recognition, and each **edge** stores traversability cost, travel time, or geometric path.

### 4.4 Hybrid Map — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Global Topological Layer] --> B[Node 1: Building Entrance]
    A --> C[Node 2: Hallway]
    A --> D[Node 3: Office Wing]

    B --> B1[Local Metric Sub Map 1]
    C --> C1[Local Metric Sub Map 2]
    D --> D1[Local Metric Sub Map 3]

    B1 --> B2[Occupancy Grid 5m x 5m]
    C1 --> C2[Occupancy Grid 20m x 3m]
    D1 --> D2[Occupancy Grid 15m x 15m]
```

> [!TIP]
> Hybrid maps are common in **autonomous vehicle road maps** (e.g., OpenStreetMap as topological, lane grids as metric).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an occupancy grid map. Mention two advantages and one disadvantage.*

**Model Answer:**

An **occupancy grid map** is a discretized 2D (or 3D) representation of the environment in which the space is divided into a uniform lattice of cells, each storing the probability that the cell is occupied by an obstacle.

- **Advantages:** (i) Probabilistic and can fuse noisy sensor data; (ii) Easy to update incrementally and visualize.
- **Disadvantage:** Memory grows quadratically with area; resolution is fixed globally, leading to inefficiency in large open spaces.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for advantages + 1 for disadvantage)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Compare metric and topological map representations in terms of memory and precision.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Metric Map | Topological Map |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | High (O(area / r²)) | Low (O(vertices + edges)) |
| Precision | High (cell-level) | Low (abstract) |
| Scalability | Poor for large areas | Excellent |
| Perceptual aliasing | Robust | Vulnerable |

*(3 marks: 1.5 for the table + 1.5 for conclusion)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the log-odds update rule for an occupancy grid map from the Bayesian posterior formulation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A robot maps a 10 m × 10 m room at a resolution of 0.25 m using a 2D occupancy grid. Calculate the total number of cells, the memory required in kilobytes (assuming 1 byte per cell), and explain what happens if the resolution is halved to 0.5 m. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the recursive Bayesian update:

$$
P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t}) = \eta \cdot P(z_t \mid m_i, x_t) \cdot P(m_i \mid z_{1:t-1}, x_{1:t-1})
$$

where `η` is the normalizer. Forming the odds ratio and taking logarithm:

$$
l_t(m_i) = \ln \frac{P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}{1 - P(m_i \mid z_{1:t}, x_{1:t})}
$$

After algebraic manipulation (detailed in Section 3.1):

$$
l_t(m_i) = l_{t-1}(m_i) + l_{inv}(m_i) - l_0
$$

with `l₀ = 0` for the uniform prior. *(Full marks for correct chain of reasoning.)*

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Number of cells along one side:

$$
N_{side} = \frac{L}{r} = \frac{10}{0.25} = 40 \text{ cells}
$$

Step 2: Total cells:

$$
N = N_{side}^2 = 40 \times 40 = 1600 \text{ cells}
$$

Step 3: Memory:

$$
M = 1600 \times 1 \text{ byte} = 1600 \text{ bytes} = 1.5625 \text{ kB}
$$

Step 4: Halving resolution to `r = 0.5 m`:

$$
N_{side}^{new} = \frac{10}{0.5} = 20 \text{ cells} \Rightarrow N^{new} = 400 \text{ cells}
$$

Memory drops to **400 bytes (0.39 kB)** — a 4× reduction. However, fine details (e.g., chair legs) are lost, demonstrating the **precision vs memory trade-off**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the functional architecture of an occupancy grid mapping system. List any two inverse sensor model parameters and their typical values. (7 marks)

**(b)** A mobile robot must choose between a metric occupancy grid and a topological map for a 500 m × 500 m warehouse. Recommend the better representation, justify with memory calculations (assume 0.2 m grid resolution, 1 byte/cell; 200 vertices, 2 edges/vertex, 24 bytes per node+edge), and state the resulting planning trade-off. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Section 4.2 mermaid diagram** for the functional flow: sensor → pose → ray casting → cell identification → log-odds update → probability conversion → updated map.

Typical inverse sensor model parameters:
- `P_occ = 0.9` (cell at beam endpoint is occupied)
- `P_free = 0.3` (cells along beam are free, low confidence)

**(b) Recommendation (7 marks):**

Memory for metric grid:

$$
N = \frac{500 \times 500}{0.2^2} = \frac{250000}{0.04} = 6{,}250{,}000 \text{ cells}
$$

$$
M_{metric} = 6.25 \text{ MB}
$$

Memory for topological map:

$$
M_{topo} = 200 \times (16 + 2 \times 8) = 200 \times 32 = 6400 \text{ bytes} = 6.25 \text{ kB}
$$

**Recommendation:** **Topological map** is preferable due to a **1000× memory advantage**. However, it sacrifices fine-grained obstacle avoidance, so a **hybrid map** is the production-grade solution: topological for high-level routing, local occupancy grids around each vertex for collision avoidance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `l₀` in the update equation** — many students write `l_t = l_{t−1} + l_inv` and lose 2 marks. Always include the prior term, even if it is zero.
> 2. **Confusing inverse sensor model with forward sensor model** — inverse model = `P(m_i | z, x)`; forward model = `P(z | m_i, x)`. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — resolution `r` is in **meters**, area in **m²`. Mixing these will produce wrong cell counts.
> 4. **Ignoring the normalizing constant `η`** in Bayes' rule is acceptable only if you explicitly state "η does not depend on m_i and hence cancels in the odds ratio." Otherwise, you lose 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Map representation** is the data structure used by a robot to encode its environment — classified into **metric, topological, hybrid, semantic, and feature-based**.
- **Occupancy grids** discretize space into uniform cells; each cell stores occupancy probability `P(m_i)`.
- **Log-odds transform** linearizes Bayes' rule into an **additive** update: `l_t = l_{t−1} + l_inv − l₀`.
- **Prior** for unknown cells is **`l₀ = 0`**, equivalent to `P = 0.5`.
- **Inverse sensor model** updates cells along a beam with `P_free` and the endpoint with `P_occ`.
- **Memory for metric grids** scales as `O(A / r²)`; **topological maps** scale as `O(V + E)`.
- **Topological maps** are compact but suffer from **perceptual aliasing**.
- **Hybrid maps** combine the geometric precision of metric maps with the structural abstraction of topological maps.
- **Feature-based maps** store sparse landmarks (points, lines, planes) with covariance `Σ_f`.
- **Semantic maps** attach object labels to spatial primitives for high-level reasoning.
- **Real-world deployments:** Roomba → 2D occupancy grid; Autonomous cars → HD hybrid maps; Mars rovers → feature-based landmark maps.
- **Key equation to memorize (KTU Hot):** `l_t(m_i) = l_{t−1}(m_i) + l_inv(m_i) − l₀`.
- **Inverse transform to remember:** `P(m_i) = exp(l) / (1 + exp(l))`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iterative Closest Point for Sparse Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Iterative Closest Point for Sparse Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Iterative Closest Point for Sparse Mapping

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iterative Closest Point (ICP)** is a classical point-cloud registration algorithm that estimates the rigid-body transformation (rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$ and translation vector $t \in \mathbb{R}^3$) that best aligns two sets of spatial points. In the context of **sparse mapping** for mobile robotics, ICP operates on a small set of geometrically significant landmarks (corners, edges, distinct features) — rather than dense laser/visual scans — to refine the robot's pose estimate and incrementally build a sparse map of the environment.

Mathematically, given two finite point sets — the **reference (model) set** $P = \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_N\}$ and the **source (data) set** $Q = \{q_1, q_2, \dots, q_M\}$ — ICP seeks the transformation parameters $(R, t)$ that minimize the mean-squared alignment error:

$$E(R, t) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\Vert p_i \;-\; (R\,q_i + t) \bigr\Vert^{2}$$

For **sparse mapping**, $N$ and $M$ are small (typically $N < 200$), and the points correspond to detected features rather than raw scan measurements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "Fitting Two Puzzle Pieces Together":**
> Imagine you have two photographs of the same object taken from slightly different angles, and someone has placed a few pushpins on the matching corners of each photo. ICP is the *gradual, iterative process* of shifting and rotating one photo until every pushpin on it lies directly on top of its corresponding pushpin on the other photo.
>
> At every step, the algorithm asks: *"For each pin on the moving photo, which pin on the fixed photo is closest?"* Then it computes the *smallest* shift + rotation that pulls all moving pins towards their closest fixed partners, applies it, and repeats. Each pass typically makes the alignment a little better, until the improvement becomes negligible.
>
> In **sparse mapping** for a mobile robot, the "pushpins" are *landmarks* (door corners, pillar edges, distinct texture blobs) extracted from sensors, and the alignment tells the robot *"how I have moved since my last observation."*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Quantities in ICP

- **Alignment error (residual):** $E(R,t)$ in meters squared ($\text{m}^2$).
- **Number of correspondences:** $N$ (dimensionless).
- **Convergence tolerance:** $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$).
- **Maximum iterations:** $k_{\max}$ (e.g., 50).
- **Transformation update magnitude:** $\Delta_R$ (radians), $\Delta_t$ (meters).
- **Point density (sparse regime):** Typically fewer than **500** features per scan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Sparse" Matters:** Dense ICP uses thousands-to-millions of points (LiDAR sweeps, depth-image pixels) and is computationally expensive. **Sparse mapping ICP** is faster, requires less memory, and is preferred in feature-based SLAM (EKF-SLAM, Graph-SLAM, ORB-SLAM) where landmarks are deliberately extracted to be uniquely identifiable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the ICP Algorithm

The classical ICP algorithm proceeds in **two alternating phases** repeated iteratively until convergence:

#### Phase A — Correspondence Search (Data Association)
- For every point $q_i$ in the source set $Q$, find the nearest neighbour in the reference set $P$.
- Distance metric: Euclidean, $d(q_i, p_j) = \Vert q_i - p_j \Vert$.
- Implementation hint: KD-Tree accelerates the search from $O(NM)$ to $O(N \log M)$.

#### Phase B — Transformation Estimation (Registration)
- Given correspondences $\{(q_i, c_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $c_i$ is the closest point to $q_i$ in $P$, compute the rigid transformation $(R, t)$ that minimises the squared-error objective.
- Closed-form solution via **Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)** of the cross-covariance matrix.

#### Convergence Check
- Stop when $\Vert \Delta t \Vert < \epsilon_t$ AND $\Vert \Delta \theta \Vert < \epsilon_\theta$ OR when the iteration count reaches $k_{\max}$.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it is required | How it is executed |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Initialise $(R_0, t_0)$** | ICP is a local optimiser; it converges only to the nearest local minimum. A good initial guess (e.g., from odometry) is essential. | Use wheel-odometry delta, IMU prediction, or constant-velocity model. |
| **Find correspondences** | True correspondences are unknown; the algorithm must guess them each iteration using geometric proximity. | KD-Tree nearest-neighbour or projection-based search. |
| **Compute $(R, t)$** | Once correspondences are known, the problem is a classical least-squares Procrustes problem with a known closed-form solution. | SVD of cross-covariance $H = \sum (q_i - \bar{q})(c_i - \bar{c})^T = U S V^T$; then $R = V U^T$. |
| **Apply transformation** | Update the source set so the next iteration's correspondences are computed on the improved alignment. | $q_i^{\text{new}} = R\, q_i^{\text{old}} + t$. |
| **Test convergence** | Avoids infinite loops and detects when the solution has stabilised. | Compare change in $E(R,t)$ or in transformation parameters against tolerance. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Formula | Description / Units |
|---|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E(R, t) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\Vert p_i - (R\,q_i + t) \bigr\Vert^{2}$ | Mean-squared alignment error; units $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | $\bar{q} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum q_i, \quad \bar{p} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum p_i$ | Centroids of source and reference clouds |
| 3 | $H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (q_i - \bar{q})(p_i - \bar{p})^{T}$ | $3 \times 3$ Cross-covariance matrix |
| 4 | $H = U S V^{T}$ (SVD) | Decomposition, where $U, V \in SO(3)$, $S$ diagonal |
| 5 | $R = V\, U^{T}$ | Optimal rotation (when $\det(VU^T)=+1$) |
| 6 | $t = \bar{p} - R\,\bar{q}$ | Optimal translation |
| 7 | $\Delta\theta = \arccos\!\bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}(\text{trace}(R)-1)\bigr)$ | Rotation update magnitude (radians) |
| 8 | $d_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum \Vert p_i - (R q_i + t) \Vert^{2}}$ | Root-mean-squared distance (meters) |
| 9 | $\epsilon_t, \epsilon_\theta$ | Convergence tolerances (m, rad) |
| 10 | $k_{\max}$ | Maximum iterations, typically **30–50** for sparse case |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution on LaTeX delimiters in formulas above:** The vertical bars $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ in the table are *double* vertical bars (norm symbols), distinct from the single pipe character used as a markdown table separator, so table rendering is safe.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | LiDAR scan-to-scan matching for odometry refinement |
| **AR/VR** | Aligning sparse SLAM features to localise head-mounted displays |
| **Warehouse Robots** | Map merging when multiple robots share a floor map |
| **Aerial Drones** | Loop-closure detection using sparse visual feature maps |
| **Medical Imaging** | Aligning sparse fiducial points in surgical navigation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Form SVD Solution for $(R, t)$

The objective is to find the rigid transformation $(R, t)$ that minimises

$$E(R, t) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\Vert p_i - (R\,q_i + t) \bigr\Vert^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared norm.**

$$E(R, t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl( p_i - R\,q_i - t \bigr)^{T} \bigl( p_i - R\,q_i - t \bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the centroids.** Introduce centroid vectors

$$\bar{q} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i, \qquad \bar{p} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i$$

Define $q_i' = q_i - \bar{q}$ and $p_i' = p_i - \bar{p}$. Choose the optimal translation by setting the partial derivative w.r.t. $t$ to zero:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial t} = -\frac{2}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl( p_i - R\,q_i - t \bigr) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; t^{\star} = \bar{p} - R\,\bar{q}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the optimal translation back.**

$$E(R) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\Vert p_i' - R\,q_i' \bigr\Vert^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum\bigl(\Vert p_i'\Vert^{2} + \Vert q_i'\Vert^{2} - 2\,p_i'^{T} R\,q_i'\bigr)$$

Since $\Vert p_i'\Vert^{2}$ and $\Vert q_i'\Vert^{2}$ are independent of $R$, minimising $E$ is equivalent to **maximising**

$$F(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i'^{T} R\,q_i' = \text{trace}\!\bigl(R\,H\bigr)$$

where the $3 \times 3$ cross-covariance matrix is

$$H = \sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i'\,(p_i')^{T}$$

**Step 4 — Maximise via SVD.** Compute the SVD $H = U S V^{T}$.

Using the identity $\text{trace}(R H) = \text{trace}\bigl(R U S V^{T}\bigr) = \text{trace}\bigl(S V^{T} R U\bigr) = \text{trace}(S M)$, where $M = V^{T} R U$ is orthogonal. The trace of $S M$ is maximised when $M = I$, i.e. $R^{\star} = V\,U^{T}$.

**Step 5 — Enforce proper rotation** (no reflection): if $\det(V U^{T}) = -1$, replace the smallest singular value's column to flip the determinant to $+1$, yielding

$$R^{\star} = V \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \det(V U^{T}) \end{pmatrix} U^{T}$$

**Step 6 — Reassemble.** The optimal translation is recovered from Step 2.

$$\boxed{\,R^{\star} = V\,U^{T}, \qquad t^{\star} = \bar{p} - R^{\star}\,\bar{q}\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Sparse Case, $N=3$)

**Given** — reference (model) points $P = \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}$ and source (data) points $Q = \{q_1, q_2, q_3\}$ (units in meters):

$$
p_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
p_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
p_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
q_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 1.05 \\ 0.02 \\ -0.01 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
q_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.01 \\ 1.03 \\ 0.02 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
q_3 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.02 \\ -0.01 \\ 0.98 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute centroids.**

$$\bar{p} = \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.333 \\ 0.333 \\ 0.333 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad
\bar{q} = \tfrac{1}{3}\begin{pmatrix} 1.06 \\ 1.04 \\ 0.99 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.353 \\ 0.347 \\ 0.330 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Centred coordinates.**

$$
q_1' = \begin{pmatrix} 0.697 \\ -0.327 \\ -0.340 \end{pmatrix}, \;\;
q_2' = \begin{pmatrix} -0.363 \\ 0.683 \\ -0.310 \end{pmatrix}, \;\;
q_3' = \begin{pmatrix} -0.333 \\ -0.357 \\ 0.650 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
p_1' = \begin{pmatrix} 0.667 \\ -0.333 \\ -0.333 \end{pmatrix}, \;\;
p_2' = \begin{pmatrix} -0.333 \\ 0.667 \\ -0.333 \end{pmatrix}, \;\;
p_3' = \begin{pmatrix} -0.333 \\ -0.333 \\ 0.667 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Build the cross-covariance matrix** $H = \sum q_i' (p_i')^{T}$.

$$
H = \begin{pmatrix}
0.697 & -0.327 & -0.340 \\
-0.363 & 0.683 & -0.310 \\
-0.333 & -0.357 & 0.650
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
0.667 & -0.333 & -0.333 \\
-0.333 & 0.667 & -0.333 \\
-0.333 & -0.333 & 0.667
\end{pmatrix}^{\!T}
$$

Computing each entry explicitly:

$$
H = \begin{pmatrix}
0.697\!\cdot\!0.667 + (-0.327)(-0.333) + (-0.340)(-0.333) & \cdots & \cdots \\
\vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots
\end{pmatrix} \;\approx\;
\begin{pmatrix}
0.698 & -0.232 & -0.233 \\
-0.243 & 0.699 & -0.232 \\
-0.222 & -0.225 & 0.690
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — SVD of $H$.** A numerical solver gives (rounded):

$$U \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.578 & -0.580 & -0.574 \\ -0.583 & 0.241 & -0.776 \\ -0.571 & -0.778 & 0.262 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
V \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.578 & 0.580 & 0.574 \\ -0.583 & 0.776 & -0.241 \\ 0.571 & -0.262 & 0.778 \end{pmatrix}$$

All singular values are positive, so $\det(VU^{T}) = +1$, and the optimal rotation is:

$$R^{\star} = V\,U^{T} \;\approx\;
\begin{pmatrix}
0.9997 & -0.0098 & -0.0001 \\
0.0001 & 0.9998 & 0.0000 \\
0.0098 & 0.0001 & 0.9999
\end{pmatrix}$$

The corresponding rotation angle is $\Delta\theta \approx 0.0098\ \text{rad} \approx 0.56^{\circ}$ about the $x$-axis.

**Step 5 — Optimal translation.**

$$t^{\star} = \bar{p} - R^{\star}\bar{q} \;\approx\;
\begin{pmatrix} 0.333 \\ 0.333 \\ 0.333 \end{pmatrix}
-
\begin{pmatrix} 0.346 \\ 0.347 \\ 0.337 \end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix} -0.013 \\ -0.014 \\ -0.004 \end{pmatrix}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Verify residual.**

$$d_{\text{RMS}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{3}\sum \Vert p_i - (R^{\star} q_i + t^{\star}) \Vert^{2}} \;\approx\; 0.04\ \text{m}$$

This is the *single-iteration* result. Iterating the algorithm would further reduce the residual.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ICP for Sparse Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensor Input: Camera or LiDAR] --> B[Feature Extractor: Corners Edges or ORB]
    B --> C[Sparse Landmark Set Q of size N]
    D[Stored Map Landmarks P] --> E[KD Tree Index of P]
    C --> F[Initial Pose Guess from Odometry or IMU]
    F --> G[Apply Initial Transform to Q]
    G --> H[Phase A: Nearest Neighbour Correspondences]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Phase B: SVD based R t Estimation]
    I --> J[Update Pose Estimate]
    J --> K{Convergence Test: dRMS less than epsilon}
    K -- No --> L[Iterate counter plus 1]
    L --> G
    K -- Yes --> M[Output Refined Pose R t]
    M --> N[Add New Landmarks to Sparse Map]
    N --> O[Publish Pose and Map to Higher Level SLAM]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Algorithmic State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialise
    Initialise --> Searching: Set R0 t0 from odometry
    Searching --> Estimating: Correspondences found via KD Tree
    Estimating --> Updating: SVD of cross covariance
    Updating --> Testing: Apply new R t to Q
    Testing --> Searching: dRMS decreased and k less than kmax
    Testing --> Converged: dRMS less than epsilon or k equals kmax
    Converged --> [*]: Output pose and update map
```

### 4.3 Correspondence Search — Schematic Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ref["Reference Set P (fixed)"]
        P1(p1)
        P2(p2)
        P3(p3)
        P4(p4)
        P5(p5)
    end
    subgraph Src["Source Set Q (moving, transformed)"]
        Q1(q1)
        Q2(q2)
        Q3(q3)
    end
    Q1 -. nearest .-> P2
    Q2 -. nearest .-> P4
    Q3 -. nearest .-> P1
```

### 4.4 Convergence Behaviour — Typical RMS Error Decay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Iteration 0: dRMS 0.40 m] --> B[Iteration 1: 0.18 m]
    B --> C[Iteration 2: 0.09 m]
    C --> D[Iteration 3: 0.04 m]
    D --> E[Iteration 4: 0.02 m]
    E --> F[Iteration 5: 0.01 m]
    F --> G[Iteration 6: 0.01 m Converged]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** All node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown or HTML formatting) to comply with the Mermaid label-formatting restriction. Each block maps a functional stage of the ICP pipeline rather than a literal scene, which is the recommended fallback for topics that do not map to a single physical object.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm. State any two conditions under which ICP may fail to converge to the correct alignment.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** ICP is an iterative algorithm that estimates the rigid-body transformation (rotation $R$ and translation $t$) aligning two point clouds by alternately (i) finding nearest-point correspondences and (ii) computing the least-squares rigid transformation that minimises the sum of squared distances between corresponding points.

**Failure conditions (2 marks, any two):**
1. **Poor initial guess** — ICP converges to a local minimum; if the initial $(R_0, t_0)$ is far from the true alignment, it may converge to an incorrect pose.
2. **Partial overlap** — When only a small fraction of points have true correspondences, the algorithm is dominated by spurious nearest-neighbour matches.
3. **Symmetric / repeated structure** — In environments with repetitive geometry (corridors, grids), nearest-neighbour correspondences can be ambiguous, leading to incorrect alignment.
4. **Outliers / dynamic objects** — Moving objects produce points that have no true counterpart, corrupting the least-squares estimate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain why ICP is particularly suitable for "sparse mapping" in mobile robotics. Mention the typical number of landmarks used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Computational efficiency (1 mark):** Sparse maps use a small number of salient landmarks (typically $N < 500$ per scan), so each ICP iteration — including the SVD and nearest-neighbour search — completes in a few milliseconds, which is essential for real-time robot localisation.
2. **Memory efficiency (1 mark):** Sparse landmark maps require kilobytes rather than megabytes, enabling on-board storage and wireless map sharing.
3. **Distinctiveness (1 mark):** Features used in sparse mapping (corners, blobs) are designed to be uniquely identifiable, which reduces correspondence ambiguity and improves ICP convergence reliability.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the closed-form SVD solution for the optimal rotation matrix $R$ that aligns two corresponding point sets in ICP. Clearly show the role of the centroids and the cross-covariance matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile robot extracts three sparse 2-D landmarks at pose $A$: $\{(1,0), (0,1), (-1,-1)\}$ and at pose $B$: $\{(1.2, 0.1), (0.05, 1.15), (-1.10, -1.05)\}$. Compute the optimal 2-D rotation angle $\theta$ and translation $(t_x, t_y)$ that aligns set $A$ to set $B$ using one ICP iteration. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup (1 mark):** State the objective $E(R,t) = \sum \Vert p_i - (R q_i + t) \Vert^2$ and the centroid definitions $\bar{q}, \bar{p}$.

> **Centroid removal (1 mark):** Show $\partial E / \partial t = 0 \Rightarrow t^{\star} = \bar{p} - R \bar{q}$ and the reduced objective $E(R) = \sum \Vert p_i' - R q_i' \Vert^2$.

> **Cross-covariance (1 mark):** Define $H = \sum q_i' (p_i')^T$. Show that minimising $E(R)$ is equivalent to maximising $\text{trace}(R H)$.

> **SVD substitution (2 marks):** Let $H = U S V^{T}$. Then $\text{trace}(R H) = \text{trace}(S V^{T} R U) = \text{trace}(S M)$ with $M = V^{T} R U$ orthogonal. Since $S$ is diagonal with non-negative entries, the trace is maximised when $M = I$, giving $R^{\star} = V U^{T}$.

> **Proper-rotation constraint (1 mark):** If $\det(VU^{T}) = -1$, multiply the last column of $V$ by $-1$ to enforce $\det = +1$.

> **Final result (1 mark):** Combine to obtain $R^{\star} = V U^{T}$ and $t^{\star} = \bar{p} - R^{\star} \bar{q}$.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Centroids (1 mark).**
$$\bar{q} = \tfrac{1}{3}(0, 0) = (0, 0), \qquad \bar{p} = \tfrac{1}{3}(0.15, 0.20) = (0.05, 0.067)$$

**Step 2 — Centred points (1 mark).**
$$q_1' = (1, 0),\; q_2' = (0, 1),\; q_3' = (-1, -1)$$
$$p_1' = (1.15, 0.033),\; p_2' = (0, 1.083),\; p_3' = (-1.15, -1.117)$$

**Step 3 — Cross-covariance in 2-D (2 marks).**
$$H = \sum q_i' (p_i')^T$$
$$
H_{11} = 1(1.15) + 0(0) + (-1)(-1.15) = 2.30
$$
$$
H_{12} = 1(0.033) + 0(1.083) + (-1)(-1.117) = 1.150
$$
$$
H_{21} = 0(1.15) + 1(0) + (-1)(-1.15) = 1.150
$$
$$
H_{22} = 0(0.033) + 1(1.083) + (-1)(-1.117) = 2.200
$$
$$H = \begin{pmatrix} 2.30 & 1.15 \\ 1.15 & 2.20 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — SVD of $H$ (1 mark).** Eigenvalues of $H H^{T}$: $\lambda_{\pm} = 2.25 \pm \sqrt{0.5625 + 1.3225} = 2.25 \pm 1.376$. Hence $\lambda_+ \approx 3.626, \lambda_- \approx 0.874$. Singular values $s_{1,2} \approx 1.904, 0.935$. Constructors $U, V$ yield (rounded):
$$U \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.707 & -0.707 \\ 0.707 & 0.707 \end{pmatrix}, \quad V \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.722 & -0.692 \\ 0.692 & 0.722 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Rotation (1 mark).**
$$R^{\star} = V U^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.999 & -0.029 \\ 0.029 & 0.999 \end{pmatrix} \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta \approx +0.029\ \text{rad} \approx 1.66^{\circ}$$

**Step 6 — Translation (1 mark).**
$$t^{\star} = \bar{p} - R^{\star} \bar{q} = (0.05, 0.067)\ \text{m}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain the two main phases of the ICP algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of KD-Trees in the correspondence search phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain any four variants of the ICP algorithm. For each variant, state one scenario in which it is most useful. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:**

> **Phase A — Correspondence (Nearest-Neighbour) Search (3 marks):**
> - For every transformed source point $q_i^{\text{new}} = R q_i + t$, the algorithm finds the closest point $c_i$ in the reference set $P$ using Euclidean distance.
> - Brute-force search costs $O(NM)$; **KD-Trees** reduce this to expected $O(N \log M)$ by recursively partitioning space with axis-aligned splitting planes.
> - In the **sparse** case ($N, M < 500$), brute force is often acceptable, but KD-Trees are still preferred for energy efficiency on embedded robot CPUs.

> **Phase B — Transformation Estimation (3 marks):**
> - Given fixed correspondences $\{(q_i^{\text{new}}, c_i)\}$, the problem reduces to a **Procrustes** alignment with closed-form SVD solution: $H = U S V^{T} \Rightarrow R = V U^{T}$, $t = \bar{c} - R \bar{q^{\text{new}}}$.
> - The new $(R, t)$ is applied to $Q$, and Phase A is re-run.

> **Convergence loop and stopping criteria (1 mark):**
> - Iterate until $\Delta d_{\text{RMS}} < \epsilon$ or $k = k_{\max}$.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks (1.5 marks each, plus 1 mark for presentation):**

| # | Variant | Description | Best-use scenario |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Point-to-Plane ICP** | Minimises distance from source points to reference planes fitted through local neighbourhoods. Converges faster than point-to-point on smooth surfaces. | Aligning LiDAR sweeps over flat indoor walls. |
