| 2 | **Generalised ICP (G-ICP)** | Treats each point as a Gaussian, combining point-to-point and point-to-plane metrics. | Robust registration under sensor noise. |
| 3 | **Trimmed ICP** | Uses only the closest $K\%$ of correspondences, ignoring the worst matches. | Environments with significant partial overlap. |
| 4 | **Sparse ICP (Bouaziz et al., 2013)** | Uses $\ell_p$-norms ($p<2$) for robustness; explicitly designed for sparse feature sets. | Feature-based SLAM with few landmarks. |
| 5 | **NICP / Colored ICP** | Incorporates colour or intensity to disambiguate correspondences. | RGB-D scanning of texture-poor scenes. |
| 6 | **Go-ICP** | Uses a branch-and-bound global search over $SE(3)$ to escape local minima. | Cases where the initial guess is unknown. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ICP with EKF-SLAM:** ICP is a *registration* algorithm; EKF-SLAM is a *probabilistic filter*. They are often combined, but the distinction is a frequent 1–2 mark loss in Part A.
> 2. **Forgetting the reflection correction:** Writing $R = V U^{T}$ without checking $\det = +1$ costs one mark in Part B derivations.
> 3. **Wrong sign in translation:** Remember $t = \bar{p} - R \bar{q}$ (model minus transformed data), not the reverse. A common slip-up under exam pressure.
> 4. **Confusing "sparse" with "small":** A sparse map is *intentionally* sampled from salient features, not merely a small dense cloud. Examiners expect this distinction in 3-mark definitions.
> 5. **Skipping units in the formula sheet:** When asked to write the RMS error or tolerance, always state units (m, m$^2$, rad). Missing units cost half a mark each.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **ICP = Iterative Closest Point** — alternates between *correspondence* and *registration* until convergence.
- [x] **Objective:** minimise $E(R, t) = \tfrac{1}{N}\sum \Vert p_i - (R q_i + t) \Vert^{2}$.
- [x] **Two phases per iteration:** (A) nearest-neighbour data association; (B) SVD-based Procrustes solve.
- [x] **Closed-form solution:** $H = \sum (q_i - \bar{q})(p_i - \bar{p})^{T} = U S V^{T}$, then $R^{\star} = V U^{T}$, $t^{\star} = \bar{p} - R^{\star} \bar{q}$.
- [x] **Proper-rotation guard:** if $\det(VU^{T}) = -1$, flip the last column of $V$ to enforce $\det = +1$ (avoids mirror reflection).
- [x] **Sparse mapping** uses typically $N < 500$ salient landmarks (corners, blobs, ORB/SIFT features) — not raw scan data.
- [x] **Initialisation matters:** ICP is a *local* optimiser; a bad initial guess from odometry leads to local-minimum divergence.
- [x] **KD-Tree** accelerates nearest-neighbour search from $O(NM)$ to $O(N \log M)$.
- [x] **Convergence tests:** $\Delta d_{\text{RMS}} < \epsilon$ OR $k = k_{\max}$ (typical $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$, $k_{\max} = 30$–$50$).
- [x] **Failure modes:** poor initial guess, partial overlap, repetitive geometry, dynamic objects/outliers.
- [x] **Variants to remember:** Point-to-Plane, Generalised-ICP, Trimmed-ICP, Sparse-ICP, Go-ICP.
- [x] **Engineering utility:** SLAM loop-closure, scan matching, map merging, AR/VR tracking, drone pose refinement.
- [x] **Units to retain:** distances in **m**, squared errors in **m$^2$**, angles in **rad** (convert to degrees only if asked).
- [x] **Examiner cue phrases** that signal ICP questions: "align two point clouds", "estimate relative pose from landmarks", "register two scans", "data association and rigid-body fit".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Octomap: Dense Mapping of Voxels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Octomap: Dense Mapping of Voxels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OctoMap (Octree-based Mapping)** is an open-source, probabilistic, flexible, and compact 3D mapping framework that explicitly represents the environment as a hierarchical **volumetric grid** using an **octree data structure**, where each volumetric element (voxel) stores the probability of being occupied, free, or unknown, updated recursively as new sensor measurements are integrated.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Basics of Mobile Robotics*, the **Octomap** paradigm is positioned as the canonical solution to the *dense 3D perception problem*. The architecture is built on three foundational pillars:

1. **Probabilistic Occupancy Estimation** – Uses Bayesian filtering to fuse noisy sensor data.
2. **Hierarchical Octree Representation** – Exploits spatial sparsity via recursive subdivision of 3D space.
3. **Efficient Memory Management** – Implements *log-odds clamping* and *tree pruning* for bounded memory usage.

The standard minimum voxel resolution is **r = 0.05 m to 0.10 m** for indoor mobile robots, with a default map size scaling from **1 m³ to thousands of m³**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Library of Bricks**

Imagine you are building a giant 3D puzzle of a room using **LEGO bricks**. Instead of placing a brick at *every single millimetre* (which would be impossibly slow and wasteful), you start with **one large brick the size of the entire room**.

- If a sensor (say, a flashlight) reveals something small inside a large brick, you **crack that big brick open** into 8 smaller bricks (an *octant* subdivision).
- You keep splitting only the regions that contain interesting information (walls, furniture, obstacles).
- Empty, open air (sky) is left as one large brick, saving enormous memory.

This is **exactly** what OctoMap does:

| LEGO Brick Concept | Octomap Equivalent |
|---|---|
| One large room-sized brick | Root node of the octree |
| Cracking into 8 pieces | Octant subdivision (depth +1) |
| A small final piece at a wall | Leaf voxel at minimum resolution |
| Ignoring empty space | Pruning of uniform nodes |

The "occupancy" of each brick is a **belief** (a probability) — not a hard yes/no. If a LiDAR beam passes *through* a brick, the brick becomes "probably free" (probability decreases). If a beam *stops inside* a brick, that brick becomes "probably occupied" (probability increases). The bricks themselves are called **voxels** (volumetric pixels).

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU 2024 Vocabulary)

- **Voxel**: A 3D volumetric pixel — the smallest addressable unit of 3D space in the map.
- **Octree**: A tree data structure in which each internal node has exactly **eight children**, recursively partitioning 3D space.
- **Occupancy Probability $P(n \mid z_{1:t})$**: The posterior probability that voxel $n$ is occupied, given all sensor measurements $z_{1:t}$ from time 1 to $t$.
- **Log-Odds $L(n)$**: A continuous, additive representation of occupancy belief defined as $L = \log \frac{P}{1-P}$.
- **Inverse Sensor Model**: The function that converts a single range measurement $z_t$ into a local occupancy update.
- **Clamping**: Forcing log-odds to remain within bounded limits $[l_{min}, l_{max}]$ to prevent numerical divergence.
- **Pruning**: Merging eight child nodes back into the parent when all children share identical occupancy states, compressing the tree.

---

## 1.4 Historical & Engineering Context

The OctoMap framework was introduced by **Hornung, Bennewitz, Dang, Kretzschmar, and Burgard (2013)** in the IEEE Transactions on Robotics. It became the de facto standard for 3D mapping in the **Robot Operating System (ROS)** ecosystem, used in applications ranging from autonomous drones (micro aerial vehicles) to autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) and humanoid robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must understand the *mapping pipeline*: Sensor Input → Coordinate Transform → Ray-casting (Free-space update) → End-point update → Tree Update → Compression (Pruning).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Octree Data Structure — Recursive Subdivision

An octree is constructed by recursively subdividing 3D space into **eight equal octants**. Each octant is represented as a node in a tree:

- **Root Node (Depth 0):** Represents the entire bounding cube of the map.
- **Depth $d$:** A node at depth $d$ represents a cube of side length $\frac{s}{2^d}$, where $s$ is the side length of the root cube.
- **Leaves:** Nodes that have no children — these store the *actual occupancy belief*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **maximum depth** $d_{max}$ of the octree determines the **minimum voxel resolution** $\Delta = \frac{s}{2^{d_{max}}}$. A typical KTU-relevant setup uses $d_{max} = 16$, giving $\Delta \approx 0.015$ m for a 1 km³ root cube.

### 2.1.1 Why Octrees? — Complexity Analysis

For a uniformly dense grid of resolution $\Delta$ covering volume $V$:

- **Memory required (3D grid):** $O\!\left(\frac{V}{\Delta^3}\right)$ cells.
- **Memory required (Octree):** $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of *non-empty* regions, often $O(V/\Delta^2)$ or less for sparse environments.

This is the **curse of dimensionality** in action — octrees break it.

---

## 2.2 Probabilistic Occupancy Estimation

The OctoMap estimates the posterior probability that a voxel $n$ is occupied, given all measurements up to time $t$:

$$P(n \mid z_{1:t}) = \text{posterior occupancy belief of voxel } n$$

To make this updateable in **constant time per voxel**, the framework uses the **log-odds** representation:

$$L(n) = \log \left( \frac{P(n \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - P(n \mid z_{1:t})} \right)$$

The key advantage: Bayesian updates become **simple additions** in log-odds space.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Octree Volume at Depth $d$ | $V_d = \dfrac{s^3}{8^d}$ | $s$ = root cube side length |
| 2 | Voxel Side Length at Depth $d$ | $\ell_d = \dfrac{s}{2^d}$ | Minimum at $d = d_{max}$ |
| 3 | Occupancy Probability (from log-odds) | $P = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-L}}$ | Inverse of logit function |
| 4 | Log-Odds (from probability) | $L = \log \dfrac{P}{1-P}$ | $L \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 5 | Recursive Bayesian Update | $L(n \mid z_{1:t}) = L(n \mid z_{1:t-1}) + L(n \mid z_t)$ | Additive in log-odds |
| 6 | Log-Odds Clamping (Update) | $L_{update} = \max(l_{min}, \min(l_{max}, L_{prev} + L_{sensor}))$ | Prevents divergence |
| 7 | Clamp Lower Bound | $l_{min} = \log \dfrac{p_{min}}{1 - p_{min}}$ | $p_{min} = 0.12$ (default) |
| 8 | Clamp Upper Bound | $l_{max} = \log \dfrac{p_{max}}{1 - p_{max}}$ | $p_{max} = 0.97$ (default) |
| 9 | Inverse Sensor Model (Free) | $L_{free} = l_{min}$ (typically) | Ray passes through voxel |
| 10 | Inverse Sensor Model (Hit) | $L_{hit} = l_{max}$ (typically) | Ray terminates in voxel |
| 11 | Pruning Condition | All 8 children share same state | Merges children → parent |
| 12 | Memory per Voxel | $1 \text{ byte}$ (quantized) or $4 \text{ byte}$ (float) | $0.05$ m → ~$10^6$ voxels/m³ |

---

## 2.4 The Mapping Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Sensor Input Reception:** A range measurement $z_t = (r, \theta, \phi)$ arrives from LiDAR, depth camera, or stereo sensor.
2. **Coordinate Transformation:** The endpoint $\mathbf{x}_{end}$ is computed in the world frame using the robot pose $\mathbf{x}_R$:  
   $\mathbf{x}_{end} = \mathbf{x}_R \oplus z_t$
3. **Ray-Casting (Free Space Update):** All voxels along the ray from sensor origin to $z_t$ are marked as *probably free* by adding $L_{free}$ to their log-odds.
4. **Endpoint Update (Hit):** The voxel containing $\mathbf{x}_{end}$ receives $L_{hit}$.
5. **Clamping:** Each updated voxel is clamped to $[l_{min}, l_{max}]$.
6. **Tree Pruning:** If a parent and all 8 children have identical clamped values, the children are deleted — the parent becomes a leaf. This is the **memory compression step**.
7. **Resolution Query:** The map can be queried at *any* desired resolution $\Delta'$ (coarser queries return aggregated children).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Octomap Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Autonomous Drones (UAVs)** | Collision-free path planning in cluttered indoor environments |
| **Search & Rescue Robots** | 3D victim/obstacle localization in collapsed buildings |
| **Autonomous Driving** | 3D obstacle detection (static + dynamic via tracked cells) |
| **Underwater AUVs** | Seabed mapping with sonar (acoustic ray-casting) |
| **AR/VR** | Real-world occlusion masks for virtual object placement |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | Whole-body navigation through volumetric occupancy grids |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why log-odds?** Multiplication of probabilities (Bayes' rule) becomes addition, and the bounded clamping $l_{min}, l_{max}$ prevents a single misbehaving sensor from permanently corrupting a voxel — a key robustness property demanded in **real-time KTU lab evaluations**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Bayes' Rule to the Log-Odds Update

We start with the recursive Bayesian filter for occupancy of voxel $n$:

$$P(n \mid z_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid n, z_{1:t-1}) \cdot P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

### Step 1: Make the Markov assumption

The measurement $z_t$ depends only on the current state, so $P(z_t \mid n, z_{1:t-1}) = P(z_t \mid n)$.

$$P(n \mid z_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid n) \cdot P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

### Step 2: Apply Bayes' rule to the denominator

$$P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1}) = P(z_t \mid n) \cdot P(n \mid z_{1:t-1}) + P(z_t \mid \overline{n}) \cdot P(\overline{n} \mid z_{1:t-1})$$

This gives the standard form but is **multiplicative** — bad for real-time updates.

### Step 3: Convert to log-odds using $L = \log \frac{P}{1-P}$

Define $L(n \mid z_{1:t}) = \log \frac{P(n \mid z_{1:t})}{1 - P(n \mid z_{1:t})}$.

Substitute and simplify using the algebraic identity $\log \frac{a}{b} = \log a - \log b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(n \mid z_{1:t}) &= \log \left[ \frac{P(z_t \mid n) \cdot P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(n \mid z_{1:t-1} \text{ updated})} \right] \\
&= \log \frac{P(z_t \mid n)}{1 - P(z_t \mid n)} + \log \frac{P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Recognize the additive form

$$
L(n \mid z_{1:t}) = L(n \mid z_{1:t-1}) + L(n \mid z_t)
$$

This is the **fundamental Octomap update equation** — purely additive. The first term is the *prior belief*, the second is the *inverse sensor model contribution*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Clamp Bound Calculation

Given default OctoMap parameters $p_{min} = 0.12$ and $p_{max} = 0.97$, derive the log-odds bounds.

### Step 1: Lower bound

$$
l_{min} = \log \left( \frac{p_{min}}{1 - p_{min}} \right) = \log \left( \frac{0.12}{0.88} \right)
$$

Numerically: $\frac{0.12}{0.88} = 0.1364$, and $\log(0.1364) = -1.992$.

### Step 2: Upper bound

$$
l_{max} = \log \left( \frac{p_{max}}{1 - p_{max}} \right) = \log \left( \frac{0.97}{0.03} \right)
$$

Numerically: $\frac{0.97}{0.03} = 32.333$, and $\log(32.333) = 3.476$.

### Step 3: Final clamp interval

$$
L \in [-1.99,\ +3.48]
$$

Any update that would push $L$ outside this interval is truncated. This guarantees a single rogue sensor reading cannot permanently mark a voxel as occupied (or free).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Memory Footprint for a Sparse Octree

Consider an indoor environment of $V = 10 \text{ m} \times 10 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m} = 300 \text{ m}^3$ mapped at $\Delta = 0.05$ m.

### Step 1: Total possible voxels (dense grid)

$$
N_{dense} = \frac{V}{\Delta^3} = \frac{300}{(0.05)^3} = \frac{300}{0.000125} = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ voxels}
$$

### Step 2: Memory at 1 byte/voxel

$$
M_{dense} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.4 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 3: Octree compression in a sparse environment

If only **5% of voxels are non-uniform** (i.e., near surfaces), the octree stores:

- **1 root node** + a few hundred large uniform nodes (free space, sky).
- For each surface voxel, an internal node path of length $d_{max} = \log_2(10/0.05) = \log_2(200) \approx 7.6 \to 8$ levels.

Approximate node count:

$$
N_{octree} \approx \underbrace{10^3}_{\text{uniform}} + \underbrace{0.05 \times 2.4 \times 10^6 \times \frac{1}{8}}_{\text{surface paths}} \approx 10^3 + 1.5 \times 10^4
$$

### Step 4: Realized memory

$$
M_{octree} \approx 1.6 \times 10^4 \text{ nodes} \times 1 \text{ byte} \approx 16 \text{ KB}
$$

**Compression ratio:** $\frac{M_{dense}}{M_{octree}} \approx \frac{2.4 \text{ MB}}{16 \text{ KB}} \approx 150\times$

This is why **OctoMap is preferred over dense voxel grids** for mobile robots with embedded hardware.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Dense 3D Grid | OctoMap (Octree) | KTU Exam Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | $O(V/\Delta^3)$ — cubic in resolution | $O(\text{surface area}/\Delta^2)$ | Octree wins for sparse env |
| **Update Time** | $O(1)$ per voxel | $O(d_{max})$ per voxel | Both fast; octree has tree traversal |
| **Query Resolution** | Fixed at $\Delta$ | Multi-resolution (any $\Delta' \geq \Delta$) | Octree wins — planning queries |
| **Probability Storage** | Per-voxel float | Per-voxel log-odds (clamped) | Both probabilistic |
| **Dynamic Object Handling** | Static only (typically) | Can mark *unknown* vs *dynamic* | Both need extension |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Moderate (recursive tree) | Octree harder to code |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Ray-Casting Length Bound

The maximum number of voxels traversed by a single ray of length $r$ through an octree is bounded by the *DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) traversal* algorithm:

$$
N_{ray} \leq \frac{r}{\Delta} + O(d_{max})
$$

For $r = 30$ m and $\Delta = 0.05$ m:

$$
N_{ray} \leq \frac{30}{0.05} + 8 = 600 + 8 = 608 \text{ voxels}
$$

This bound is **critical for KTU 14-mark questions** on real-time performance, as it determines the upper-bound computational cost per sensor update on an embedded platform like the NVIDIA Jetson.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Octree Subdivision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Root Node Volume 8m Cubed"]
    subdivA1["Octant 1 Free"]
    subdivA2["Octant 2 Free"]
    subdivA3["Octant 3 MIXED Subdivide"]
    subdivA4["Octant 4 Free"]
    subdivA5["Octant 5 MIXED Subdivide"]
    subdivA6["Octant 6 Occupied Leaf"]
    subdivA7["Octant 7 Free"]
    subdivA8["Octant 8 Free"]

    subdivA3 --> subA3a["Sub Octant 1 Occupied"]
    subdivA3 --> subA3b["Sub Octant 2 Free"]
    subdivA3 --> subA3c["Sub Octant 3 Free"]
    subdivA3 --> subA3d["Sub Octant 4 Free"]
    subdivA3 --> subA3e["Sub Octant 5 Free"]
    subdivA3 --> subA3f["Sub Octant 6 Free"]
    subdivA3 --> subA3g["Sub Octant 7 Free"]
    subdivA3 --> subA3h["Sub Octant 8 Free"]

    subdivA5 --> subA5a["Sub Octant 1 Free"]
    subdivA5 --> subA5b["Sub Octant 2 MIXED"]
    subdivA5 --> subA5c["Sub Octant 3 Free"]
    subdivA5 --> subA5d["Sub Octant 4 Free"]
    subdivA5 --> subA5e["Sub Octant 5 Free"]
    subdivA5 --> subA5f["Sub Octant 6 Free"]
    subdivA5 --> subA5g["Sub Octant 7 Free"]
    subdivA5 --> subA5h["Sub Octant 8 Free"]

    subA5b --> subA5b1["Sub Sub Octant 1 Free"]
    subA5b2["Sub Sub Octant 2 Occupied Leaf"]

    rootA --> subdivA1
    rootA --> subdivA2
    rootA --> subdivA3
    rootA --> subdivA4
    rootA --> subdivA5
    rootA --> subdivA6
    rootA --> subdivA7
    rootA --> subdivA8

    subdivA5 --> subA5b2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The root (depth 0) is an **8 m × 8 m × 8 m** cube. Octants 3 and 5 contain partial surfaces, so they are recursively subdivided. All other octants are uniform (either fully free or fully occupied) and remain as leaves — saving memory.

---

## 4.2 Octomap Update Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA["Sensor Source LiDAR Depth Cam Stereo"]
    poseA["Robot Pose Estimator Odometry SLAM"]
    coordA["Coordinate Transformer World Frame"]
    rayA["Ray Caster Module DDA Traversal"]
    freeA["Free Space Voxel Updater Add L free"]
    hitA["Hit Endpoint Updater Add L hit"]
    clampA["Log Odds Clamper l min to l max"]
    pruneA["Tree Pruner Compress Uniform Nodes"]
    treeA["Octree Database In Memory"]
    queryA["Multi Resolution Query Interface"]

    sensorA --> coordA
    poseA --> coordA
    coordA --> rayA
    rayA --> freeA
    rayA --> hitA
    freeA --> clampA
    hitA --> clampA
    clampA --> pruneA
    pruneA --> treeA
    treeA --> queryA
    treeA --> pruneA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-time loop:** On a mobile robot, this pipeline runs at the sensor rate (typically **10–30 Hz** for LiDAR). The feedback loop from tree back to pruning is critical — without it, the tree would grow unboundedly as the robot moves.

---

## 4.3 Log-Odds State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unknown
    Unknown --> Free : Ray passes through
    Unknown --> Occupied : Ray terminates
    Free --> Unknown : Reduce belief temporarily
    Free --> Occupied : Strong hit in voxel
    Occupied --> Free : Many clear traversals
    Occupied --> ClampedMax : L exceeds l max
    Free --> ClampedMin : L below l min
    ClampedMax --> Occupied
    ClampedMin --> Free
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The clamping states ($L_{clamped}$) are what give OctoMap its **forgetting/robustness** property — old erroneous sensor hits eventually decay, but never fully disappear unless the clamping bounds are reset.

---

## 4.4 Mapping Session Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA["Initialize Root Node Volume s Cubed"]
    measA["Receive Measurement z t"]
    updA["Update Voxels in Ray"]
    clampB["Apply Clamping"]
    pruneB["Prune Uniform Branches"]
    fullA["Map Full Check Memory Limit"]
    contA["Continue Mapping"]
    saveA["Serialize Map to File Octomap BT Format"]
    doneA["End Mapping Session"]

    initA --> measA
    measA --> updA
    updA --> clampB
    clampB --> pruneB
    pruneB --> fullA
    fullA --> contA
    fullA --> saveA
    contA --> measA
    saveA --> doneA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define OctoMap. What is the role of the octree data structure in 3D mapping?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** OctoMap is an open-source 3D mapping framework that uses an octree-based hierarchical data structure to represent the environment as a set of volumetric voxels, each storing a probabilistic estimate of occupancy.

**Role of Octree (2 marks):** The octree recursively partitions 3D space into eight equal octants, allowing:
- **Multi-resolution representation** — query at any desired voxel size.
- **Memory efficiency** — uniform regions are stored as single large nodes instead of millions of small voxels.
- **Fast updates and queries** — logarithmic time complexity $O(\log V)$ for traversal.

The octree thus overcomes the *curse of dimensionality* of dense 3D grids.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]**
*Why does OctoMap use the log-odds representation for occupancy instead of storing probabilities directly?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Additive updates (1 mark):** The Bayesian update becomes a simple addition $L(n \mid z_{1:t}) = L(n \mid z_{1:t-1}) + L(n \mid z_t)$, avoiding floating-point multiplication and division at every step.

2. **Numerical stability (1 mark):** Probabilities $P$ saturate at 0 and 1; small floating-point errors cause divergence. Log-odds can be clamped to finite bounds $[l_{min}, l_{max}]$, ensuring bounded memory and preventing a single sensor error from permanently corrupting a voxel.

3. **Efficient pruning (1 mark):** After clamping, voxels with identical quantized log-odds values can be merged, compressing the octree dynamically.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the probabilistic occupancy estimation framework used in OctoMap. Derive the log-odds update equation from Bayes' rule. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the octree data structure. How does OctoMap achieve memory compression through pruning? (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution A(a) — Probabilistic Occupancy & Log-Odds Derivation (7 marks)**

**1. Problem Statement (1 mark):**  
Given a sequence of noisy range measurements $z_{1:t}$ from time 1 to $t$, we want to compute the posterior probability that a voxel $n$ is occupied.

$$P(n \mid z_{1:t})$$

**2. Recursive Bayesian Filter (1 mark):**  
By Bayes' rule and the Markov assumption (measurement depends only on current state):

$$P(n \mid z_{1:t}) = \frac{P(z_t \mid n) \cdot P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}{P(z_t \mid z_{1:t-1})}$$

**3. Conversion to Log-Odds (2 marks):**  
Define $L(n) = \log \frac{P(n)}{1 - P(n)}$. Substituting into Bayes' rule and using the identity $\log \frac{a}{b} = \log a - \log b$:

$$
L(n \mid z_{1:t}) = \log \frac{P(z_t \mid n)}{1 - P(z_t \mid n)} + \log \frac{P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}{1 - P(n \mid z_{1:t-1})}
$$

**4. Final Additive Form (1 mark):**

$$
L(n \mid z_{1:t}) = L(n \mid z_{1:t-1}) + L(n \mid z_t)
$$

This is the **OctoMap update equation** — purely additive.

**5. Inverse Sensor Model (1 mark):**  
$L(n \mid z_t)$ is the *inverse sensor model*. For a beam passing through voxel $n$ (free space), it contributes a negative value $L_{free}$. For the endpoint voxel (occupied), it contributes a positive value $L_{hit}$. Default values: $L_{free} = -0.4$, $L_{hit} = +0.85$.

**6. Clamping (1 mark):**  
The accumulated log-odds is clamped to $[l_{min}, l_{max}] = [-1.99, +3.48]$ to ensure boundedness.

---

#### **Solution A(b) — Octree Structure and Pruning (7 marks)**

**1. Octree Definition (1 mark):**  
An octree is a tree data structure where each internal node has **exactly eight children**, recursively partitioning 3D space. The root node represents the entire map bounding cube; each child represents one octant of the parent's volume.

**2. Subdivision Logic (2 marks):**

| Depth $d$ | Cube Side Length | Voxel Count at Depth |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $s$ (root) | 1 |
| 1 | $s/2$ | 8 |
| 2 | $s/4$ | 64 |
| $d$ | $s/2^d$ | $8^d$ |
| $d_{max}$ | $\Delta$ (min) | $8^{d_{max}}$ |

Maximum depth $d_{max} = 16$ gives $\Delta = 0.015$ m for a 1 km³ cube.

**3. Leaf Storage (1 mark):**  
Only leaf nodes store occupancy log-odds values. Internal nodes store aggregated information (e.g., maximum/minimum child occupancy for fast queries).

**4. Pruning Mechanism (2 marks):**  
After each update, the tree is traversed bottom-up. If all 8 children of a node are leaves AND have identical clamped occupancy values, the children are deleted, and the parent becomes a leaf. This compresses uniform regions (e.g., large empty spaces) into single nodes — **the key memory optimization**.

**5. Memory Comparison (1 mark):**  
A 300 m³ room at $\Delta = 0.05$ m requires ~2.4 MB as a dense grid, but only ~16 KB as a pruned octree (compression ratio ~150×).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU Supplementary Exam, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the OctoMap mapping pipeline. Explain the roles of ray-casting, free-space update, and hit update with their corresponding log-odds values. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare OctoMap with a dense 3D voxel grid in terms of memory, update speed, query flexibility, and dynamic environment handling. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution B(a) — Mapping Pipeline (7 marks)**

**1. Pipeline Overview (1 mark):**  
The OctoMap pipeline processes each range measurement through the stages: Sensor Input → Coordinate Transform → Ray-Casting → Free-Space Update → Hit Update → Clamping → Pruning.

**2. Coordinate Transform (1 mark):**  
The sensor endpoint $\mathbf{x}_{end}$ is transformed into the world frame using the robot pose $\mathbf{x}_R$:

$$\mathbf{x}_{end} = \mathbf{x}_R \oplus z_t$$

**3. Ray-Casting (2 marks):**  
A 3D ray is traced from the sensor origin to $\mathbf{x}_{end}$ using the **Amanatides–Woo voxel traversal algorithm** (DDA). Each voxel intersected by the ray (excluding the endpoint) receives a **free-space update**:

$$L_{free} = -0.4 \text{ (default)}$$

This represents the belief that "the ray passed through this voxel without hitting anything."

**4. Hit Update (1 mark):**  
The terminal voxel (containing $\mathbf{x}_{end}$) receives a **hit update**:

$$L_{hit} = +0.85 \text{ (default)}$$

This represents "the ray terminated inside this voxel, so it is likely occupied."

**5. Clamping and Pruning (2 marks):**  
Each updated $L$ is clamped to $[-1.99, +3.48]$. After all updates, the tree is pruned bottom-up. Typical update rate: **10–30 Hz** for a Velodyne LiDAR.

---

#### **Solution B(b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Criterion | Dense 3D Grid (3.5 marks) | OctoMap (3.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | $O(V/\Delta^3)$ — scales as inverse cube of resolution; for 300 m³ at 5 cm → 2.4 MB minimum | $O(\text{surface}/\Delta^2)$ — scales with surface area; for same scene → ~16 KB (150× compression) |
| **Update Speed** | Constant $O(1)$ per ray-crossed voxel; no tree traversal | $O(d_{max})$ per voxel due to tree descent; ~8 levels typical |
| **Query Flexibility** | Single fixed resolution $\Delta$ | Multi-resolution: any $\Delta' \geq \Delta$ by aggregating children |
| **Dynamic Objects** | Requires external tracking — grid only stores final state | Same limitation, but with clamping, transient objects can be *aged out* by sufficient free updates |
| **Implementation** | Simple 3D array | Recursive tree with allocation/deletion |
| **Best Use Case** | Small bounded volumes, fixed resolution, GPU parallelization | Large sparse environments, mobile robots, embedded platforms |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common 2-mark Deductions):**
> 1. **Confusing log-odds sign:** Students often write $L_{free}$ as positive. It is **negative** (free space *decreases* occupancy belief).
> 2. **Forgetting clamping bounds:** Without clamping, the update equation $L_{prev} + L_{sensor}$ can diverge. Always mention the clamp operation.
> 3. **Misidentifying the unit cell:** The octree does *not* use cubic voxels in a flat grid — each voxel at depth $d$ is the cube of side $s/2^d$, defined by the tree hierarchy.
> 4. **Skipping the inverse sensor model:** The KTU marking scheme allocates 1–2 marks specifically for the *numerical* log-odds values $L_{free}, L_{hit}$ and their interpretation.
> 5. **Forgetting the ray-cast cost:** A single 30 m LiDAR beam touches ~600 voxels — this must be in the time-complexity discussion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Octomap: Dense Mapping of Voxels**

- [ ] **Octomap** is a **probabilistic 3D mapping framework** using **octree hierarchical data structures**.
- [ ] **Voxel** = 3D volumetric pixel; smallest addressable unit; minimum resolution $\Delta = s / 2^{d_{max}}$.
- [ ] **Octree** = tree with **8 children per internal node**; root at depth 0, leaves at $d_{max}$.
- [ ] **Occupancy probability** $P(n \mid z_{1:t})$ is the posterior belief that voxel $n$ is occupied.
- [ ] **Log-odds** $L = \log \frac{P}{1-P}$ enables **additive Bayesian updates**: $L_t = L_{t-1} + L_{sensor}$.
- [ ] **Inverse sensor model** — $L_{free} < 0$ (ray passes through), $L_{hit} > 0$ (ray terminates).
- [ ] **Clamping bounds** $l_{min} = -1.99$, $l_{max} = +3.48$ prevent numerical divergence and enable forgetting.
- [ ] **Pruning** merges 8 identical child leaves into a single parent leaf — the key memory optimization.
- [ ] **Multi-resolution queries** are a unique advantage of octrees (e.g., 0.05 m for obstacle avoidance, 0.5 m for global planning).
- [ ] **Memory scaling**: $O(\text{surface}/\Delta^2)$ for octree vs $O(V/\Delta^3)$ for dense grids — **huge savings in sparse environments**.
- [ ] **Ray-casting cost**: A 30 m beam at 0.05 m resolution touches **~600 voxels** (DDA traversal bound).
- [ ] **Default map resolution for mobile robots**: $\Delta = 0.05$ m to $0.10$ m; $d_{max} = 14$–$16$.
- [ ] **ROS integration**: `octomap_server` and `octomap_ros` packages provide ready-to-use map servers.
- [ ] **Output format**: `.bt` (binary tree) and `.ot` (octree text) — both lossless.
- [ ] **Key disadvantage**: Octree updates are not parallelizable on standard CPUs (though GPU variants exist) — relevant for real-time KTU lab tasks.
- [ ] **Pipeline order**: Sensor → Pose → Transform → Ray-cast → Free update → Hit update → Clamp → Prune.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive the OctoMap update equation," always start with Bayes' rule, *state the Markov assumption explicitly*, show the conversion to log-odds *step by step*, and *conclude with the additive form*. This 4-step structure reliably scores full marks on KTU 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RGB-D Mapping: Dense Mapping of Surfaces
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT724 - BASICS OF MOBILE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RGB-D Mapping: Dense Mapping of Surfaces

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RGB-D Mapping** is the process of constructing a **dense three-dimensional (3D) representation** of an environment by fusing synchronized **RGB (color)** and **Depth** measurements acquired from an RGB-D sensor. Unlike sparse feature-based mapping (e.g., visual SLAM using only ORB/SIFT keypoints), dense mapping reconstructs the **full geometry** of visible surfaces at or near sensor resolution, producing a continuous (or voxelized) surface model.

An **RGB-D sensor** is an active or passive imaging device that simultaneously produces:
- A color image $I_{rgb}(u,v) \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ (RGB channels).
- A per-pixel depth image $I_{d}(u,v) \in \mathbb{R}$ (metric distance along the optical axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The dense mapping problem requires sensor fusion across **multiple viewpoints** so that depth measurements from different robot poses are registered into a **common world frame** $\mathbf{W}$, producing a globally consistent 3D surface model.

Standard sensor families used in KTU-referenced texts:
| Sensor | Principle | Range | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Microsoft Kinect v1 | Structured IR Light | 0.8 – 4.0 m | Indoor mapping |
| Kinect v2 / Azure | Time-of-Flight | 0.5 – 4.5 m | Mobile robots |
| Intel RealSense D435 | Active Stereo IR | 0.1 – 10 m | SLAM research |
| PMD Pico Flexx | ToF | 0.1 – 4 m | Embedded robots |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Sculptor Analogy:** Imagine a blindfolded sculptor asked to recreate a clay statue. Each time they touch the statue with their fingertips, they get one *touch point* with a distance reading. After thousands of touches from many angles, they can build a mental 3D model. The RGB-D camera is the *fingertip* — every pixel of the depth image is one *touch* — and dense mapping is the cumulative mental model (or in the robot's case, the explicit 3D point cloud / voxel grid). The "RGB" part is like feeling the *color* of each touch, and "Dense" means touching *every* point, not just a few corners.

**A second analogy — the 3D Photocopier:** Hold a 3D scanner over a coffee mug. Unlike a normal camera (which only captures the silhouette in 2D), the RGB-D scanner captures, for every pixel, *how far that point is*. Sweep the scanner around, and the software stitches these depth slices together to produce a 3D model dense enough to print.

## 1.3 Why "Dense" Mapping? — Distinguishing from Sparse Methods

| Property | Sparse Mapping (e.g., ORB-SLAM) | Dense RGB-D Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| Points used | ~100 – 500 keypoints/frame | Every pixel (e.g., 640×480 ≈ 307,200 pts) |
| Output | Sparse 3D point cloud + graph | Truncated SDF, voxel grid, mesh |
| Compute cost | Low (CPU real-time) | High (often GPU accelerated) |
| Application | Localization, loop closure | Obstacle avoidance, navigation, AR/VR |
| Surface continuity | Discrete, fragmented | Continuous, manifold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **primary engineering trade-off** in dense mapping is between **reconstruction fidelity** (resolution, completeness, accuracy) and **computational/memory cost** (voxel grid size, fusion rate). KTU expects students to discuss **TSDF volumetric fusion** as the canonical dense-mapping solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The RGB-D Mapping Pipeline — Six Logical Steps

1. **Sensor Data Acquisition:** Capture synchronized $\{I_{rgb}^{(t)}, I_{d}^{(t)}\}$ at time $t$ from the moving RGB-D camera.
2. **Camera Pose Estimation:** Obtain the 6-DoF sensor pose $\mathbf{T}^{(t)}_{WC} \in SE(3)$ from an odometry/SLAM front-end (e.g., ICP, visual odometry, IMU fusion).
3. **Depth Pre-processing:** Apply bilateral filtering / inpainting to fill depth holes caused by reflective, transparent, or distant surfaces.
4. **Back-Projection (Unprojection):** Convert each valid depth pixel $(u,v,d)$ into a 3D point $\mathbf{p}_C = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ in the camera frame using the **pinhole inverse model**.
5. **Global Registration (Pose Application):** Transform each $\mathbf{p}_C$ into the world frame: $\mathbf{p}_W = \mathbf{T}^{(t)}_{WC} \cdot \mathbf{p}_C$.
6. **Volumetric Fusion / Surface Reconstruction:** Accumulate points into a global representation (TSDF, occupancy grid, or mesh) and extract the implicit/explicit surface.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Pinhole Camera Model (Forward & Inverse)

The standard pinhole projection of a 3D camera-frame point $\mathbf{p}_C = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)^{T}$ onto the image plane is:

$$u = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x \qquad v = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

where $f_x, f_y$ are **focal lengths in pixels**, and $(c_x, c_y)$ is the **principal point** — all four are stored in the camera **intrinsic matrix** $\mathbf{K}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The intrinsic matrix is a **property of the sensor**, not the robot pose. KTU students must remember that the same $\mathbf{K}$ is used for every frame, while the extrinsics $\mathbf{T}_{WC}$ change with robot motion.

### 2.2.2 Depth-to-3D Back-Projection (Inverse)

Given a pixel $(u, v)$ with measured depth $d = Z_c$, the back-projected 3D point in the camera frame is:

$$X_c = \frac{(u - c_x)}{f_x} \, d \qquad Y_c = \frac{(v - c_y)}{f_y} \, d \qquad Z_c = d$$

This can be written compactly as:

$$\mathbf{p}_C = d \cdot \mathbf{K}^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

where $\tilde{\mathbf{m}} = (u, v, 1)^{T}$ is the **homogeneous image coordinate**.

### 2.2.3 World-Frame Transformation

Once $\mathbf{p}_C$ is obtained, transforming it into the global world frame requires the camera-to-world rigid-body transform $\mathbf{T}_{WC} \in SE(3)$:

$$\mathbf{p}_W = \mathbf{R}_{WC} \, \mathbf{p}_C + \mathbf{t}_{WC}$$

$$\mathbf{p}_W = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{WC} & \mathbf{t}_{WC} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{p}_C \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\mathbf{R}_{WC} \in SO(3)$ is a **3×3 rotation matrix** (det = +1, orthonormal), and $\mathbf{t}_{WC} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the translation vector. The pose $\mathbf{T}_{WC}$ is what the SLAM/odometry front-end supplies per frame.

### 2.2.4 Truncated Signed Distance Function (TSDF)

The TSDF is the **gold standard** in dense RGB-D mapping. For any voxel center $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ in the world frame, the **signed distance** to the nearest true surface is:

$$\text{SDF}(\mathbf{v}) = \text{sgn}\bigl(\mathbf{n}^{T}(\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x}_{s})\bigr) \cdot \bigl\Vert \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x}_{s} \bigr\Vert$$

where $\mathbf{x}_s$ is the nearest point on the surface and $\mathbf{n}$ is the surface normal at $\mathbf{x}_s$.

For computational tractability, the SDF is **truncated** to a small band $\pm \mu$ around the surface:

$$\text{TSDF}(\mathbf{v}) = \begin{cases} \text{SDF}(\mathbf{v}) & \text{if } \vert \text{SDF}(\mathbf{v}) \vert \leq \mu \\ \text{undefined} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\mu$ is a user-defined **truncation distance** (typically $3\times$ to $5\times$ the voxel size). The truncation keeps memory bounded — only voxels *near* observed surfaces are stored, not the entire 3D volume.

### 2.2.5 Per-Frame TSDF Update (Fusion)

When a new depth frame $I_{d}^{(t)}$ is observed from pose $\mathbf{T}_{WC}^{(t)}$, each voxel $\mathbf{v}$ along the camera ray is updated using a **running weighted average**:

$$\text{TSDF}_{k}(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) \cdot \text{TSDF}_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_{k}(\mathbf{v}) \cdot \text{tsdf}_{k}(\mathbf{v})}{W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_{k}(\mathbf{v})}$$

$$W_{k}(\mathbf{v}) = W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_{k}(\mathbf{v})$$

where $w_{k}(\mathbf{v})$ is a per-frame weight (commonly $w = \cos(\theta) / Z_{v}^{2}$ to downweight grazing-angle and far-away measurements).

### 2.2.6 Iterative Closest Point (ICP) for Pose Refinement

ICP iteratively aligns two point sets $\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{p}_i\}$ (current) and $\mathcal{Q} = \{\mathbf{q}_i\}$ (target) by minimizing:

$$\min_{\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\Vert \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_{c(i)} \bigr\Vert^{2}$$

subject to $\mathbf{R}^{T}\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\det(\mathbf{R}) = +1$. The correspondence $c(i)$ is established by nearest-neighbor search in $\mathcal{Q}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Key Symbols / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u = f_x X_c / Z_c + c_x$ | Pinhole projection (x-axis) | $f_x$: pixel focal length, $c_x$: pixel |
| 2 | $v = f_y Y_c / Z_c + c_y$ | Pinhole projection (y-axis) | $f_y$: pixel focal length, $c_y$: pixel |
| 3 | $X_c = (u - c_x) d / f_x$ | Depth → 3D (x) | $d$: meters |
| 4 | $Y_c = (v - c_y) d / f_y$ | Depth → 3D (y) | $d$: meters |
| 5 | $Z_c = d$ | Depth → 3D (z) | $d$: meters |
| 6 | $\mathbf{p}_W = \mathbf{R}_{WC} \mathbf{p}_C + \mathbf{t}_{WC}$ | Camera-to-world transform | $\mathbf{R}_{WC} \in SO(3)$ |
| 7 | $\text{TSDF}(\mathbf{v}) = \text{sgn}(\cdot) \cdot \min\bigl(\lVert \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x}_s \rVert, \mu\bigr)$ | Truncated signed distance | $\mu$: meters (typically 0.03) |
| 8 | $\text{TSDF}_{k} = (W_{k-1}\text{TSDF}_{k-1} + w_k \text{tsdf}_k) / W_k$ | Volumetric fusion update | $W_k$: scalar weight |
| 9 | $w_k = \cos(\theta) / Z_v^{2}$ | Per-frame weight | $\theta$: ray-voxel angle, $Z_v$: m |
| 10 | $\min \sum \lVert \mathbf{R}\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{q}_{c(i)} \rVert^{2}$ | ICP objective | $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3)$ |
| 11 | $\mathbf{R}^{\star} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{U}^{T}$ from $\mathbf{H} = \sum \mathbf{p}_i \mathbf{q}_i^{T}$ | SVD-based ICP solution | $\mathbf{U}\Sigma\mathbf{V}^{T} = \text{svd}(\mathbf{H})$ |
| 12 | $\text{rmse} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum (d_i - \hat{d}_i)^{2}}$ | Depth/TSDF error metric | unit: meters |
| 13 | $\text{resolution} = \frac{\text{voxel size}}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | Theoretical surface accuracy | meters |
| 14 | $\rho_{\text{ray}} = d_{\max} / (u_{\max} - u_{\min})$ | Angular resolution per pixel | rad / pixel |
| 15 | $F = \#\text{surface voxels} / \#\text{occupied voxels}$ | Surface fidelity ratio | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Memorize equations **3–6** (depth back-projection + world transform) and **7–8** (TSDF definition + fusion update) at minimum. These are the most frequently asked in 14-mark derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMR):** Dense maps feed obstacle avoidance planners (e.g., Euclidean Signed Distance Fields in MoveIt / Nav2).
- **AR/VR (Apple ARKit, Microsoft HoloLens):** Real-time TSDF fusion is the core of on-device scene understanding.
- **Construction & Mining Metrology:** Surveying robots build mm-accurate as-built 3D models of building sites.
- **Domestic Service Robots (Roomba-class, Stretch):** Dense maps enable semantic room segmentation and grasp-pose estimation.
- **Medical Robotics:** Intra-operative 3D reconstruction of patient anatomy from depth endoscopes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Depth-Pixel to 3D Point (Pinhole Back-Projection)

**Goal:** Show that a depth image pixel $(u, v, d)$ can be unprojected into a 3D point using the camera intrinsics.

**Starting Point — the pinhole projection** of a camera-frame point $\mathbf{p}_C = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)^{T}$ is given by:

$$u = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

**Step 1:** Isolate the scaled coordinates $(X_c / Z_c)$ and $(Y_c / Z_c)$ by subtracting the principal point and dividing by the focal length:

$$\frac{X_c}{Z_c} = \frac{u - c_x}{f_x}, \quad \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} = \frac{v - c_y}{f_y}$$

**Step 2:** Recall that the depth measurement $d$ from an RGB-D sensor is, by definition, the **$Z$-coordinate** of the observed surface in the camera frame:

$$Z_c = d$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $Z_c = d$ into the ratios from Step 1, then multiply both sides by $d$:

$$X_c = \frac{(u - c_x)}{f_x} \, d, \quad Y_c = \frac{(v - c_y)}{f_y} \, d$$

**Step 4:** Assemble the result as a single 3D column vector $\mathbf{p}_C$:

$$\mathbf{p}_C = \begin{pmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{pmatrix} = d \begin{pmatrix} (u - c_x) / f_x \\ (v - c_y) / f_y \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Express in compact form using the homogeneous image coordinate $\tilde{\mathbf{m}} = (u, v, 1)^{T}$ and the inverse intrinsics $\mathbf{K}^{-1}$:

$$\mathbf{p}_C = d \cdot \mathbf{K}^{-1} \, \tilde{\mathbf{m}}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{K}^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/f_x & 0 & -c_x/f_x \\ 0 & 1/f_y & -c_y/f_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Every pixel in a depth image can be unprojected into a 3D point *individually* without any prior knowledge of the scene — that's the defining power of an RGB-D sensor. A monocular camera, by contrast, only gives the *direction* $(u,v)$ and must triangulate depth across frames.

## 3.2 Derivation B — ICP Closed-Form Solution via SVD

**Goal:** Derive the optimal rotation $\mathbf{R}^{\star}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}^{\star}$ that align two corresponding point sets.

**Step 1 — Setup the objective.** Given correspondences $\{(\mathbf{p}_i, \mathbf{q}_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, define the error function:

$$E(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\Vert \mathbf{q}_i - \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{t} \bigr\Vert^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Optimal translation.** Set $\partial E / \partial \mathbf{t} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{t}} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl(\mathbf{q}_i - \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{t}\bigr) = 0$$

Solving for $\mathbf{t}$ gives:

$$\mathbf{t}^{\star} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R} \bar{\mathbf{p}}, \quad \text{where } \bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{p}_i, \quad \bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{q}_i$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to remove translation.** Let $\mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}}$ and $\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}}$. Then:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl\Vert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_i \bigr\Vert^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl( \lVert \mathbf{y}_i \rVert^{2} + \lVert \mathbf{x}_i \rVert^{2} - 2 \mathbf{y}_i^{T} \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_i \bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Maximize the cross term.** The first two sums are constant in $\mathbf{R}$, so minimizing $E$ is equivalent to maximizing:

$$J(\mathbf{R}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{y}_i^{T} \mathbf{R} \mathbf{x}_i = \text{tr}\!\Bigl(\mathbf{R} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{y}_i^{T}\Bigr) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{R} \mathbf{H})$$

where the $3 \times 3$ cross-covariance matrix is:

$$\mathbf{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{y}_i^{T}$$

**Step 5 — SVD of $\mathbf{H}$.** Compute the singular value decomposition:

$$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}, \quad \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3} \text{ orthogonal}, \quad \boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the rotation orthonormality constraint** ($\mathbf{R}^{T}\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$). The standard result is:

$$\mathbf{R}^{\star} = \mathbf{V} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{T}) \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{U}^{T}$$

The diagonal matrix in the middle is the identity unless $\det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{T}) = -1$, in which case a reflection correction is applied to prevent $\mathbf{R}$ from being a *reflection* (det = −1, invalid for rigid motion).

**Step 7 — Compute the final translation:**

$$\mathbf{t}^{\star} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}^{\star} \bar{\mathbf{p}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **det correction step**. Without it, ICP can converge to a mirror solution (reflection), which has $\det(\mathbf{R}) = -1$ and is *not* a valid rigid-body transform in $SE(3)$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

## 3.3 Derivation C — TSDF Update Rule (Running Average)

**Goal:** Derive the recursive weighted-mean formula used to fuse multiple depth observations into a single TSDF voxel value.

**Step 1 — Goal:** Maintain a running estimate of the mean signed distance at voxel $\mathbf{v}$ over $k$ observations, where each observation contributes weight $w_j$ and value $\text{tsdf}_j$:

$$\bar{x}_k = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j \, \text{tsdf}_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j}$$

**Step 2 — Recursive form.** Write the numerator and denominator for $k$ and $k-1$:

$$N_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j \, \text{tsdf}_j = N_{k-1} + w_k \text{tsdf}_k, \quad D_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_j = D_{k-1} + w_k$$

**Step 3 — Combine.** Recognizing that $N_{k-1} = D_{k-1} \bar{x}_{k-1}$:

$$\bar{x}_k = \frac{D_{k-1} \bar{x}_{k-1} + w_k \text{tsdf}_k}{D_{k-1} + w_k}$$

**Step 4 — In TSDF notation** with $W_k = D_k$:

$$\text{TSDF}_k(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) \cdot \text{TSDF}_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_k(\mathbf{v}) \cdot \text{tsdf}_k(\mathbf{v})}{W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_k(\mathbf{v})}$$

**Step 5 — Convergence.** If all weights are equal, $\bar{x}_k \to \bar{x}_{\infty}$ as $k \to \infty$ — i.e., the TSDF estimate converges to the true mean distance as more frames are fused.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a weighted average, not a hard assignment?** Because the same voxel is observed multiple times from different poses, each with different noise levels and ray-incidence angles. The weight $w_k$ lets us trust some observations more than others (e.g., near, perpendicular rays > far, grazing rays).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Dense Mapping Methods

| Criterion | Volumetric TSDF | Point-Cloud Fusion | Surfel / Mesh |
|---|---|---|---|
| Representation | Voxel grid of signed distances | Unordered set of 3D points | Oriented discs or polygon mesh |
| Memory | $O(V)$ for $V$ voxels in truncation band | $O(P)$ for $P$ points | $O(S)$ for $S$ surfels |
| Update cost | Per-voxel ray marching (heavy) | Append + optional filter | Per-surfel blending |
| Surface extraction | **Marching Cubes** (zero level set) | Direct / Poisson reconstruction | Mesh decimation |
| Best for | High-quality offline reconstruction | Real-time visualization | Rendering, AR |
| KTU-referenced implementation | KinectFusion (Newcombe et al., 2011) | ElasticFusion, RTAB-Map | SurfelMeshing, InfiniTAM |
| Noise robustness | Excellent (averaging) | Moderate | Good |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End RGB-D Dense Mapping Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RGB-D Sensor] --> B[Synchronized RGB Image + Depth Image]
    B --> C[Depth Pre-processing: Bilateral Filter + Hole Inpainting]
    C --> D[Pose Estimation: ICP / Visual Odometry / IMU Fusion]
    D --> E[Camera Intrinsics: K Matrix]
    E --> F[Back-Projection: Depth Pixel to 3D Point Cloud]
    F --> G[World Frame Transform: apply T_WC]
    G --> H[Volumetric TSDF Fusion: Update Voxel Grid]
    H --> I[Marching Cubes: Extract Zero Level Set]
    I --> J[Polygon Mesh / Dense 3D Map]
    H --> K[Occupancy Grid for Navigation]
    J --> L[Mobile Robot: Localization + Planning + AR Visualization]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 TSDF Voxel Update Logic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New Depth Frame] --> B[Cast Ray from Camera Origin through Pixel]
    B --> C[For each Voxel v on Ray]
    C --> D{v within truncation band mu?}
    D -- No --> Z[Skip Voxel]
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute Signed Distance: SDF v]
    E --> F[Truncate: tsdf = clamp SDF to plus or minus mu]
    F --> G[Compute Weight: w = cos theta divided by Z squared]
    G --> H[Weighted Average Update: TSDF k = W k-1 TSDF k-1 + w tsdf divided by W k-1 + w]
    H --> I[Increment Weight: W k = W k-1 + w]
    I --> J[Mark Voxel as Observed]
```

## 4.3 ICP Iterative Registration Loop (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Point Cloud P from Frame k-1] --> B[Target Point Cloud Q from Frame k]
    B --> C[Establish Correspondences: nearest neighbor in Q for each p in P]
    C --> D[Compute Centroids: p bar and q bar]
    D --> E[Form Cross Covariance: H = sum x_i y_i transpose]
    E --> F[SVD: H = U Sigma V transpose]
    F --> G[Compute R star = V diag 1 1 det V U transpose U transpose]
    G --> H[Compute t star = q bar - R star p bar]
    H --> I[Apply Transform: P new = R star P + t star]
    I --> J{Error below threshold or max iterations reached?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Output Final Pose T WC for Frame k]
```

## 4.4 Pinhole Camera Back-Projection Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pixel Coordinates u, v] --> B[Subtract Principal Point: u minus cx, v minus cy]
    B --> C[Divide by Focal Length: divide by fx, divide by fy]
    C --> D[Multiply by Depth d]
    D --> E[3D Point in Camera Frame: Xc, Yc, Zc equals d]
    E --> F[Apply Rotation R WC]
    F --> G[Apply Translation t WC]
    G --> H[3D Point in World Frame: Xw, Yw, Zw]
    E -. Extrinsic T WC .-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram Instead of a 2D Camera Sketch?** A perspective-view drawing of a pinhole camera is best drawn freehand on paper. The Mermaid block diagram above captures the *mathematical* data flow — which is what the KTU examiner expects students to draw under exam time pressure.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> *Define RGB-D mapping. List any two advantages of dense mapping over sparse feature-based SLAM.*

**Model Answer (verbatim points an examiner expects):**

**Definition:** RGB-D mapping is the process of building a dense 3D model of an environment using synchronized **color (RGB)** and **per-pixel depth (D)** images from an RGB-D camera, fusing measurements across multiple sensor poses into a common world frame.

**Two Advantages (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Complete surface reconstruction** — captures *every* visible surface point, enabling accurate obstacle representation for navigation and manipulation, unlike sparse maps that only have isolated keypoints.
2. **No feature extraction dependency** — does not require distinctive texture or repeatable keypoints, hence works in low-texture industrial corridors where ORB/SIFT SLAM fails.
3. *(Bonus 3rd point)* Provides **metric, scale-accurate geometry** directly from depth, avoiding the scale-ambiguity problem of monocular SLAM.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> *What is a Truncated Signed Distance Function (TSDF) in the context of dense RGB-D mapping? Why is the SDF "truncated"?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A TSDF is a volumetric representation in which each voxel stores the **signed perpendicular distance** from its center to the nearest true surface in the scene, truncated to a bounded interval $\bigl[-\mu, +\mu\bigr]$ around the surface. Voxels with $|\text{SDF}| > \mu$ are marked as **undefined** (or "free space beyond truncation") and excluded from fusion.

**Why truncated? (1 mark):** Truncation limits the **active region** of the volume to a thin band around observed surfaces. This:
- Reduces **memory** from $O(V_{\text{volume}})$ to $O(V_{\text{band}}) \ll O(V_{\text{volume}})$.
- Speeds up **ray casting** during integration (only voxels in the band are updated).
- Avoids storing meaningless large-distance values that have no contribution to surface reconstruction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **pinhole camera model** and derive the equations to back-project a 2D depth pixel $(u, v, d)$ into a 3D point in the **camera frame** and then transform it into the **world frame** using the camera extrinsic matrix $\mathbf{T}_{WC}$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Truncated Signed Distance Function (TSDF)** representation for dense 3D mapping. Derive the **weighted-average update rule** for fusing a new depth frame into an existing TSDF voxel grid. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pinhole Model (2 marks).** In the pinhole camera model, a 3D point $\mathbf{p}_C = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)^{T}$ in the camera frame is projected onto the image plane at pixel $(u, v)$ according to:

$$u = f_x \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

where $f_x, f_y$ are the **focal lengths** (in pixels) and $(c_x, c_y)$ is the **principal point** (image center). The matrix form uses intrinsics $\mathbf{K}$:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{m}} = \frac{1}{Z_c}\mathbf{K} \begin{pmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Back-Projection (3 marks).** The depth value at pixel $(u, v)$ measured by the RGB-D sensor is $d = Z_c$. Solving the projection equations for $(X_c, Y_c)$:

$$X_c = \frac{(u - c_x)}{f_x} \, d, \quad Y_c = \frac{(v - c_y)}{f_y} \, d, \quad Z_c = d$$

In compact matrix form:

$$\mathbf{p}_C = d \cdot \mathbf{K}^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — World-Frame Transformation (2 marks).** Let the camera pose be $\mathbf{T}_{WC} \in SE(3)$ with rotation $\mathbf{R}_{WC}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}_{WC}$. The world-frame coordinate is:

$$\mathbf{p}_W = \mathbf{R}_{WC} \mathbf{p}_C + \mathbf{t}_{WC} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{p}_W \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R}_{WC} & \mathbf{t}_{WC} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{p}_C \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Block Diagram:**

```
Pixel (u, v, d)  →  Subtract (cx, cy)  →  Divide by (fx, fy)  →  Multiply by d
       ↓
   3D point in camera frame pc
       ↓
   Apply R_WC and t_WC
       ↓
   3D point in world frame pw
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — TSDF Definition (2 marks).** For any 3D point $\mathbf{v}$ in the world frame, the **signed distance function** to the nearest true surface point $\mathbf{x}_s$ (with surface normal $\mathbf{n}$) is:

$$\text{SDF}(\mathbf{v}) = \text{sgn}\!\bigl(\mathbf{n}^{T}(\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x}_s)\bigr) \cdot \lVert \mathbf{v} - \mathbf{x}_s \rVert$$

The sign is **negative** in front of the surface (toward the camera), **positive** behind, and **zero** on the surface. The **truncated** version clamps to a band of half-width $\mu$ around the surface:

$$\text{TSDF}(\mathbf{v}) = \max\!\bigl(-\mu, \min(\mu, \text{SDF}(\mathbf{v}))\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Per-Frame Measurement (2 marks).** When a new depth frame $I_{d}^{(k)}$ is available, the camera ray from $\mathbf{c}^{(k)}$ to the observed surface point $\mathbf{x}_{s}^{(k)}$ is ray-marched. For each voxel $\mathbf{v}$ in the truncation band, the per-frame TSDF is:

$$\text{tsdf}_k(\mathbf{v}) = \text{depth}_{\text{surface}}(\mathbf{v}) - \text{depth}_{\text{voxel}}(\mathbf{v})$$

where $\text{depth}_{\text{surface}}$ is the measured distance along the ray to the surface and $\text{depth}_{\text{voxel}}$ is the distance to the voxel center.

**Step 3 — Weighted Update (3 marks).** To fuse the new measurement with the existing TSDF, a **recursive weighted average** is used. With old weight $W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v})$ and old TSDF $\text{TSDF}_{k-1}(\mathbf{v})$:

$$\text{TSDF}_{k}(\mathbf{v}) = \frac{W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) \cdot \text{TSDF}_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_k(\mathbf{v}) \cdot \text{tsdf}_k(\mathbf{v})}{W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_k(\mathbf{v})}$$

$$W_{k}(\mathbf{v}) = W_{k-1}(\mathbf{v}) + w_k(\mathbf{v})$$

The per-frame weight $w_k = \cos(\theta)/Z_v^{2}$ down-weights **grazing-angle** ($\theta \to 90°$) and **far-away** ($Z_v$ large) measurements, since both have higher depth noise.

**Surface Extraction (zero level set):**

$$\text{Surface} = \{\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{3} : \text{TSDF}(\mathbf{v}) = 0\}$$

In practice, this is computed using the **Marching Cubes** algorithm on the discrete voxel grid.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **(a)** Describe the **Iterative Closest Point (ICP) algorithm** for aligning two RGB-D point clouds. Derive the **closed-form rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}^{\star}$** using the SVD of the cross-covariance matrix. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compare **volumetric TSDF mapping**, **point-cloud fusion**, and **surfel-based mapping** for mobile robot applications. Justify which is most suitable for real-time indoor SLAM on a low-power embedded platform. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Problem Statement (1 mark).** Given source point cloud $\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{p}_i\}$ and target $\mathcal{Q} = \{\mathbf{q}_i\}$, find $\mathbf{R} \in SO(3), \mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ minimizing:

$$E(\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lVert \mathbf{q}_i - \mathbf{R} \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{t} \rVert^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Iterative Algorithm Outline (2 marks):**
1. Initialize $\mathbf{R}_0 = \mathbf{I}$, $\mathbf{t}_0 = \mathbf{0}$.
2. For each iteration $j$:
   - **Correspondence step:** $c(i) = \arg\min_k \lVert \mathbf{q}_k - \mathbf{R}_{j-1} \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{t}_{j-1} \rVert$ (nearest-neighbor in target).
   - **Alignment step:** Solve closed-form $(\mathbf{R}_j, \mathbf{t}_j)$ using correspondences.
   - **Convergence test:** Stop if $\lVert \Delta \mathbf{R} \rVert + \lVert \Delta \mathbf{t} \rVert < \epsilon$.

**Step 3 — Closed-Form Derivation (4 marks):**

*Optimal translation:* Centering the data with $\bar{\mathbf{p}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum \mathbf{p}_i$ and $\bar{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum \mathbf{q}_i$, the optimal translation is:

$$\mathbf{t}^{\star} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R} \bar{\mathbf{p}}$$

*Cross-covariance matrix:*

$$\mathbf{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}})(\mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}})^{T}$$

*SVD of $\mathbf{H}$:*

$$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^{T}$$

*Optimal rotation (with det correction):*

$$\mathbf{R}^{\star} = \mathbf{V} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{T}) \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{U}^{T}$$

*Substitute back:*

$$\mathbf{t}^{\star} = \bar{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{R}^{\star} \bar{\mathbf{p}}$$

**Convergence:** ICP is **locally convergent** — requires a good initial alignment (typically within 30° rotation and 0.5 m translation). For larger displacements, a **coarse-to-fine pyramid** or **feature-based pre-alignment** is needed.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Volumetric TSDF | Point-Cloud Fusion | Surfel-Based |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory | High (voxel grid) | Moderate (raw points) | Moderate (oriented discs) |
| Real-time on CPU | Difficult | Easy | Moderate |
| Real-time on GPU | Excellent (KinectFusion) | Good | Good |
| Surface smoothness | Excellent (averaged) | Noisy, no topology | Good (mesh from discs) |
| Loop closure | Requires re-integration | Easy (submap replace) | Moderate |
| Best for | Offline high-quality | Online low-power | AR rendering |

**Justification for Low-Power Embedded SLAM (3 marks):**
For a low-power embedded mobile robot (e.g., Jetson Nano, Raspberry Pi + RealSense), the recommended choice is **point-cloud fusion** with an octree (e.g., RTAB-Map / ORB-SLAM3 with depth). Reasons:
1. **Memory bounded** by octree resolution; TSDF voxel grids require fixed memory and waste voxels in empty space.
2. **CPU-only** real-time performance is achievable; TSDF on CPU struggles beyond 256³ voxel grids.
3. **Loop closure integration** is straightforward — old submap point clouds can be replaced without re-marching the volume.
4. **No GPU dependency** — TSDF-based KinectFusion-class systems require a CUDA-capable GPU.

Volumetric TSDF is preferred only when **highest geometric fidelity** is required (e.g., metrology, AR/VR headsets with dedicated GPU). Surfel mapping is a balanced compromise, popular in systems like ElasticFusion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in RGB-D Mapping Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the principal-point offset** $(c_x, c_y)$ when back-projecting — partial credit only if at least one of the four intrinsics is used correctly. (-1.5 marks)
> 2. **Writing the ICP objective without the rigid-body constraint** $\mathbf{R}^{T}\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{I}$ — KTU treats this as a *mathematically ill-posed* statement. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Omitting the determinant correction** $\det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{T})$ in the SVD-based ICP solution — yields an invalid reflection. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Confusing TSDF with an occupancy grid** — TSDF stores *signed distance*, not binary/free/occupied. Examiners explicitly check for this distinction. (-1.5 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting the truncation** $\mu$ — calling it a plain SDF. Always write "truncated" SDF. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the unit of depth** ($d$ in **meters**, not millimeters). RealSense and Kinect return depth in meters; some ROS drivers in mm. Mis-scaling by 1000× propagates catastrophically. (-2 marks)
> 7. **Writing TSDF as a *function* of the *camera* instead of the *voxel*** — examiners deduct for confusing the dependent variable. Always write $\text{TSDF}(\mathbf{v})$ with $\mathbf{v}$ being a *voxel center in the world frame*.
> 8. **No diagram** in a 7-mark "with a neat diagram" question — minimum 2 marks reserved for a labeled block diagram or pinhole schematic. Drawing only equations → lose all 2.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RGB-D Mapping — 60-Second Recall Sheet**

- ☐ **RGB-D sensor** = color (RGB) + per-pixel depth (D), both spatially and temporally registered.
- ☐ **Intrinsics $\mathbf{K}$** = $\{f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y\}$ — fixed for a given sensor; do not change with pose.
- ☐ **Extrinsics $\mathbf{T}_{WC}$** = sensor pose in world — changes every frame; supplied by odometry/SLAM.
- ☐ **Back-projection formula (memorize):** $X_c = (u - c_x) d / f_x, \; Y_c = (v - c_y) d / f_y, \; Z_c = d$.
- ☐ **World transform (memorize):** $\mathbf{p}_W = \mathbf{R}_{WC} \mathbf{p}_C + \mathbf{t}_{WC}$.
- ☐ **TSDF** = signed distance to nearest surface, **truncated** to $\pm \mu$ (typical $\mu \approx 3 \times$ voxel size).
- ☐ **TSDF update** = recursive weighted average: $T_k = (W_{k-1} T_{k-1} + w_k t_k) / (W_{k-1} + w_k)$.
- ☐ **Per-frame weight** $w = \cos(\theta) / Z^2$ — favors near, perpendicular rays; penalizes grazing/far rays.
- ☐ **Surface = zero level set** of TSDF; extracted via Marching Cubes.
- ☐ **ICP** aligns two point clouds; closed-form via SVD of cross-covariance $\mathbf{H} = \sum (\mathbf{p}_i - \bar{\mathbf{p}})(\mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}})^{T}$.
- ☐ **Determinant correction** in ICP: $\mathbf{R}^{\star} = \mathbf{V} \cdot \text{diag}(1, 1, \det(\mathbf{V}\mathbf{U}^{T})) \cdot \mathbf{U}^{T}$ — prevents reflection.
- ☐ **ICP is locally convergent** — needs good initial guess; use pyramid or feature-based pre-alignment.
- ☐ **Three dense representations:** TSDF (voxel grid), point cloud (raw), surfel (oriented disc).
- ☐ **Trade-off:** TSDF = best surface quality, highest memory; point cloud = lowest memory, no topology; surfel = balanced.
- ☐ **Low-power platforms → point-cloud fusion (with octree)**; **High-end GPU → TSDF / KinectFusion**.
- ☐ **KinectFusion** (Newcombe et al., 2011) is the canonical TSDF-based dense SLAM system — cite by name in exam answers.
- ☐ **Marching Cubes** is the algorithm to extract a polygon mesh from the TSDF zero level set.
- ☐ **Depth pre-processing** = bilateral filter + hole inpainting; necessary because real RGB-D sensors have ~5–15% missing pixels.
- ☐ **Evaluation metrics:** RMSE of TSDF (m), surface coverage (%), completeness (%).
